- **Common Mistakes:** Treating Impressions as a proxy for genuine attention or message reception, when it only confirms an opportunity for a user to see the Ad, not that they consciously registered it.
- **Professional Notes:** Impressions alone, absent Reach and Frequency context, are of limited diagnostic value — the three are conventionally interpreted together.

### MF1.3.29 Clicks

- **Official Name:** Clicks
- **Alternative Names:** Link Clicks (the specific, most commonly optimized/reported click type; "Clicks (all)" is the broader superset including non-link clicks such as reactions or profile visits)
- **Definition:** The count of times users clicked on an Ad, most commonly reported and optimized as Link Clicks specifically — clicks that navigate the user toward the advertiser's specified destination (a URL, App store, Instant Form, or Messenger/WhatsApp thread).
- **Business Purpose:** Represents the first concrete behavioral response beyond passive viewing, indicating a user's interest was sufficient to act.
- **Meta Optimization Purpose:** The primary optimization currency for the Traffic Objective (MF1.3.12) via the Link Clicks Optimization Goal; also a key numerator for CTR (MF1.4).
- **Inputs:** User interactions with a delivered Ad.
- **Outputs:** A click count, segmentable into Link Clicks vs. all Clicks.
- **Dependencies:** Requires active Delivery and a Creative with a clickable destination.
- **Relationships:** A component of CTR (MF1.4); a common precursor step toward Landing Page Views, Conversions, and other downstream Events (MF1.6).
- **Lifecycle:** Accrues continuously as Delivery occurs.
- **Ownership:** A measured Metric resulting from user behavior; not directly set by the advertiser.
- **Examples:** 12,000 Link Clicks from 500,000 Impressions, yielding a CTR of 2.4%.
- **Common Mistakes:** Conflating "Clicks (all)" with "Link Clicks" when reporting or optimizing, materially overstating genuine traffic-driving performance since "Clicks (all)" includes non-navigational interactions.
- **Professional Notes:** A high Click volume with low downstream Landing Page Views is a common diagnostic pattern indicating landing-page load performance or click-fraud/bot-traffic concerns, addressed further in MF1.4 and MF1.7 (Attribution Vocabulary).

### MF1.3.30 Conversions

- **Official Name:** Conversions
- **Alternative Names:** Conversion Events (in the specific sense of the Events used to measure them)
- **Definition:** The count of times a defined Optimization Event (such as Purchase, Lead, or Complete Registration) occurred and was attributed to exposure to or interaction with an Ad, within the applicable Attribution Window.
- **Business Purpose:** Represents the count of genuine business outcomes (sales, leads, registrations) an advertiser's spend produced.
- **Meta Optimization Purpose:** The primary optimization currency for the Leads and Sales Objectives (MF1.3.12); the numerator for CPA and the basis for Purchase-value-driven ROAS (both MF1.4).
- **Inputs:** Pixel, Conversions API, or App SDK event data, matched to ad exposure/interaction via Attribution.
- **Outputs:** A conversion count, and (for value-tracked Events like Purchase) an associated conversion value total.
- **Dependencies:** Requires a properly configured Pixel/Conversions API/Dataset (MF1.3.6–MF1.3.8) and a defined Optimization Event; subject to Attribution Window and Attribution Model settings (MF1.7).
- **Relationships:** A specialization of Event (MF1.3.9) in its attributed-to-advertising sense; the numerator of CPA and a factor in ROAS (MF1.4).
- **Lifecycle:** Accrues continuously subject to Attribution Window recency (a conversion may be attributed and reported up to the Attribution Window's length after the triggering ad interaction).
- **Ownership:** A measured Metric, but critically dependent on the advertiser's own Event tracking implementation quality (Matching Quality, MF1.6).
- **Examples:** 340 Purchase conversions attributed within a 7-day-click Attribution Window.
- **Common Mistakes:** Comparing Conversion counts across time periods or accounts without accounting for differing Attribution Window or Attribution Model settings, which can make outcomes look inconsistent for reasons unrelated to actual performance change.
- **Professional Notes:** Conversion counts reported by Meta are modeled/attributed figures, not a ground-truth ledger — see MF1.7 (Attribution Vocabulary) for the full treatment of why Meta-reported and source-of-truth (e.g., CRM or e-commerce platform) conversion counts commonly diverge.

### MF1.3.31 Purchase

- **Official Name:** Purchase
- **Alternative Names:** None in current common usage.
- **Definition:** A specific, standard Event (MF1.3.9) representing the completion of a transaction, typically carrying an associated monetary value and currency, and the single most common Optimization Event for the Sales Objective.
- **Business Purpose:** Represents realized revenue directly attributable to advertising.
- **Meta Optimization Purpose:** The Event most commonly used as the Optimization Goal for Sales-objective Ad Sets, and the value input to ROAS (MF1.4).
- **Inputs:** A completed transaction on the advertiser's website, app, or Catalog-driven checkout experience, fired via Pixel, Conversions API, or App SDK.
- **Outputs:** A Purchase Event record including a value and currency, attributed via Attribution Window/Model.
- **Dependencies:** Requires Pixel, Conversions API, or App SDK integration at the point of transaction completion.
- **Relationships:** A specialization of Event (MF1.3.9); the value input to ROAS; the anchor Event for Catalog Sales optimization (via Product Set, MF1.3.36).
- **Lifecycle:** Fires once per completed transaction; may be deduplicated against a browser-side and server-side firing of the same underlying transaction (Deduplication, MF1.6).
- **Ownership:** Owned/implemented by the advertiser's website/app/e-commerce platform integration.
- **Examples:** A Purchase Event firing with `value: 89.99, currency: USD` upon checkout completion.
- **Common Mistakes:** Failing to pass accurate transaction value on the Purchase Event, which silently breaks ROAS calculation even though the Purchase count itself continues to look correct.
- **Professional Notes:** Server-side (Conversions API) Purchase Events are increasingly critical for accurate measurement as browser-side tracking degrades due to privacy changes — Meta's Matching Quality scoring (MF1.6) specifically rewards well-implemented server-side matching for high-value Events like Purchase.

### MF1.3.32 Lead

- **Official Name:** Lead
- **Alternative Names:** None in current common usage.
- **Definition:** A specific, standard Event (MF1.3.9) representing a user expressing interest by submitting contact or qualifying information, whether via a website form (Pixel/Conversions API), an Instant Form (MF1.3.35) natively within Meta's platform, Messaging, or a phone Call.
- **Business Purpose:** Represents a prospective customer's expressed interest, the primary business outcome for the Leads Objective.
- **Meta Optimization Purpose:** The Optimization Event most commonly used as the Optimization Goal for Leads-objective Ad Sets, whichever lead-capture mechanism (website, Instant Form, Messaging, Calls) is in use.
- **Inputs:** A form submission, Instant Form completion, qualifying message exchange, or tracked phone call.
- **Outputs:** A Lead Event record, optionally carrying qualifying field data (for Instant Forms) or conversation content (for Messaging-based leads).
- **Dependencies:** Requires Pixel/Conversions API integration (website Leads), an Instant Form configuration (native Leads), or Messaging/Call tracking setup, matching the chosen lead-capture mechanism.
- **Relationships:** A specialization of Event (MF1.3.9); the anchor Event for the Leads Objective (MF1.3.12) across all its supported conversion locations.
- **Lifecycle:** Fires once per qualifying submission/interaction; downstream Lead quality (whether it converts to a real customer) is typically tracked outside Meta's platform, in the advertiser's own CRM.
- **Ownership:** Owned/implemented by the advertiser's website, Instant Form configuration, or Messaging/Call setup.
- **Examples:** A user completing a native Instant Form directly within a Facebook Ad without leaving the platform, generating a Lead Event.
- **Common Mistakes:** Optimizing purely for Lead volume without any downstream lead-quality feedback loop, which can lead the Auction's Optimization Goal to favor low-intent, low-quality submissions if that is what appears easiest to generate at volume.
- **Professional Notes:** Meta supports feeding offline lead-quality outcomes (e.g., "qualified" vs. "unqualified," from the advertiser's CRM) back via Conversions API/Offline Events, allowing the Optimization Goal to indirectly account for lead quality over time — a materially different, higher-maturity setup than optimizing for raw Lead volume alone.

---

*(MF1.3 batch 5 — lead-capture and catalog objects.)*

### MF1.3.33 Messaging Conversation

- **Official Name:** Messaging Conversation
- **Alternative Names:** Conversation (Messenger/Instagram/WhatsApp)
- **Definition:** A tracked message exchange initiated by a user with a Business's Messenger, Instagram Direct, or WhatsApp presence as a direct result of interacting with an Ad, used both as an Optimization Event and as a Business outcome in its own right (e.g., for the Engagement or Leads Objectives).
- **Business Purpose:** Represents a user-initiated, conversational entry point into the Business's sales or support funnel, often a precursor to a Lead or Purchase.
- **Meta Optimization Purpose:** A supported Optimization Event for both Engagement and Leads Objectives; Meta's delivery system can optimize either for conversation starts or for a deeper in-conversation outcome (e.g., a qualifying message reply) depending on configuration.
- **Inputs:** A user tapping a Messenger/Instagram/WhatsApp destination Call to Action on an Ad and sending at least one message.
- **Outputs:** A Messaging Conversation Event, and optionally deeper in-thread outcome Events if configured.
- **Dependencies:** Requires a connected Page (for Messenger), Instagram Account (for Instagram Direct), or WhatsApp Business integration.
- **Relationships:** A specialization of Event (MF1.3.9); a supported conversion location for both Engagement and Leads Objectives (MF1.5).
- **Lifecycle:** Fires at conversation start; a single conversation may generate multiple downstream tracked outcomes over its life.
- **Ownership:** Owned by the Business's Page/Instagram Account/WhatsApp integration and whoever operates the resulting conversation (human agent or automated response system).
- **Examples:** A user messaging a Page directly from an Instagram Story Ad to ask about product availability.
- **Common Mistakes:** Optimizing purely for conversation starts without measuring or feeding back genuine business outcomes from those conversations, similarly to the Lead-quality feedback gap described in MF1.3.32.
- **Professional Notes:** As with Lead, Meta supports feeding back structured conversation outcomes to allow deeper optimization than raw conversation-start volume, though this requires deliberate integration effort beyond the default setup.

### MF1.3.34 Instant Form

- **Official Name:** Instant Form
- **Alternative Names:** Lead Form, Lead Ad Form
- **Definition:** A native, in-platform form that opens directly within Facebook or Instagram when a user taps an Ad's Call to Action, allowing Lead submission without leaving the platform or loading an external landing page.
- **Business Purpose:** Reduces user drop-off from page-load friction by keeping the entire Lead-capture experience inside the platform the user is already using.
- **Meta Optimization Purpose:** A supported conversion location for the Leads Objective; because the entire flow is native to Meta's platform, matching and Attribution for Instant Form Leads is typically more precise than website-based Lead tracking, which depends on external Pixel/Conversions API fidelity.
- **Inputs:** A form definition (qualifying questions, pre-filled fields drawn from the user's Meta profile information) authored by the advertiser.
- **Outputs:** A Lead Event (MF1.3.32) containing the submitted field data.
- **Dependencies:** Requires a Page (Instant Forms are configured and owned at the Page level).
- **Relationships:** A specific mechanism for producing a Lead Event; an alternative to website-based or Messaging-based Lead capture.
- **Lifecycle:** Defined once, reused across multiple Ads/Ad Sets; submissions accrue continuously while active.
- **Ownership:** Created and owned by the Page administrator.
- **Examples:** An Instant Form with three qualifying questions (budget range, timeline, contact number) used across a Leads-objective Campaign.
- **Common Mistakes:** Adding too many qualifying questions, increasing per-Lead data quality but reducing completion rate — a deliberate quality/volume trade-off that should be a conscious decision, not a default oversight.
- **Professional Notes:** Instant Form Leads are typically lower-friction and higher-volume but can be lower-intent than website-form Leads requiring the user to leave the platform; comparative quality should be evaluated with downstream CRM feedback (see MF1.3.32's Professional Notes).

### MF1.3.35 Catalog

- **Official Name:** Catalog
- **Alternative Names:** Product Catalog, Commerce Catalog
- **Definition:** A structured container of product or inventory data (items, prices, availability, images, identifiers) uploaded or synced by an advertiser, used to power Dynamic Creative at scale — most notably Catalog Sales/Advantage+ Catalog Ads and Collection-format Ads.
- **Business Purpose:** Allows advertisers with many products (e-commerce, travel, real estate, auto) to run relevant, personalized ads without manually creating a Creative for every individual item.
- **Meta Optimization Purpose:** Enables the delivery system to dynamically select and render the most relevant Product(s) to a given user at auction time, combining Catalog data with the user's own browsing/purchase behavior (retargeting) or with Advantage+ Catalog Ads' broader prospecting logic.
- **Inputs:** A structured feed of product items, each with required fields (ID, title, price, availability, image, link) and optional fields (category, custom labels).
- **Outputs:** Dynamically assembled Ad Creative populated from the Catalog's item data.
- **Dependencies:** Requires a Pixel or App SDK integration reporting product-level view/cart/purchase Events (for retargeting use cases) and typically a connected Dataset (MF1.3.8).
- **Relationships:** Organized into one or more Product Sets (MF1.3.36); a Business-level asset (parallel to Pixel/Page, per MF1.2.3), reusable across many Campaigns.
- **Lifecycle:** Created once, synced/updated continuously (via feed refresh) as inventory and pricing change.
- **Ownership:** Owned by the Business; typically managed by an e-commerce platform integration or a scheduled feed upload process.
- **Examples:** A Catalog of 50,000 SKUs synced daily from an e-commerce platform, used to power dynamic retargeting ads showing each user the specific products they previously viewed.
- **Common Mistakes:** Allowing Catalog data to go stale (incorrect price or out-of-stock items still shown as available), directly damaging user trust and post-click experience.
- **Professional Notes:** Catalog data quality (completeness, freshness, image quality) is a frequently underestimated performance lever for Catalog Sales campaigns, often having more impact on outcomes than Bid Strategy or Budget tuning.

### MF1.3.36 Product Set

- **Official Name:** Product Set
- **Alternative Names:** None in current common usage.
- **Definition:** A named, filtered subset of a Catalog's items (e.g., by category, price range, or custom label), used to scope which items a specific Ad Set or Ad is eligible to dynamically show.
- **Business Purpose:** Allows an advertiser to run distinct campaigns for different product categories or business lines from within a single, unified Catalog.
- **Meta Optimization Purpose:** Scopes the pool of items the delivery system can dynamically select from for a given Ad Set, allowing Optimization Goal and Bid Strategy to be tuned differently per Product Set (e.g., higher-margin items bid more aggressively).
- **Inputs:** A Catalog and a filter rule (by field values) defining which items belong to the set.
- **Outputs:** A scoped item pool available to Ad Sets referencing this Product Set.
- **Dependencies:** Requires a parent Catalog.
- **Relationships:** A named subset of a Catalog (MF1.3.35); referenced by an Ad Set to scope its dynamic Creative eligibility.
- **Lifecycle:** Defined once, automatically updates its membership as underlying Catalog items' field values change relative to the filter rule.
- **Ownership:** Owned by the Business, scoped within a specific Catalog.
- **Examples:** A "Clearance Items" Product Set filtering the Catalog to items tagged with a specific custom label, used to power a dedicated clearance-sale Ad Set.
- **Common Mistakes:** Defining overlapping Product Sets used across sibling Ad Sets targeting the same Audience, causing the same items to compete against each other in the Auction.
- **Professional Notes:** Product Set granularity is a strategic segmentation decision similar in kind to Audience segmentation — too granular fragments Learning Phase data across many small Ad Sets, too broad prevents differentiated bidding/budgeting by product economics.

### MF1.3.37 Creative Asset

- **Official Name:** Creative Asset
- **Alternative Names:** Asset (in Creative context)
- **Definition:** An individual image, video, or other media file that serves as one component of a Creative (MF1.3.17), as distinct from the surrounding copy (Primary Text, Headline) and configuration (Call to Action, destination).
- **Business Purpose:** The visual/audio component that carries most of a Creative's emotional and attention-grabbing impact.
- **Meta Optimization Purpose:** Asset quality, format-fit to Placement, and freshness are direct inputs to Quality Ranking (MF1.3.44) and are the primary lever addressed by Creative Testing and Creative Refresh (both MF1.9/MF1.10).
- **Inputs:** An uploaded image or video file, or an asset selected from a connected Catalog (MF1.3.35).
- **Outputs:** The rendered visual/audio portion of an Ad as seen by the user.
- **Dependencies:** Must conform to each eligible Placement's format requirements (aspect ratio, duration, file size) to be eligible for that Placement.
- **Relationships:** A component of Creative (MF1.3.17); the object combined dynamically by Dynamic Creative (MF1.9) across multiple variants.
- **Lifecycle:** Uploaded once, may be reused across many Creatives/Ads; subject to Creative Fatigue (MF1.9) tracked at the level of the Ad(s) using it.
- **Ownership:** Created by the advertiser's creative team or agency; owned by the Business.
- **Examples:** A single 9:16 vertical video file used as the primary Creative Asset for a set of Story and Reels placements.
- **Common Mistakes:** Reusing a single Feed-optimized (1:1 or 4:5) Creative Asset unmodified across Story/Reels Placements (9:16), resulting in poor framing or cropping and degraded Quality Ranking.
- **Professional Notes:** The full Creative Asset format/testing/fatigue vocabulary is addressed exhaustively in MF1.9, per this Framework's scope — this entry establishes only the object's identity and its place in the Domain Architecture.

---

*(MF1.3 batch 6 — reporting, attribution, and ranking objects. This is the final batch of Core Domain Objects; MF1.3 closes after this section.)*

### MF1.3.38 Reporting Dimension

- **Official Name:** Reporting Dimension
- **Alternative Names:** Dimension
- **Definition:** A categorical axis (such as Campaign, Ad Set, Ad, Objective, or a time period) along which Metrics can be grouped, filtered, or compared in reporting output, as distinct from a Breakdown (MF1.3.39), which further subdivides results by a delivery/audience characteristic.
- **Business Purpose:** Allows an advertiser to view performance at the structural or temporal granularity relevant to a given decision (e.g., per-Campaign trend over time, or per-Ad comparison within one Ad Set).
- **Meta Optimization Purpose:** Determines the grain at which Analysis (specialized from `F1.2`/`F2.4`) is performed — the same underlying Delivery data yields different insights depending on whether it is examined at Campaign, Ad Set, or Ad Dimension.
- **Inputs:** A selection of one or more structural or temporal axes (Campaign, Ad Set, Ad, day/week/month).
- **Outputs:** A grouped/filtered reporting table or chart.
- **Dependencies:** Requires underlying Delivery/Metric data to exist at the selected Dimension's grain.
- **Relationships:** Orthogonal to Breakdown (MF1.3.39) — a report may combine a Dimension (e.g., Campaign) with a Breakdown (e.g., by Placement) simultaneously.
- **Lifecycle:** Selected per report/view; not a persistent object, but a reporting-time construct.
- **Ownership:** Selected by the person or system building a given report or dashboard view.
- **Examples:** A report grouped by the Campaign Dimension, showing one row per Campaign with aggregated Metrics.
- **Common Mistakes:** Confusing Dimension (structural/temporal grouping) with Breakdown (delivery/audience-characteristic subdivision), leading to mislabeled or miscombined reporting requests.
- **Professional Notes:** The full Reporting vocabulary (Metrics, Dimensions, Breakdowns, Comparison, Benchmarks, Confidence, Statistical Significance, Trend) is defined exhaustively in MF1.11, per this Framework's scope.

### MF1.3.39 Breakdown

- **Official Name:** Breakdown
- **Alternative Names:** None in current common usage.
- **Definition:** A further subdivision of reporting results by a delivery or audience characteristic — such as Age, Gender, Region, Placement, Device Platform, or Time of Day — applied on top of a chosen Reporting Dimension.
- **Business Purpose:** Reveals which segments of delivery are driving (or dragging down) aggregate performance, supporting more targeted optimization decisions than aggregate Metrics alone allow.
- **Meta Optimization Purpose:** Breakdown data is descriptive/reporting-only — it does not itself alter the Auction or Optimization Goal, but informs subsequent Audience, Placement, or Bid Strategy adjustments made by the advertiser.
- **Inputs:** Delivery and Metric data tagged with the relevant characteristic (age bracket, gender, region, placement surface, device, hour).
- **Outputs:** A further-subdivided reporting table (e.g., performance by Age × Gender within one Campaign).
- **Dependencies:** Requires sufficient Delivery volume within each subdivision to be statistically meaningful (see Statistical Significance, MF1.11).
- **Relationships:** Applied on top of a Reporting Dimension (MF1.3.38); a common input to Audience Exclusion or Placement-narrowing decisions.
- **Lifecycle:** Selected per report/view; not a persistent object.
- **Ownership:** Selected by the person or system building a given report or dashboard view.
- **Examples:** A Placement Breakdown revealing that Audience Network delivers materially worse CPA than Feed and Stories within the same Ad Set.
- **Common Mistakes:** Acting on a Breakdown segment with too little Delivery volume to be statistically meaningful, mistaking normal small-sample variance for a genuine performance difference.
- **Professional Notes:** Over-narrowing based on Breakdown findings (e.g., excluding a Placement or demographic entirely) can shrink the eligible Auction pool enough to trigger Learning Limited — Breakdown-driven decisions should weigh this trade-off explicitly.

### MF1.3.40 Metric

- **Official Name:** Metric
- **Alternative Names:** Performance Metric
- **Definition:** A single, named, quantifiable measure of Delivery or outcome performance (e.g., Impressions, CTR, ROAS), each with a defined calculation and business meaning, collectively cataloged in MF1.4.
- **Business Purpose:** Provides the standardized vocabulary and calculation basis through which performance is discussed, compared, and acted upon across the organization.
- **Meta Optimization Purpose:** Every Optimization Goal (MF1.3.13) and every Recommendation, Alert, or Health evaluation (as specialized from the Generic Framework Series) is ultimately expressed in terms of one or more Metrics.
- **Inputs:** Raw Delivery and Event data.
- **Outputs:** A calculated numeric value, interpretable per its own definition's Healthy/Warning/Critical guidance (MF1.4).
- **Dependencies:** Varies per Metric — some depend only on Delivery (e.g., Impressions), others on Attribution-dependent Conversion data (e.g., ROAS).
- **Relationships:** The generic parent concept specialized by every specific measure named in MF1.4 (e.g., CPM, CTR, ROAS — MF1.3.41–MF1.3.46 in this catalog establish only the most cross-cutting examples; the complete set is in MF1.4).
- **Lifecycle:** Calculated continuously or on-demand as underlying data accrues.
- **Ownership:** A computed value; not directly set by the advertiser.
- **Examples:** CTR, calculated as Link Clicks divided by Impressions.
- **Common Mistakes:** Treating every Metric as equally meaningful for every Objective, rather than recognizing that each Objective (MF1.5) has its own primary and secondary Metrics, with others being merely diagnostic or irrelevant.
- **Professional Notes:** This entry establishes Metric as a Core Domain Object/concept; the full Metrics Dictionary with formulas, ranges, and misinterpretation guidance for every individual Metric is delivered in MF1.4, per this Framework's required structure.

### MF1.3.41 Attribution

- **Official Name:** Attribution
- **Alternative Names:** Conversion Attribution
- **Definition:** The methodology by which Meta's system determines that a given Conversion (MF1.3.30) should be credited to a specific Ad exposure or interaction, governed by an Attribution Window (the recency limit for crediting) and an Attribution Model (the logic for assigning credit, e.g., last-click).
- **Business Purpose:** Establishes the causal link an advertiser relies on to judge whether their advertising spend produced a given business outcome.
- **Meta Optimization Purpose:** Directly determines which Conversions are counted toward a given Ad Set's Optimization Goal performance, and therefore which outcomes the Auction's bidding and delivery decisions are being driven by.
- **Inputs:** Ad exposure/interaction events, Conversion Events, an Attribution Window setting, and an Attribution Model setting.
- **Outputs:** A set of Conversions credited to specific Ads/Ad Sets/Campaigns.
- **Dependencies:** Requires properly implemented Pixel/Conversions API/App SDK tracking with adequate Matching Quality (MF1.6).
- **Relationships:** Governs which Conversions (MF1.3.30) are counted in CPA and ROAS (MF1.4) calculations for a given Ad Set.
- **Lifecycle:** Applied continuously as new interaction and Conversion data arrives, within the bounds of the configured Attribution Window's recency.
- **Ownership:** The Attribution Window and Model are configured by the advertiser (subject to platform-level constraints); the underlying attribution computation itself is performed by Meta's system.
- **Examples:** A Purchase occurring 3 days after a user clicked an Ad, credited under a 7-day-click Attribution Window.
- **Common Mistakes:** Comparing performance across time periods, accounts, or platforms using inconsistent Attribution Window/Model settings, producing an apples-to-oranges comparison that appears to be a performance change but is actually a measurement-methodology change.
- **Professional Notes:** The complete Attribution vocabulary (Click Attribution, View Attribution, Attribution Windows, Attribution Models, Incrementality, Assisted Conversion, Conversion Path) is defined exhaustively in MF1.7, per this Framework's scope.

### MF1.3.42 ROAS

- **Official Name:** ROAS
- **Alternative Names:** Return on Ad Spend
- **Definition:** A derived Metric equal to total attributed Conversion value (typically Purchase value) divided by total Spend, expressed as a ratio (e.g., 4.2, commonly read as "4.2x").
- **Business Purpose:** Directly expresses the revenue efficiency of advertising spend, the single most business-outcome-proximate Metric available for the Sales Objective.
- **Meta Optimization Purpose:** The basis for a "Minimum ROAS" Bid Strategy control (MF1.3.20) and the most common top-line success Metric evaluated for Sales-objective Campaigns.
- **Inputs:** Total attributed Purchase (or other value-tracked Conversion) value and total Spend, over the same period and under the same Attribution settings.
- **Outputs:** A ratio value.
- **Dependencies:** Requires accurate Purchase value passing (MF1.3.31) and a consistent Attribution Window/Model (MF1.3.41).
- **Relationships:** A specialization of Metric (MF1.3.40); the primary success Metric for the Sales Objective (MF1.5); directly consumed by the Bid Strategy's ROAS-floor control.
- **Lifecycle:** Calculated continuously as Spend and attributed Conversion value accrue.
- **Ownership:** A computed Metric; not directly set by the advertiser.
- **Examples:** $12,000 in attributed Purchase value against $3,000 in Spend, yielding a ROAS of 4.0.
- **Common Mistakes:** Treating Meta-reported ROAS as identical to true blended business ROAS, ignoring that Meta's figure reflects only its own Attribution Model's view and may diverge materially from a source-of-truth (e.g., CRM/e-commerce platform) revenue figure.
- **Professional Notes:** Full Healthy/Warning/Critical range guidance for ROAS, which varies meaningfully by industry and margin structure, is provided in MF1.4 rather than as a fixed universal number.

### MF1.3.43 CPA

- **Official Name:** CPA
- **Alternative Names:** Cost Per Action, Cost Per Result (when "Result" refers to the Ad Set's configured Optimization Event)
- **Definition:** A derived Metric equal to total Spend divided by the count of a specified Conversion or Optimization Event (e.g., cost per Purchase, cost per Lead).
- **Business Purpose:** Expresses the cost efficiency of acquiring one unit of a specific business outcome.
- **Meta Optimization Purpose:** The primary efficiency Metric for the Leads and App Promotion Objectives (where a single monetizable value like Purchase value is typically unavailable or less central than for Sales), and a common secondary Metric even for Sales.
- **Inputs:** Total Spend and a count of the specified Conversion/Event, over the same period and under the same Attribution settings.
- **Outputs:** A currency-denominated cost-per-unit value.
- **Dependencies:** Requires the specified Event to be properly tracked and attributed.
- **Relationships:** A specialization of Metric (MF1.3.40); the primary success Metric for the Leads Objective and commonly for App Promotion (MF1.5).
- **Lifecycle:** Calculated continuously as Spend and the specified Event count accrue.
- **Ownership:** A computed Metric; not directly set by the advertiser.
- **Examples:** $4,500 Spend against 90 Leads, yielding a CPA (cost per Lead) of $50.
- **Common Mistakes:** Comparing CPA across Ad Sets or Campaigns optimizing for different Events (e.g., cost per Lead vs. cost per completed Purchase) as though they were the same Metric on the same scale.
- **Professional Notes:** "CPA" is frequently used generically to mean "cost per whatever this Ad Set's Optimization Event is" — precise reporting should always state which specific Event a given CPA figure refers to, to avoid the common-mistake pattern above.

### MF1.3.44 Quality Ranking

- **Official Name:** Quality Ranking
- **Alternative Names:** Ad Quality Ranking
- **Definition:** One of three relative-diagnostic rankings Meta provides per Ad, comparing the perceived quality of that Ad's Creative and post-click experience against other ads competing for the same Audience, expressed as a relative standing (e.g., "Below Average," "Average," "Above Average") rather than an absolute score.
- **Business Purpose:** Gives the advertiser a diagnostic signal for whether Creative or landing-page quality — as opposed to Bid or Budget — may be limiting Delivery or driving up cost.
- **Meta Optimization Purpose:** A direct input to the Auction's Total Value calculation (MF1.3.18) alongside Bid and estimated action rate — an Ad with poor Quality Ranking will generally need a higher Bid to win the same Auction as a higher-quality competitor.
- **Inputs:** Signals such as post-click landing page load performance, prior user feedback (e.g., hides, reports), and perceived Creative quality relative to competing ads.
- **Outputs:** A relative ranking category, viewable per Ad in reporting.
- **Dependencies:** Requires sufficient Delivery volume to be calculated; unavailable/unranked for very low-volume Ads.
- **Relationships:** One of three ranking signals (alongside Engagement Rate Ranking, MF1.3.45, and Conversion Rate Ranking, MF1.3.46) evaluated at the Ad level; a direct input to the Auction.
- **Lifecycle:** Recalculated on an ongoing basis as relative competitive conditions and the Ad's own performance evolve.
- **Ownership:** A computed diagnostic signal; not directly set by the advertiser, only influenced through Creative and landing-page improvements.
- **Examples:** An Ad ranked "Below Average" on Quality Ranking due to a slow-loading landing page, despite strong Creative.
- **Common Mistakes:** Interpreting Quality Ranking as an absolute quality score rather than a relative-to-competitors standing, which can shift even without any change to the Ad itself if competing ads' quality changes.
- **Professional Notes:** All three ranking signals are diagnostic tools for explaining Auction competitiveness and cost, not levers the advertiser sets directly — improvement always comes through Creative, landing-page, or targeting changes upstream.

### MF1.3.45 Engagement Rate Ranking

- **Official Name:** Engagement Rate Ranking
- **Alternative Names:** None in current common usage.
- **Definition:** One of three relative-diagnostic rankings Meta provides per Ad, comparing the Ad's expected engagement rate (reactions, comments, shares, clicks) against other ads competing for the same Audience.
- **Business Purpose:** Gives the advertiser a diagnostic signal for whether an Ad's ability to generate audience interaction is competitively strong or weak.
- **Meta Optimization Purpose:** A direct input to the Auction's Total Value calculation alongside Quality Ranking and Conversion Rate Ranking; particularly relevant diagnostic context for Engagement-objective Ad Sets.
- **Inputs:** Predicted and observed engagement behavior relative to competing ads for the same Audience.
- **Outputs:** A relative ranking category, viewable per Ad in reporting.
- **Dependencies:** Requires sufficient Delivery volume to be calculated.
- **Relationships:** One of three ranking signals (alongside Quality Ranking, MF1.3.44, and Conversion Rate Ranking, MF1.3.46) evaluated at the Ad level.
- **Lifecycle:** Recalculated on an ongoing basis as relative competitive conditions and the Ad's own performance evolve.
- **Ownership:** A computed diagnostic signal; not directly set by the advertiser, only influenced through Creative and messaging changes.
- **Examples:** An Ad ranked "Above Average" on Engagement Rate Ranking due to strong comment/share activity relative to competing ads.
- **Common Mistakes:** Using Engagement Rate Ranking as a primary success Metric for non-Engagement Objectives, where it is diagnostic context rather than the actual business outcome being pursued.
- **Professional Notes:** A Below Average Engagement Rate Ranking combined with an Above Average Quality Ranking can indicate a Creative that is well-received in quality terms but not compelling enough to prompt active interaction — a useful differential diagnosis pattern.

### MF1.3.46 Conversion Rate Ranking

- **Official Name:** Conversion Rate Ranking
- **Alternative Names:** None in current common usage.
- **Definition:** One of three relative-diagnostic rankings Meta provides per Ad, comparing the Ad's expected conversion rate against other ads optimizing for a similar Conversion Event and competing for the same Audience.
- **Business Purpose:** Gives the advertiser a diagnostic signal for whether an Ad's ability to drive the desired downstream Conversion is competitively strong or weak, independent of Quality or Engagement signals.
- **Meta Optimization Purpose:** A direct input to the Auction's Total Value calculation alongside Quality Ranking and Engagement Rate Ranking; particularly relevant diagnostic context for Leads and Sales Ad Sets.
- **Inputs:** Predicted and observed conversion behavior relative to competing ads optimizing for a similar Conversion Event.
- **Outputs:** A relative ranking category, viewable per Ad in reporting.
- **Dependencies:** Requires sufficient Conversion volume to be calculated; often unavailable for low-volume or newly launched Ads.
- **Relationships:** One of three ranking signals (alongside Quality Ranking, MF1.3.44, and Engagement Rate Ranking, MF1.3.45) evaluated at the Ad level.
- **Lifecycle:** Recalculated on an ongoing basis as relative competitive conditions and the Ad's own Conversion performance evolve.
- **Ownership:** A computed diagnostic signal; not directly set by the advertiser, only influenced through Creative, offer, and landing/post-click-experience changes.
- **Examples:** An Ad ranked "Below Average" on Conversion Rate Ranking despite Above Average Quality and Engagement Rankings, pointing diagnostically toward a landing-page or offer mismatch rather than a Creative problem.
- **Common Mistakes:** Diagnosing a CPA or ROAS problem purely from Bid Strategy or Budget review without checking Conversion Rate Ranking, which can reveal that the root cause is competitive/creative/offer-related rather than a bidding or budgeting issue.
- **Professional Notes:** Reading all three Ranking signals together (Quality, Engagement Rate, Conversion Rate) as a differential-diagnosis triad is standard professional practice for isolating whether an underperformance root cause is Creative-level, interest-level, or offer/landing-level.

---

This completes **MF1.3 Core Domain Objects** (46 objects, MF1.3.1–MF1.3.46), covering the full Primary Structural Chain, the Business-Identity Layer, and the core measurement/attribution/ranking vocabulary referenced throughout the remaining sections of this Framework.

## MF1.4 Metrics Dictionary

Every entry below uses the 11-field format required for Metric entries: Definition, Formula, Calculation, Business Meaning, Optimization Meaning, Healthy Range, Warning Range, Critical Range, Dependencies, Influencing Factors, Common Misinterpretations. Ranges are expressed as general professional guidance, not universal constants — they vary by industry, funnel stage, and account maturity, and are explicitly flagged as such per MAIFS.15's self-contained-metrics quality standard. Ranges reuse the healthy/warning/critical vocabulary already established by the Architecture's own health-scoring structural position (§6) and the Generic Framework Series' Quality Dimension concept (F9.4), specialized here with concrete domain numbers for the first time in the entire document lineage.

### MF1.4.1 CPM (Cost Per Mille)

- **Definition:** The cost incurred per one thousand Impressions delivered.
- **Formula:** `(Spend / Impressions) × 1000`
- **Calculation:** Total Spend divided by total Impressions over the same period, multiplied by 1,000.
- **Business Meaning:** Reflects the base cost of reaching an audience, independent of any click or conversion behavior.
- **Optimization Meaning:** A rising CPM with stable Audience/Placement settings typically signals increased Auction competition; a falling CPM can signal reduced competition or improved Quality Ranking (MF1.3.44).
- **Healthy Range:** Broad-Awareness Placements, e.g., $2–$12 (highly market- and season-dependent).
- **Warning Range:** Sudden increases of 30%+ over a 7-day trailing average without a corresponding Audience/Placement/Budget change.
- **Critical Range:** Sustained CPM increases of 75%+ with no offsetting improvement in downstream Metrics, indicating the Ad Set may no longer be competitive in its current Auction segment.
- **Dependencies:** Impressions (MF1.3.28), Spend, Audience (MF1.3.15), Placement (MF1.3.16), seasonal Auction demand.
- **Influencing Factors:** Time of year (e.g., Q4 holiday competition), Audience breadth, Placement selection, Quality Ranking, macro ad-market demand.
- **Common Misinterpretations:** Treating CPM in isolation as "good" or "bad" without considering downstream CTR/CVR — a higher CPM Audience can still be more cost-efficient overall if its downstream conversion behavior is proportionally stronger.

### MF1.4.2 CTR (Click-Through Rate)

- **Definition:** The rate at which users who saw an Ad went on to click it.
- **Formula:** `(Link Clicks / Impressions) × 100`
- **Calculation:** Total Link Clicks divided by total Impressions over the same period, expressed as a percentage.
- **Business Meaning:** Indicates how compelling the Creative and offer are at prompting an initial behavioral response.
- **Optimization Meaning:** A primary Creative-quality diagnostic signal, and a component of the Auction's estimated action rate for click-optimizing Ad Sets.
- **Healthy Range:** Roughly 1.0%–2.5% for Feed-based Traffic/Engagement Ad Sets (varies significantly by Placement and industry).
- **Warning Range:** Below 0.8% sustained over a full Learning Phase cycle, or a decline of 30%+ from a previously stable baseline.
- **Critical Range:** Below 0.4% sustained, typically indicating severe Creative Fatigue (MF1.9) or fundamental Audience/Creative mismatch.
- **Dependencies:** Link Clicks (MF1.3.29), Impressions (MF1.3.28), Creative (MF1.3.17), Placement (MF1.3.16).
- **Influencing Factors:** Creative freshness/fatigue, Audience relevance, Placement mix, offer strength, ad format (video vs. static).
- **Common Misinterpretations:** Conflating "Clicks (all)" with "Link Clicks" when calculating CTR (see MF1.3.29's Common Mistakes), inflating the figure with non-navigational interactions.

### MF1.4.3 CPC (Cost Per Click)

- **Definition:** The average cost incurred per Link Click.
- **Formula:** `Spend / Link Clicks`
- **Calculation:** Total Spend divided by total Link Clicks over the same period.
- **Business Meaning:** Expresses the direct cost of driving one instance of traffic toward the advertiser's destination.
- **Optimization Meaning:** A function of both CPM and CTR (`CPC = CPM / (CTR × 10)` when CTR is expressed as a percentage) — a CPC problem should always be decomposed into its CPM and CTR components to identify the true root cause.
- **Healthy Range:** Roughly $0.30–$2.00 for Feed-based Traffic Ad Sets (highly industry- and geography-dependent).
- **Warning Range:** A 40%+ increase over trailing baseline without a corresponding CTR decline explaining the full change (suggesting a CPM-driven cause instead).
- **Critical Range:** CPC at a level that makes the Traffic Objective's cost-per-visit economically unsustainable relative to downstream conversion value.
- **Dependencies:** Spend, Link Clicks (MF1.3.29); derived from CPM (MF1.4.1) and CTR (MF1.4.2).
- **Influencing Factors:** Same factors as CPM and CTR, combined.
- **Common Misinterpretations:** Optimizing CPC directly as a goal in itself, rather than treating it as a diagnostic decomposition of CPM and CTR — CPC is rarely a primary KPI (MF1.5) in its own right.

### MF1.4.4 CPL (Cost Per Lead)

- **Definition:** The average cost incurred per Lead Event (MF1.3.32).
- **Formula:** `Spend / Leads`
- **Calculation:** Total Spend divided by total Lead Event count over the same period and Attribution settings.
- **Business Meaning:** Expresses the direct acquisition cost of one prospective customer's expressed interest.
- **Optimization Meaning:** The primary efficiency Metric (a specific case of CPA, MF1.3.43) for the Leads Objective (MF1.3.12/MF1.5).
- **Healthy Range:** Highly industry-dependent (e.g., low single-digit dollars for high-volume consumer offers, hundreds of dollars for high-value B2B/real estate/financial offers) — no universal number; must be benchmarked against the advertiser's own historical baseline or the Architecture's benchmark mechanism (§26 lineage).
- **Warning Range:** A 30%+ increase over the advertiser's own established baseline without a corresponding increase in Lead quality.
- **Critical Range:** CPL exceeding the advertiser's known customer lifetime value or acceptable acquisition cost ceiling.
- **Dependencies:** Spend, Lead Events (MF1.3.32); Attribution Window/Model (MF1.3.41).
- **Influencing Factors:** Lead-capture mechanism (Instant Form vs. website vs. Messaging — MF1.3.32's Professional Notes), qualifying-question friction, offer strength, Audience quality.
- **Common Misinterpretations:** Optimizing purely for lower CPL without a lead-quality feedback loop, which can drive the Optimization Goal toward high-volume, low-intent submissions (see MF1.3.32's Common Mistakes).

### MF1.4.5 CPA (Cost Per Action / Cost Per Result)

- See MF1.3.43 for the full Core Domain Object definition. This entry adds the Metrics Dictionary's numeric range guidance.
- **Healthy Range:** Must be benchmarked against the advertiser's own known acceptable cost per the specific Event being measured (e.g., acceptable CPA for a Purchase vs. for a Complete Registration are entirely different figures) — no single universal range applies across differing Event types.
- **Warning Range:** A 25%+ increase over the advertiser's own established per-Event baseline, sustained over a full Learning Phase cycle.
- **Critical Range:** CPA exceeding the known profitable/acceptable threshold for that specific Event and business model.
- **Influencing Factors:** Same as CPL above, generalized to whichever Event is being measured.
- **Common Misinterpretations:** Comparing CPA figures across Ad Sets optimizing for different Events as if directly comparable (see MF1.3.43's Common Mistakes).

### MF1.4.6 ROAS (Return on Ad Spend)

- See MF1.3.42 for the full Core Domain Object definition. This entry adds the Metrics Dictionary's numeric range guidance.
- **Healthy Range:** Commonly cited general-commerce benchmark of 3.0–4.0x or higher, though genuinely healthy ROAS depends entirely on the advertiser's own margin structure — a low-margin business may need 6x+ to be profitable, while a high-margin business may be profitable well below 3x.
- **Warning Range:** ROAS trending downward 20%+ from a stable trailing baseline without a corresponding known cause (e.g., seasonality, deliberate Audience expansion).
- **Critical Range:** ROAS below the advertiser's own known breakeven point given their margin structure.
- **Influencing Factors:** Purchase value accuracy, Attribution Window/Model, product margin structure, seasonality, Audience Saturation.
- **Common Misinterpretations:** Treating Meta-reported ROAS as equivalent to true blended business profitability (see MF1.3.42's Common Mistakes and Professional Notes).

### MF1.4.7 Frequency

- See MF1.3.26 for the full Core Domain Object definition. This entry adds the Metrics Dictionary's numeric range guidance.
- **Healthy Range:** Roughly 1.5–3.0 for broad Awareness/prospecting Audiences over a 7-day window; considerably higher (5–10+) can remain healthy for small, high-intent retargeting Audiences.
- **Warning Range:** Frequency exceeding 4–5 for a broad prospecting Audience without corresponding CTR/CVR stability, suggesting emerging Audience Saturation (MF1.8).
- **Critical Range:** Frequency exceeding 8–10 for a broad prospecting Audience with declining CTR and rising CPM, strongly indicating Creative Fatigue (MF1.9) and Audience Saturation compounding together.
- **Influencing Factors:** Audience size, Placement breadth, Budget/schedule duration, Creative rotation cadence.
- **Common Misinterpretations:** Applying one universal Frequency threshold across all Audience types and funnel stages (see MF1.3.26's Common Mistakes).

### MF1.4.8 Reach & Impressions

- See MF1.3.27 and MF1.3.28 for the full Core Domain Object definitions. Reach and Impressions are volume Metrics rather than efficiency ratios and are not typically assigned Healthy/Warning/Critical ranges in isolation — their health is expressed through Frequency (MF1.4.7, their derived ratio) and through pacing consistency against the Ad Set's Budget (MF1.3.21), rather than as standalone absolute numbers.
- **Dependencies:** Delivery (MF1.3.25), Budget (MF1.3.21), schedule duration.
- **Influencing Factors:** Budget level, Audience size, Placement breadth, Bid Strategy competitiveness.
- **Common Misinterpretations:** Summing Reach across groupings that share overlapping Audiences (see MF1.3.27's Common Mistakes); treating raw Impressions volume as a proxy for genuine attention (see MF1.3.28's Common Mistakes).

### MF1.4.9 Video Metrics — ThruPlays & Video Watch Percentages

- **Definition:** A family of Metrics measuring video Creative viewership depth: ThruPlays (views to completion or to 15 seconds, whichever comes first), and Video Watched Percentage breakpoints (25%/50%/75%/95%/100%).
- **Formula:** ThruPlay Rate = `(ThruPlays / Impressions) × 100`; each Watched Percentage breakpoint = `(Views reaching that breakpoint / Video Plays) × 100`.
- **Calculation:** Counted directly from video-playback tracking events returned per video Ad.
- **Business Meaning:** Indicates how much of a video's message actually reached the viewer, as distinct from a mere Impression.
- **Optimization Meaning:** The primary optimization currency for the Video Views sub-profile of the Awareness Objective (MF1.3.12); a strong drop-off between early (25%) and late (75%/95%) breakpoints indicates the video loses viewer attention partway through.
- **Healthy Range:** ThruPlay Rate roughly 10%–30% depending on video length and Placement; a 25%-to-75% retention drop-off of less than 50% is generally considered healthy pacing.
- **Warning Range:** A steep drop-off (60%+ viewer loss) between the 25% and 50% breakpoints, indicating an early-video engagement problem.
- **Critical Range:** ThruPlay Rate below 5% combined with severe early drop-off, indicating the video Creative is not holding attention at all.
- **Dependencies:** Video Creative Asset (MF1.3.37), Placement (video-capable surfaces), Impressions.
- **Influencing Factors:** Video length, pacing of the message/hook within the first few seconds, sound-off viewing norms on Feed/Stories, Placement mix.
- **Common Misinterpretations:** Treating a video Ad's standard Impressions/CTR the same way as a static image Ad's, without accounting for video-specific viewership depth Metrics that better reflect message delivery for that format.

### MF1.4.10 Engagement Metrics — Post Engagement & Cost Per Engagement

- **Definition:** Post Engagement counts total interactions with an Ad's post unit (reactions, comments, shares, and clicks combined); Cost Per Engagement expresses Spend per such interaction.
- **Formula:** Cost Per Engagement = `Spend / Post Engagements`.
- **Calculation:** Total Spend divided by total Post Engagement count over the same period.
- **Business Meaning:** Reflects the breadth of social interaction and organic amplification (via shares/comments) an Ad generates beyond its paid Reach.
- **Optimization Meaning:** The primary optimization currency for the Post Engagement sub-type of the Engagement Objective (MF1.3.12/MF1.5).
- **Healthy Range:** Highly Creative- and industry-dependent; benchmarked against the advertiser's own historical Post Engagement rate baseline rather than a universal figure.
- **Warning Range:** Cost Per Engagement rising 30%+ over trailing baseline without a Creative or Audience change explaining it.
- **Critical Range:** Engagement Rate Ranking (MF1.3.45) falling to "Below Average" alongside rising Cost Per Engagement, indicating the Creative is losing competitive interaction appeal.
- **Dependencies:** Post Engagements (reactions, comments, shares, clicks), Spend.
- **Influencing Factors:** Creative novelty/fatigue, message relevance, community-management responsiveness to comments (which can itself drive further engagement).
- **Common Misinterpretations:** Treating high Post Engagement as automatically indicative of strong business outcomes — engagement is a mid-funnel signal, not a guarantee of downstream Lead or Purchase behavior.

### MF1.4.11 CPI (Cost Per Install)

- **Definition:** The average cost incurred per App Install Event.
- **Formula:** `Spend / App Installs`
- **Calculation:** Total Spend divided by total App Install Event count over the same period.
- **Business Meaning:** Expresses the direct acquisition cost of one new app user.
- **Optimization Meaning:** The primary efficiency Metric for the App Promotion Objective's App Installs sub-type (MF1.3.12/MF1.5); a specific case of CPA (MF1.3.43).
- **Healthy Range:** Highly platform- (iOS vs. Android) and category-dependent (e.g., games vs. utility vs. finance apps have very different typical CPI); benchmarked against the advertiser's own baseline and known user lifetime value.
- **Warning Range:** A 30%+ increase over established baseline without a corresponding increase in post-install engagement quality.
- **Critical Range:** CPI exceeding the advertiser's known acceptable acquisition cost given typical user lifetime value.
- **Dependencies:** Spend, App Install Events (via App SDK/Meta's mobile measurement integration).
- **Influencing Factors:** Platform (iOS/Android) differences in Auction competitiveness, app category, Creative/store-listing alignment, seasonal app-install demand.
- **Common Misinterpretations:** Judging App Promotion campaign success on CPI alone without considering post-install App Events (e.g., in-app purchase, registration), which better reflect genuine user value than install volume alone.

### MF1.4.12 CVR (Conversion Rate)

- **Definition:** The rate at which users who clicked (or, in some reporting contexts, were shown) an Ad went on to complete a defined Conversion Event.
- **Formula:** `(Conversions / Link Clicks) × 100` (click-based CVR) — an Impression-based variant is sometimes used for full-funnel view, calculated as `(Conversions / Impressions) × 100`.
- **Calculation:** Total Conversion Event count divided by total Link Clicks (or Impressions, for the Impression-based variant) over the same period and Attribution settings.
- **Business Meaning:** Indicates how effectively traffic driven by the Ad converts into the desired business outcome once it arrives at the destination.
- **Optimization Meaning:** A key diagnostic for isolating whether an underperformance root cause is upstream (Creative/Audience, reflected in CTR) or downstream (landing page/offer, reflected in CVR).
- **Healthy Range:** Roughly 2%–5% for e-commerce Purchase CVR (highly industry-, price-point-, and traffic-quality-dependent); Lead-generation CVR is often materially higher due to lower commitment.
- **Warning Range:** A 25%+ decline from established baseline without a corresponding traffic-quality or Audience change explaining it.
- **Critical Range:** CVR low enough, combined with healthy CTR and Conversion Rate Ranking data, to indicate a landing-page, checkout-flow, or offer-level problem rather than an advertising problem.
- **Dependencies:** Conversions (MF1.3.30), Link Clicks (MF1.3.29) or Impressions, Attribution Window/Model.
- **Influencing Factors:** Landing page load speed and relevance, checkout/form friction, offer strength, price competitiveness, traffic quality (Audience relevance).
- **Common Misinterpretations:** Attributing a CVR decline entirely to advertising quality when the root cause frequently lies in the post-click landing page or checkout experience, outside the Ad itself.

---

## MF1.5 Objectives Dictionary

Each of the 6 Meta Objectives (MF1.3.12) is documented below with the full merged field set required by both governing instructions: **Official Goal, Business Goal/Purpose, Meta Optimization Logic, AI Interpretation, Typical Funnel Stage, Supported Conversion Locations, Supported Optimization Events (every sub-type, preserved without omission), Required Infrastructure, Expected User Intent, Success Signals/Primary KPIs, Failure Signals/Secondary KPIs, Common Use Cases, Common Mistakes, Decision Rules.** Every optimization sub-type under every Objective is documented individually — none are summarized or merged together, per this Framework's explicit no-summarization requirement.

### MF1.5.1 Awareness

- **Official Goal:** Maximize the number of people who see an Ad, or maximize a specific dimension of brand recall/recognition, without requiring any specific action beyond viewing.
- **Business Goal / Purpose:** Build top-of-funnel familiarity with a Business, product, or message ahead of any direct-response ask.
- **Meta Optimization Logic:** The Auction (MF1.3.18) optimizes purely for efficient delivery against the chosen Optimization Event (Reach, Impressions, ThruPlays, or estimated Ad Recall Lift) — no downstream Conversion Event is required or considered.
- **AI Interpretation:** Signals under this Objective should be interpreted as top-of-funnel breadth indicators; low CTR/CVR under Awareness is expected and not itself a failure signal, unlike under Traffic/Leads/Sales.
- **Typical Funnel Stage:** Top of funnel.
- **Supported Conversion Locations:** None required (Awareness does not require a destination Conversion Event) — the "destination" is exposure itself, optionally combined with a Website/App/Business Page link for incidental traffic.
- **Supported Optimization Events (sub-types, all preserved):**
  - **Reach** — optimizes to maximize unique users reached within Budget, independent of Frequency.
  - **Impressions / Brand Awareness** — optimizes to maximize total Impressions delivered (relevant for pure exposure-volume goals) and/or an estimated Ad Recall Lift metric.
  - **Video Views (ThruPlays)** — optimizes specifically for video viewership depth (see MF1.4.9); Meta's system predicts likelihood of a ThruPlay per user rather than raw Reach/Impressions.
  - **Ad Recall Lift** — optimizes toward users statistically more likely to remember seeing the Ad if surveyed, modeled from Meta's Brand Lift study data rather than a directly observable per-impression event.
- **Required Infrastructure:** None beyond a Page (and, if used, an Instagram Account) — no Pixel/Conversions API/Dataset required, since no Conversion Event is being measured.
- **Expected User Intent:** Passive; users are not expected to take deliberate action, only to be exposed to and register the message.
- **Success Signals / Primary KPIs:** Reach (MF1.3.27), CPM (MF1.4.1), Frequency (MF1.4.7) within healthy range, ThruPlay Rate/video watch percentages (MF1.4.9) for video sub-types, estimated Ad Recall Lift for that sub-type.
- **Failure Signals / Secondary KPIs:** Rapidly rising Frequency without proportional Reach growth (Audience Saturation, MF1.8), CPM rising without an external market explanation, steep early video drop-off (MF1.4.9).
- **Common Use Cases:** New product/brand launches, seasonal brand campaigns, pre-launch hype building ahead of a later Traffic/Leads/Sales push.
- **Common Mistakes:** Judging Awareness campaigns by CTR or CVR, Metrics that are simply not what this Objective optimizes for; running Awareness Ad Sets indefinitely on the same Creative without monitoring Frequency, causing preventable Audience Saturation.
- **Decision Rules:** If Frequency exceeds the Warning Range (MF1.4.7) without a Reach-growth or Creative-refresh action taken, a Recommendation/Alert (per the Generic Framework's Decision/Recommendation concepts, F4.x, as specialized here) should trigger a Creative Refresh (MF1.10) or Audience Expansion (MF1.8) suggestion.

### MF1.5.2 Traffic

- **Official Goal:** Drive users to a chosen destination — a website, app, Messenger/Instagram/WhatsApp thread, or phone call — maximizing the volume of such visits within Budget.
- **Business Goal / Purpose:** Move users from platform exposure to an owned or conversational destination where further engagement or eventual conversion can occur.
- **Meta Optimization Logic:** The Auction optimizes for the estimated likelihood a user will complete the chosen navigation action (Link Click, Landing Page View, or a destination-specific action).
- **AI Interpretation:** Traffic-objective signals should be interpreted as intermediate-funnel indicators — strong Traffic Metrics do not guarantee downstream Conversion success and should not be conflated with Leads/Sales-objective outcomes.
- **Typical Funnel Stage:** Upper-to-mid funnel.
- **Supported Conversion Locations:** Website, App, Messenger, Instagram, WhatsApp, Calls.
- **Supported Optimization Events (sub-types, all preserved):**
  - **Link Clicks** — optimizes for raw click volume toward the destination; the most common and lowest-friction Traffic sub-type.
  - **Landing Page Views** — optimizes specifically for users whose click also results in a fully loaded destination page, filtering out clicks that bounce before the page loads (a stricter, generally higher-intent signal than Link Clicks alone).
  - **Calls** — optimizes for users likely to initiate a phone call to the Business directly from the Ad.
  - **Messenger / Instagram / WhatsApp** — optimizes for users likely to open a conversation thread with the Business as the "destination" rather than a URL (overlapping conceptually with Messaging Conversation, MF1.3.33, but scoped here to the Traffic Objective's navigation-intent framing rather than the Engagement Objective's conversational-depth framing).
- **Required Infrastructure:** A Pixel (for Landing Page Views measurement) recommended but not strictly required for Link Clicks; a connected Page for Messenger/Instagram Traffic; Call tracking configuration for Calls.
- **Expected User Intent:** Moderate; users are expected to take one deliberate low-commitment action (a click or a call/message initiation) but not necessarily to complete a deeper Conversion.
- **Success Signals / Primary KPIs:** CTR (MF1.4.2), CPC (MF1.4.3), Landing Page View rate (for that sub-type), Call/Message initiation volume.
- **Failure Signals / Secondary KPIs:** High Link Clicks with disproportionately low Landing Page Views (indicating a landing-page load or click-quality problem — see MF1.3.29's Common Mistakes and Professional Notes); CPC rising without a CTR explanation (CPM-driven, per MF1.4.3).
- **Common Use Cases:** Blog/content traffic building, driving visits to a newly launched page, low-commitment lead-in campaigns ahead of a retargeting-based Leads/Sales push.
- **Common Mistakes:** Using raw Link Clicks as the Optimization Event when Landing Page Views would better filter for genuine destination-arrival intent; treating Traffic-objective Metrics as directly comparable to Leads/Sales-objective Metrics.
- **Decision Rules:** If Link Clicks are healthy but Landing Page Views are disproportionately low, a Recommendation should point toward landing-page performance/load-speed investigation rather than Creative or Audience changes.

### MF1.5.3 Engagement

- **Official Goal:** Maximize interaction with an Ad's post unit or with the Business's conversational channels — reactions, comments, shares, video views, or message conversations.
- **Business Goal / Purpose:** Build social proof, community interaction, and conversational relationships with an Audience, ranging from lightweight social interaction to deeper messaging-based conversations.
- **Meta Optimization Logic:** The Auction optimizes for the estimated likelihood of the specific chosen engagement action (Post Engagement, Video View, Message, Call, or a Conversion Event if configured for the Conversions sub-type).
- **AI Interpretation:** Engagement signals span a wide intent spectrum from very passive (a video view) to fairly deliberate (initiating a Messaging Conversation) — interpretation must always be scoped to the specific sub-type in use, not treated as a single uniform signal.
- **Typical Funnel Stage:** Upper-to-mid funnel, with the Messaging and Conversions sub-types extending into mid-to-lower funnel.
- **Supported Conversion Locations:** On-post (native interaction), Messenger, Instagram, WhatsApp, Calls, Website/App (for the Conversions sub-type).
- **Supported Optimization Events (sub-types, all preserved):**
  - **Messaging** — optimizes for users likely to start a Messaging Conversation (MF1.3.33) with the Business.
  - **Video Views** — optimizes for video viewership (overlapping mechanically with the Awareness Objective's Video Views sub-type, but framed here around interactive/engagement intent rather than pure exposure).
  - **Post Engagement** — optimizes for reactions, comments, shares, and clicks on the Ad's post unit (see MF1.4.10).
  - **Conversions** — optimizes for a defined Conversion Event reached via an engagement-oriented Creative/flow (e.g., an engagement-style Ad that still drives to a Website Conversion).
  - **Calls** — optimizes for users likely to initiate a phone call directly from the Ad, in an engagement-oriented framing.
- **Required Infrastructure:** A connected Page (all sub-types); Pixel/Conversions API for the Conversions sub-type; WhatsApp Business integration for WhatsApp Messaging.
- **Expected User Intent:** Variable by sub-type — passive for Video Views, low-commitment for Post Engagement, moderate-to-high for Messaging and Conversions.
- **Success Signals / Primary KPIs:** Post Engagement volume/Cost Per Engagement (MF1.4.10), Messaging Conversation volume, video watch metrics (MF1.4.9) for the Video Views sub-type, Conversion volume for the Conversions sub-type.
- **Failure Signals / Secondary KPIs:** High engagement volume with negligible downstream business-relevant outcome (e.g., high Post Engagement with no corresponding Messaging or Conversion growth when those are the actual business goal); Engagement Rate Ranking (MF1.3.45) trending "Below Average."
- **Common Use Cases:** Community-building content, customer-service-oriented Messaging campaigns, product-question conversational funnels, WhatsApp-based commerce conversations.
- **Common Mistakes:** Selecting Post Engagement as the Optimization Event when the actual business goal is Messaging-based conversation volume, mismatching the Optimization Event to the true desired outcome; treating vanity engagement metrics as proxies for business outcomes without a genuine link established.
- **Decision Rules:** If the business goal is conversational lead qualification, the Optimization Event should be Messaging, not Post Engagement or Video Views — a Recommendation should flag a sub-type/business-goal mismatch where a Messaging-oriented Business Page runs a Post-Engagement-optimized Ad Set with negligible Messaging Conversation output.

### MF1.5.4 Leads

- **Official Goal:** Maximize the number of qualifying prospective-customer contact submissions, via whichever lead-capture mechanism is configured.
- **Business Goal / Purpose:** Build a pipeline of prospective customers for follow-up by sales, support, or marketing teams, typically outside Meta's own platform (in a CRM).
- **Meta Optimization Logic:** The Auction optimizes for the estimated likelihood of a Lead Event (MF1.3.32) via the configured conversion location (website, Instant Form, Messaging, Calls), optionally informed by fed-back offline lead-quality signals (MF1.3.32's Professional Notes).
- **AI Interpretation:** Leads-objective signals should be interpreted with an explicit awareness that Meta's own reported Lead count reflects submission volume, not qualified-lead or closed-deal volume, unless offline conversion feedback has been integrated.
- **Typical Funnel Stage:** Mid funnel.
- **Supported Conversion Locations:** Website, Instant Forms, Messenger, Instagram, WhatsApp, Calls.
- **Supported Optimization Events (sub-types, all preserved):**
  - **Website Leads** — optimizes for a Lead Event fired via Pixel/Conversions API upon a website form submission.
  - **Instant Forms** — optimizes for native, in-platform Instant Form (MF1.3.34) completions without the user leaving Meta's app.
  - **Messaging** — optimizes for a qualifying Lead outcome reached through a Messaging Conversation flow (e.g., a structured message-based qualification sequence).
  - **Calls** — optimizes for users likely to initiate a phone call treated as the qualifying Lead action.
  - **Conversions (CRM-fed)** — optimizes using offline/CRM-fed Lead-quality Conversion Events, allowing the system to bias toward users resembling historically qualified Leads rather than raw submission volume.
- **Required Infrastructure:** Pixel/Conversions API (Website Leads), an Instant Form configuration on the Page (Instant Forms), Messaging setup (Messaging sub-type), Call tracking (Calls sub-type), CRM-to-Meta offline event feed (Conversions/CRM-fed sub-type).
- **Expected User Intent:** Moderate-to-high; users are expected to deliberately submit contact/qualifying information, a meaningfully higher-commitment action than a Traffic-objective click.
- **Success Signals / Primary KPIs:** CPL (MF1.4.4), Lead volume, form/Instant Form completion rate, downstream qualified-lead rate where CRM feedback is integrated.
- **Failure Signals / Secondary KPIs:** Low CPL with disproportionately poor downstream qualified-lead rate (indicating volume-over-quality optimization drift, per MF1.3.32's Common Mistakes); high Instant Form abandonment rate relative to opens.
- **Common Use Cases:** B2B lead generation, real estate inquiries, financial-services consultations, high-consideration-purchase inquiry capture.
- **Common Mistakes:** Optimizing purely for CPL without ever feeding qualified-lead outcomes back to Meta, permanently capping optimization quality at "cheap submissions" rather than "valuable prospects"; over-lengthening Instant Form qualifying questions past the point where completion-rate loss outweighs the lead-quality gain.
- **Decision Rules:** If CPL is healthy but downstream sales-qualified rate (external CRM data) is poor over a sustained period, a Recommendation should suggest integrating CRM-fed offline Conversions to redirect optimization toward quality rather than raw volume.

### MF1.5.5 App Promotion

- **Official Goal:** Maximize app installs, or maximize a specific in-app Event (post-install engagement, registration, purchase) among users likely to perform it.
- **Business Goal / Purpose:** Grow an app's user base and/or drive valuable in-app behavior among an existing or newly acquired app user base.
- **Meta Optimization Logic:** The Auction optimizes for the estimated likelihood of an App Install Event or a specified post-install App Event, using signals from the App SDK/Meta's mobile measurement integration.
- **AI Interpretation:** App Promotion signals should be interpreted with platform (iOS/Android) context always attached, since privacy-driven measurement differences between platforms (particularly iOS's App Tracking Transparency constraints) materially affect signal completeness and should not be misread as a performance difference.
- **Typical Funnel Stage:** Mid-to-lower funnel (App Installs sub-type is mid-funnel acquisition; App Events sub-type is lower-funnel value optimization among an already-acquired base).
- **Supported Conversion Locations:** App (App Store/Play Store install flow, and in-app Event tracking thereafter).
- **Supported Optimization Events (sub-types, all preserved):**
  - **App Installs** — optimizes purely for the App Install Event, maximizing new-user acquisition volume within Budget.
  - **App Events** — optimizes for a specified post-install Event (e.g., registration completion, level achieved, in-app purchase), targeting users likely to both install and subsequently perform that deeper action, generally at a higher CPI/CPA than pure Installs but with higher post-install value.
- **Required Infrastructure:** App SDK integration (Meta SDK or a supported Mobile Measurement Partner) reporting installs and in-app Events back to Meta.
- **Expected User Intent:** Moderate for App Installs (users must complete an app-store download flow); higher for App Events (users must install and then engage further).
- **Success Signals / Primary KPIs:** CPI (MF1.4.11) for the App Installs sub-type; cost per specified in-app Event and post-install retention/value Metrics for the App Events sub-type.
- **Failure Signals / Secondary KPIs:** Low CPI with poor post-install engagement/retention, indicating the Optimization Event is acquiring low-value installs (a mismatch parallel to the Leads Objective's volume-vs-quality pattern); measurement gaps from platform-level tracking constraints being misread as a genuine performance drop.
- **Common Use Cases:** Mobile game user acquisition, subscription-app trial signups, e-commerce app installs paired with a subsequent in-app Purchase Event optimization.
- **Common Mistakes:** Optimizing exclusively for App Installs long-term without ever transitioning to an App Events-based Optimization Goal once sufficient in-app Event volume exists, missing the opportunity to optimize for genuinely valuable users rather than raw install count.
- **Decision Rules:** Once an app has sufficient in-app Event volume (e.g., Purchase or Registration) to support Learning Phase exit under an App Events Optimization Goal, a Recommendation should suggest transitioning from App Installs to App Events optimization to target higher-value users.

### MF1.5.6 Sales

- **Official Goal:** Maximize purchases or purchase value among users likely to complete a transaction, whether on a website, within an app, via a Catalog-driven experience, or through Messaging/Calls.
- **Business Goal / Purpose:** Directly drive revenue-generating transactions, the most bottom-of-funnel, business-outcome-proximate Objective.
- **Meta Optimization Logic:** The Auction optimizes for the estimated likelihood (and, where a value-optimization Bid Strategy is used, estimated value) of a Purchase or equivalent transaction Conversion Event, drawing on Pixel/Conversions API/Catalog signals.
- **AI Interpretation:** Sales-objective signals should be interpreted as the most directly revenue-linked in the entire vocabulary, and should be evaluated primarily through ROAS (MF1.4.6) and CPA (MF1.4.5) rather than upper-funnel Metrics like CTR alone, which are diagnostic support signals here rather than primary KPIs.
- **Typical Funnel Stage:** Bottom of funnel.
- **Supported Conversion Locations:** Website, App, Catalog (Advantage+ Catalog Ads), Messenger, Instagram, WhatsApp, Calls.
- **Supported Optimization Events (sub-types, all preserved):**
  - **Website Conversions** — optimizes for a Purchase (or other value-tracked) Conversion Event fired via Pixel/Conversions API on the advertiser's website.
  - **Catalog Sales / Advantage+ Catalog Ads** — optimizes using a connected Catalog and Product Set (MF1.3.35/MF1.3.36) to dynamically show relevant products, most commonly for retargeting users who viewed or added specific items, but also supporting broader prospecting.
  - **Messaging** — optimizes for a Purchase or transaction completed through a Messaging Conversation flow (conversational commerce).
  - **Calls** — optimizes for users likely to complete a purchase-related phone call.
  - **App Conversions (Sales-objective variant)** — optimizes for in-app Purchase Events specifically, distinguished from the App Promotion Objective's App Events sub-type by its explicit revenue-value framing and typical use of value-based (ROAS-floor) Bid Strategies.
- **Required Infrastructure:** Pixel and/or Conversions API with accurate Purchase value passing (Website Conversions, Messaging, Calls sub-types); a connected Catalog and Dataset (Catalog Sales sub-type); App SDK integration with in-app Purchase Event tracking (App Conversions sub-type).
- **Expected User Intent:** High; users are expected to complete a full transaction, the highest-commitment action in the Objective taxonomy.
- **Success Signals / Primary KPIs:** ROAS (MF1.4.6), CPA per Purchase (MF1.4.5), Purchase volume and value.
- **Failure Signals / Secondary KPIs:** ROAS below the advertiser's known breakeven point, CVR (MF1.4.12) declining despite healthy upstream CTR (indicating a landing/checkout problem rather than an advertising problem), Catalog data staleness (per MF1.3.35's Common Mistakes) causing poor Catalog Sales relevance.
- **Common Use Cases:** E-commerce direct-response campaigns, retargeting abandoned-cart users via Catalog Sales, conversational commerce via WhatsApp/Messenger, subscription or high-ticket purchase campaigns via Calls.
- **Common Mistakes:** Allowing Purchase value to be passed inaccurately or inconsistently, silently corrupting ROAS calculation while Purchase count still appears normal (per MF1.3.31's Common Mistakes); setting an unrealistically aggressive ROAS-floor Bid Strategy that restricts Delivery volume below the level needed to exit Learning Phase.
- **Decision Rules:** If ROAS is below breakeven and CVR/Conversion Rate Ranking (MF1.3.46) indicate a landing-page/offer issue rather than a Delivery/Auction issue, a Recommendation should point toward landing-page/checkout investigation rather than Bid Strategy or Budget adjustment.

---

## MF1.6 Events Dictionary

Each standard Event is documented with: Definition, Typical Trigger Point, Funnel Position, Value-Carrying (whether it typically carries a monetary value), Primary Objective Association, Common Mistakes. Standard Events are pre-defined names in Meta's taxonomy (as distinct from Custom Events, MF1.6.13); every Event is a specialization of the generic Event concept (MF1.3.9).

### MF1.6.1 Page View

- **Definition:** Fires when a user loads any page on the advertiser's website with the Pixel/Conversions API installed.
- **Typical Trigger Point:** Any page load, site-wide.
- **Funnel Position:** Top of on-site funnel (broadest, least qualified signal).
- **Value-Carrying:** No.
- **Primary Objective Association:** Foundational for Custom Audience creation (MF1.8); rarely used directly as an Optimization Event due to its low specificity.
- **Common Mistakes:** Using Page View as an Optimization Event when a more specific downstream Event (View Content, Add To Cart) would better reflect genuine intent.

### MF1.6.2 View Content

- **Definition:** Fires when a user views a specific piece of content, typically a product or listing detail page.
- **Typical Trigger Point:** Loading a product/detail page.
- **Funnel Position:** Upper-mid funnel.
- **Value-Carrying:** Optionally (e.g., product price).
- **Primary Objective Association:** Common Custom Audience source for retargeting under Sales; occasionally used as an Optimization Event for early-funnel Catalog engagement.
- **Common Mistakes:** Firing View Content generically on every page rather than specifically on content/product pages, diluting its specificity as a retargeting/audience signal.

### MF1.6.3 Search

- **Definition:** Fires when a user performs an on-site search.
