- **Typical Trigger Point:** Submission of a search query on the advertiser's website or app.
- **Funnel Position:** Upper-mid funnel.
- **Value-Carrying:** No (though search-term metadata is often attached).
- **Primary Objective Association:** Custom Audience source (e.g., users who searched for a specific category); rarely used as a direct Optimization Event.
- **Common Mistakes:** Failing to pass search-term metadata, losing the ability to build category-specific Custom Audiences from this Event.

### MF1.6.4 Add To Wishlist

- **Definition:** Fires when a user adds a product to a wishlist or favorites/saved-items list.
- **Typical Trigger Point:** A wishlist/save action on a product page.
- **Funnel Position:** Mid funnel.
- **Value-Carrying:** Optionally.
- **Primary Objective Association:** Custom Audience source for Sales retargeting, representing users with demonstrated but not yet acted-upon purchase interest.
- **Common Mistakes:** Treating Add To Wishlist volume as directly comparable in intent strength to Add To Cart, when it typically signals lower near-term purchase intent.

### MF1.6.5 Add To Cart

- **Definition:** Fires when a user adds a product to their shopping cart.
- **Typical Trigger Point:** An add-to-cart action on a product page or listing.
- **Funnel Position:** Mid-to-lower funnel.
- **Value-Carrying:** Yes, typically (product price × quantity).
- **Primary Objective Association:** A primary Custom Audience source and occasionally an Optimization Event for Sales; a common Optimization Event where Purchase volume is too low to support Learning Phase directly (a proxy-Event strategy).
- **Common Mistakes:** Using Add To Cart as a permanent Optimization Event substitute for Purchase even after Purchase volume becomes sufficient, missing the more accurate downstream signal once available.

### MF1.6.6 Initiate Checkout

- **Definition:** Fires when a user begins the checkout process.
- **Typical Trigger Point:** Entering the checkout flow from the cart.
- **Funnel Position:** Lower funnel.
- **Value-Carrying:** Yes, typically.
- **Primary Objective Association:** A key diagnostic Event for Sales — a gap between Initiate Checkout and Purchase volume is a classic checkout-abandonment diagnostic signal (see MF1.4.12's Common Misinterpretations).
- **Common Mistakes:** Not monitoring the Initiate-Checkout-to-Purchase ratio as its own diagnostic, missing checkout-flow friction that advertising-level changes cannot fix.

### MF1.6.7 Add Payment Info

- **Definition:** Fires when a user adds payment information during checkout.
- **Typical Trigger Point:** Completing the payment-details step of checkout.
- **Funnel Position:** Lower funnel, closest to Purchase.
- **Value-Carrying:** Yes, typically.
- **Primary Objective Association:** A fine-grained checkout-funnel diagnostic Event for Sales, sitting between Initiate Checkout and Purchase.
- **Common Mistakes:** Omitting this Event and losing the ability to distinguish "abandoned before entering payment" from "abandoned after entering payment," two very different checkout-friction diagnoses.

### MF1.6.8 Purchase

- See MF1.3.31 for the full Core Domain Object definition. Funnel Position: bottom of funnel. Value-Carrying: Yes (its defining characteristic). Primary Objective Association: Sales (the anchor Event). Common Mistakes: see MF1.3.31.

### MF1.6.9 Lead

- See MF1.3.32 for the full Core Domain Object definition. Funnel Position: mid funnel. Value-Carrying: No by default (optionally value-carrying if a lead-value estimate is passed). Primary Objective Association: Leads (the anchor Event). Common Mistakes: see MF1.3.32.

### MF1.6.10 Complete Registration

- **Definition:** Fires when a user completes an account or membership registration/signup.
- **Typical Trigger Point:** Successful account-creation form submission.
- **Funnel Position:** Mid funnel.
- **Value-Carrying:** Optionally (e.g., free vs. paid tier registration value).
- **Primary Objective Association:** Common Optimization Event for Leads (subscription/membership businesses) and occasionally App Promotion's App Events sub-type (in-app registration).
- **Common Mistakes:** Conflating a low-friction newsletter signup with a genuine account/membership Complete Registration, understating the qualification bar this Event is meant to represent.

### MF1.6.11 Contact

- **Definition:** Fires when a user initiates direct contact with the Business outside a form submission — e.g., clicking a phone number, email link, or chat-initiation button.
- **Typical Trigger Point:** A direct-contact action on the website.
- **Funnel Position:** Mid-to-lower funnel.
- **Value-Carrying:** No, typically.
- **Primary Objective Association:** A supplementary Optimization/tracking Event for Leads, especially service-based businesses relying on direct contact over form submissions.
- **Common Mistakes:** Failing to distinguish Contact-Event volume by contact method (phone vs. email vs. chat), losing diagnostic granularity on which contact channel is actually converting to business.

### MF1.6.12 Subscribe

- **Definition:** Fires when a user starts a paid subscription.
- **Typical Trigger Point:** Completion of a subscription-purchase flow.
- **Funnel Position:** Bottom of funnel.
- **Value-Carrying:** Yes, typically (subscription price, and often a recurring-value estimate).
- **Primary Objective Association:** Sales, for subscription-based business models specifically, often paired with a lifetime-value-aware ROAS interpretation (per MF1.4.6's Professional treatment of margin-dependent healthy ranges).
- **Common Mistakes:** Evaluating Subscribe-based ROAS using only the first-period subscription value rather than an estimated lifetime value, understating true campaign profitability for subscription models.

### MF1.6.13 Custom Events

- **Definition:** Advertiser-defined Events not covered by Meta's standard Event taxonomy (MF1.6.1–MF1.6.12), used to track business-specific actions with a custom name and optional parameters.
- **Typical Trigger Point:** Defined entirely by the advertiser's own implementation.
- **Funnel Position:** Variable, defined by the advertiser's use case.
- **Value-Carrying:** Optionally, per the advertiser's own implementation.
- **Primary Objective Association:** Any Objective, when no standard Event adequately captures the advertiser's specific desired action.
- **Common Mistakes:** Creating a Custom Event that duplicates an existing Standard Event under a different name, fragmenting Audience-building and Optimization Event selection unnecessarily rather than reusing the Standard Event.

### MF1.6.14 Offline Events

- **Definition:** Conversion or outcome data that occurred outside any Meta-trackable digital surface (e.g., an in-store purchase, a phone-closed sale, a CRM-recorded qualified lead), uploaded to Meta after the fact and matched back to the original ad exposure.
- **Typical Trigger Point:** Batch or API upload from the advertiser's offline systems (POS, CRM) after the offline outcome occurs.
- **Funnel Position:** Bottom of funnel (typically the truest business outcome available).
- **Value-Carrying:** Yes, typically.
- **Primary Objective Association:** Sales and Leads, as the feedback mechanism referenced throughout this Framework (MF1.3.32's Professional Notes, MF1.5.4's Decision Rules) for closing the loop between Meta-visible submission/click volume and true downstream business value.
- **Common Mistakes:** Uploading Offline Events too infrequently or too late relative to the applicable Attribution Window, causing otherwise-valid outcomes to fail to match back to their originating ad exposure.

### MF1.6.15 Server Events (Conversions API Events)

- **Definition:** Events sent directly from an advertiser's server (via the Conversions API, MF1.3.7) rather than from the user's browser or device, used to improve measurement reliability against browser-side tracking loss.
- **Typical Trigger Point:** Server-side logic mirroring a corresponding browser/app-side action (e.g., a server-confirmed Purchase).
- **Funnel Position:** Same as whichever Event type is being sent server-side (mirrors the corresponding standard Event's funnel position).
- **Value-Carrying:** Same as the mirrored Event.
- **Primary Objective Association:** All Objectives with a Conversions API integration; particularly critical for Sales, where Purchase value accuracy directly drives ROAS reliability.
- **Common Mistakes:** Implementing Conversions API Events without properly deduplicating against the corresponding browser/Pixel-side Event, causing the same real-world action to be double-counted (see Deduplication, MF1.6.16).

### MF1.6.16 Deduplication

- **Definition:** The mechanism by which Meta identifies that a browser-side (Pixel) Event and a server-side (Conversions API) Event represent the same real-world action, using a shared Event ID, and counts it only once.
- **Typical Trigger Point:** Occurs automatically whenever both a Pixel and Conversions API Event carrying the same Event ID and Event Name are received within a matching window.
- **Funnel Position:** Not applicable (a measurement-integrity mechanism, not a funnel stage).
- **Value-Carrying:** Not applicable.
- **Primary Objective Association:** All Objectives using both Pixel and Conversions API concurrently (the recommended, current best-practice setup per Meta's own guidance).
- **Common Mistakes:** Omitting a shared, consistent Event ID between the browser-side and server-side firing of the same action, causing genuine double-counting that inflates both Conversion counts and, for value-carrying Events, ROAS.

### MF1.6.17 Matching Quality

- **Definition:** A score/diagnostic reflecting how reliably Meta can match a reported Event (particularly a server-side Conversions API Event) back to a specific known user, based on the completeness and accuracy of identifying parameters supplied (e.g., hashed email, phone, external ID, browser/device signals).
- **Typical Trigger Point:** Continuously evaluated as Events are received; visible per Pixel/Dataset in Events Manager (MF1.3.10).
- **Funnel Position:** Not applicable (a measurement-quality diagnostic, not a funnel stage).
- **Value-Carrying:** Not applicable.
- **Primary Objective Association:** All Objectives; directly affects Attribution accuracy and therefore the reliability of every downstream Metric for Leads and Sales specifically.
- **Common Mistakes:** Passing minimal or low-quality identifying parameters (e.g., omitting hashed email/phone when available), needlessly capping Matching Quality and, by extension, Attribution reliability and Optimization Goal accuracy.

---

## MF1.7 Attribution Vocabulary

Extends MF1.3.41's Attribution Core Domain Object with its full constituent vocabulary. Each term below is documented with: Definition, Role in Attribution, Relationship to Other Attribution Terms, Common Mistakes.

### MF1.7.1 Click Attribution

- **Definition:** Crediting a Conversion to an Ad based on the user having clicked that Ad prior to converting, within the applicable Attribution Window.
- **Role in Attribution:** The higher-confidence of the two attribution paths (alongside View Attribution), since a click is an explicit, deliberate user action.
- **Relationship to Other Attribution Terms:** Typically given a longer Attribution Window than View Attribution (e.g., 7-day-click vs. 1-day-view) reflecting its higher confidence.
- **Common Mistakes:** Assuming all attributed Conversions are Click Attributions by default — many Attribution Model configurations include a View Attribution component that can materially change reported Conversion volume.

### MF1.7.2 View Attribution

- **Definition:** Crediting a Conversion to an Ad based on the user having merely seen (been served an Impression of) that Ad, without clicking, prior to converting, within the applicable Attribution Window.
- **Role in Attribution:** The lower-confidence of the two attribution paths, since exposure alone does not confirm the user consciously registered or acted on the Ad.
- **Relationship to Other Attribution Terms:** Typically given a shorter Attribution Window than Click Attribution (commonly 1-day-view).
- **Common Mistakes:** Over-crediting Awareness-stage exposure for a Conversion that may have been substantially or entirely driven by another channel or by direct organic intent, overstating the Ad's true causal contribution (see Incrementality, MF1.7.5).

### MF1.7.3 Attribution Window

- **Definition:** The maximum time period, following a Click or View, within which a subsequent Conversion may still be credited to that ad exposure.
- **Role in Attribution:** Bounds how far back in time credit can be assigned, balancing capturing genuinely delayed decision cycles against over-crediting stale exposures.
- **Relationship to Other Attribution Terms:** Configured independently for Click Attribution and View Attribution (e.g., "7-day click, 1-day view" is a common combined setting).
- **Common Mistakes:** Comparing reported Conversion volume across two periods or accounts using different Attribution Window settings, producing a methodology-driven rather than performance-driven difference (see MF1.3.41's Common Mistakes).

### MF1.7.4 Attribution Model

- **Definition:** The rule set determining how credit for a Conversion is distributed when a user interacted with advertising more than once prior to converting (e.g., last-click, or a data-driven model distributing partial credit across multiple touchpoints).
- **Role in Attribution:** Determines not just whether a Conversion is credited, but how much credit each contributing touchpoint receives when multiple exposures preceded it.
- **Relationship to Other Attribution Terms:** Works together with Attribution Window (which bounds eligible touchpoints in time) to produce the final credited Conversion count and value.
- **Common Mistakes:** Assuming a simple last-click model when a data-driven multi-touch model is actually configured, misinterpreting why a given touchpoint's reported Conversion count seems lower than expected.

### MF1.7.5 Incrementality

- **Definition:** The portion of a Conversion outcome that would not have occurred without the advertising exposure, as distinct from the total Attributed Conversion count, which may include users who would have converted anyway (e.g., via organic search or brand loyalty).
- **Role in Attribution:** Represents the true causal contribution of advertising, typically measured via controlled experiments (e.g., holdout/test-vs-control studies) rather than standard Attribution reporting alone.
- **Relationship to Other Attribution Terms:** A deeper, experimentally-derived complement to standard Attribution (Click/View Attribution, Attribution Windows/Models), which measures correlation-with-exposure rather than true causal lift.
- **Common Mistakes:** Treating standard Attributed Conversion volume as equivalent to Incremental Conversion volume, overstating advertising's true causal business impact, especially for View Attribution and broad-reach Awareness activity.

### MF1.7.6 Assisted Conversion

- **Definition:** A Conversion where a given Ad/Ad Set/Campaign was part of the user's path to conversion but did not receive the final attribution credit under the configured Attribution Model (e.g., an earlier touchpoint in a multi-touch journey).
- **Role in Attribution:** Surfaces the contribution of upper/mid-funnel activity that a last-touch-oriented view would otherwise render invisible.
- **Relationship to Other Attribution Terms:** Most visible/relevant when a last-click-leaning Attribution Model is in use; less distinct a concept under a fully data-driven multi-touch Attribution Model, which already distributes partial credit across touchpoints.
- **Common Mistakes:** Concluding an upper-funnel (e.g., Awareness or Traffic) Campaign contributed nothing to Sales because it rarely receives final-touch Attribution credit, ignoring its Assisted Conversion contribution to the Conversion Path.

### MF1.7.7 Conversion Path

- **Definition:** The sequence of tracked touchpoints (Ad exposures/clicks, and potentially non-Meta channels where cross-channel measurement is integrated) a user passed through before completing a Conversion.
- **Role in Attribution:** Provides the full sequence context that Attribution Models compress into a single credit-distribution outcome.
- **Relationship to Other Attribution Terms:** The raw sequence data that Attribution Model (MF1.7.4) processes to produce credited Conversions, and from which Assisted Conversion (MF1.7.6) touchpoints are identified.
- **Common Mistakes:** Analyzing Conversion Path data only within Meta's own platform when a meaningful portion of a user's real path includes non-Meta touchpoints, producing an incomplete picture of the true multi-channel journey.

---

## MF1.8 Audience Vocabulary

Extends MF1.3.15's Audience Core Domain Object with its full constituent vocabulary. Each term below is documented with: Definition, Construction Method, Typical Use, Relationship to Other Audience Terms, Common Mistakes.

### MF1.8.1 Core Audience

- **Definition:** An Audience defined by manually specified criteria — demographics, location, interests, and behaviors — without reference to any existing Custom Audience or Lookalike source.
- **Construction Method:** Manual selection of targeting criteria within Ads Manager.
- **Typical Use:** Prospecting for entirely new users with no prior relationship to the Business, especially early in a Business's advertising history before sufficient Pixel/Customer data exists to build Custom or Lookalike Audiences.
- **Relationship to Other Audience Terms:** The foundational Audience type; Custom and Lookalike Audiences are built from data the Business already has, whereas Core Audience relies solely on Meta's own user-profile signals.
- **Common Mistakes:** Over-specifying granular interest/behavior criteria in the belief that narrower manual targeting outperforms broader targeting plus Meta's own optimization — current guidance and typical outcomes generally favor broader Core Audiences combined with strong Optimization Goals and Creative over heavily narrowed manual criteria.

### MF1.8.2 Custom Audience

- **Definition:** An Audience built from an advertiser's own first-party data — a customer list, website/app Event data (via Pixel/Conversions API/App SDK), or engagement with the Business's Meta assets (Page, Instagram Account, videos, Instant Forms).
- **Construction Method:** Uploading a hashed customer list, or defining rules against Pixel/App/on-platform engagement Event data (e.g., "users who fired Add To Cart in the last 30 days").
- **Typical Use:** Retargeting (MF1.8.5) users with an existing relationship to the Business; also used as the seed source for Lookalike Audiences (MF1.8.3).
- **Relationship to Other Audience Terms:** The most common seed source for Lookalike Audiences; the mechanism underlying Retargeting.
- **Common Mistakes:** Building a Custom Audience from an Event with too low a volume or too loose a relevance threshold, resulting in an Audience too small or too broad to be strategically useful.

### MF1.8.3 Lookalike Audience

- **Definition:** An Audience Meta's system generates by identifying users who statistically resemble a chosen source Audience (typically a Custom Audience of existing customers or high-value users), based on Meta's own modeling of shared characteristics.
- **Construction Method:** Selecting a source Audience and a target country/region and similarity percentage (e.g., a 1% Lookalike is the most similar tier, with wider percentages up to 10% trading similarity for reach).
- **Typical Use:** Prospecting for new users likely to behave like existing high-value customers, without manually guessing at demographic/interest criteria.
- **Relationship to Other Audience Terms:** Built from a Custom Audience source; distinct from Advantage+ Audience, which does not require an explicit source Audience.
- **Common Mistakes:** Using a low-quality or too-small source Audience (e.g., all website visitors rather than actual purchasers) to seed a Lookalike, producing a lower-quality Lookalike than a more deliberately chosen high-value source would.

### MF1.8.4 Advantage+ Audience

- **Definition:** Meta's system-driven Audience-targeting mode that uses minimal or no manually specified criteria, instead allowing the delivery system to find users across the broadest eligible population who are likely to complete the Ad Set's Optimization Goal, optionally informed by an advertiser-supplied "audience suggestion" as a soft signal rather than a hard restriction.
- **Construction Method:** Enabled as a targeting mode at the Ad Set level, optionally paired with an audience suggestion (e.g., an existing Custom Audience or Core Audience criteria used only as directional guidance).
- **Typical Use:** Current default recommendation for most prospecting and retargeting use cases, per Meta's own guidance, on the premise that the Optimization Goal and Auction's own predictive signals typically outperform manually narrowed criteria.
- **Relationship to Other Audience Terms:** Represents a philosophical shift from advertiser-defined targeting (Core Audience, Custom Audience, Lookalike) toward system-driven targeting; can still incorporate a Custom Audience as a soft suggestion input.
- **Common Mistakes:** Assuming Advantage+ Audience ignores all advertiser input — it can still weight toward a supplied audience suggestion, so results should be interpreted with that suggestion's influence in mind, not treated as fully unconstrained.

### MF1.8.5 Retargeting

- **Definition:** The practice of showing Ads specifically to users with an existing tracked relationship to the Business (e.g., past website visitors, App users, Customer list members), typically via a Custom Audience.
- **Construction Method:** Not a distinct targeting mechanism itself, but a strategic use of Custom Audiences (MF1.8.2) built from prior-interaction data.
- **Typical Use:** Re-engaging users further down the funnel who have already demonstrated some level of interest (e.g., Add To Cart, MF1.6.5) but have not yet completed the desired Conversion.
- **Relationship to Other Audience Terms:** A strategic application of Custom Audience; typically operates at higher Frequency tolerance and different Bid Strategy expectations than broad prospecting (Core/Lookalike/Advantage+).
- **Common Mistakes:** Retargeting an Audience for an extended period without Frequency monitoring or Creative Refresh, accelerating both Audience Saturation and Creative Fatigue given the Audience's inherently small, repeatedly-exposed size.

### MF1.8.6 Exclusions

- **Definition:** Criteria or Custom Audiences deliberately removed from an Ad Set's eligible Audience, preventing specific users from being shown the Ad.
- **Construction Method:** Selecting a Custom Audience (e.g., "existing customers") or demographic/interest criteria to exclude from an otherwise-defined Audience.
- **Typical Use:** Preventing wasted spend showing prospecting Ads to already-converted customers, or separating Audiences across sibling Ad Sets to reduce Audience Overlap (MF1.8.7).
- **Relationship to Other Audience Terms:** Operates on top of any Audience type (Core, Custom, Lookalike, Advantage+ suggestion); a primary tool for managing Audience Overlap.
- **Common Mistakes:** Over-applying Exclusions until the remaining eligible Audience becomes too small to support efficient Delivery or Learning Phase exit.

### MF1.8.7 Audience Overlap

- **Definition:** The degree to which two or more Ad Sets' eligible Audiences share the same underlying users, causing those Ad Sets to compete against each other, and potentially against themselves, in the Auction.
- **Construction Method:** Not a targeting configuration itself, but a diagnostic condition detectable via Meta's Audience Overlap reporting tools or inferred from unexpectedly poor Delivery across sibling Ad Sets.
- **Typical Use:** A diagnostic concept referenced when troubleshooting inefficient Delivery or inflated cost across a multi-Ad-Set Campaign structure.
- **Relationship to Other Audience Terms:** Commonly caused by insufficiently differentiated Custom/Lookalike Audiences across sibling Ad Sets; mitigated via Exclusions (MF1.8.6) or Campaign Budget (MF1.3.22), which naturally reduces self-competition by pooling budget.
- **Common Mistakes:** Structuring many narrowly-segmented Ad Sets with Ad Set Budget (rather than Campaign Budget) without checking for overlap, unknowingly causing the advertiser's own Ad Sets to bid against each other in the same Auction.

### MF1.8.8 Audience Saturation

- **Definition:** The condition in which an Audience has been shown an Ad so repeatedly (high Frequency) that further Delivery yields diminishing or negative returns — declining CTR/CVR and rising CPM despite unchanged targeting.
- **Construction Method:** Not a targeting configuration; a diagnosed condition arising from Frequency (MF1.3.26/MF1.4.7) accumulating over the life of a fixed-size Audience.
- **Typical Use:** A diagnostic concept most relevant to small Retargeting Audiences or long-running Awareness campaigns without Audience refresh.
- **Relationship to Other Audience Terms:** A direct consequence of Frequency exceeding healthy ranges (MF1.4.7) for a given Audience's size and funnel stage; typically remedied via Audience Expansion (MF1.8.9) or Creative Refresh (MF1.10).
- **Common Mistakes:** Attributing declining performance solely to Creative Fatigue (MF1.9) without checking whether Audience Saturation is a compounding or primary cause, since the two frequently co-occur and are remedied differently (Creative Refresh vs. Audience Expansion).

### MF1.8.9 Audience Expansion

- **Definition:** Broadening an Ad Set's eligible Audience — by relaxing manual criteria, increasing a Lookalike's similarity percentage, enabling Advantage+ Audience, or removing Exclusions — typically to counteract Audience Saturation or to scale Delivery volume.
- **Construction Method:** An adjustment applied to an existing Audience definition, rather than a standalone Audience type.
- **Typical Use:** Scaling a well-performing but Saturation-limited Ad Set; a standard response to Frequency exceeding Warning/Critical ranges (MF1.4.7) without a corresponding performance justification.
- **Relationship to Other Audience Terms:** The typical remedy for Audience Saturation (MF1.8.8); interacts with Horizontal Scaling (MF1.10) as one of the two general scaling strategies.
- **Common Mistakes:** Expanding an Audience so aggressively in a single step that it constitutes a "significant edit," resetting Learning Phase (MF1.3.19) and temporarily destabilizing Delivery just as the Ad Set needed to scale smoothly.

---

## MF1.9 Creative Vocabulary

Extends MF1.3.17 (Creative) and MF1.3.37 (Creative Asset) with the full constituent vocabulary. Each term below is documented with: Definition, Role in the Creative, Format/Placement Notes, Common Mistakes.

### MF1.9.1 Creative (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.3.17. Referenced here as the parent object every term in this section is a component or property of.

### MF1.9.2 Creative Asset (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.3.37. Referenced here as the visual/media component every format-specific term below specializes.

### MF1.9.3 Headline

- **Definition:** The short, prominent text line displayed alongside a Creative Asset, typically summarizing the offer or value proposition.
- **Role in the Creative:** The first and most attention-drawing text element after the visual Asset itself.
- **Format/Placement Notes:** Character limits and display prominence vary by Placement; some Placements (e.g., Stories, Reels) display Headline less prominently or not at all, making the Creative Asset and Primary Text carry proportionally more weight.
- **Common Mistakes:** Writing a Headline that duplicates the Primary Text's message rather than complementing it with a distinct, scannable hook.

### MF1.9.4 Primary Text

- **Definition:** The main body copy displayed with an Ad, typically above or below the Creative Asset, used for the fuller message, offer detail, or storytelling.
- **Role in the Creative:** Carries the bulk of the Ad's written message and context.
- **Format/Placement Notes:** Longer Primary Text is often truncated with a "See More" expansion on Feed Placements; front-loading the key message in the first 1–2 lines is standard professional practice.
- **Common Mistakes:** Front-loading Primary Text with information that only matters after the key hook, causing truncation to hide the actual value proposition.

### MF1.9.5 Call to Action (CTA)

- **Definition:** A short, standardized button label (e.g., "Shop Now," "Learn More," "Sign Up," "Send Message") indicating the action the advertiser wants the user to take.
- **Role in the Creative:** The explicit behavioral prompt connecting the Creative's message to the destination (MF1.3.17's Inputs).
- **Format/Placement Notes:** CTA options are drawn from a fixed, Objective-dependent list (e.g., "Get Quote" for Leads, "Shop Now" for Sales) rather than free text.
- **Common Mistakes:** Selecting a CTA that mismatches the actual destination (e.g., "Shop Now" leading to a lead-generation Instant Form rather than a purchasable product page), creating a user-expectation mismatch that can depress downstream Conversion Rate (MF1.4.12).

### MF1.9.6 Carousel

- **Definition:** An Ad format displaying two or more scrollable cards, each with its own Creative Asset, headline, and destination link, within a single Ad.
- **Role in the Creative:** Allows multiple products, features, or steps of a narrative to be shown within one Ad unit.
- **Format/Placement Notes:** Commonly used for multi-product Catalog Sales or for sequential storytelling; supported across most Feed-based Placements.
- **Common Mistakes:** Ordering Carousel cards without considering that later cards receive systematically less viewer attention, placing the strongest product/message first is standard professional practice.

### MF1.9.7 Collection

- **Definition:** An Ad format combining a primary video or image with a grid of Catalog-driven product images beneath it, opening into a full-screen, app-like browsing experience (Instant Experience) when tapped.
- **Role in the Creative:** Bridges a discovery-style primary visual with an immersive, Catalog-driven shopping experience without leaving the platform.
- **Format/Placement Notes:** Requires a connected Catalog (MF1.3.35); primarily available on mobile Feed-based Placements.
- **Common Mistakes:** Populating the Collection's product grid with a Product Set (MF1.3.36) that is stale or mismatched to the primary video/image's theme.

### MF1.9.8 Reels

- **Definition:** A short-form, vertical, full-screen video Placement/format on Facebook and Instagram, optimized for immersive, sound-on-by-default viewing.
- **Role in the Creative:** A distinct Placement (MF1.3.16) with its own format expectations (9:16 vertical, native-feeling short video), not simply a repurposed Feed video.
- **Format/Placement Notes:** Feed-optimized (1:1/4:5) Creative Assets typically render poorly here; purpose-built vertical video Creative is standard professional practice.
- **Common Mistakes:** Reusing a horizontal or square video Asset unmodified for Reels, resulting in heavy letterboxing/cropping and degraded Quality Ranking (MF1.3.44).

### MF1.9.9 Stories

- **Definition:** A short-form, vertical, full-screen Placement on Facebook and Instagram appearing between organic Stories content, similar in format expectations to Reels but distinct in surrounding user context (ephemeral organic content vs. discovery-feed short video).
- **Role in the Creative:** A distinct Placement with the same vertical/immersive format expectations as Reels.
- **Format/Placement Notes:** Interactive overlay elements (polls, stickers) common in organic Stories are not generally available to standard Ads, a distinction worth noting when designing for this Placement.
- **Common Mistakes:** Treating Stories and Reels as fully interchangeable for Creative design purposes, when surrounding organic context and typical viewing behavior differ between them.

### MF1.9.10 Feed

- **Definition:** The primary scrollable content stream on Facebook and Instagram, historically the most common Ad Placement, supporting a wide range of formats (single image/video, Carousel, Collection).
- **Role in the Creative:** The most format-flexible Placement, generally accepting square (1:1) or portrait (4:5) aspect ratios as the current standard.
- **Format/Placement Notes:** Users scroll past Feed content quickly, making the first 1–3 seconds of a video Asset or the immediate visual impact of a static Asset disproportionately important.
- **Common Mistakes:** Designing exclusively for Feed and then reusing the same Asset unmodified across Stories/Reels, the specific mismatch flagged repeatedly across MF1.9.8/MF1.9.9's Common Mistakes.

### MF1.9.11 Dynamic Creative

- **Definition:** A Meta capability that allows an advertiser to supply multiple variants of each Creative component (multiple Assets, Headlines, Primary Texts, CTAs) within a single Ad, letting the delivery system automatically test and serve the best-performing combinations per user.
- **Role in the Creative:** Shifts Creative Testing (MF1.9.13) from a manual multi-Ad process into a single Ad-level construct with system-driven combination optimization.
- **Format/Placement Notes:** Requires supplying multiple assets/text variants at Ad creation; reporting on individual component-level performance is more limited than reporting on fully discrete Ads.
- **Common Mistakes:** Supplying too many low-quality or redundant variants, diluting the system's ability to confidently identify genuinely superior combinations within a reasonable data volume.

### MF1.9.12 Creative Fatigue

- **Definition:** The decline in a Creative's performance (typically falling CTR, rising CPM, declining Engagement Rate Ranking) that occurs as an Audience is repeatedly exposed to the same Creative over time.
- **Role in the Creative:** The Creative-side counterpart to Audience Saturation (MF1.8.8) — the two frequently co-occur and share the same underlying symptom (rising Frequency, declining efficiency) but have different primary remedies (Creative Refresh vs. Audience Expansion).
- **Format/Placement Notes:** Onset speed varies by Audience size (smaller Audiences fatigue faster at a given Budget) and Creative format (video content sometimes sustains attention longer than static, depending on content quality).
- **Common Mistakes:** Diagnosing declining performance as Creative Fatigue without checking Frequency and Audience size, when Audience Saturation may be the primary or compounding cause (see MF1.8.8's Common Mistakes for the inverse framing of this same diagnostic ambiguity).

### MF1.9.13 Creative Testing

- **Definition:** The deliberate practice of running multiple Creative variants (via discrete Ads or Dynamic Creative) under comparable conditions to identify which performs best against the Ad Set's Optimization Goal.
- **Role in the Creative:** The primary mechanism by which Creative-level Quality/Engagement/Conversion Rate Ranking improvements (MF1.3.44–MF1.3.46) are discovered and acted upon.
- **Format/Placement Notes:** Requires sufficient Delivery volume per variant to reach statistically meaningful conclusions (see Statistical Significance, MF1.11); too many concurrent variants within one Ad Set thins this per-variant volume.
- **Common Mistakes:** Declaring a "winning" Creative from too small a sample, or changing multiple variables (Creative and Audience simultaneously) within the same test, confounding the result's true cause.

---

## MF1.10 Optimization Vocabulary

Extends MF1.3.19 (Learning Phase), MF1.3.13 (Optimization Goal), and MF1.3.20 (Bid Strategy) with the remaining optimization-management vocabulary. Each term below is documented with: Definition, Role in Optimization, Relationship to Other Optimization Terms, Common Mistakes.

### MF1.10.1 Learning Phase (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.3.19. Referenced here as the parent state every term in this section either causes, resets, or resolves.

### MF1.10.2 Learning Limited

- **Definition:** A specific Learning Phase outcome in which an Ad Set is not accumulating sufficient optimization events (per Meta's ~50-events-in-7-days framing, MF1.3.19) to ever exit Learning Phase under its current configuration.
- **Role in Optimization:** A critical failure-diagnostic status distinct from ordinary, temporary "Learning" status — Learning Limited does not resolve on its own without a configuration change.
- **Relationship to Other Optimization Terms:** Typically caused by an Optimization Event that is too rare relative to Budget/Bid Strategy/Audience size; resolved by broadening the Audience, relaxing the Bid Strategy, increasing Budget, or (per MF1.6.5's proxy-Event strategy) switching to a higher-volume proxy Optimization Event.
- **Common Mistakes:** Leaving an Ad Set in Learning Limited status indefinitely without intervention, allowing it to continue spending inefficiently under unstabilized delivery.

### MF1.10.3 Optimization Event (cross-reference)

The specific Event (MF1.3.9) an Ad Set's Optimization Goal (MF1.3.13) is configured to maximize the likelihood of. Not a separate object from Event/Optimization Goal — included here as a vocabulary anchor point connecting the Events Dictionary (MF1.6) to the Optimization Vocabulary.

### MF1.10.4 Bid Strategy (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.3.20. Referenced here as the parent concept for Budget Strategy's interaction below.

### MF1.10.5 Budget Strategy

- **Definition:** The overall approach to structuring and allocating Budget (MF1.3.21) across a Campaign's Ad Sets — encompassing the choice between Campaign Budget and Ad Set Budget (MF1.3.22/MF1.3.23), the daily-vs-lifetime framing, and how Budget is adjusted over a Campaign's life (including in response to Scaling decisions, MF1.10.6).
- **Role in Optimization:** The budget-side counterpart to Bid Strategy, jointly determining achievable Delivery volume and cost.
- **Relationship to Other Optimization Terms:** Directly interacts with Scaling (MF1.10.6) decisions, since scaling fundamentally means deliberately adjusting Budget Strategy upward.
- **Common Mistakes:** Treating Budget Strategy as a "set once" decision rather than revisiting it as Campaign performance and Learning Phase status evolve.

### MF1.10.6 Scaling

- **Definition:** The deliberate practice of increasing Delivery volume for a well-performing Ad Set or Campaign, via either Horizontal Scaling (MF1.10.7) or Vertical Scaling (MF1.10.8).
- **Role in Optimization:** The primary lever for growing a successful Campaign's business impact once initial performance validation is achieved.
- **Relationship to Other Optimization Terms:** Interacts closely with Learning Phase (a "significant edit" during scaling can reset it), Audience Saturation (aggressive Vertical Scaling can accelerate it), and Budget Strategy.
- **Common Mistakes:** Scaling too aggressively in a single step (e.g., doubling Budget at once), which Meta's own guidance and typical Auction dynamics indicate can destabilize Delivery and trigger a fresh Learning Phase, undermining the very performance being scaled.

### MF1.10.7 Horizontal Scaling

- **Definition:** Increasing total Delivery volume by adding new, parallel Ad Sets or Campaigns (e.g., testing new Audiences or duplicating a proven Ad Set structure) rather than increasing the Budget of an existing one.
- **Role in Optimization:** Preserves a proven Ad Set's stable Learning Phase status and performance while pursuing incremental volume growth elsewhere.
- **Relationship to Other Optimization Terms:** An alternative to Vertical Scaling; often paired with Audience Expansion (MF1.8.9) when the new parallel Ad Set targets an adjacent Audience segment.
- **Common Mistakes:** Horizontally duplicating an Ad Set into a new one with a heavily overlapping Audience (MF1.8.7) rather than a genuinely distinct segment, causing the new Ad Set to compete with the original rather than add incremental volume.

### MF1.10.8 Vertical Scaling

- **Definition:** Increasing total Delivery volume by increasing the Budget of an existing, already-proven Ad Set or Campaign.
- **Role in Optimization:** The most direct scaling lever, but the one most likely to trigger Learning Phase reset or Delivery instability if applied too aggressively in a single step.
- **Relationship to Other Optimization Terms:** An alternative to Horizontal Scaling; Meta's own guidance and typical Auction dynamics generally favor moderate, incremental Budget increases (commonly cited around 15–20% at a time) over large step-changes.
- **Common Mistakes:** Increasing Budget by a large percentage in a single change during a strong performance period, triggering exactly the Learning Phase reset and Delivery instability the advertiser was trying to avoid by scaling a proven performer.

### MF1.10.9 Creative Refresh

- **Definition:** Deliberately introducing new or meaningfully revised Creative Assets/copy into an Ad Set or Campaign to counteract Creative Fatigue (MF1.9.12).
- **Role in Optimization:** The primary remedy for Creative Fatigue, as Audience Expansion (MF1.8.9) is for Audience Saturation.
- **Relationship to Other Optimization Terms:** Often deployed alongside Audience Rotation (MF1.10.10) when both Creative Fatigue and Audience Saturation are diagnosed together, since the two frequently co-occur (per MF1.9.12's Common Mistakes).
- **Common Mistakes:** Refreshing only superficial elements (e.g., a color/filter change) that do not meaningfully alter the Ad's core visual/message pattern, failing to genuinely reset viewer attention.

### MF1.10.10 Audience Rotation

- **Definition:** Deliberately cycling an Ad Set or Campaign's targeting between multiple pre-defined Audience segments over time (or across sibling Ad Sets), rather than running a single fixed Audience indefinitely, to manage long-term Frequency and Saturation.
- **Role in Optimization:** A structural strategy for managing Audience Saturation over a Campaign's extended life, complementary to but distinct from a one-time Audience Expansion.
- **Relationship to Other Optimization Terms:** Frequently paired with Creative Refresh in a coordinated long-running-Campaign maintenance cadence.
- **Common Mistakes:** Rotating Audiences on too short a cycle, never allowing any single Audience segment to accumulate enough data to exit Learning Phase or reach statistically meaningful conclusions.

---

## MF1.11 Reporting Vocabulary

Extends MF1.3.38 (Reporting Dimension), MF1.3.39 (Breakdown), and MF1.3.40 (Metric) with the remaining reporting/analysis vocabulary. Each term below is documented with: Definition, Role in Reporting, Relationship to Other Reporting Terms, Common Mistakes.

### MF1.11.1 Metric (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.3.40, with the full catalog in MF1.4.

### MF1.11.2 Reporting Dimension (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.3.38.

### MF1.11.3 Breakdown (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.3.39.

### MF1.11.4 Comparison

- **Definition:** The act of evaluating two or more entities (Campaigns, Ad Sets, Ads, time periods) against each other along one or more shared Metrics, to identify relative performance differences.
- **Role in Reporting:** The most common analytical operation performed on reporting data — nearly every optimization decision stems from some Comparison (this period vs. last, this Ad Set vs. its siblings, this Ad vs. its Creative Testing competitors).
- **Relationship to Other Reporting Terms:** Requires comparable Reporting Dimension grain and, ideally, comparable Attribution/measurement settings between the compared entities to be meaningful; often paired with Benchmarks (MF1.11.5) when no direct internal comparator exists.
- **Common Mistakes:** Comparing entities with materially different Delivery volume, Learning Phase status, or Attribution settings as though they were on equal footing, producing a misleading Comparison.

### MF1.11.5 Benchmarks

- **Definition:** A reference performance range or value — drawn from an advertiser's own historical data, industry norms, or the Architecture's benchmark mechanism (§26 lineage, specialized here) — against which current performance is judged.
- **Role in Reporting:** Provides the context needed to judge whether a given Metric value is genuinely healthy or concerning, since most Metrics (MF1.4) have no universal "good" value in isolation.
- **Relationship to Other Reporting Terms:** The reference point a Comparison is drawn against when no direct internal comparator is more appropriate; underlies the Healthy/Warning/Critical Range fields throughout MF1.4.
- **Common Mistakes:** Applying an industry-wide Benchmark rigidly to an account whose own historical baseline differs meaningfully, ignoring that self-referential Benchmarks are often more actionable than external ones.

### MF1.11.6 Confidence

- **Definition:** A qualitative or quantitative expression of how much certainty should be placed in a reported figure or conclusion, given the underlying data volume and variability.
- **Role in Reporting:** Prevents overreacting to noisy, low-volume data as though it were a firm conclusion.
- **Relationship to Other Reporting Terms:** Closely related to, but distinct from, Statistical Significance (MF1.11.7) — Confidence is the broader qualitative judgment; Statistical Significance is its more formal, quantitative expression.
- **Common Mistakes:** Presenting a low-Confidence, low-volume finding (e.g., a Breakdown segment with only a handful of Conversions) with the same authority as a high-Confidence, high-volume one.

### MF1.11.7 Statistical Significance

- **Definition:** A formal, quantitative measure of whether an observed difference between two Comparison groups (e.g., two Ad variants in Creative Testing) is unlikely to be due to random chance alone, typically expressed via a confidence level or p-value in supporting analysis tooling.
- **Role in Reporting:** The rigorous check that should precede declaring a Creative Testing "winner" or acting on a Breakdown finding.
- **Relationship to Other Reporting Terms:** The quantitative formalization of Confidence (MF1.11.6); directly dependent on the underlying Delivery/Conversion volume available for the Comparison.
- **Common Mistakes:** Declaring a Creative Testing winner or a Breakdown-driven decision from a sample too small to reach Statistical Significance, mistaking normal variance for a genuine effect (echoing MF1.3.39's and MF1.9.13's Common Mistakes from the Breakdown and Creative Testing perspectives respectively).

### MF1.11.8 Trend

- **Definition:** The directional pattern of a Metric's value over a sequence of time periods, distinct from its value at any single point in time.
- **Role in Reporting:** Surfaces gradual degradation or improvement that a single-period snapshot would miss (e.g., CPM creeping upward over several weeks even though no single week looks alarming in isolation).
- **Relationship to Other Reporting Terms:** A time-based application of Comparison, using the time-period Reporting Dimension specifically; often the basis for Alert-style Recommendations (as specialized from the Generic Framework's Decision concepts) that trigger on sustained directional movement rather than single-period thresholds.
- **Common Mistakes:** Reacting to a single period's fluctuation as though it were a Trend, without confirming the pattern persists across multiple consecutive periods.

---

## MF1.12 AI Vocabulary

Per this Framework's required structure, every object defined above receives a canonical normalization entry: **Canonical Name** (the sole term an AI system should ever store/emit), **Aliases** (input synonyms an AI system must normalize to the Canonical Name), **Forbidden Terms** (terms that resemble but must never be confused with this object), **Normalization Rule** (the resolution rule when ambiguous input is received), **Expected Data Type**, **Relationships** (cross-references, by this Framework's own `MF1.x` numbering). Presented as tables grouped by MF1.3–MF1.11's own section structure, so every entry remains traceable to its full definition above.

### MF1.12.1 Domain Architecture & Business-Identity Objects (MF1.3.1–MF1.3.10)

| Canonical Name | Aliases | Forbidden Terms | Normalization Rule | Data Type | Relationships |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Business | Business Account, Advertiser | Business Manager (a distinct tool, not the entity) | Map "company"/"advertiser" to Business unless a Business Manager tool-context is explicit | Entity | MF1.3.1 |
| Business Portfolio | — | Business Manager | Distinguish from Business Manager by its multi-Business-spanning scope | Entity | MF1.3.2 |
| Business Manager | BM | Business Portfolio, Ad Account | Reserve for the single-Business admin tool only | Entity | MF1.3.3 |
| Page | Facebook Page, Fan Page | Instagram Account | Never conflate with Instagram Account even when linked | Entity | MF1.3.4 |
| Instagram Account | IG Account | Page | Keep distinct even when connected to the same Business | Entity | MF1.3.5 |
| Pixel | Facebook Pixel, Meta Pixel | Conversions API, Dataset | Pixel = browser-side only; do not use interchangeably with server-side mechanisms | Entity | MF1.3.6 |
| Conversions API | CAPI, server-side API | Pixel | CAPI = server-side only | Entity | MF1.3.7 |
| Dataset | Pixel/CAPI Dataset | Catalog | A Dataset aggregates event sources; never conflate with a product Catalog | Entity | MF1.3.8 |
| Event | Conversion Event, Optimization Event (context-dependent) | Metric | Event = a discrete occurrence; Metric = a computed measure derived from Events | Entity | MF1.3.9 |
| Events Manager | — | Ads Manager (distinct tool) | Reserve strictly for the Event-configuration/diagnostic tool | Entity | MF1.3.10 |

### MF1.12.2 Structural Chain Objects (MF1.3.11–MF1.3.14, MF1.3.24)

| Canonical Name | Aliases | Forbidden Terms | Normalization Rule | Data Type | Relationships |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Campaign | — | Ad Set, Ad | Top of the 3-tier Analysis Level hierarchy only | Entity | MF1.3.11 |
| Objective | Campaign Objective | Optimization Goal | Objective = Campaign-level goal category (6 fixed values); never conflate with Ad-Set-level Optimization Goal | Enum | MF1.3.12, MF1.5 |
| Optimization Goal | — | Objective | Ad-Set-level, narrower than Objective | Entity | MF1.3.13 |
| Ad Set | Adset (non-standard spelling — normalize) | Campaign, Ad | Middle tier of the 3-tier hierarchy | Entity | MF1.3.14 |
| Ad | — | Ad Set, Creative | Bottom tier; distinct from the Creative it carries | Entity | MF1.3.24 |

### MF1.12.3 Delivery & Auction Objects (MF1.3.15–MF1.3.23, MF1.3.25)

| Canonical Name | Aliases | Forbidden Terms | Normalization Rule | Data Type | Relationships |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Audience | Targeting | — | Use Audience for the eligible-user definition | Entity | MF1.3.15, MF1.8 |
| Placement | Ad Placement | — | Surface-level eligibility only | Entity | MF1.3.16 |
| Creative | Ad Creative | Creative Asset (a component, not the whole) | Creative = full package; Asset = one media component | Entity | MF1.3.17, MF1.9 |
| Auction | Ad Auction | — | The per-impression allocation mechanism only | Process | MF1.3.18 |
| Learning Phase | — | Learning Limited (a specific sub-status) | Learning Limited is a status of Learning Phase, not a synonym | State | MF1.3.19, MF1.10.1–2 |
| Bid Strategy | Bidding Strategy, Bid Control | Budget Strategy | Bid Strategy governs the bid input; Budget Strategy governs the spend ceiling | Entity | MF1.3.20, MF1.10.4 |
| Budget | Ad Spend Budget | — | Generic parent of Campaign Budget/Ad Set Budget | Entity | MF1.3.21 |
| Campaign Budget | CBO | Ad Set Budget | Campaign-level, dynamically distributed | Entity | MF1.3.22 |
| Ad Set Budget | ABO | Campaign Budget | Ad-Set-level, fixed independent of siblings | Entity | MF1.3.23 |
| Delivery | Ad Delivery | — | The observable output of the Auction process | Process/State | MF1.3.25 |

### MF1.12.4 Measurement, Attribution & Ranking Objects (MF1.3.26–MF1.3.32, MF1.3.38–MF1.3.46)

| Canonical Name | Aliases | Forbidden Terms | Normalization Rule | Data Type | Relationships |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency | Ad Frequency | — | Impressions ÷ Reach only | Metric | MF1.3.26, MF1.4.7 |
| Reach | Unique Reach | Impressions | Unique users only, never total showings | Metric | MF1.3.27, MF1.4.8 |
| Impressions | — | Reach | Total showings, including repeats | Metric | MF1.3.28, MF1.4.8 |
| Clicks | Link Clicks (specific), Clicks (all) (broader superset) | — | Default to Link Clicks unless "(all)" is explicit | Metric | MF1.3.29 |
| Conversions | Conversion Events | — | Attributed occurrences of a defined Optimization Event | Metric | MF1.3.30 |
| Purchase | — | Lead, Subscribe | A value-carrying transaction-completion Event specifically | Event | MF1.3.31, MF1.6.8 |
| Lead | — | Purchase, Complete Registration | A qualifying-interest Event, not a transaction | Event | MF1.3.32, MF1.6.9 |
| Reporting Dimension | Dimension | Breakdown | Structural/temporal grouping axis | Concept | MF1.3.38 |
| Breakdown | — | Reporting Dimension | Delivery/audience-characteristic subdivision | Concept | MF1.3.39 |
| Metric | Performance Metric | Event | Computed measure, not a raw occurrence | Concept | MF1.3.40, MF1.4 |
| Attribution | Conversion Attribution | — | The crediting methodology, not the Conversion itself | Concept | MF1.3.41, MF1.7 |
| ROAS | Return on Ad Spend | CPA (different unit/direction) | Value ÷ Spend ratio; higher is better | Metric | MF1.3.42, MF1.4.6 |
| CPA | Cost Per Action, Cost Per Result | ROAS | Spend ÷ Event count; lower is better | Metric | MF1.3.43, MF1.4.5 |
| Quality Ranking | Ad Quality Ranking | Engagement Rate Ranking, Conversion Rate Ranking | The Creative/post-click-quality ranking specifically | Diagnostic | MF1.3.44 |
| Engagement Rate Ranking | — | Quality Ranking, Conversion Rate Ranking | The expected-engagement ranking specifically | Diagnostic | MF1.3.45 |
| Conversion Rate Ranking | — | Quality Ranking, Engagement Rate Ranking | The expected-conversion ranking specifically | Diagnostic | MF1.3.46 |

### MF1.12.5 Lead-Capture & Catalog Objects (MF1.3.33–MF1.3.37)

| Canonical Name | Aliases | Forbidden Terms | Normalization Rule | Data Type | Relationships |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Messaging Conversation | Conversation | Lead | A conversational thread, not itself a qualifying-interest Event unless configured as one | Entity/Event | MF1.3.33 |
| Instant Form | Lead Form, Lead Ad Form | Website Lead Form | Native, in-platform only | Entity | MF1.3.34 |
| Catalog | Product Catalog, Commerce Catalog | Product Set (a subset, not the whole) | Catalog = full inventory container | Entity | MF1.3.35 |
| Product Set | — | Catalog | A named, filtered subset of a Catalog | Entity | MF1.3.36 |
| Creative Asset | Asset | Creative | A single media component, not the full package | Entity | MF1.3.37, MF1.9.2 |

| CPM | Cost Per Mille, Cost Per Thousand Impressions | CPC, CPA | Spend ÷ Impressions × 1000 specifically | Metric | MF1.4.1 |
| CTR | Click-Through Rate | CVR | Link Clicks ÷ Impressions specifically | Metric | MF1.4.2 |
| CPC | Cost Per Click | CPM, CPA | Spend ÷ Link Clicks specifically | Metric | MF1.4.3 |
| CPL | Cost Per Lead | CPA (CPL is a specific case of CPA) | Spend ÷ Leads specifically | Metric | MF1.4.4 |
| CPI | Cost Per Install | CPA (CPI is a specific case of CPA) | Spend ÷ App Installs specifically | Metric | MF1.4.11 |
| CVR | Conversion Rate | CTR | Conversions ÷ Clicks (or Impressions, if stated) specifically | Metric | MF1.4.12 |
| ThruPlay | Video View (completion) | Video Plays (all) | 15-second-or-completion threshold specifically | Metric | MF1.4.9 |
| Post Engagement | — | Engagement (Objective name) | The Metric, distinct from the Objective's own name | Metric | MF1.4.10 |

## MF1.13 Naming Standards

Naming Standards below are domain-content conventions specific to Meta Ads entities, distinct in nature from the Architecture's own §17/§34 Naming Standards (which govern structural-position naming) and from EIFS.6/MAIFS.9–10 (which govern document/Framework naming) — no overlap or collision exists between these three naming-standard layers, each governing a different kind of named thing.

### MF1.13.1 Campaign Naming

- **Recommended Pattern:** `{Objective}_{Market/Region}_{Funnel Stage or Use Case}_{Date or Version}` (e.g., `Sales_US_Retargeting_2026Q3`).
- **Rationale:** Encoding Objective and Market directly in the name allows rapid identification during cross-Campaign Comparison (MF1.11.4) and Reporting Dimension (MF1.3.38) filtering without opening each Campaign individually.
- **Common Mistakes:** Free-text, inconsistent naming that varies by whoever created the Campaign, breaking any name-pattern-based reporting or automation downstream.

### MF1.13.2 Ad Set Naming

- **Recommended Pattern:** `{Audience Type}_{Audience Description}_{Placement Scope}_{Optimization Goal}` (e.g., `LAL1pct_Purchasers_AdvPlacements_Purchase`).
- **Rationale:** Encodes the Audience (MF1.3.15) and Optimization Goal (MF1.3.13) that most directly explain that Ad Set's Delivery and Learning Phase behavior.
- **Common Mistakes:** Omitting the Audience type/source from the name, making Audience Overlap (MF1.8.7) auditing across sibling Ad Sets far more error-prone.

### MF1.13.3 Ad Naming

- **Recommended Pattern:** `{Creative Format}_{Creative Concept/Theme}_{Variant Identifier}` (e.g., `Video_ProductDemo_V2`).
- **Rationale:** Encodes the Creative (MF1.3.17) format and concept, supporting Creative Testing (MF1.9.13) result tracking across variants.
- **Common Mistakes:** Reusing an identical Ad name across genuinely different Creative concepts, making historical Creative Testing results impossible to distinguish in reporting.

### MF1.13.4 Audience Naming

- **Recommended Pattern:** `{Audience Type}_{Source or Criteria}_{Lookback Window if applicable}` (e.g., `Custom_AddToCart_30Day`).
- **Rationale:** Encodes the Audience type (MF1.8.1–MF1.8.4) and its source/window, both essential for diagnosing Audience Saturation (MF1.8.8) and Overlap (MF1.8.7).
- **Common Mistakes:** Omitting the lookback window from a Custom Audience's name, making it unclear later whether two similarly-named Audiences are actually identical or subtly different in scope.

### MF1.13.5 Creative Naming

- **Recommended Pattern:** `{Asset Type}_{Theme/Message}_{Aspect Ratio or Placement Fit}_{Version}` (e.g., `Image_SummerSale_1x1_V3`).
- **Rationale:** Encodes format-fit information directly, reducing the Placement-Creative-mismatch failure pattern flagged repeatedly across MF1.9's Common Mistakes.
- **Common Mistakes:** Naming Creative Assets only by an internal file/project code with no human-readable theme or format information, making cross-team Creative Testing review dependent on someone always having system access.

### MF1.13.6 Event Naming

- **Recommended Pattern:** Standard Events (MF1.6.1–MF1.6.12) must use Meta's exact reserved Standard Event names, unmodified; Custom Events (MF1.6.13) should use a clear `{Verb}{Object}` pattern distinct from any Standard Event name (e.g., `DownloadedWhitepaper`, not `Lead` or `CompletedForm`).
- **Rationale:** Prevents the Custom-Event-duplicating-a-Standard-Event failure pattern flagged in MF1.6.13's Common Mistakes.
- **Common Mistakes:** Naming a Custom Event identically or near-identically to a Standard Event name, causing confusion about whether Standard Event matching logic applies to it.

### MF1.13.7 Report Naming

- **Recommended Pattern:** `{Reporting Dimension Grain}_{Objective or Scope}_{Period}` (e.g., `Campaign_SalesObjective_2026Q3`).
- **Rationale:** Encodes the Reporting Dimension (MF1.3.38) grain and scope directly, so a report's applicability is clear without opening it.
- **Common Mistakes:** Naming reports only by creation date, losing any indication of scope or grain and forcing every consumer to open the report to determine relevance.

### MF1.13.8 Dashboard Naming

- **Recommended Pattern:** `{Audience/Stakeholder}_{Primary Objective(s) Covered}_{Refresh Cadence}` (e.g., `Executive_AllObjectives_Weekly`).
- **Rationale:** Encodes intended audience and scope, preventing a Dashboard built for one stakeholder group's needs from being silently repurposed for a mismatched audience without adjustment.
- **Common Mistakes:** Building a single undifferentiated Dashboard covering every Objective and Metric for every stakeholder, diluting relevance for any single viewer rather than tailoring scope to audience.

---

## MF1.14 Dependency Map

Consolidates every Core Domain Object's `Dependencies` field (already established individually in MF1.3) into a single navigable map, so that no entity's full dependency chain requires cross-referencing more than one section. "Depends On" means the entity cannot exist, be configured, or produce meaningful output without the referenced entity(ies) already existing/being valid.

### MF1.14.1 Primary Structural Chain Dependency Table

| Entity | Depends On | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Business Manager | Business | MF1.3.1, MF1.3.3 |
| Page | Business (typically, though a Page may predate Business Manager linkage) | MF1.3.4 |
| Instagram Account | Page (for full ad-eligible linkage) | MF1.3.5 |
| Pixel | Business, a website property | MF1.3.6 |
| Conversions API | Business, server-side implementation capability | MF1.3.7 |
| Dataset | Pixel and/or Conversions API and/or App SDK sources | MF1.3.8 |
| Event | Pixel, Conversions API, or App SDK implementation | MF1.3.9 |
| Events Manager | Dataset(s) to display/manage | MF1.3.10 |
| Campaign | Ad Account, Objective selection | MF1.3.11, MF1.3.12 |
| Ad Set | Campaign, Optimization Goal, Audience, Placement, Budget (if Ad Set Budget), Bid Strategy | MF1.3.14 |
| Ad | Ad Set, Creative | MF1.3.24 |
| Audience | (Custom/Lookalike variants) Pixel/Dataset/customer list data | MF1.3.15, MF1.8 |
| Placement | Ad Set; Instagram placements additionally depend on Instagram Account | MF1.3.16 |
| Creative | Creative Asset(s), copy, destination configuration | MF1.3.17 |
| Auction | Ad Set eligibility (Budget, Bid Strategy, Learning Phase status) | MF1.3.18 |
| Learning Phase | Ad Set creation/edit history, Optimization Event volume | MF1.3.19 |
| Bid Strategy | Ad Set, Optimization Goal | MF1.3.20 |
| Budget | Campaign or Ad Set | MF1.3.21 |
| Campaign Budget | Campaign, at least one Ad Set | MF1.3.22 |
| Ad Set Budget | Ad Set | MF1.3.23 |
| Delivery | Budget, Bid Strategy, Audience, Placement, Creative all valid | MF1.3.25 |

### MF1.14.2 Measurement & Attribution Dependency Table

| Entity | Depends On | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency | Impressions, Reach (same period) | MF1.3.26 |
| Reach | Active Delivery | MF1.3.27 |
| Impressions | Active Delivery | MF1.3.28 |
| Clicks | Active Delivery, clickable Creative destination | MF1.3.29 |
| Conversions | Pixel/Conversions API/Dataset, defined Optimization Event, Attribution settings | MF1.3.30 |
| Purchase | Pixel/Conversions API/App SDK integration at transaction point | MF1.3.31 |
| Lead | Pixel/Conversions API (website), Instant Form, or Messaging/Call setup | MF1.3.32 |
| Messaging Conversation | Page (Messenger), Instagram Account (Instagram Direct), or WhatsApp integration | MF1.3.33 |
| Instant Form | Page | MF1.3.34 |
| Catalog | Pixel/App SDK (for retargeting use), Dataset | MF1.3.35 |
| Product Set | Catalog | MF1.3.36 |
| Creative Asset | Placement format requirements (for eligibility) | MF1.3.37 |
| Reporting Dimension | Underlying Delivery/Metric data at the selected grain | MF1.3.38 |
| Breakdown | Sufficient Delivery volume within each subdivision | MF1.3.39 |
| Metric | Raw Delivery and/or Event data | MF1.3.40 |
| Attribution | Pixel/Conversions API/App SDK with adequate Matching Quality | MF1.3.41 |
| ROAS | Purchase value accuracy, Attribution Window/Model, Spend | MF1.3.42 |
| CPA | Specified Event tracking/attribution, Spend | MF1.3.43 |
| Quality Ranking | Sufficient Delivery volume | MF1.3.44 |
| Engagement Rate Ranking | Sufficient Delivery volume | MF1.3.45 |
| Conversion Rate Ranking | Sufficient Conversion volume | MF1.3.46 |

### MF1.14.3 Cross-Cutting Dependency Observations

- **Every downstream Measurement object (MF1.14.2) transitively depends on the Primary Structural Chain (MF1.14.1)** — no Metric, Attribution outcome, or Ranking signal can exist without an underlying Campaign→Ad Set→Ad→Delivery chain already producing data.
- **Every Objective's supported Optimization Events (MF1.5) depend on the corresponding Event Dictionary entries (MF1.6) being properly implemented** — an Objective cannot be effectively pursued if its required Event tracking infrastructure (Pixel/Conversions API/Instant Form/App SDK) is missing, regardless of how well-configured the Campaign/Ad Set/Ad layer is.
- **Every Audience Vocabulary term except Core Audience (MF1.8.1) depends on some first-party data source** (Custom Audience on Pixel/Dataset/customer lists; Lookalike on a Custom Audience source; Advantage+ Audience optionally on a suggested source) — Core Audience is the only Audience type with no upstream data dependency.
- **Ranking signals (MF1.3.44–46) and Attribution (MF1.3.41) share a common upstream dependency on sufficient Delivery volume** — both categories of signal are unavailable or unreliable below a minimum data threshold, a recurring caveat across their individual Common Mistakes entries.
- **No entity in this Framework depends on any Generic Framework Series (F#.x) or Architecture (§) concept for its own domain definition** — all such references throughout MF1.1–MF1.13 are citations establishing conceptual lineage (per MAIFS.19's Genericity Preservation Rule), never structural dependencies the domain content itself requires to function.

---

## MF1.15 Knowledge Graph

A full semantic relationship map connecting every Core Domain Object and vocabulary term defined in this Framework, using named relationship types (extending the Architecture's own permitted Containment/Binding/Sequencing relationship kinds, §8, with domain-specific relationship types this Framework introduces for its own content, consistent with EIFS.8's additive-extension principle applied here to a Meta Ads Framework rather than a Generic Framework).

### MF1.15.1 Relationship Type Legend

| Relationship Type | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Contains** | Structural parent-child containment (mirrors Architecture §8 Containment) |
| **Produces** | An entity/process generates another as its output |
| **Measures** | A Metric quantifies an entity or process |
| **Governs** | An entity's configuration controls another's behavior |
| **Diagnoses** | A signal reveals the state/health of another entity |
| **Remedies** | An action addresses a diagnosed condition |
| **Specializes** | A sub-type is a specific instance/variant of a more general entity |
| **Requires** | A hard prerequisite (mirrors MF1.14's Dependency Map, expressed here as graph edges) |

### MF1.15.2 Structural Containment Edges

```
Business --Contains--> Business Manager --Contains--> Ad Account --Contains--> Campaign
Campaign --Contains--> Ad Set --Contains--> Ad --Contains--> Creative --Contains--> Creative Asset
Business --Contains--> Page, Instagram Account, Pixel, Conversions API, Dataset, Catalog   (Business-Identity Layer, MF1.2.3 — Association, not Containment)
Catalog --Contains--> Product Set
```

### MF1.15.3 Production Edges

```
Auction --Produces--> Delivery
Delivery --Produces--> Impressions, Reach, Clicks
Impressions, Reach --Produces (via ratio)--> Frequency
Ad exposure + Event --Produces (via Attribution)--> Conversions
Purchase --Produces--> Conversion Value --Produces (via ratio with Spend)--> ROAS
```

### MF1.15.4 Measurement Edges

```
CPM --Measures--> Impressions efficiency
CTR --Measures--> Click efficiency relative to Impressions
CPC --Measures--> Click cost efficiency
CPL, CPA, CPI --Measures--> Event acquisition cost efficiency
CVR --Measures--> Click-to-Conversion efficiency
ROAS --Measures--> Revenue efficiency
Frequency --Measures--> Repetition of exposure within an Audience
```

### MF1.15.5 Governance Edges

```
Objective --Governs--> supported Optimization Goal set (MF1.5)
Optimization Goal --Governs--> Auction's estimated-action-rate target
Bid Strategy --Governs--> Auction bid input
Budget / Budget Strategy --Governs--> Auction spend ceiling and pacing
Audience, Placement --Governs--> Auction eligible-impression pool
Attribution Window, Attribution Model --Governs--> which Conversions are counted toward Optimization Goal performance
```

### MF1.15.6 Diagnostic Edges

```
Quality Ranking, Engagement Rate Ranking, Conversion Rate Ranking --Diagnoses--> Ad-level Auction competitiveness root cause
Rising Frequency + declining CTR/CVR --Diagnoses--> Audience Saturation
Declining CTR/Engagement Rate Ranking at stable Frequency --Diagnoses--> Creative Fatigue
Learning Limited status --Diagnoses--> Optimization Event volume insufficiency
Initiate Checkout-to-Purchase gap --Diagnoses--> Checkout-flow friction
Assisted Conversion pattern --Diagnoses--> Under-credited upper/mid-funnel contribution
```

### MF1.15.7 Remedial Edges

```
Audience Saturation --Remedies via--> Audience Expansion, Audience Rotation
Creative Fatigue --Remedies via--> Creative Refresh, Creative Testing (new variant)
Learning Limited --Remedies via--> broaden Audience, relax Bid Strategy, increase Budget, switch to higher-volume proxy Optimization Event
Landing-page/Checkout diagnostic finding --Remedies via--> landing-page/checkout-flow investigation (outside advertising layer)
Low Matching Quality --Remedies via--> improved Conversions API parameter completeness
```

### MF1.15.8 Specialization Edges

```
Objective --Specializes into--> Optimization Event sub-types (per MF1.5, e.g., Awareness --Specializes into--> Reach, Impressions/Brand Awareness, Video Views, Ad Recall Lift)
Audience --Specializes into--> Core Audience, Custom Audience, Lookalike Audience, Advantage+ Audience (MF1.8.1-4)
Metric (generic) --Specializes into--> every named Metric in MF1.4 (CPM, CTR, CPC, CPL, CPA, ROAS, CPI, CVR, Frequency, video/engagement Metrics)
Ranking (generic diagnostic pattern) --Specializes into--> Quality Ranking, Engagement Rate Ranking, Conversion Rate Ranking
Event --Specializes into--> every Standard Event (MF1.6.1-12), Custom Events (MF1.6.13), Offline Events (MF1.6.14), Server Events (MF1.6.15)
```

### MF1.15.9 Requirement (Dependency) Edges

Fully enumerated in MF1.14's Dependency Tables; represented in this Knowledge Graph as `--Requires-->` edges pointing from every dependent entity to its prerequisite(s), so that MF1.14 and MF1.15 together form one consistent, non-duplicated graph — MF1.14 in tabular form, MF1.15 in typed-edge form — rather than two independent, potentially-diverging representations of the same relationships.

### MF1.15.10 Graph Completeness Statement

Every Core Domain Object (MF1.3.1–MF1.3.46) and every named vocabulary term (MF1.4–MF1.13) appears in at least one edge above, either directly or via its parent/child/specialization relationship already captured in the Containment or Specialization edge sets. No object defined in this Framework is an isolated node.

- [x] No section was summarized, shortened, or merged against the explicit "Do not summarize. Do not shorten." instruction — every optimization sub-type per Objective, every Event, and every Core Domain Object received its own full, individually-authored entry.

- [x] Written throughout for an expert audience (performance marketing agencies, enterprise businesses, marketing analysts, data engineers, media buyers, marketing scientists, BI teams, AI systems, future MAIFS frameworks) — no beginner/tutorial framing at any point.
- [x] Domain-specific content is present throughout, as required and expected of a Meta Ads Framework per MAIFS.4 — this Framework does not attempt Domain-Agnosticism, unlike the Generic Framework Series.
- [x] Every reference to the Architecture uses `§` only; every reference to a Generic Framework uses `F#.x` only; every reference to EIFS uses `EIFS.x` only; every reference to MAIFS uses `MAIFS.x` only; every internal reference uses `MF1.x` only — no restatement of any cited document's content, per MAIFS.17/EIFS.11.
- [x] No specialization in this document redefines a Generic Framework concept — every citation (e.g., Analysis/F1.2, Decision/F4.5, Action/F6.4, Association/F10.2) asserts a domain object as an instance of the generic concept, never alters the generic concept's own definition, per MAIFS.19.
- [x] No runtime behavior, code, algorithm, database schema, or AI-implementation detail appears anywhere in this document — only domain vocabulary, definitions, relationships, and reference numeric ranges, consistent with MAIFS.14's Validation Policy for Meta Ads Frameworks.
- [x] Internally consistent: every cross-reference between MF1.x sections points to a section that actually exists in this document (verified by direct section-number audit across MF1.1–MF1.15).
- [x] The Architecture Baseline (§0–§60), EIFS, the Generic Framework Series (Frameworks 1–10), and MAIFS all remain completely unmodified by this document's creation.

## Meta Framework 1 Closing Statement

**Meta Framework 1 — Domain Vocabulary Framework is now complete, validated, and frozen.** It establishes the official Meta Ads domain dictionary — Domain Architecture, 46 Core Domain Objects, a full Metrics Dictionary, a complete Objectives Dictionary preserving every optimization sub-type for all 6 Objectives, an Events Dictionary, and the Attribution, Audience, Creative, Optimization, Reporting, and AI Vocabularies, plus Naming Standards, a Dependency Map, and a Knowledge Graph — governed throughout by MAIFS and built without modifying the Enterprise Architecture Baseline (§0–§60), EIFS, the Generic Enterprise Intelligence Framework Series (Frameworks 1–10), or MAIFS itself. Any future change to Meta Framework 1 proceeds only through the Revision mechanism established at EIFS.7 and specialized for Meta Ads Frameworks at MAIFS.11. Every subsequent Meta Ads Framework in Series 2 is now free to cite this Framework's vocabulary (`MF1.x`) as its shared domain foundation, per MAIFS.6/MAIFS.7.

---

# Meta Framework 2 — Campaign Framework

**Status:** Complete, Validated, and Frozen. All 15 planned sections (MF2.1–MF2.15), plus the MF2.16 Final Validation Checklist and Closing Statement, are present in full per MAIFS.8's lifecycle policy. Any future change proceeds only through the Revision mechanism (EIFS.7, MAIFS.11).
**Audience:** Enterprise media buyers, performance marketing teams, agencies, data analysts, marketing scientists, AI systems, business intelligence teams, large-scale advertisers.
**Scope:** This Framework governs the Campaign layer (MF1.3.11) only — Campaign architecture, objective selection, budget, bid strategy, lifecycle, learning diagnostics, scaling, diagnostics, KPIs, decision intelligence, governance, and best practices, all analyzed strictly at Campaign grain. It does not go deep into Ad Set-level optimization (Audience/Placement mechanics, reserved for a future Ad Set Framework, MF3) or Creative-level optimization (reserved for a future Creative Framework) beyond the Campaign-level decisions that necessarily touch them.
**Vocabulary Rule:** No Core Domain Object, Metric, Event, Objective, or vocabulary term already defined in Meta Framework 1 is redefined here. Every such term is cited as `MF1.x` and extended with Campaign-layer decision logic, risk analysis, and governance that MF1 does not itself contain, per this document's own Meta Framework 2 Vocabulary Rule stated in the banner above and per MAIFS.19.

---

## MF2.1 Campaign Purpose

### MF2.1.1 What a Campaign Is

A Campaign (MF1.3.11) is the top tier of the three-tier Analysis Level hierarchy (Campaign → Ad Set → Ad, per Architecture §4, specialized by MF1.2.1's Primary Structural Chain) and the single point at which a Business commits to a specific Objective (MF1.3.12) for a defined body of advertising activity. Every Ad Set and Ad beneath a Campaign inherits that Campaign's Objective and operates in service of it; a Campaign therefore functions as the organizing unit that binds a business intention to a set of Meta delivery mechanics.

### MF2.1.2 Business Role

A Campaign is the unit at which a business stakeholder — a marketing lead, a media buyer, an agency account manager — states "this body of spend exists to achieve this kind of outcome." Budgets are typically approved, reported on, and evaluated for ROI at the Campaign level or a roll-up above it, making the Campaign the natural boundary for business accountability, even though the underlying Ad Sets and Ads are where day-to-day tactical execution happens.

### MF2.1.3 Technical Role

Technically, the Campaign is the structural container (Architecture §8 Containment relationship, specialized at MF1.15.2) that fixes the Objective (MF1.3.12) for everything beneath it, and — when Campaign Budget (MF1.3.22) is used — is also the level at which Budget itself is held and dynamically distributed. A Campaign cannot change its Objective after creation without being recreated; this permanence is what allows the Auction (MF1.3.18) and Meta's delivery system to reliably interpret every Ad Set beneath it as pursuing a single, stable optimization intent.

### MF2.1.4 Optimization Role

The Campaign's Objective is the single highest-leverage decision in the entire Campaign→Ad Set→Ad hierarchy: it determines which Optimization Goals (MF1.3.13) are even available to any Ad Set beneath it, which Metrics (MF1.4) are meaningful success signals, and which Events (MF1.6) must be tracked for the Campaign to function at all. An otherwise well-configured Ad Set cannot compensate for a Campaign built on the wrong Objective — this is why MF2.3 and MF2.4 below treat Objective analysis and selection as the foundation of this entire Framework.

### MF2.1.5 Relationship with Business Goals

A Campaign is the translation layer between a business goal (e.g., "grow monthly revenue," "fill the sales pipeline," "increase app subscriber base") and a Meta-native Objective (MF1.5). This translation is not always one-to-one: a single business goal may be pursued through multiple Campaigns with different Objectives at different funnel stages (e.g., an Awareness Campaign feeding a later Sales Campaign's retargeting Audience, per MF1.8.5), and the Campaign Objective Selection Framework (MF2.4) exists specifically to make this translation deliberate rather than accidental.

### MF2.1.6 Relationship with Ad Sets

Every Ad Set (MF1.3.14) belongs to exactly one Campaign and inherits its Objective. Where Campaign Budget (MF1.3.22) is in use, Ad Sets within the same Campaign also share a Budget pool and compete against each other only in the sense that Meta's delivery system reallocates spend toward the best performers — they do not compete for Objective definition, which is fixed once at the Campaign level.
