This Framework treats the boundary between Campaign-level decisions (Objective, Campaign Budget policy, overall structure) and Ad-Set-level decisions (Audience, Placement, granular Bid Strategy) as a hard scope line, per this document's stated Scope.

### MF2.1.7 Relationship with Meta's Delivery System

The Campaign's Objective is the primary signal Meta's Auction (MF1.3.18) and broader delivery system use to interpret what "success" means for every impression opportunity beneath it. Changing a Campaign's effective goal after launch is only possible by changing the Optimization Goal at the Ad Set level within the Objective's supported set (MF1.5), or by building an entirely new Campaign — the Campaign Objective itself is the immovable anchor point the entire delivery system's optimization logic is built around.

---

## MF2.2 Campaign Architecture

### MF2.2.1 Campaign Hierarchy

```
Business (MF1.3.1) → Ad Account → Campaign (MF1.3.11)
                                       ├── Objective (MF1.3.12)              [fixed at creation]
                                       ├── Campaign Budget (MF1.3.22)        [optional, Campaign-level]
                                       └── Ad Set (MF1.3.14) [1..N]
                                              ├── Optimization Goal (MF1.3.13)
                                              ├── Ad Set Budget (MF1.3.23)   [if not using Campaign Budget]
                                              ├── Audience (MF1.3.15), Placement (MF1.3.16), Bid Strategy (MF1.3.20)
                                              └── Ad (MF1.3.24) [1..N] → Creative (MF1.3.17)
```

This diagram specializes the Architecture's own Containment relationship (§8) and MF1.2.1's Primary Structural Chain; it introduces no new structural position, only a Campaign-scoped view of the chain MF1 already established.

### MF2.2.2 Campaign Lifecycle (Overview)

A Campaign moves through Planning, Creation, Learning, Stabilization, Scaling, Maintenance, Decline, Retirement, and Archiving. This overview exists here only to establish that Campaign Architecture and Campaign Lifecycle are two views of the same object — the full stage-by-stage treatment, including per-stage actions and risks, is delivered in MF2.7, per this Framework's own section structure.

### MF2.2.3 Campaign Ownership

A Campaign is owned, at any given time, by whichever person or system (media buyer, agency account manager, or an automated rules/bidding system) holds edit rights over its Objective-derived configuration and, where used, its Campaign Budget. Ownership is a governance concern with its own dedicated treatment in MF2.13 (Campaign Governance); this entry establishes only that ownership is always attributable to a specific accountable party, never ambiguous or shared without an explicit approval workflow (MF2.13.4).

### MF2.2.4 Campaign Dependencies

A Campaign depends on: an Ad Account (existing prior to any Campaign); a valid Objective selection (MF1.3.12) made at creation and immutable thereafter; at least one Ad Set to ever produce Delivery (MF1.3.25); and, for any Objective beyond pure Awareness, the Event-tracking infrastructure (Pixel/Conversions API/App SDK/Instant Forms, per MF1.14.1–MF1.14.2) required by its chosen Optimization Events. This is a Campaign-scoped restatement of MF1.14's Dependency Map, not a new dependency system — no dependency asserted here contradicts MF1.14.

### MF2.2.5 Campaign Governance (Overview)

Every Campaign is subject to naming, versioning, audit, and approval rules, given full treatment in MF2.13. This entry establishes only that Campaign Architecture and Campaign Governance are related but distinct concerns: Architecture describes what a Campaign structurally is and depends on; Governance describes who may change it, how, and under what record-keeping requirements.

### MF2.2.6 Campaign State Transitions

A Campaign moves through a bounded set of operational states over its life: **Draft** (being configured, not yet submitted for Delivery) → **In Review** (where an approval workflow, MF2.13.4, is in effect) → **Active** (eligible for Delivery) → **Paused** (temporarily withdrawn from the Auction, retaining all configuration) → **Completed** (a scheduled end date reached) → **Archived** (retained for historical/reporting reference, no longer editable in the ordinary course of business). A Paused Campaign may return to Active; a Completed or Archived Campaign is not expected to return to Active without an explicit re-creation or reactivation decision, which is itself a governance event (MF2.13).

### MF2.2.7 Campaign Relationships — Consolidated

| Related Concept | Nature of Relationship | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Business | A Campaign belongs to exactly one Ad Account, itself belonging to one Business | MF1.3.1, MF1.14.1 |
| Objective | Fixed at Campaign creation; governs every Ad Set's available Optimization Goals | MF1.3.12, MF2.1.4 |
| Budget | Optionally held and dynamically distributed at the Campaign level (Campaign Budget) | MF1.3.21–22, MF2.5 |
| Optimization | The Campaign's Objective bounds which Optimization Goals/Events are available beneath it | MF1.3.13, MF1.5 |
| Delivery | The Campaign is never itself the unit of Delivery — Delivery occurs at the Ad Set/Ad level, but Campaign-level Budget and Objective bound it | MF1.3.25 |
| Measurement | Campaign-level reporting aggregates Ad-Set/Ad-level Metrics under the Campaign's Reporting Dimension | MF1.3.38, MF1.4 |
| Learning | Campaign-level edits (e.g., Objective is immutable, but Campaign Budget changes) can indirectly reset Ad Set-level Learning Phase | MF1.3.19, MF2.8 |
| Reporting | The Campaign is the most common Reporting Dimension grain for business-level performance review | MF1.3.38, MF1.11 |

---

## MF2.3 Campaign Objectives

Each Objective is analyzed below using its MF1.5 definition as the unmodified base (Official Goal, Business Goal, Meta Optimization Logic, Supported Conversion Locations, Supported Optimization Events, Required Infrastructure, Success/Failure Signals — all cited, never restated in full) plus 13 new Campaign-layer fields this Framework introduces: Business Purpose, Optimization Purpose, Typical Funnel Position, Required Infrastructure (Campaign-level confirmation), Advantages, Limitations, Ideal Business Types, Decision Criteria, Scaling Readiness, Common Failure Reasons, Common Misconfigurations, Risk Analysis, Optimization Priorities, and a Decision Tree. Every optimization sub-type listed under MF1.5 is preserved individually below in its own inheritance table row — none are merged or omitted.

### MF2.3.1 Awareness (base definition: MF1.5.1)

- **Business Purpose:** Build top-of-funnel familiarity ahead of any direct-response ask, most valuable when later-funnel Campaigns (Traffic/Leads/Sales) will depend on a warmed, recognizable brand.
- **Optimization Purpose:** Maximize efficient exposure/recall (Reach, Impressions, ThruPlays, or Ad Recall Lift) rather than any downstream Conversion.
- **Typical Funnel Position:** Top of funnel — the entry point of the full-funnel Campaign structure this Framework treats a Business's total Campaign portfolio as potentially spanning.
- **Required Infrastructure (Campaign-level):** None beyond a Page/Instagram Account; the absence of a Pixel/Conversions API requirement makes Awareness the lowest-infrastructure-barrier Campaign to launch.
- **Advantages:** Lowest infrastructure barrier; builds a Custom Audience source (via video-view/engagement Custom Audiences) usable by later Campaigns; typically the lowest CPM Objective, maximizing exposure per dollar.
- **Limitations:** Produces no directly measurable revenue outcome on its own; success is only provable in combination with a later-funnel Campaign or a dedicated Brand Lift study (MF1.7.5's Incrementality concept).
- **Ideal Business Types:** New brand/product launches, businesses with long consideration cycles, businesses building a retargeting Audience pool ahead of a Sales push.
- **Decision Criteria:** Choose Awareness when no reliable Conversion tracking exists yet, when a genuinely new audience segment must be introduced to the brand before any direct-response ask is credible, or when the specific business goal is measurable recall/reach rather than any Conversion.
- **Scaling Readiness:** Generally high — Awareness Campaigns tolerate larger Budget increases without the Learning Phase (MF1.3.19) instability risk that Conversion-optimizing Campaigns face, since the Optimization Event (Reach/Impressions) is high-volume by nature.
- **Common Failure Reasons:** Running Awareness indefinitely with no defined transition point to a direct-response Campaign, leaving business impact unproven; allowing Frequency (MF1.4.7) to exceed healthy range unchecked, wasting spend on Audience Saturation (MF1.8.8) rather than incremental Reach.
- **Common Misconfigurations:** Selecting Awareness when a measurable Conversion goal already exists and adequate tracking is in place (a Traffic/Leads/Sales Objective would better serve the actual business need); using a single static Creative for the Campaign's entire life without a Creative Refresh (MF1.10.9) cadence.
- **Risk Analysis:** Low financial risk per impression (typically the cheapest CPM Objective) but high strategic risk if the business cannot connect Awareness spend to any downstream outcome, since no Conversion data exists to defend the spend in a budget review.
- **Optimization Priorities:** (1) Healthy Frequency management, (2) Reach efficiency (CPM), (3) Video/Recall depth metrics where applicable, in that order — Conversion-adjacent Metrics are explicitly out of scope for this Objective's own optimization priority ordering.
- **Decision Tree:**
```
Does a measurable Conversion goal already exist with adequate tracking?
 ├─ No  → Is the goal building brand recognition/recall specifically? → Yes → USE AWARENESS
 │                                                                    → No  → tracking gap must be resolved before objective selection (see MF2.4)
 └─ Yes → Awareness is not the correct Objective for this goal → see MF2.3.2–MF2.3.6
```

**MF2.3.1.1 Awareness Optimization Sub-Type Inheritance Table**

| Sub-Type | MF1 Reference | Inherited KPIs | Inherited Diagnostics |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reach | MF1.5.1 | Reach, CPM (MF1.4.1, MF1.4.8) | Audience Saturation (MF1.8.8) |
| Impressions / Brand Awareness | MF1.5.1 | Impressions, CPM, estimated Ad Recall Lift | Frequency-driven diagnostic (MF1.4.7) |
| Video Views (ThruPlays) | MF1.5.1 | ThruPlay Rate, watch-percentage breakpoints (MF1.4.9) | Early drop-off diagnostic (MF1.4.9) |
| Ad Recall Lift | MF1.5.1 | Modeled Ad Recall Lift (from Brand Lift study data) | Incrementality (MF1.7.5) cross-check |

### MF2.3.2 Traffic (base definition: MF1.5.2)

- **Business Purpose:** Move users from platform exposure to an owned or conversational destination for further engagement or eventual conversion.
- **Optimization Purpose:** Maximize the estimated likelihood of the chosen navigation action (Link Click, Landing Page View, Call, or Messenger/Instagram/WhatsApp thread open).
- **Typical Funnel Position:** Upper-to-mid funnel.
- **Required Infrastructure (Campaign-level):** A Pixel recommended for Landing Page Views measurement; Call tracking for the Calls sub-type; a connected Page for Messenger/Instagram sub-types.
- **Advantages:** Low commitment barrier for users, producing high volume relative to Leads/Sales; useful as a lower-cost proxy signal Campaign ahead of a Conversion-heavy Campaign once enough Pixel data accrues.
- **Limitations:** Traffic volume alone does not guarantee any downstream business value; Link Clicks specifically can include low-intent or even non-genuine traffic if the "Clicks (all)" distinction is mismanaged (MF1.3.29).
- **Ideal Business Types:** Content publishers, businesses building initial Pixel/website data volume, businesses running lower-commitment lead-in campaigns ahead of retargeting.
- **Decision Criteria:** Choose Traffic when the immediate goal is destination visits rather than a deeper Conversion, or when insufficient Conversion volume exists yet to make a Leads/Sales Campaign viable (i.e., as a deliberate Campaign Objective Maturity Model stepping stone, MF2.4.5).
- **Scaling Readiness:** Moderate-to-high; click-based Optimization Events are generally higher-volume than Conversion Events, giving Traffic Campaigns an easier Learning Phase exit and therefore an easier scaling path than Leads/Sales.
- **Common Failure Reasons:** Optimizing for Link Clicks rather than Landing Page Views when landing-page load performance is a known issue, driving volume that never actually arrives; never transitioning to a Conversion-based Campaign once sufficient Pixel data accrues, permanently under-optimizing for the true business goal.
- **Common Misconfigurations:** Running Traffic to a destination that itself demands a deeper Conversion (e.g., driving Traffic to a checkout page without any Sales-objective retargeting Campaign following up); mismatched CTA selection versus the actual destination.
- **Risk Analysis:** Low direct financial risk per click, but a meaningful opportunity-cost risk if Traffic is run in isolation indefinitely without a clear activation path to a Conversion-oriented Campaign.
- **Optimization Priorities:** (1) CTR and CPC health, (2) Landing Page View rate versus Link Click volume (diagnosing click-quality), (3) downstream Pixel data accumulation rate in service of a future Leads/Sales transition.
- **Decision Tree:**
```
Is destination-arrival volume itself the business goal (e.g., content readership)?
 ├─ Yes → USE TRAFFIC (Link Clicks or Landing Page Views sub-type per intent strength needed)
 └─ No  → Is Conversion tracking infrastructure in place and Conversion volume achievable?
            ├─ Yes → prefer Leads/Sales directly (MF2.3.4/MF2.3.6)
            └─ No  → USE TRAFFIC as a deliberate maturity-model stepping stone (MF2.4.5) while infrastructure matures
```

**MF2.3.2.1 Traffic Optimization Sub-Type Inheritance Table**

| Sub-Type | MF1 Reference | Inherited KPIs | Inherited Diagnostics |
|---|---|---|---|
| Link Clicks | MF1.5.2 | CTR, CPC (MF1.4.2–3) | Clicks(all)-vs-Link-Clicks conflation (MF1.3.29) |
| Landing Page Views | MF1.5.2 | Landing Page View rate | Click-to-LPV drop-off (landing-page load diagnostic) |
| Calls | MF1.5.2 | Call initiation volume | — |
| Messenger / Instagram / WhatsApp | MF1.5.2 | Conversation initiation volume | Messaging Conversation quality gap (MF1.3.33) |

### MF2.3.3 Engagement (base definition: MF1.5.3)

- **Business Purpose:** Build social proof, community interaction, and conversational relationships spanning a wide intent spectrum from passive video viewing to deliberate Messaging.
- **Optimization Purpose:** Maximize the estimated likelihood of the chosen engagement action (Messaging, Video View, Post Engagement, Conversions, or Calls).
- **Typical Funnel Position:** Upper-to-mid funnel, extending to mid-to-lower funnel for the Messaging and Conversions sub-types.
- **Required Infrastructure (Campaign-level):** A connected Page (all sub-types); Pixel/Conversions API for the Conversions sub-type; WhatsApp Business integration for WhatsApp Messaging.
- **Advantages:** Flexible across a wide range of business goals from lightweight brand interaction to genuine conversational commerce; Post Engagement sub-type is typically low-cost per interaction.
- **Limitations:** The widest intent spectrum of any Objective makes sub-type selection unusually consequential — a mismatch here (per MF1.5.3's Common Mistakes) is more common than in narrower Objectives.
- **Ideal Business Types:** Community-driven brands, customer-service-oriented businesses, WhatsApp/Messenger-native commerce operations.
- **Decision Criteria:** Choose Engagement specifically when the business goal is genuinely conversational or social-interaction-based; choose the Messaging sub-type specifically (not Post Engagement) when the real goal is conversation volume, per MF1.5.3's Decision Rules.
- **Scaling Readiness:** Variable by sub-type — Post Engagement and Video Views scale similarly to Awareness (high-volume, low-friction Optimization Events); Messaging and Conversions scale more like Leads/Sales (lower-volume, higher-friction Optimization Events).
- **Common Failure Reasons:** Selecting Post Engagement when Messaging-based conversation volume is the actual business goal (the specific mismatch MF1.5.3 flags); never establishing a feedback loop from Messaging Conversations to genuine business outcomes.
- **Common Misconfigurations:** Running the Conversions sub-type without adequate Pixel/Conversions API maturity, effectively duplicating a Sales Campaign's infrastructure requirement under an Engagement label without Engagement's typical lower-friction advantage.
- **Risk Analysis:** Low-to-moderate; the main risk is strategic (optimizing for the wrong sub-type, producing vanity metrics with no business linkage) rather than financial.
- **Optimization Priorities:** (1) Sub-type/business-goal alignment (the single highest-leverage decision within this Objective, per MF1.5.3's Decision Rules), (2) Engagement Rate Ranking (MF1.3.45) health, (3) downstream conversation-to-outcome feedback where the Messaging sub-type is used.
- **Decision Tree:**
```
Is the real business goal conversational (lead qualification, customer support, commerce-via-chat)?
 ├─ Yes → USE ENGAGEMENT, Messaging sub-type
 └─ No  → Is the goal video attention specifically?
            ├─ Yes → USE ENGAGEMENT, Video Views sub-type (or Awareness, per MF2.3.1, if pure exposure rather than interaction is the intent)
            └─ No  → Is the goal lightweight social proof/interaction volume?
                       ├─ Yes → USE ENGAGEMENT, Post Engagement sub-type
                       └─ No  → reconsider Objective entirely (Leads/Sales likely more appropriate)
```

**MF2.3.3.1 Engagement Optimization Sub-Type Inheritance Table**

| Sub-Type | MF1 Reference | Inherited KPIs | Inherited Diagnostics |
|---|---|---|---|
| Messaging | MF1.5.3 | Messaging Conversation volume (MF1.3.33) | Conversation-quality feedback gap |
| Video Views | MF1.5.3 | Video watch metrics (MF1.4.9) | Early drop-off diagnostic |
| Post Engagement | MF1.5.3 | Post Engagement volume, Cost Per Engagement (MF1.4.10) | Engagement Rate Ranking (MF1.3.45) |
| Conversions | MF1.5.3 | Conversion volume (MF1.3.30) | Same diagnostics as Sales' Conversions sub-type |
| Calls | MF1.5.3 | Call initiation volume | — |

### MF2.3.4 Leads (base definition: MF1.5.4)

- **Business Purpose:** Build a pipeline of prospective customers for follow-up by sales, support, or marketing teams, typically outside Meta's platform in a CRM.
- **Optimization Purpose:** Maximize the estimated likelihood of a Lead Event via the configured conversion location, optionally informed by fed-back offline lead-quality signals.
- **Typical Funnel Position:** Mid funnel.
- **Required Infrastructure (Campaign-level):** Pixel/Conversions API (Website Leads), an Instant Form configuration (Instant Forms), Messaging setup, Call tracking, or a CRM-to-Meta offline event feed (CRM-fed Conversions sub-type).
- **Advantages:** Instant Forms sub-type offers the lowest-friction, highest-completion-rate lead capture available on the platform; CRM-fed Conversions sub-type allows optimization toward quality rather than raw volume once integrated.
- **Limitations:** Meta-reported Lead volume reflects submissions, not qualified prospects, unless offline feedback is integrated — the single most common failure mode for this Objective (MF1.3.32's Professional Notes, MF1.5.4's Common Mistakes).
- **Ideal Business Types:** B2B, real estate, financial services, high-consideration-purchase businesses where a direct online Purchase is not the natural first conversion step.
- **Decision Criteria:** Choose Leads when the business's actual sales process requires human follow-up before a transaction can close, making a direct Sales-objective Purchase Event premature or nonexistent.
- **Scaling Readiness:** Moderate; CPL (MF1.4.4) is highly variable by industry, and Learning Phase exit depends on Lead volume relative to Budget, which is often lower-volume than click- or engagement-based Optimization Events.
- **Common Failure Reasons:** Never integrating CRM-fed lead-quality feedback, permanently capping optimization at "cheap submissions" rather than "valuable prospects" (MF1.5.4's Decision Rules); over-lengthening Instant Form qualifying questions past the point where completion-rate loss outweighs quality gain.
- **Common Misconfigurations:** Using Website Leads sub-type without adequate Pixel/Conversions API Matching Quality (MF1.6.17), degrading Attribution reliability for the Campaign's entire Optimization Goal.
- **Risk Analysis:** Moderate; the central risk is quality-blind optimization drift, which is invisible in Meta's own reporting until CRM data is cross-referenced, making this Objective's risk more likely to go undetected than most others.
- **Optimization Priorities:** (1) CPL and Lead volume against Budget, (2) sub-type/friction fit (Instant Form vs. Website vs. Messaging) to the business's actual qualification needs, (3) CRM-fed quality feedback integration as the highest-leverage long-term investment for this Objective.
- **Decision Tree:**
```
Does the business's sales process require human qualification/follow-up before a transaction can occur?
 ├─ Yes → USE LEADS
 │         ├─ Native, low-friction capture acceptable? → Instant Forms sub-type
 │         ├─ Own-site/CRM-integrated preferred?        → Website sub-type
 │         └─ Conversational qualification preferred?    → Messaging sub-type
 └─ No  → a direct-transaction Objective (Sales, MF2.3.6) is likely more appropriate
```

**MF2.3.4.1 Leads Optimization Sub-Type Inheritance Table**

| Sub-Type | MF1 Reference | Inherited KPIs | Inherited Diagnostics |
|---|---|---|---|
| Website | MF1.5.4 | CPL (MF1.4.4) | Matching Quality (MF1.6.17) |
| Instant Forms | MF1.5.4 | Form completion rate (MF1.3.34) | Qualifying-question friction |
| Messaging | MF1.5.4 | Messaging Conversation volume (MF1.3.33) | Conversation-quality feedback gap |
| Calls | MF1.5.4 | Call initiation volume | — |
| Conversions (CRM-fed) | MF1.5.4 | Qualified-lead rate (external CRM) | Volume-vs-quality drift (MF1.3.32) |

### MF2.3.5 App Promotion (base definition: MF1.5.5)

- **Business Purpose:** Grow an app's user base and/or drive valuable in-app behavior among an existing or newly acquired app user base.
- **Optimization Purpose:** Maximize the estimated likelihood of an App Install Event or a specified post-install App Event.
- **Typical Funnel Position:** Mid-to-lower funnel (App Installs is mid-funnel acquisition; App Events is lower-funnel value optimization).
- **Required Infrastructure (Campaign-level):** App SDK integration (Meta SDK or a supported Mobile Measurement Partner).
- **Advantages:** Directly measurable acquisition (CPI, MF1.4.11) and, via the App Events sub-type, direct measurement of downstream in-app value, not just acquisition volume.
- **Limitations:** Platform-level privacy constraints (particularly iOS App Tracking Transparency) can materially reduce measurement completeness, a limitation genuinely intrinsic to this Objective rather than a configuration error.
- **Ideal Business Types:** Mobile games, subscription apps, e-commerce apps with a mature in-app Purchase or Registration Event.
- **Decision Criteria:** Choose App Installs when acquisition volume itself is the current priority (e.g., early app growth stage); transition to App Events once sufficient in-app Event volume exists to target higher-value users specifically (MF1.5.5's Decision Rules).
- **Scaling Readiness:** Moderate; heavily platform-dependent (iOS typically scales less predictably than Android given measurement constraints) and Event-volume-dependent for the App Events sub-type.
- **Common Failure Reasons:** Remaining on App Installs indefinitely after sufficient in-app Event volume exists to support App Events optimization, missing the opportunity to target higher-value users (MF1.5.5's Common Mistakes); misreading platform-level measurement gaps as a genuine performance drop.
- **Common Misconfigurations:** Optimizing for an App Event with too low a volume to exit Learning Phase, effectively stranding the Campaign in Learning Limited (MF1.10.2) indefinitely.
- **Risk Analysis:** Moderate-to-high on iOS specifically, given measurement-completeness constraints that can make genuine performance difficult to distinguish from measurement gaps without careful diagnostic discipline.
- **Optimization Priorities:** (1) CPI against known acceptable acquisition cost, (2) post-install engagement/retention quality (not install volume alone), (3) timely transition from App Installs to App Events once volume allows.
- **Decision Tree:**
```
Does sufficient in-app Event volume exist to support Learning Phase exit under an App Events Optimization Goal?
 ├─ No  → USE APP PROMOTION, App Installs sub-type (build acquisition + Event volume first)
 └─ Yes → USE APP PROMOTION, App Events sub-type (optimize for value, not just volume)
```

**MF2.3.5.1 App Promotion Optimization Sub-Type Inheritance Table**

| Sub-Type | MF1 Reference | Inherited KPIs | Inherited Diagnostics |
|---|---|---|---|
| App Installs | MF1.5.5 | CPI (MF1.4.11) | Platform (iOS/Android) measurement-completeness gap |
| App Events | MF1.5.5 | Cost per specified in-app Event, retention/value metrics | Same platform measurement-completeness gap |

### MF2.3.6 Sales (base definition: MF1.5.6)

- **Business Purpose:** Directly drive revenue-generating transactions — the most bottom-of-funnel, business-outcome-proximate Objective.
- **Optimization Purpose:** Maximize the estimated likelihood (or, under value-optimization, estimated value) of a Purchase or equivalent transaction Conversion Event.
- **Typical Funnel Position:** Bottom of funnel.
- **Required Infrastructure (Campaign-level):** Pixel and/or Conversions API with accurate Purchase value passing; a connected Catalog and Dataset for the Catalog Sales sub-type; App SDK with in-app Purchase Event tracking for the App Conversions sub-type.
- **Advantages:** The most directly revenue-linked Objective, with ROAS (MF1.4.6) as a self-contained business-outcome Metric requiring no external cross-reference to judge success, unlike Leads' CPL.
- **Limitations:** Requires the most mature measurement infrastructure of any Objective (accurate Purchase value passing is a hard prerequisite for its primary KPI to mean anything); a low-margin business needs meaningfully higher ROAS than a high-margin one to be genuinely profitable, so no universal "good ROAS" number applies (MF1.4.6).
- **Ideal Business Types:** E-commerce, subscription commerce, any business with a fully online, trackable transaction flow.
- **Decision Criteria:** Choose Sales when a transaction can be completed and tracked (website, app, Catalog, or Messaging/Calls) without requiring human sales qualification first — the mirror-image decision criterion to Leads (MF2.3.4).
- **Scaling Readiness:** Variable — Website Conversions with mature Pixel data scale well under Vertical Scaling (MF1.10.8); Catalog Sales scales especially well via Horizontal Scaling (MF1.10.7) across Product Sets (MF1.3.36).
- **Common Failure Reasons:** Inaccurate or inconsistent Purchase value passing, silently corrupting ROAS while Purchase count still looks normal (MF1.3.31's Common Mistakes); an unrealistically aggressive ROAS-floor Bid Strategy restricting Delivery below the volume needed to exit Learning Phase.
- **Common Misconfigurations:** Allowing Catalog data staleness (MF1.3.35) to degrade Catalog Sales relevance; running Website Conversions optimization before Pixel/Conversions API Matching Quality (MF1.6.17) is adequate to support reliable Attribution.
- **Risk Analysis:** High — this Objective carries the most direct financial stakes of the six, since its entire value proposition (ROAS) is only as trustworthy as its underlying value-tracking implementation.
- **Optimization Priorities:** (1) ROAS and CPA against known breakeven/acceptable thresholds, (2) CVR (MF1.4.12) and Conversion Rate Ranking (MF1.3.46) as root-cause diagnostics distinguishing advertising problems from landing/checkout problems, (3) Catalog/Product Set data quality where the Catalog Sales sub-type is used.
- **Decision Tree:**
```
Can the transaction itself be completed and reliably tracked without human sales qualification?
 ├─ No  → a Leads-objective Campaign (MF2.3.4) is likely more appropriate
 └─ Yes → USE SALES
            ├─ Single-product/service, own website checkout → Website Conversions sub-type
            ├─ Many products, retargeting or broad catalog-driven → Catalog Sales sub-type
            ├─ Conversational commerce preferred                → Messaging sub-type
            └─ Phone-closed high-ticket transaction              → Calls sub-type
```

**MF2.3.6.1 Sales Optimization Sub-Type Inheritance Table**

| Sub-Type | MF1 Reference | Inherited KPIs | Inherited Diagnostics |
|---|---|---|---|
| Website Conversions | MF1.5.6 | ROAS, CPA (MF1.4.5–6) | CVR/Conversion Rate Ranking (MF1.4.12, MF1.3.46) |
| Catalog Sales | MF1.5.6 | ROAS, per-Product-Set performance | Catalog/Product Set data staleness (MF1.3.35) |
| Messaging | MF1.5.6 | Conversation-to-Purchase rate | Conversation-quality feedback gap |
| Calls | MF1.5.6 | Call-to-Purchase rate | — |

---

## MF2.4 Campaign Objective Selection Framework

### MF2.4.1 How Businesses Choose Objectives

Objective selection should be driven by three questions, answered in order: (1) What is the true business outcome being pursued (recognition, traffic, interaction, a qualified prospect, an app user, or a transaction)? (2) What measurement infrastructure (MF1.14) already exists to track that outcome reliably? (3) What Conversion/Event volume can realistically be generated within Budget to support Learning Phase exit (MF1.3.19) for that outcome? An Objective chosen from business intent alone, without checking (2) and (3), is the single most common root cause of the Common Failure Reasons documented per-Objective in MF2.3.

### MF2.4.2 Objective Decision Flow

```
START
  │
  ▼
Is there a directly trackable transaction (Purchase, Subscribe, in-app Purchase)?
  ├─ Yes → Is human sales qualification required first? → Yes → LEADS (MF2.3.4)
  │                                                       → No  → SALES (MF2.3.6)
  │                                                                (or APP PROMOTION if the transaction is in-app-value-driven acquisition, MF2.3.5)
  └─ No  → Is the goal app installation/usage specifically?
             ├─ Yes → APP PROMOTION (MF2.3.5)
             └─ No  → Is the goal conversational/social interaction?
                        ├─ Yes → ENGAGEMENT (MF2.3.3)
                        └─ No  → Is the goal destination-visit volume?
                                   ├─ Yes → TRAFFIC (MF2.3.2)
                                   └─ No  → AWARENESS (MF2.3.1)
```

### MF2.4.3 Business Scenarios

- **Scenario — New e-commerce brand, no Pixel history:** Begin with Traffic (building Pixel data volume) or a short Awareness phase if brand recognition is genuinely near zero, then transition to Sales once sufficient Pixel Event volume exists to support Learning Phase exit under a Purchase Optimization Goal.
- **Scenario — Established B2B service business:** Leads is almost always correct from day one, since the transaction itself cannot be completed online; the primary risk is CPL-only optimization without CRM-fed quality feedback (MF2.3.4).
- **Scenario — Mobile game studio, pre-launch:** App Promotion, App Installs sub-type, transitioning to App Events (e.g., Level Achieved, in-app Purchase) once install volume is sufficient to support that Optimization Goal's Learning Phase exit.
- **Scenario — Subscription-box e-commerce with high AOV and long consideration cycle:** A combined structure is often correct — an Awareness or Engagement Campaign feeding a Custom Audience, retargeted by a Sales Campaign — rather than a single Objective in isolation, per MF2.1.5's translation-layer treatment of business goals to Objectives.

### MF2.4.4 Wrong-Objective Consequences

Selecting the wrong Objective does not merely underperform — it structurally prevents the correct Metrics from ever being optimized for, since the Auction (MF1.3.18) only ever optimizes toward the configured Optimization Goal. Concretely: a Traffic-objective Campaign run for a business that actually needs Purchase volume will never optimize toward purchase-likely users, no matter how long it runs or how much Budget it receives, because Traffic's Auction signal is click-likelihood, not purchase-likelihood — the two are correlated but materially different predictive targets. This is the single most consequential and most avoidable failure mode this entire Framework addresses.

### MF2.4.5 Objective Migration and the Objective Maturity Model

Businesses commonly progress through a maturity sequence as measurement infrastructure and data volume grow:

```
Stage 1 — No tracking infrastructure:          AWARENESS or TRAFFIC (Link Clicks)
Stage 2 — Pixel/Conversions API installed,
           low Event volume:                   TRAFFIC (Landing Page Views) or ENGAGEMENT (Conversions, low volume)
Stage 3 — Adequate Event volume, no value
           tracking or CRM feedback yet:       LEADS (Website/Instant Forms) or SALES (Website Conversions, count-based Bid Strategy)
Stage 4 — Mature value tracking + CRM
           feedback integrated:                SALES (value-optimized Bid Strategy) or LEADS (CRM-fed Conversions sub-type)
```

Migration between stages is a deliberate Campaign-level decision (typically a new Campaign under the new Objective, since Objective is immutable per Campaign, MF2.1.3) and should be triggered by measurement-maturity milestones, not by a fixed calendar schedule — moving to Stage 4 before value-tracking data is actually reliable simply produces an untrustworthy ROAS/CPA figure rather than genuine progress.

### MF2.4.6 Objective Decision Matrix

| Business Signal | Awareness | Traffic | Engagement | Leads | App Promotion | Sales |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| No tracking infrastructure yet | Best fit | Good fit | Fair fit | Poor fit | Poor fit | Poor fit |
| Transaction requires human qualification | Poor fit | Fair fit | Fair fit | Best fit | N/A | Poor fit |
| Transaction is fully self-service online | Poor fit | Fair fit | Fair fit | Poor fit | N/A | Best fit |
| Goal is app-specific acquisition/value | Poor fit | Poor fit | Poor fit | Poor fit | Best fit | N/A |
| Goal is pure brand recall, no action | Best fit | Poor fit | Poor fit | Poor fit | Poor fit | Poor fit |
| Conversational commerce/support is core model | Fair fit | Fair fit | Best fit | Fair fit | N/A | Fair fit |

---

## MF2.5 Campaign Budget Framework

Base definitions (Budget, Campaign Budget/CBO, Ad Set Budget/ABO) are fixed at MF1.3.21–MF1.3.23 and are cited, not restated, throughout this section.

### MF2.5.1 Campaign Budget vs. CBO vs. ABO — Campaign-Layer Framing

At the Campaign layer, the single governing decision is which of the two Budget modes (MF1.3.22 Campaign Budget/CBO, MF1.3.23 Ad Set Budget/ABO) the Campaign operates under — this decision is made once per Campaign and shapes every subsequent Budget-related decision documented in this section.

### MF2.5.2 Budget Lifecycle

```
Set (at Campaign or Ad Set creation) → Monitored (against pacing/Delivery) → Adjusted (Vertical Scaling, MF1.10.8, or reduction) → Redistributed (CBO only, continuous, system-driven) → Exhausted/Renewed (daily reset, or lifetime Budget concluding) → Retired (Campaign paused/archived)
```

### MF2.5.3 Budget Allocation

Under Campaign Budget, allocation across Ad Sets is dynamic and system-driven, shifting toward Ad Sets predicted to deliver the best results for the shared Optimization Goal (MF1.3.22). Under Ad Set Budget, allocation is static and advertiser-fixed per Ad Set (MF1.3.23). The Campaign-layer decision is which allocation philosophy — system-optimized pooling or advertiser-guaranteed fixed spend — best matches the Campaign's actual strategic need (e.g., guaranteed A/B test exposure favors Ad Set Budget; pure performance maximization favors Campaign Budget).

### MF2.5.4 Budget Governance

Budget changes at the Campaign level are a governance-sensitive action: a large Budget change is both a financial commitment change and a Delivery-stability risk (via Learning Phase reset, MF1.3.19). This Framework requires that any Budget change exceeding a Business's own defined "significant change" threshold (commonly aligned with Meta's own significant-edit definition, MF1.3.19) pass through the approval workflow defined in MF2.13.4, rather than being applied unilaterally.

### MF2.5.5 Budget Limitations

- Campaign Budget's dynamic allocation is not fully predictable or explainable at the individual-Ad-Set level in real time, which can create discomfort for stakeholders expecting even spend distribution (MF1.3.22's Common Mistakes).
- Ad Set Budget forfeits the cross-Ad-Set efficiency gains Campaign Budget provides, a deliberate trade-off that should be consciously chosen, not defaulted into out of unfamiliarity (MF1.3.23's Common Mistakes).
- Minimum Budget thresholds exist below which an Ad Set cannot realistically exit Learning Phase for a given Optimization Event's typical cost — Budget below this threshold is a common root cause of Learning Limited (MF1.10.2).

### MF2.5.6 Budget Optimization

Budget optimization at the Campaign layer means ensuring Budget level is proportionate to the chosen Optimization Event's typical cost (avoiding the Learning Limited risk above) while remaining within the Business's actual approved spend authority (MF2.13.3). It is distinct from, and a prerequisite to, Ad-Set-level Bid Strategy optimization (MF2.6) — an under-funded Ad Set cannot be rescued by Bid Strategy tuning alone.

### MF2.5.7 Budget Scaling

Budget-driven scaling follows the Vertical Scaling discipline established at MF1.10.8: moderate, incremental increases (commonly cited around 15–20% at a time) rather than large step-changes, specifically to avoid triggering an avoidable Learning Phase reset during a Campaign's strongest performance window. The full Scaling treatment (including Horizontal Scaling as a Budget-growth alternative) is in MF2.9.

### MF2.5.8 Budget Redistribution

Under Campaign Budget, redistribution across Ad Sets is continuous and automatic (MF1.3.22). Under Ad Set Budget, redistribution requires a manual decision by the Campaign owner (MF2.2.3) to move Budget from an underperforming Ad Set to a stronger one — this manual redistribution is itself a Campaign-governance-relevant action subject to MF2.13's approval and audit rules where it exceeds the Business's defined significant-change threshold.

### MF2.5.9 Budget Diagnostics

| Symptom | Likely Cause | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Ad Set stuck in Learning Limited | Budget too low relative to Optimization Event's typical cost | MF1.10.2, MF2.5.5 |
| Uneven, unpredictable per-Ad-Set spend under CBO | Expected system behavior, not a defect — see MF1.3.22's Common Mistakes | MF1.3.22 |
| Delivery destabilizes shortly after a Budget increase | Increase exceeded a safe incremental scaling step | MF1.10.8, MF2.9 |
| One Ad Set consistently starved of spend under ABO | Manual Budget allocation not revisited despite performance divergence | MF2.5.8 |

### MF2.5.10 Budget Risks

- **Overspend risk:** Lifetime Budget misconfiguration (e.g., an unintentionally large lifetime figure entered where a daily figure was intended) — a governance/audit-rule concern addressed in MF2.13.
- **Underspend/inefficiency risk:** Budget too conservative to ever exit Learning Phase, wasting the spend that is delivered on an unstabilized Ad Set.
- **Instability risk:** Aggressive Vertical Scaling destabilizing a previously well-performing Campaign, per MF1.10.8's Common Mistakes.
- **Allocation-opacity risk:** Under CBO, an unexplainable-seeming spend shift being misdiagnosed as a system error rather than legitimate performance-driven reallocation, leading to an unnecessary and disruptive manual override.

### MF2.5.11 Budget Decision Matrix

| Strategic Need | Recommended Budget Mode | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Maximize overall Campaign efficiency, trust system allocation | Campaign Budget (CBO) | Dynamic reallocation toward best performers, per MF1.3.22 |
| Guarantee fixed spend per Ad Set regardless of relative performance | Ad Set Budget (ABO) | Fixed allocation independent of siblings, per MF1.3.23 |
| Running a deliberate A/B test between two Ad Sets | Ad Set Budget (ABO) | Prevents CBO's dynamic allocation from confounding the comparison |
| Small number of highly differentiated Ad Sets (e.g., distinct partner Audiences with contractual minimums) | Ad Set Budget (ABO) | Protects contractual/strategic delivery guarantees |
| Large number of similar-quality Ad Sets, performance still being learned | Campaign Budget (CBO) | Lets the system discover the best performers without manual guesswork |

---

## MF2.6 Campaign Bid Strategy Framework

Extends MF1.3.20's generic Bid Strategy definition with the specific named Bid Strategy types available in Meta's platform, none of which MF1 individually catalogued — this section adds that catalog as new Campaign-layer content, per this Framework's Vocabulary Rule.

### MF2.6.1 Highest Volume

- **Purpose:** Fully automatic bidding that maximizes the number of Optimization Events delivered within Budget, with no cost ceiling constraint.
- **Strengths:** Simplest to configure; typically the fastest and most reliable path through Learning Phase (MF1.3.19), since the Auction (MF1.3.18) has maximum flexibility to bid competitively.
- **Weaknesses:** No cost predictability — average cost per Event can vary significantly period to period as Auction competition changes.
- **When to Use:** New Campaigns still establishing a cost baseline; Campaigns where volume matters more than cost predictability (e.g., early Awareness/Traffic Campaigns).
- **When NOT to Use:** Campaigns operating against a hard, known-critical cost ceiling (e.g., a Sales Campaign with a firm breakeven ROAS requirement).
- **Risk Level:** Low delivery risk, moderate cost-predictability risk.
- **Scaling Impact:** Generally the most scaling-friendly strategy, since it imposes no artificial ceiling that could constrain Delivery growth (MF1.10.6).
- **Learning Impact:** Fastest, most reliable Learning Phase exit of the five strategies, due to maximum bidding flexibility.

### MF2.6.2 Cost Cap

- **Purpose:** Automatic bidding constrained to keep the *average* cost per Optimization Event at or below an advertiser-set cap, while still maximizing volume within that constraint.
- **Strengths:** Balances cost control with delivery flexibility better than Bid Cap, since Meta's system can still bid above the cap for some auctions as long as the average stays under it.
- **Weaknesses:** Setting the cap too aggressively relative to realistic Auction cost restricts Delivery volume, risking Learning Limited (MF1.10.2).
- **When to Use:** Campaigns with an established cost baseline (from prior Highest Volume data) that need cost discipline without sacrificing all delivery flexibility.
- **When NOT to Use:** Brand-new Campaigns with no cost history — the cap is likely to be miscalibrated in either direction.
- **Risk Level:** Moderate — well-calibrated Cost Cap is safe; poorly calibrated Cost Cap is a common Learning Limited root cause.
- **Scaling Impact:** Scales acceptably if the cap is periodically recalibrated against current Auction conditions as Budget grows; a static cap can become an unintended ceiling as competition increases.
- **Learning Impact:** Moderate — Learning Phase exit depends on the cap being loose enough to sustain adequate Event volume.

### MF2.6.3 Bid Cap

- **Purpose:** Manual bidding constraining the *maximum single bid* the Campaign's Ad Sets submit into any individual Auction, giving the advertiser the most granular cost control of the five strategies.
- **Strengths:** The tightest possible cost control, useful when even short-term cost spikes are unacceptable.
- **Weaknesses:** Requires the most Auction expertise to calibrate correctly; a Bid Cap set even slightly below competitive levels can severely restrict or halt Delivery entirely.
- **When to Use:** Highly experienced media buyers with detailed Auction cost data, in situations demanding strict per-impression cost discipline.
- **When NOT to Use:** Any Campaign without detailed, recent Auction cost data to calibrate against — this is the highest-expertise-requirement strategy and the least forgiving of miscalibration.
- **Risk Level:** High — the most common cause of severe, hard-to-diagnose Delivery restriction among the five strategies.
- **Scaling Impact:** Poor without active, frequent recalibration — a static Bid Cap is the most likely of the five strategies to become an unintended scaling ceiling as Auction competition shifts.
- **Learning Impact:** High risk of Learning Limited (MF1.10.2) if miscalibrated, since an overly conservative Bid Cap directly throttles the Event volume needed to exit Learning Phase.

### MF2.6.4 Minimum ROAS (ROAS Floor)

- **Purpose:** Automatic, value-based bidding that pursues the highest Purchase volume achievable while keeping *average* ROAS (MF1.4.6) at or above an advertiser-set floor.
- **Strengths:** Directly ties the Bid Strategy to the Sales Objective's primary business KPI (ROAS) rather than a proxy cost metric.
- **Weaknesses:** Requires accurate, reliable Purchase value passing (MF1.3.31's Common Mistakes) to function meaningfully — a corrupted value signal makes this strategy actively harmful, not just ineffective.
- **When to Use:** Mature Sales Campaigns with reliable Purchase value tracking and a known breakeven ROAS threshold.
- **When NOT to Use:** Any Campaign without verified, accurate Purchase value passing, or any Objective other than Sales/value-tracked Conversions where ROAS is not the relevant KPI.
- **Risk Level:** Moderate-to-high — safe with verified value tracking, high-risk (silently) without it, since the strategy will appear to function normally while optimizing against corrupted data.
- **Scaling Impact:** Good once value tracking is verified — this strategy is specifically designed to support Vertical Scaling (MF1.10.8) while protecting profitability.
- **Learning Impact:** Moderate — Learning Phase exit depends on sufficient value-tracked Purchase volume, generally requiring more data than a count-based Optimization Event.

### MF2.6.5 Highest Value

- **Purpose:** Automatic, value-based bidding that maximizes total Purchase value delivered within Budget, without an explicit ROAS floor constraint.
- **Strengths:** Directly optimizes for revenue maximization rather than cost-efficiency, appropriate when growth (not margin protection) is the current strategic priority.
- **Weaknesses:** No cost/ROAS ceiling — average ROAS may decline as the strategy pursues volume, without an automatic guardrail against unprofitable spend.
- **When to Use:** Growth-stage Sales Campaigns explicitly prioritizing revenue scale over margin, or Campaigns where a known-healthy ROAS margin provides enough buffer that an explicit floor is unnecessary.
- **When NOT to Use:** Campaigns with tight margins where any meaningful ROAS decline would be immediately unprofitable — Minimum ROAS is the safer choice in that situation.
- **Risk Level:** Moderate — the risk is entirely in the absence of a profitability guardrail, not in Delivery instability.
- **Scaling Impact:** Strong — this strategy is well-suited to aggressive Vertical Scaling when the business goal is genuinely growth-over-margin.
- **Learning Impact:** Similar to Minimum ROAS — dependent on adequate value-tracked Purchase volume for Learning Phase exit.

### MF2.6.6 Bid Strategy Selection Summary

| Bid Strategy | Cost Control | Delivery Flexibility | Expertise Required | Best Objective Fit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Highest Volume | None | Highest | Low | Any Objective, especially new Campaigns |
| Cost Cap | Average-cost | High | Moderate | Any Objective with an established cost baseline |
| Bid Cap | Per-impression, strict | Lowest | High | Any Objective, only with detailed recent Auction data |
| Minimum ROAS | Average ROAS floor | Moderate-high | Moderate | Sales, with verified value tracking |
| Highest Value | None (value-maximizing) | Highest | Moderate | Sales, growth-prioritized |

---

## MF2.7 Campaign Lifecycle

Extends MF2.2.2's overview and MF2.2.6's State Transitions with a full stage-by-stage treatment. Each stage is documented with: Purpose, Key Activities, Entry Criteria, Exit Criteria, Risks.

### MF2.7.1 Planning

- **Purpose:** Translate a business goal into a specific Objective (MF2.4), Budget commitment (MF2.5), and measurement-readiness assessment before any Campaign is created.
- **Key Activities:** Objective selection (MF2.4.1–2), infrastructure/tracking readiness check (MF1.14), Budget approval (MF2.13.4), initial KPI target setting (MF2.11).
- **Entry Criteria:** A defined business goal exists.
- **Exit Criteria:** Objective, Budget, and required tracking infrastructure are all confirmed ready.
- **Risks:** Skipping infrastructure readiness verification, the single most common root cause of downstream Learning Limited (MF1.10.2) and unreliable Attribution (MF1.3.41) issues.

### MF2.7.2 Creation

- **Purpose:** Configure the Campaign, its Budget mode, and its initial Ad Set(s) in the platform.
- **Key Activities:** Campaign naming (MF2.13.1/MF1.13.1), Objective and Budget mode selection (immutable once set, MF2.1.3/MF2.5.1), initial Ad Set and Creative setup (outside this Framework's Campaign-only scope beyond configuration confirmation).
- **Entry Criteria:** Planning stage complete and approved.
- **Exit Criteria:** Campaign and at least one Ad Set are configured and submitted for Delivery.
- **Risks:** Naming Standards non-compliance (MF1.13.1) causing downstream reporting/audit friction; Objective misconfiguration, which is uncorrectable without recreating the Campaign (MF2.1.3).

### MF2.7.3 Learning

- **Purpose:** Allow Meta's delivery system to accumulate sufficient data to stabilize Delivery predictions (MF1.3.19).
- **Key Activities:** Monitoring for Learning Limited risk (MF1.10.2); deliberately avoiding "significant edits" that would reset progress.
- **Entry Criteria:** Campaign is Active and receiving Delivery.
- **Exit Criteria:** Sufficient Optimization Event volume accumulated to exit Learning Phase (full diagnostic treatment in MF2.8).
- **Risks:** Premature edits resetting Learning Phase repeatedly (MF1.3.19's Common Mistakes); judging performance from Learning-Phase-only data (MF1.3.19's Professional Notes).

### MF2.7.4 Stabilization

- **Purpose:** Confirm the Campaign's post-Learning-Phase performance is genuinely representative before any scaling decision is made.
- **Key Activities:** Establishing a trusted performance baseline against MF2.11's KPI library; confirming Delivery status is "Active," not "Limited."
- **Entry Criteria:** Learning Phase exited.
- **Exit Criteria:** A stable, multi-period performance baseline exists.
- **Risks:** Scaling too early, before a genuine baseline exists, making it impossible to distinguish scaling-driven change from ordinary post-Learning-Phase variance.

### MF2.7.5 Scaling

- **Purpose:** Deliberately grow a validated, stable Campaign's Delivery volume and business impact.
- **Key Activities:** Full treatment in MF2.9 — Vertical Scaling, Horizontal Scaling, and their respective safe-scaling rules.
- **Entry Criteria:** Stabilization stage confirms a trustworthy, healthy performance baseline.
- **Exit Criteria:** A new, larger stable performance baseline is established at the scaled Budget/structure level.
- **Risks:** Fully addressed in MF2.9's Scaling Risks.

### MF2.7.6 Maintenance

- **Purpose:** Sustain a stable, mature Campaign's performance over an extended operating period.
- **Key Activities:** Routine health monitoring against MF2.10's Diagnostics Framework; scheduled Creative Refresh (MF2.7.7) and Audience Expansion (MF2.7.8) cadences.
- **Entry Criteria:** Campaign has reached a stable, scaled (or intentionally not-scaled) operating state.
- **Exit Criteria:** Not a terminal exit — Maintenance persists until Decline (MF2.7.10) begins.
- **Risks:** Passive maintenance without proactive Creative/Audience refresh cadence, allowing Creative Fatigue (MF1.9.12) and Audience Saturation (MF1.8.8) to develop undetected until Decline is already underway.

### MF2.7.7 Creative Refresh (Lifecycle Stage View)

- **Purpose:** Proactively counteract Creative Fatigue (MF1.9.12, MF1.10.9) before it materially degrades performance.
- **Key Activities:** Scheduled Creative rotation cadence; Creative Testing (MF1.9.13) of new variants ahead of full retirement of the existing Creative.
- **Entry Criteria:** Typically triggered on a fixed cadence during Maintenance, or reactively when Frequency/CTR diagnostics (MF2.10) indicate onset.
- **Exit Criteria:** New Creative validated and fully rotated in.
- **Risks:** Refreshing only superficially (MF1.9.12's Common Mistakes parallel — MF1.10.9's own Common Mistakes), failing to genuinely reset viewer attention.

### MF2.7.8 Audience Expansion (Lifecycle Stage View)

- **Purpose:** Proactively counteract Audience Saturation (MF1.8.8, MF1.8.9) before it materially degrades performance.
- **Key Activities:** Widening Lookalike percentage, enabling/expanding Advantage+ Audience, removing overly restrictive Exclusions (MF1.8.6).
- **Entry Criteria:** Typically triggered reactively when Frequency diagnostics (MF2.10) indicate onset, or proactively ahead of a Scaling stage (MF2.7.5).
- **Exit Criteria:** Expanded Audience validated and stable.
- **Risks:** Expanding aggressively enough in one step to constitute a significant edit, resetting Learning Phase precisely when stability was needed (MF1.8.9's Common Mistakes).

### MF2.7.9 Optimization

- **Purpose:** Continuous, incremental improvement of an already-stable Campaign short of a full Scaling or Refresh/Expansion event.
- **Key Activities:** Bid Strategy tuning within the safe bounds established in MF2.6; Budget Strategy micro-adjustments (MF2.5.6); acting on Decision Intelligence outputs (MF2.12).
- **Entry Criteria:** Campaign is stable and past initial Scaling.
- **Exit Criteria:** Not a terminal exit — Optimization is continuous until Decline begins.
- **Risks:** Over-optimizing (excessive small edits) rather than allowing sufficient data accumulation between changes to judge each change's actual effect.

### MF2.7.10 Decline

- **Purpose:** Recognize and respond to a genuine, sustained performance deterioration rather than normal variance.
- **Key Activities:** Root-cause diagnosis via MF2.10's Diagnostics Framework, distinguishing Creative Fatigue, Audience Saturation, rising Auction competition, or genuine market/seasonal shift.
- **Entry Criteria:** Sustained (multi-period, not single-period per MF1.11.8's Trend discipline) decline in primary KPIs (MF2.11).
- **Exit Criteria:** Either successful remediation (returning to Maintenance/Optimization) or a decision to proceed to Retirement.
- **Risks:** Reacting to normal single-period variance as Decline (violating MF1.11.8's Trend discipline); conversely, failing to recognize genuine Decline promptly, extending wasted spend.

### MF2.7.11 Retirement

- **Purpose:** Formally end a Campaign's active Delivery once remediation is not viable or the business goal it served no longer applies.
- **Key Activities:** Pausing or ending the Campaign; capturing final performance data for the historical record; documenting the retirement rationale for governance/audit purposes (MF2.13).
- **Entry Criteria:** A Decline-stage diagnosis concludes remediation is not viable, or the underlying business goal has concluded.
- **Exit Criteria:** Campaign transitions to Paused or Completed state (MF2.2.6).
- **Risks:** Retiring a Campaign without documenting the rationale, losing institutional knowledge that would otherwise inform future Objective/Budget/Creative decisions for similar Campaigns.

### MF2.7.12 Archiving

- **Purpose:** Retain a retired Campaign's full configuration and performance history for long-term reporting, audit, and institutional-knowledge purposes.
- **Key Activities:** Transition to Archived state (MF2.2.6); ensure historical Reporting Dimension (MF1.3.38) access is preserved.
- **Entry Criteria:** Campaign has reached Retirement and no further activity is expected.
- **Exit Criteria:** Not applicable — Archiving is the terminal lifecycle state.
- **Risks:** Losing accessible historical data needed for future Comparison (MF1.11.4) or Benchmarking (MF1.11.5) against new Campaigns of a similar type.

---

## MF2.8 Campaign Learning Diagnostics

Extends MF1.3.19 (Learning Phase) and MF1.10.2 (Learning Limited) with Campaign-layer diagnostic and recovery treatment.

### MF2.8.1 Learning Phase (Campaign-Layer Diagnostic View)

At the Campaign layer, Learning Phase should be monitored in aggregate across all Ad Sets, since a Campaign's overall reported performance during an early period reflects a blend of Ad Sets that may be in genuinely different Learning Phase states — a Campaign-level average can mask one Ad Set's healthy exit and another's Learning Limited status.

### MF2.8.2 Learning Limited (Campaign-Layer Diagnostic View)

**Causes (Campaign-layer level):** Budget mismatched to the Objective's typical Optimization Event cost (MF2.5.5); an overly restrictive Bid Strategy (MF2.6.2–3); an Audience too narrow for the Optimization Event's required volume (MF1.8's Exclusions/Core Audience over-narrowing); an Optimization Event genuinely too rare for the Campaign's current data volume (e.g., a high-value, low-frequency Conversion).

**Recovery Methods:**
1. Broaden the Audience (Audience Expansion, MF1.8.9/MF2.7.8) — the lowest-disruption first step.
2. Relax the Bid Strategy (e.g., move from Bid Cap to Cost Cap, or Cost Cap to Highest Volume, MF2.6) — the second step if Audience broadening alone is insufficient.
3. Increase Budget (MF2.5.7) — proportionate to the gap between current and required Event volume, not an arbitrary increase.
4. Switch to a higher-volume proxy Optimization Event (e.g., Add To Cart instead of Purchase, MF1.6.5) as a temporary measure while the above steps mature the Campaign's data — never a permanent substitute once volume allows.

**Best Practices:** Diagnose the specific cause (via MF2.10's Diagnostics Framework) before applying a Recovery Method — applying Budget increases to an Audience-narrowness problem, for example, wastes spend without resolving the underlying constraint.

### MF2.8.3 Learning Exit

- **Definition (Campaign-layer):** The point at which every (or, practically, the great majority of) an Ad Set within a Campaign has accumulated sufficient Optimization Event volume to be considered stable, per MF1.3.19.
- **Campaign-Layer Signal:** A Campaign's own reported Metrics become trustworthy for Stabilization-stage (MF2.7.4) baseline-setting only once Learning Exit has occurred across its Ad Sets — a Campaign-level KPI review conducted before this point risks the same misinterpretation flagged in MF1.3.19's Professional Notes, just aggregated across more Ad Sets.

### MF2.8.4 Learning Reset

- **Causes:** Any "significant edit" (per Meta's own definition, reused throughout MF1 — changes to Audience, Creative, Optimization Goal, or Bid Strategy) applied to an Ad Set within the Campaign; at the Campaign layer, a Campaign Budget change large enough to materially alter delivery dynamics can indirectly trigger this at the Ad Set level even without a direct Ad-Set-level edit.
- **Recovery Methods:** No Recovery Method exists to avoid a genuine, necessary Learning Reset — the correct practice is prevention: batching necessary changes together (rather than making them serially, each triggering its own reset) and scheduling changes during lower-stakes periods rather than mid-scaling or mid-critical-campaign-window.
- **Best Practices:** Maintain a change log (per MF2.13.2's Version Control) specifically to distinguish "expected reset from a deliberate, necessary edit" from "unexpected reset requiring root-cause investigation" when Learning Phase status is observed to have reset.

### MF2.8.5 Campaign-Layer Learning Diagnostics Summary Table

| Observed Signal | Diagnosis | Recovery Reference |
|---|---|---|
| One or more Ad Sets show "Learning" indefinitely, never progressing | Learning Limited | MF2.8.2 |
| Campaign-level Metrics fluctuate heavily period to period with no external cause | Learning Phase not yet exited across all Ad Sets | MF2.8.3 |
| Learning status unexpectedly restarts after a routine change | Learning Reset from an unlogged or unrecognized significant edit | MF2.8.4 |
| Campaign-level Metrics look stable but one Ad Set is Learning Limited | Aggregate masking — always diagnose at Ad-Set grain, not Campaign grain alone | MF2.8.1 |

---

## MF2.9 Campaign Scaling Framework

Extends MF1.10.6–MF1.10.8 (Scaling, Horizontal Scaling, Vertical Scaling) with full Campaign-layer operational treatment.

### MF2.9.1 Vertical Scaling (Campaign-Layer Treatment)

Increasing an existing, validated Campaign's Budget (MF2.5.7) to grow its Delivery volume. At the Campaign layer, Vertical Scaling is applied either to a Campaign Budget total (redistributed automatically across Ad Sets, MF1.3.22) or to an individual Ad Set's Budget (MF1.3.23) — the Campaign owner (MF2.2.3) must decide which grain the increase applies at, since the two produce materially different Delivery effects.

### MF2.9.2 Horizontal Scaling (Campaign-Layer Treatment)

Growing total Delivery volume by adding new Ad Sets (within the existing Campaign) or entirely new Campaigns (Campaign Duplication, MF2.9.3) targeting adjacent Audience segments, rather than increasing existing Budget. Campaign-layer Horizontal Scaling decisions include whether to add the new capacity within the existing Campaign (sharing its Objective and, if used, Campaign Budget) or as a wholly new Campaign (appropriate when testing a materially different strategic approach, per MF2.2.1's hierarchy).

### MF2.9.3 Duplication

- **Definition:** Creating a copy of a proven, validated Campaign or Ad Set structure, typically to test it against a new Audience segment or Placement configuration without risking the original's stable Learning Phase status.
- **Role in Scaling:** The primary mechanism by which Horizontal Scaling (MF2.9.2) is executed in practice.
- **Risk:** Duplicating into a Audience segment with high Audience Overlap (MF1.8.7) against the original, causing self-competition rather than incremental volume — the same risk flagged at MF1.10.7's Common Mistakes.

### MF2.9.4 Expansion

- **Definition (Campaign-layer):** Broadening an existing Campaign's reach — via Audience Expansion (MF1.8.9/MF2.7.8), Placement Expansion (enabling Advantage+ Placements where previously restricted), or geographic expansion — without necessarily increasing Budget.
- **Role in Scaling:** An alternative or complement to pure Budget-driven Vertical Scaling, useful when the current Audience/Placement configuration, not Budget, is the binding constraint on further growth.
- **Risk:** As with Audience Expansion generally, expanding too many dimensions simultaneously in one step risks a significant edit and Learning Reset (MF2.8.4) precisely when stability is most valuable.

### MF2.9.5 Budget Increases (Safe Practice)

Following MF1.10.8's guidance directly: incremental increases in the range of approximately 15–20% at a time, with a stabilization pause between increases (aligned to the Stabilization stage, MF2.7.4) before applying the next increase. A Campaign owner should never apply a second increase before confirming the previous one has stabilized.

### MF2.9.6 Safe Scaling Rules (Consolidated)

1. Only scale a Campaign that has completed Stabilization (MF2.7.4) — never scale directly out of Learning Phase.
2. Prefer incremental Vertical Scaling (MF2.9.1/MF2.9.5) over large step-changes; prefer Horizontal Scaling (MF2.9.2) when Audience/Placement constraints, not Budget, are the binding limit.
3. Never combine a Budget increase with a Creative change, Audience change, and Bid Strategy change in the same action — isolate variables so that any resulting performance shift can be attributed to a specific cause.
4. Allow a full stabilization period (typically a complete Learning Phase cycle) between successive scaling actions.
5. Monitor Frequency (MF1.4.7) and Ranking signals (MF1.3.44–46) throughout scaling — Audience Saturation and Creative Fatigue often surface for the first time only once volume increases meaningfully.
6. Maintain a change log (MF2.13.2) of every scaling action, its magnitude, and its outcome, to build an increasingly reliable, Business-specific scaling calibration over time.

### MF2.9.7 Scaling Risks

| Risk | Cause | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Learning Phase reset mid-scale | Budget increase or Audience change exceeded significant-edit threshold | MF2.9.5's incremental-increase discipline |
| Delivery instability / cost spike | Budget increase outpaced the Auction's ability to find equally efficient additional impressions | MF2.9.6 rule 1–2 |
| Audience Overlap from Duplication | New Ad Set/Campaign's Audience insufficiently differentiated from the original | MF2.9.3's risk treatment, MF1.8.7 |
| Masked root cause | Multiple variables changed simultaneously, making the true driver of a performance shift unidentifiable | MF2.9.6 rule 3 |
| Premature scaling | Scaling attempted before Stabilization (MF2.7.4) confirmed a genuine baseline | MF2.9.6 rule 1 |

### MF2.9.8 Recovery Plans (Post-Scaling-Failure)

1. **Immediate:** Revert the most recent scaling action (Budget, Audience, or Placement change) to its prior known-stable configuration.
2. **Diagnostic:** Run the Campaign through MF2.10's Diagnostics Framework to confirm the specific root cause (Learning Reset, Audience Saturation, Auction competition shift) before attempting to scale again.
3. **Stabilization:** Allow a full fresh Stabilization period (MF2.7.4) at the reverted configuration before any further scaling attempt.
4. **Re-attempt:** Resume scaling only via MF2.9.6's Safe Scaling Rules, applying a smaller increment than the one that triggered the failure.

---

## MF2.10 Campaign Diagnostics Framework

Enterprise diagnostic models for the most common Campaign-layer performance symptoms. Each model cites MF1.4's Metrics Dictionary and MF1.3's Ranking signals as its underlying evidence base, and produces a Root Cause and Corrective Action rather than merely restating the symptom.

### MF2.10.1 Performance Decline (General Model)

```
Sustained decline in primary KPI (MF2.11) over 3+ consecutive periods (MF1.11.8 Trend discipline — never diagnose from 1 period)
 │
 ├─ Is Frequency (MF1.4.7) rising disproportionately? ────────────→ Audience Saturation (MF1.8.8) → Remedy: Audience Expansion (MF2.7.8)
 ├─ Is CTR/Engagement Rate Ranking declining at stable Frequency? → Creative Fatigue (MF1.9.12) → Remedy: Creative Refresh (MF2.7.7)
 ├─ Is CPM rising with no Frequency/Audience change? ─────────────→ Auction Competition increase (MF2.10.9) → Remedy: see MF2.10.9
 ├─ Is Delivery status "Limited"? ─────────────────────────────────→ Learning/Delivery Failure (MF2.10.7–8) → Remedy: see relevant section
 └─ None of the above? ────────────────────────────────────────────→ External/seasonal factor — validate against Benchmarks (MF1.11.5) before acting
```

### MF2.10.2 CPA Increase

- **Root Cause Analysis:** Decompose CPA (MF1.4.5) into its constituents — has CPM risen (Auction Competition), has CTR fallen (Creative Fatigue or Audience mismatch), or has CVR fallen (landing-page/checkout/offer issue, outside this Campaign layer's direct control but diagnosable from Campaign-level data)?
- **Decision Tree:**
```
CPA rising
 ├─ CPM rising, CTR/CVR stable → Auction Competition (MF2.10.9)
 ├─ CTR falling, CPM/CVR stable → Creative Fatigue (MF1.9.12) or Audience Saturation (MF1.8.8) — check Frequency to distinguish
 ├─ CVR falling, CPM/CTR stable → Landing-page/checkout/offer issue (escalate outside Campaign layer)
 └─ All three stable, CPA still rising → Attribution/Matching Quality degradation (MF1.6.17) — verify tracking integrity first
```
- **Corrective Actions:** Match the specific decomposed cause to its established remedy (Creative Refresh, Audience Expansion, landing-page investigation, or tracking-integrity repair) rather than applying a generic Bid Strategy or Budget change that does not address the actual root cause.

### MF2.10.3 ROAS Drop

- **Root Cause Analysis:** ROAS (MF1.4.6) drops from either declining Purchase value/volume (numerator) or rising Spend without proportional value (denominator) — decompose before acting, per MF1.3.42's Common Mistakes.
- **Decision Tree:**
```
ROAS declining
 ├─ Purchase volume/value declining, Spend stable → CVR/Conversion Rate Ranking issue (MF1.3.46) → landing/offer investigation
 ├─ Spend rising, Purchase volume/value stable → CPA/CPM-driven (see MF2.10.2)
 ├─ Purchase value passing recently changed/integration modified → verify Purchase value accuracy first (MF1.3.31's Common Mistakes) before any other diagnosis
 └─ Attribution Window/Model recently changed → methodology-driven change, not a genuine performance change (MF1.3.41's Common Mistakes)
```
- **Corrective Actions:** Verify measurement integrity (Purchase value accuracy, Attribution settings) before any Bid Strategy or Budget response — a large share of apparent ROAS drops are measurement artifacts, not genuine performance changes.

### MF2.10.4 CTR Drop

- **Root Cause Analysis:** Distinguish Creative-side causes (Fatigue) from Audience-side causes (Saturation) from Placement-mix shifts.
- **Decision Tree:**
```
CTR declining
 ├─ Frequency also rising → Audience Saturation (MF1.8.8) likely primary or compounding cause
 ├─ Frequency stable, Engagement Rate Ranking declining → Creative Fatigue (MF1.9.12)
 ├─ Placement mix recently shifted (e.g., more Audience Network delivery) → Placement-driven CTR variance, not a Creative/Audience problem
 └─ None of the above → check for a genuine market/competitive shift via Benchmarks (MF1.11.5)
```
- **Corrective Actions:** Creative Refresh for Fatigue; Audience Expansion/Rotation for Saturation; Placement review (not necessarily restriction — see MF1.3.16's Common Mistakes on over-restricting Placement) for a Placement-mix cause.

### MF2.10.5 Frequency Increase

- **Root Cause Analysis:** Confirm whether Frequency (MF1.4.7) is rising because Reach has plateaued (Audience exhausted at current Budget) or because Budget/Impressions grew disproportionately without matching Reach growth.
- **Decision Tree:**
```
Frequency rising
 ├─ Reach plateaued, Impressions still growing → Audience is saturated/exhausted at current Budget → Audience Expansion (MF2.7.8)
 └─ Reach growing too, just slower than Impressions → Expected behavior at current Audience size — monitor, not yet a failure signal
```
- **Corrective Actions:** Audience Expansion or Rotation (MF1.8.9, MF1.10.10) once Frequency exceeds the Warning/Critical Range appropriate to the Campaign's Objective and Audience type (MF1.4.7) — never apply a single universal threshold across all Campaigns.

### MF2.10.6 Delivery Failure

- **Root Cause Analysis:** Confirm whether the Campaign/Ad Set is not delivering due to Budget exhaustion, a Bid Strategy too restrictive for current Auction conditions, a Creative/Placement compliance rejection, or a genuinely exhausted eligible Audience.
- **Decision Tree:**
```
Delivery status = "Not Delivering" or severely "Limited"
 ├─ Budget exhausted for the period → expected, not a failure — resumes next period
 ├─ Bid Cap/Cost Cap set too low for current Auction conditions → MF2.6.2–3's calibration guidance
 ├─ Creative flagged/rejected → Creative-level compliance issue, outside this Campaign layer's direct remediation scope
 └─ Audience/Placement eligible pool too small → Audience Expansion or Placement broadening (MF1.8.9, MF1.3.16)
```
- **Corrective Actions:** Address the specific identified cause; never respond to Delivery Failure with an untargeted Budget increase before confirming Budget was actually the binding constraint.

### MF2.10.7 Learning Failure

- Fully addressed at MF2.8.2 (Learning Limited) — cross-referenced here as a Diagnostics Framework entry point. Symptom: one or more Ad Sets remain in "Learning" status well beyond the typical ~7-day/50-event window (MF1.3.19) without progressing.

### MF2.10.8 Budget Saturation

- **Root Cause Analysis:** Confirm whether the Campaign has reached the point where additional Budget no longer produces proportional additional Delivery — a sign the Audience/Placement pool, not Budget, is now the binding constraint.
- **Decision Tree:**
```
Marginal Delivery per additional Budget dollar declining
 ├─ Audience/Placement pool at or near exhaustion → Horizontal Scaling (MF2.9.2) or Expansion (MF2.9.4) rather than further Vertical Scaling
 └─ Auction competition has risen, reducing efficiency at the same Budget → see Auction Competition (MF2.10.9)
```
- **Corrective Actions:** Shift from Vertical Scaling to Horizontal Scaling/Expansion once Budget Saturation is confirmed, per MF2.9's Safe Scaling Rules.

### MF2.10.9 Auction Competition

- **Root Cause Analysis:** Rising CPM/CPA with stable Audience, Placement, Creative, and Bid Strategy configuration most commonly reflects increased competitive demand in the Auction (MF1.3.18) — often seasonal (e.g., Q4 holiday demand) or category-specific (a competitor increasing spend).
- **Decision Tree:**
```
CPM/CPA rising, internal configuration unchanged
 ├─ Coincides with a known seasonal/competitive period → Expected — validate against historical same-period Benchmarks (MF1.11.5)
 └─ No known external cause → Investigate Quality/Engagement/Conversion Rate Ranking (MF1.3.44–46) — a relative competitive quality decline can also raise effective cost even without external demand change
```
- **Corrective Actions:** Where genuinely external/seasonal, adjust expectations and Budget rather than treating it as a fixable internal fault; where Ranking-driven, apply Creative Refresh or landing-page improvement rather than accepting the higher cost as unavoidable.

---

## MF2.11 Campaign KPI Framework

The official Campaign-layer KPI library, organizing MF1.4's Metrics Dictionary entries by their role in Campaign-level decision-making rather than redefining any of them. Every KPI below cites its MF1.4 (or MF1.3, for Ranking signals) source; no formula or numeric range is restated.

### MF2.11.1 Primary KPIs (by Objective)

| Objective | Primary KPI | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Awareness | Reach, CPM | MF1.3.27, MF1.4.1 |
| Traffic | CTR, CPC | MF1.4.2–3 |
| Engagement | Post Engagement volume / Messaging Conversation volume (sub-type-dependent) | MF1.4.10, MF1.3.33 |
| Leads | CPL | MF1.4.4 |
| App Promotion | CPI | MF1.4.11 |
| Sales | ROAS | MF1.4.6 |

### MF2.11.2 Secondary KPIs

Frequency (MF1.4.7), CPM (for non-Awareness Objectives, as a cost-baseline diagnostic), CVR (MF1.4.12), Quality/Engagement/Conversion Rate Rankings (MF1.3.44–46) — Metrics that contextualize or explain the Primary KPI's movement without being the primary decision criterion themselves.

### MF2.11.3 Leading Indicators

Metrics that move before a Primary KPI shift becomes visible, giving early warning: Frequency trend (precedes CTR/CVR decline from Audience Saturation, MF1.8.8), Ranking signal trend (precedes CPA/CPM increase from competitive/quality shifts), Learning Phase status (precedes any reliable Primary KPI reading at all).

### MF2.11.4 Lagging Indicators

Metrics that confirm an outcome has already occurred: ROAS and CPA (MF1.4.5–6) are inherently lagging relative to the Delivery and Conversion activity that produced them; Attribution-window-bound Conversion counts (MF1.3.41) are lagging by definition, since a Conversion may not be attributed until well after the triggering interaction.

### MF2.11.5 Financial KPIs

ROAS (MF1.4.6), CPA (MF1.4.5), CPL (MF1.4.4), CPI (MF1.4.11), total Spend against Budget (MF1.3.21) — Metrics with direct currency-denominated business meaning.

### MF2.11.6 Operational KPIs

Delivery status (MF1.3.25), Learning Phase status (MF1.3.19), Budget pacing (spend rate against period Budget) — Metrics describing whether the Campaign is mechanically functioning as configured, independent of whether that configuration is currently optimal.

### MF2.11.7 Efficiency KPIs

CPM, CTR, CPC, CVR (MF1.4.1–3, MF1.4.12) — Metrics expressing output per unit of input (Impressions per dollar, Clicks per Impression, Conversions per Click) rather than absolute volume or currency outcome.

### MF2.11.8 Growth KPIs

Reach growth rate, Budget/Delivery volume growth rate over successive Scaling stages (MF2.9), Horizontal Scaling's incremental-volume contribution — Metrics specifically tracking the Campaign's trajectory over time rather than its state at a point in time (per MF1.11.8's Trend concept).

### MF2.11.9 Diagnostic KPIs

Quality Ranking, Engagement Rate Ranking, Conversion Rate Ranking (MF1.3.44–46), Frequency (MF1.4.7), Matching Quality (MF1.6.17) — Metrics whose primary value is root-cause diagnosis (MF2.10) rather than direct success measurement.

### MF2.11.10 KPI Selection Rule

A Campaign's dashboard/reporting configuration should always surface its Objective-appropriate Primary KPI (MF2.11.1) most prominently, with Secondary/Diagnostic KPIs available but not competing visually for attention — the same principle established generically at MF1.11.5's Benchmarks entry and MF1.13.8's Dashboard Naming guidance, applied here specifically to Campaign-level KPI presentation.

---

## MF2.12 Campaign Decision Intelligence

AI-consumable decision rules, each specializing the Generic Framework Series' Decision concept (`F4.5` Decision Taxonomy — specifically the Selective/Deferred Decision types) with concrete Campaign-layer content. Every rule is fully self-contained: Observed Metrics, Likely Cause, Confidence Level, Recommended Action, Priority, Expected Outcome, Risk.

### MF2.12.1 Rule: Sustained Frequency Rise with CTR Decline

- **Observed Metrics:** Frequency (MF1.4.7) rising 3+ consecutive periods; CTR (MF1.4.2) declining over the same window; Reach plateaued.
- **Likely Cause:** Audience Saturation (MF1.8.8).
- **Confidence Level:** High, when both signals co-occur and Reach has genuinely plateaued (not merely slowed).
- **Recommended Action:** Audience Expansion (MF1.8.9/MF2.7.8) — widen Lookalike percentage or enable Advantage+ Audience.
- **Priority:** High — compounding degradation risk (Creative Fatigue often follows if unaddressed).
- **Expected Outcome:** Frequency reduction, CTR recovery within one Learning Phase cycle post-expansion.
- **Risk:** Learning Reset if the expansion constitutes a significant edit — apply MF2.9.6's incremental-change discipline.

### MF2.12.2 Rule: CTR Decline with Stable Frequency
