- **Definition (Ad-Set-configuration-scoped):** A Budget expressed as a maximum average daily spend, allowing some day-to-day variance (commonly up to double on any single day) while targeting the set average over a week.
- **Configuration Role:** The most common Budget type for ongoing, indefinite-duration Ad Sets (Always On scheduling, MF3.8.1).
- **Common Mistakes:** Setting a Daily Budget so low that even normal day-to-day Auction variance risks triggering Learning Limited (MF3.10.2) on higher-competition days.

### MF3.7.3 Lifetime Budget

- **Definition (Ad-Set-configuration-scoped):** A Budget expressed as a fixed total to be spent across the Ad Set's entire scheduled run (requiring a defined start and end date, MF3.8.2), with Meta's system pacing spend across that period.
- **Configuration Role:** Appropriate for fixed-duration campaigns (a specific promotional window, an event-driven push) where total spend certainty matters more than daily-spend flexibility.
- **Common Mistakes:** Entering a Lifetime Budget figure in a field expecting a Daily Budget (or vice versa) — a data-entry error with materially different financial consequences, flagged as a specific Budget Risk at MF2.5.10.

### MF3.7.4 Budget Scheduling

- **Definition:** Configuring how a Lifetime Budget's spend is paced across its scheduled run — evenly, or with Ad Scheduling (MF3.8) concentrating spend during specific dayparts.
- **Configuration Role:** Allows a Business to align spend pacing with known demand patterns (e.g., concentrating a Lifetime Budget's spend during business hours for a Calls-optimized Ad Set, MF3.5.14).
- **Common Mistakes:** Configuring Budget Scheduling that conflicts with the Ad Set's Optimization Event availability (e.g., concentrating spend during hours when the destination landing page or call center is unavailable).

### MF3.7.5 Spend Limits

- **Definition:** Account- or Campaign-level maximum spend guardrails, distinct from any individual Ad Set's own Budget, that cap total spend across all contained Ad Sets regardless of their individual Budget settings.
- **Configuration Role:** A governance safety net (MF2.13.8's Compliance Rules) protecting against the aggregate-overspend risk that individual Ad-Set-level Budget review alone might miss.
- **Common Mistakes:** Relying solely on individual Ad Set Budgets for spend control without a Campaign- or Account-level Spend Limit as a backstop against configuration error.

### MF3.7.6 Bid Strategy Interaction

Budget and Bid Strategy (MF2.6) interact directly: a Cost Cap or Bid Cap (MF2.6.2–3) set too aggressively relative to the Ad Set's Budget can prevent that Budget from ever being fully spent, while Highest Volume or Highest Value (MF2.6.1/5) will spend available Budget as efficiently as the Auction allows without an explicit ceiling. Configuration guidance: always review Budget and Bid Strategy together, never independently — MF2.6.6's Bid Strategy Selection Summary should be cross-checked against the Ad Set's actual configured Budget level before finalizing either.

### MF3.7.7 Budget Constraints

- Minimum Budget thresholds exist below which an Ad Set cannot realistically exit Learning Phase for its configured Optimization Event's typical cost (MF2.5.5) — this is the binding constraint most Budget Control decisions must respect first.
- Campaign-level Spend Limits (MF3.7.5) or overall Account spend authority (MF2.13.3) may cap what an individual Ad Set's Budget can be set to, regardless of what its Optimization Event's typical cost would otherwise justify.

### MF3.7.8 Budget Diagnostics

| Symptom | Likely Cause | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Ad Set never fully spends its Daily/Lifetime Budget | Bid Strategy (Cost Cap/Bid Cap) too restrictive relative to Budget | MF3.7.6, MF2.6.2–3 |
| Ad Set spends unevenly, spiking on some days | Normal Daily Budget variance (up to ~2x on a single day) — not necessarily a fault | MF3.7.2 |
| Ad Set stuck in Learning Limited despite adequate Bid Strategy | Budget itself below the minimum threshold for the Optimization Event's typical cost | MF2.5.5, MF3.7.7 |
| Aggregate Campaign spend exceeds expectation despite individual Ad Set Budgets looking correct | Missing or misconfigured Campaign/Account-level Spend Limit | MF3.7.5 |

### MF3.7.9 Budget Decision Rules

1. Size an Ad Set's Budget against its specific configured Optimization Event's typical cost (MF1.4's relevant Metric — CPL, CPA, CPI, or CPM depending on Objective/Event), never against an arbitrary round number.
2. Always review Budget and Bid Strategy jointly (MF3.7.6) before finalizing either.
3. Use Lifetime Budget only when a genuinely fixed-duration run with a known start/end date applies (MF3.8.2); default to Daily Budget for ongoing Ad Sets.
4. Maintain a Campaign- or Account-level Spend Limit (MF3.7.5) as a backstop regardless of how carefully individual Ad Set Budgets are configured.
5. Apply any Budget increase through the incremental Vertical Scaling discipline (MF2.9.5), never as an isolated, unreviewed jump.

---

## MF3.8 Scheduling Framework

### MF3.8.1 Always On

- **Definition:** An Ad Set configured with no defined end date, running indefinitely under a Daily Budget (MF3.7.2) until manually paused or retired.
- **Configuration Role:** The default scheduling mode for ongoing, evergreen Ad Sets (Maintenance-stage Campaigns, MF2.7.6).
- **Common Mistakes:** Leaving an Always On Ad Set unreviewed for extended periods, allowing Creative Fatigue (MF1.9.12) or Audience Saturation (MF1.8.8) to develop without the natural review checkpoint a defined end date would otherwise prompt.

### MF3.8.2 Start & End Date

- **Definition:** Explicit boundaries on an Ad Set's active Delivery window, required for Lifetime Budget (MF3.7.3) and optional (as a deliberate constraint) for Daily Budget.
- **Configuration Role:** Appropriate for promotional windows, product launches, or event-driven pushes with a genuinely fixed duration.
- **Common Mistakes:** Setting a Start Date immediately at Campaign approval time without accounting for the Learning Phase (MF1.3.19) period needed before performance stabilizes — a short fixed-duration Ad Set may end before ever exiting Learning Phase, wasting the majority of its Budget on unstabilized Delivery.

### MF3.8.3 Dayparting

- **Definition:** Restricting or weighting Delivery toward specific hours of the day and/or days of the week, available in combination with a Lifetime Budget (MF3.7.4's Budget Scheduling) or via a Bid Strategy compatible with scheduled Delivery.
- **Configuration Role:** Aligns spend with known demand patterns — e.g., concentrating a Leads Ad Set's Delivery during hours when a sales team is actually available to follow up.
- **Common Mistakes:** Applying Dayparting restrictive enough to meaningfully shrink the Auction's eligible time window without accounting for the Learning Phase volume impact (MF3.8.9) of doing so.

### MF3.8.4 Time Zone

- **Definition:** The reference time zone against which Dayparting, Start/End Date, and Daily Budget resets are evaluated — typically the Ad Account's configured time zone, not necessarily the Audience's local time zone.
- **Configuration Role:** Critical for Dayparting accuracy when an Ad Set's Audience spans multiple time zones — a Business Hours restriction (MF3.8.5) configured in the Ad Account's time zone may not actually align with the Audience's local business hours.
- **Common Mistakes:** Assuming Dayparting automatically adjusts to each individual user's local time zone across a geographically broad Audience, when it is evaluated against the Ad Account's single configured time zone.

### MF3.8.5 Business Hours

- **Definition:** A Dayparting configuration specifically aligned to the Business's own hours of operation, most relevant for Calls (MF3.5.14) or Messaging (MF3.5.8) Optimization Events requiring a live human response.
- **Configuration Role:** Directly connects Scheduling to Optimization Event viability — a Calls-optimized Ad Set delivering outside Business Hours generates Calls no one can answer, undermining the Optimization Event's entire value.
- **Common Mistakes:** Optimizing for Calls or Messaging without any Business Hours Dayparting constraint, allowing Delivery to continue generating unanswerable contact attempts outside staffed hours.

### MF3.8.6 Call Campaign Scheduling

- **Definition:** The specific application of Business Hours Dayparting (MF3.8.5) to Ad Sets configured with the Calls Optimization Event (MF3.5.14), commonly with a tighter alignment than general Messaging scheduling given phone calls' real-time, unqueued nature.
- **Configuration Role:** The most operationally sensitive Scheduling configuration in this Framework, since a missed call (unlike a queued Message) typically cannot be recovered.
- **Common Mistakes:** Scheduling Call Campaign Delivery right up to the exact minute call-center staff sign off, without a buffer accounting for calls initiated just before closing that ring after hours.

### MF3.8.7 Seasonal Scheduling

- **Definition:** Deliberately time-bound Ad Set scheduling aligned to a known recurring seasonal demand pattern (e.g., back-to-school, summer travel).
- **Configuration Role:** Should be planned with Learning Phase timing in mind (MF3.8.9) — launching too close to the peak demand window risks the Ad Set still being in Learning Phase when peak demand (and peak Auction Competition, MF2.10.9) arrives.
- **Common Mistakes:** Launching a Seasonal Ad Set with insufficient lead time before the actual seasonal peak, forcing it through Learning Phase during the highest-competition, highest-cost period rather than beforehand.

### MF3.8.8 Holiday Scheduling

- **Definition:** Scheduling adjustments specifically around major holidays, accounting for both demand shifts (often sharply higher Auction Competition, per MF2.10.9) and Business operational changes (e.g., no Call-handling staff on a given holiday).
- **Configuration Role:** Requires coordinating both Budget expectations (higher CPM/CPA is often normal and expected, not a fault, per MF2.10.9's seasonal guidance) and Business Hours Dayparting (MF3.8.5) for any holiday-specific closures.
- **Common Mistakes:** Treating a holiday-period cost increase as a Diagnostics Framework (MF3.12) failure signal requiring corrective action, rather than an expected, Benchmark-validated (MF1.11.5) seasonal pattern.

### MF3.8.9 Learning Impact

Scheduling decisions interact directly with Learning Phase (MF1.3.19): a Dayparting restriction, a short fixed Start/End Date window, or Seasonal/Holiday timing that reduces the effective hours or days an Ad Set can accumulate Optimization Events will proportionally slow Learning Phase exit — this is the unifying consideration behind every Common Mistakes entry in this section, and should be checked explicitly whenever a Scheduling constraint is being considered for an Ad Set that has not yet exited Learning Phase.

---

## MF3.9 Delivery Framework

Explains Meta's delivery system from the Ad Set's own perspective, extending MF1.3.18 (Auction) and MF1.3.25 (Delivery) without redefining either.

### MF3.9.1 Auction Entry

An Ad Set's Ads enter the Auction (MF1.3.18) for a given impression opportunity only when the Ad Set is Active, within its Schedule (MF3.8), has available Budget (MF3.7), and the specific user falls within its configured Audience (MF3.3) and eligible Placement (MF3.4) — Auction Entry is the gating step before any Bid/Ad-Quality evaluation occurs.

### MF3.9.2 Auction Eligibility

Beyond basic Auction Entry gating, eligibility also depends on the Ad Set not being subject to a Delivery restriction (MF3.9.6's Limited Delivery) or a Creative-level compliance rejection (outside this Framework's Creative-deep-dive scope, MF3.1.6). An Ad Set can satisfy Auction Entry's basic gates while still failing full Auction Eligibility due to one of these restrictions.

### MF3.9.3 Estimated Action Rate

The Auction's prediction of how likely a specific user is to complete the Ad Set's configured Optimization Event (MF3.5) if shown a specific Ad — one of the three inputs (alongside Bid and Ad Quality) to the Auction's Total Value calculation (MF1.3.18). Configuration guidance: this is the component every Optimization Event selection decision in MF3.5 ultimately shapes — a well-matched Optimization Event produces a more confident, more favorable Estimated Action Rate than a poorly-matched one.

### MF3.9.4 Bid

The Ad Set's Bid input, governed by its configured Bid Strategy (MF2.6). Auction Entry does not guarantee a won impression — Bid, combined with Estimated Action Rate and Ad Quality, determines the Auction's Total Value calculation and therefore the actual win/loss outcome per opportunity.

### MF3.9.5 Ad Quality

The Ranking-signal-informed quality input to the Auction (MF1.3.44–46: Quality Ranking, Engagement Rate Ranking, Conversion Rate Ranking), evaluated at the Ad level but directly affecting the Ad Set's overall Auction competitiveness in aggregate across all its Ads.

### MF3.9.6 Learning Phase (Delivery-System View)

Cross-referenced from MF1.3.19/MF3.2.8 — included here as the Delivery Framework's own entry point, since Learning Phase status is the primary lens through which Delivery predictability should be interpreted at any given moment. Full diagnostic treatment is in MF3.10.

### MF3.9.7 Delivery Status

The Ad Set's reported operational state: **Active** (eligible and delivering normally), **Learning** (within the initial data-accumulation period, MF1.3.19), **Limited** (a specific restriction is constraining Delivery, MF3.9.8), or **Not Delivering** (Auction Entry itself is failing — Budget exhausted, Schedule inactive, or a compliance issue). Configuration guidance: Delivery Status should be the first thing checked whenever an Ad Set's reported Metrics look unexpected — a Learning or Limited status changes how every subsequent Metric should be interpreted (MF1.3.19's Professional Notes).

### MF3.9.8 Limited Delivery

A Delivery Status specifically indicating a binding constraint is preventing full Auction participation — most commonly an overly restrictive Bid Strategy (MF2.6.2–3), an Audience too narrow (MF3.3.13), or insufficient Budget (MF3.7.7) relative to the Optimization Event's typical cost. Limited Delivery is functionally the Delivery-status manifestation of the same underlying causes documented at MF3.10.2's Learning Limited — the two are closely related but not identical: an Ad Set can show Limited Delivery even post-Learning-Exit if a Bid/Budget constraint tightens after initial stabilization.

### MF3.9.9 Delivery Diagnostics (Cross-Reference)

The full Delivery-symptom diagnostic treatment (High CPM, No Delivery, Budget Restriction, Bid Restriction, etc.) is provided in MF3.12's Ad Set Diagnostics Framework, to avoid duplicating diagnostic content across two sections — this entry establishes only that Delivery Framework and Diagnostics Framework are closely coupled, with MF3.9 explaining the mechanism and MF3.12 explaining the symptom-to-cause mapping.

### MF3.9.10 Auction Competition (Delivery-System View)

Cross-referenced from MF2.10.9 — rising CPM/CPA at the Ad Set level with no internal configuration change most commonly reflects increased Auction Competition from other advertisers pursuing the same Audience/Placement/Optimization Event combination. This is a Delivery-system-level phenomenon the Ad Set owner cannot directly control, only respond to (via Bid Strategy, Budget, or expectation adjustment, per MF2.10.9's Corrective Actions).

---

## MF3.10 Learning Framework

Since Learning Phase is natively an Ad-Set-level concept (MF3.1.3), this section provides the deepest treatment of it in the entire document lineage — extending MF1.3.19, MF1.10.1–2, and MF2.8's Campaign-layer overview with full Ad-Set-operational depth.

### MF3.10.1 Learning Phase (Ad-Set-Layer Full Treatment)

At the Ad Set layer, Learning Phase is evaluated per Ad Set individually — Meta's system accumulates Optimization Event data specific to that Ad Set's exact configuration (Audience, Placement, Optimization Goal, Bid Strategy, Creative set) until predictions stabilize. Every other section in this Framework that references "significant edit resets Learning Phase" is describing an interaction with this section's subject.

### MF3.10.2 Learning Limited (Ad-Set-Layer Full Treatment)

The specific Learning Phase outcome where insufficient Optimization Event volume accumulates to ever stabilize under current configuration. At the Ad Set layer, this is diagnosed by comparing actual accumulated Event count against the ~50-events-in-7-days reference point (MF1.3.19) and confirming the rate of accumulation, not just the current count, is insufficient to reach that threshold within a reasonable further period.

### MF3.10.3 Learning Exit

The point at which accumulated Optimization Event volume is sufficient for Meta's system to consider its Delivery predictions for this Ad Set's specific configuration stable. Ad-Set-layer significance: this is the trigger condition for the Campaign Lifecycle's Stabilization stage (MF2.7.4) to become meaningful — Metric review conducted before Learning Exit at the Ad Set level is exactly the misinterpretation flagged at MF1.3.19's Professional Notes.

### MF3.10.4 Learning Reset

Triggered by any "significant edit" to the Ad Set's configuration: Audience, Placement (in some cases), Creative set, Optimization Goal, or Bid Strategy changes. Ad-Set-layer significance: because nearly every section of this Framework (Audience Configuration, Placement, Optimization, Bid Strategy, Budget) describes an Ad-Set-level configuration lever, nearly every operational decision in this document carries at least latent Learning Reset risk, which is why MF3.10.11's Recovery Strategy and MF2.9.6's Safe Scaling Rules both emphasize isolating and batching changes.

### MF3.10.5 Required Events

The minimum Optimization Event volume/rate needed for Learning Exit, expressed by Meta as approximately 50 events within a rolling 7-day window (MF1.3.19). Ad-Set-layer significance: this figure is the target every Budget Decision Rule (MF3.7.9), Optimization Event selection (MF3.5), and Audience configuration (MF3.3) decision should be checked against before finalizing an Ad Set's launch configuration.

### MF3.10.6 Optimization Stability

The state, following Learning Exit, in which an Ad Set's Delivery predictions and resulting Metrics behave predictably and are trustworthy for Stabilization-stage baseline-setting (MF2.7.4) and subsequent Scaling decisions (MF3.13). Ad-Set-layer significance: Optimization Stability, not mere Learning Exit alone, is the true precondition for Scaling — a just-exited Ad Set may need an additional observation period before its baseline is considered genuinely stable.

### MF3.10.7 Scaling Effects (on Learning)

Both Vertical and Horizontal Scaling (MF3.13) carry Learning-Phase implications: a large Vertical Scaling (Budget) increase can itself constitute or trigger conditions resembling a significant edit if it substantially changes the Ad Set's effective Auction behavior; Horizontal Scaling via Duplication (MF2.9.3) creates an entirely new Ad Set that must independently pass through its own full Learning Phase, never inheriting the original's stabilized status.

### MF3.10.8 Budget Effects (on Learning)

Budget level directly determines whether sufficient Optimization Event volume (MF3.10.5) can accumulate within a reasonable period — this is the most common single lever behind Learning Limited (MF2.5.5, MF3.7.7) and the first Recovery Method typically evaluated once Audience broadening (the usually-preferred first step) has been ruled out or already applied.

### MF3.10.9 Audience Effects (on Learning)

Audience size and specificity directly bound the eligible population Meta's system can draw Optimization Events from — an Audience too narrow (MF3.3.1's over-narrowing risk, MF3.3.7's over-layering risk) is frequently the primary Learning Limited cause, and Audience Expansion (MF3.3.5) is accordingly the typically-preferred first Recovery Method (MF2.8.2).

### MF3.10.10 Creative Effects (on Learning)

While Creative itself is outside this Framework's deep scope (MF3.1.6), Creative composition directly affects Learning: too many concurrent Ad variants within one Ad Set (MF1.9.13's Common Mistakes) thins the Optimization Event data available to any single variant, and a Creative change constitutes a significant edit (MF3.10.4) — Creative-related Learning Reset risk should be weighed the same as any Audience/Bid Strategy change when planning an edit.

### MF3.10.11 Recovery Strategy

The full ordered Recovery Method sequence, consistent with and extending MF2.8.2's Campaign-layer treatment, applied at native Ad-Set grain:
1. **Audience Expansion** (MF3.3.5) — typically the lowest-disruption, first-choice remedy.
2. **Bid Strategy relaxation** (e.g., Bid Cap → Cost Cap → Highest Volume, MF2.6) — the second step.
3. **Budget increase** (MF3.7.9), sized against the specific volume gap, not an arbitrary amount — the third step.
4. **Temporary proxy Optimization Event** (e.g., Add To Cart in place of Purchase, MF1.6.5) — a deliberate, temporary fourth step, explicitly not a permanent substitute once volume allows reversion to the true target Event.

**Best Practice:** Apply exactly one Recovery Method at a time and allow a full observation period (MF3.10.5's Required Events window) before applying the next, to preserve the ability to attribute the eventual Learning Exit to a specific, identified fix — directly mirroring MF2.9.6's Safe Scaling Rules variable-isolation discipline.

---

## MF3.11 Ad Set KPI Framework

The official Ad-Set-layer KPI library, organizing MF1.4's Metrics Dictionary and MF1.3's Ranking signals by their role in Ad-Set-level decision-making, extending MF2.11's Campaign-layer KPI Framework with the finer-grained categories specific to this layer's Audience/Placement/Delivery/Learning/Auction control mechanics. No Metric is redefined; every entry cites its MF1 source.

### MF3.11.1 Primary KPIs (by Optimization Event)

| Optimization Event | Primary KPI | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Conversions/Purchases | ROAS or CPA (Objective-dependent) | MF1.4.5–6 |
| Landing Page Views/Link Clicks | CTR, CPC | MF1.4.2–3 |
| Reach/Impressions | Reach/Impressions volume, CPM | MF1.3.27–28, MF1.4.1 |
| ThruPlay/Video Views | ThruPlay Rate, watch-percentage | MF1.4.9 |
| Post Engagement | Cost Per Engagement | MF1.4.10 |
| Messages | Messaging Conversation volume | MF1.3.33 |
| Leads | CPL | MF1.4.4 |
| App Events | Cost per specified in-app Event | MF1.4.11 (CPI parallel) |
| Value | ROAS | MF1.4.6 |
| Catalog | ROAS, per-Product-Set performance | MF1.4.6, MF1.3.36 |
| Calls | Call initiation volume/cost | MF1.3.16-adjacent |

### MF3.11.2 Secondary KPIs

CVR (MF1.4.12), Frequency (MF1.4.7), CPM as a cost-baseline diagnostic across non-Awareness Optimization Events — Metrics contextualizing the Primary KPI without being the Ad Set's own direct success criterion.

### MF3.11.3 Efficiency KPIs

CPM, CTR, CPC, CVR (MF1.4.1–3, MF1.4.12) — output-per-unit-input Metrics, most directly shaped by the Ad Set's own Audience/Placement/Optimization Event configuration among all KPI categories in this library.

### MF3.11.4 Audience KPIs

Frequency (MF1.4.7) as the primary Audience-health signal; Audience Overlap (MF1.8.7) reporting-tool output; Reach growth rate as an Audience-breadth indicator distinct from raw Reach volume.

### MF3.11.5 Delivery KPIs

Delivery Status (MF1.3.25/MF3.9.7) as a categorical KPI; Impressions and Reach volume; Budget pacing (spend rate against configured Budget, MF3.7).

### MF3.11.6 Financial KPIs

ROAS, CPA, CPL, CPI (MF1.4.5–6, MF1.4.4, MF1.4.11) — the same currency-denominated Metrics as MF2.11.5's Campaign-layer Financial KPIs, evaluated here at native Ad Set grain rather than Campaign roll-up.

### MF3.11.7 Learning KPIs

Learning Phase status (MF1.3.19), accumulated Optimization Event count against the Required Events threshold (MF3.10.5), days-in-Learning-Phase as a pace indicator.

### MF3.11.8 Auction KPIs

Estimated Action Rate (qualitative/relative, not directly exposed as a number but inferable from Delivery efficiency trends, MF3.9.3), Quality/Engagement/Conversion Rate Rankings (MF1.3.44–46), effective CPM relative to Auction Competition Benchmarks (MF2.10.9, MF1.11.5).

### MF3.11.9 KPI Selection Rule

An Ad Set's own reporting view should surface its configured Optimization Event's Primary KPI (MF3.11.1) most prominently, with Learning KPIs (MF3.11.7) given elevated visibility specifically during the Learning Phase stage (MF2.7.3) and de-emphasized once Optimization Stability (MF3.10.6) is confirmed — extending the same principle established at MF2.11.10 to this layer's Learning-Phase-aware reporting need.

---

## MF3.12 Ad Set Diagnostics Framework

Enterprise diagnostic models for the 15 most common Ad-Set-layer issues. Each model is documented with: Symptoms, Metrics, Root Causes, Confidence Level, Recommended Fixes, Priority, Recovery Time.

### MF3.12.1 Audience Too Small

- **Symptoms:** Ad Set stuck in Learning status well beyond the typical window; Delivery volume far below Budget-implied expectations.
- **Metrics:** Low absolute Reach; Optimization Event count well below the Required Events threshold (MF3.10.5).
- **Root Causes:** Over-narrowed Core Audience criteria (MF3.3.1); over-layered combined criteria (MF3.3.7); an overly-narrow Lookalike percentage (MF3.3.3) or Custom Audience source.
- **Confidence Level:** High when Reach is confirmed low relative to Budget and geography.
- **Recommended Fixes:** Audience Expansion (MF3.3.5) — widen Lookalike percentage, reduce layered criteria, or shift toward Advantage+ Audience (MF3.3.4).
- **Priority:** High.
- **Recovery Time:** Typically one further Learning Phase cycle (up to ~7 days) post-expansion.

### MF3.12.2 Audience Too Broad

- **Symptoms:** Adequate Delivery volume but weak Conversion Rate Ranking (MF1.3.46) or CVR (MF1.4.12) relative to Benchmark; Optimization Event volume sufficient but quality low.
- **Metrics:** High Reach/Impressions, low CVR, high CPA relative to Objective Benchmark (MF1.11.5).
- **Root Causes:** Advantage+ Audience with a weak or absent Audience Signal (MF3.3.6); Core Audience criteria too generic for the specific Optimization Event's actual target population.
- **Confidence Level:** Moderate — requires ruling out landing-page/offer-side causes (MF2.10.2's CVR decomposition) before concluding Audience breadth is the primary driver.
- **Recommended Fixes:** Supply a stronger, more relevant Audience Signal (MF3.3.6); consider a narrower, higher-relevance Custom Audience or Lookalike source in place of pure Advantage+ Audience.
- **Priority:** Moderate.
- **Recovery Time:** One to two Learning Phase cycles, since narrowing itself constitutes a significant edit (MF3.10.4).

### MF3.12.3 Audience Overlap

- **Symptoms:** Sibling Ad Sets within the same Campaign show mutually poor or declining Delivery efficiency; one Ad Set appears to cannibalize another's Reach.
- **Metrics:** Meta's Audience Overlap reporting tool showing a high overlap percentage between named Ad Sets; unexpectedly high combined Frequency across siblings.
- **Root Causes:** Insufficiently differentiated Custom/Lookalike sources across sibling Ad Sets (MF3.3.9); Duplication (MF2.9.3) without adequate Audience differentiation.
- **Confidence Level:** High once confirmed via the Overlap reporting tool directly.
- **Recommended Fixes:** Apply Exclusions (MF3.3.8) between siblings; consolidate under Campaign Budget (MF1.3.22) to let the system naturally arbitrate; or differentiate the Audience sources more substantially.
- **Priority:** Moderate-to-High, especially under Ad Set Budget where the system does not self-correct for it.
- **Recovery Time:** Immediate to one Learning Phase cycle, depending on whether the fix constitutes a significant edit.

### MF3.12.4 Learning Limited

- Fully treated at MF3.10.2 and MF2.8.2; cross-referenced here as a Diagnostics Framework entry. **Symptoms:** Learning status persists beyond ~7 days/50 events. **Metrics:** Optimization Event accumulation rate below the Required Events threshold (MF3.10.5). **Root Causes:** Budget, Bid Strategy, or Audience mismatch to the Optimization Event's typical volume/cost. **Confidence Level:** High. **Recommended Fixes:** MF3.10.11's ordered Recovery Strategy. **Priority:** High. **Recovery Time:** One further Learning Phase cycle per Recovery Method applied, applied sequentially.

### MF3.12.5 No Delivery

- **Symptoms:** Zero or near-zero Impressions despite an Active status and available Budget.
- **Metrics:** Delivery Status reporting "Not Delivering"; zero or negligible Spend against available Budget.
- **Root Causes:** Schedule not yet active or already ended (MF3.8.2); Budget already exhausted for the period; a Creative-level compliance rejection (outside this Framework's deep scope, MF3.1.6); an Audience/Placement combination with a near-zero eligible population.
- **Confidence Level:** High — this is typically among the most mechanically diagnosable issues in this Framework.
- **Recommended Fixes:** Verify Schedule and Budget status first (the lowest-effort checks); escalate to Creative compliance review or Audience/Placement broadening only after ruling those out.
- **Priority:** Critical — no Delivery means the Ad Set is not functioning at all.
- **Recovery Time:** Immediate once the specific blocking cause is identified and corrected.

### MF3.12.6 High CPM

- **Symptoms:** Cost per thousand Impressions (MF1.4.1) rising or persistently elevated relative to the Business's own Benchmark.
- **Metrics:** CPM trend against historical baseline (MF1.11.5); Placement Breakdown (MF1.3.39) to isolate whether the increase is surface-specific.
- **Root Causes:** Rising Auction Competition (MF2.10.9, MF3.9.10); overly restrictive Placement configuration (MF3.4.1's Common Mistakes); declining Ad Quality Ranking (MF1.3.44) raising effective cost.
- **Confidence Level:** Moderate until decomposed against Benchmark and Placement Breakdown data.
- **Recommended Fixes:** Broaden Placement toward Advantage+ Placements (MF3.4.2) if currently Manual; investigate Quality Ranking if Auction Competition alone does not explain the full increase; adjust Budget/ROAS expectations if genuinely seasonal (MF3.8.7–8).
- **Priority:** Moderate, escalating to High if it threatens Objective-specific breakeven thresholds (e.g., Sales' ROAS floor).
- **Recovery Time:** Variable — Placement broadening can show effect within one Learning Phase cycle; genuine external Auction Competition may not resolve until the underlying demand shift subsides.

### MF3.12.7 High Frequency

- Fully treated at MF2.10.5 and MF1.4.7; cross-referenced here. **Symptoms:** Frequency exceeding Objective/Audience-type-appropriate Warning/Critical Range. **Metrics:** Frequency trend against Reach plateau. **Root Causes:** Audience Saturation (MF1.8.8) from a fixed, exhausted Audience. **Confidence Level:** High. **Recommended Fixes:** Audience Expansion (MF3.3.5) or Refresh/Rotation (MF3.3.11). **Priority:** Moderate-to-High depending on co-occurring CTR/CVR decline. **Recovery Time:** One Learning Phase cycle post-expansion.

### MF3.12.8 Low CTR

- **Symptoms:** Click-through rate (MF1.4.2) below Objective/format-appropriate Warning Range.
- **Metrics:** CTR trend; Frequency (to distinguish Saturation from Fatigue); Engagement Rate Ranking (MF1.3.45); Placement Breakdown (to isolate a format-mismatch cause, MF1.9's Common Mistakes).
- **Root Causes:** Creative Fatigue (MF1.9.12) at stable Frequency; Audience Saturation (MF1.8.8) at rising Frequency; Creative-Placement format mismatch (MF3.4's per-Placement guidance).
- **Confidence Level:** High once decomposed against Frequency and Placement Breakdown.
- **Recommended Fixes:** Creative Refresh (MF1.10.9) for Fatigue; Audience Expansion for Saturation; format-native Creative rework for a Placement mismatch.
- **Priority:** Moderate-to-High.
- **Recovery Time:** One Learning Phase cycle post-fix, given Creative/Audience changes constitute significant edits (MF3.10.4).

### MF3.12.9 High CPC

- **Symptoms:** Cost per click (MF1.4.3) elevated relative to Benchmark.
- **Metrics:** CPC decomposed into its CPM and CTR constituents (MF1.4.3's own decomposition guidance).
- **Root Causes:** Either a CPM-driven cause (MF3.12.6) or a CTR-driven cause (MF3.12.8) — CPC itself is rarely a primary root cause, per MF1.4.3's Common Misinterpretations.
- **Confidence Level:** High once decomposed — CPC should never be diagnosed as its own independent root cause.
- **Recommended Fixes:** Apply the appropriate CPM (MF3.12.6) or CTR (MF3.12.8) fix based on the decomposition result.
- **Priority:** Inherits the priority of its underlying CPM/CTR cause.
- **Recovery Time:** Inherits the recovery time of its underlying CPM/CTR cause.

### MF3.12.10 Poor CVR

- **Symptoms:** Conversion Rate (MF1.4.12) below Objective-appropriate Benchmark despite healthy upstream CTR.
- **Metrics:** CVR trend; Conversion Rate Ranking (MF1.3.46); Initiate-Checkout-to-Purchase ratio for Sales-objective Ad Sets (MF1.6.6).
- **Root Causes:** Landing-page/checkout/offer issue (outside this Framework's direct remediation scope, but diagnosable from Ad-Set-level data); Audience Too Broad (MF3.12.2) delivering low-relevance traffic despite adequate volume.
- **Confidence Level:** Moderate-to-High once CTR is confirmed healthy, isolating the cause downstream of the click itself.
- **Recommended Fixes:** Escalate to landing-page/checkout investigation if Audience relevance is confirmed adequate; otherwise apply the Audience Too Broad fix (MF3.12.2).
- **Priority:** High, given direct Financial KPI impact (MF3.11.6).
- **Recovery Time:** Variable — landing-page fixes are outside this Framework's direct control and timeline; Audience-driven fixes follow the standard one-Learning-Phase-cycle pattern.

### MF3.12.11 Poor ROAS

- Fully treated at MF2.10.3; cross-referenced here at Ad-Set grain. **Symptoms:** ROAS (MF1.4.6) below breakeven. **Metrics:** Purchase value/volume trend versus Spend trend. **Root Causes:** Purchase value-passing degradation (MF1.3.31), CVR/Conversion Rate Ranking issue (MF3.12.10), or genuine CPA-driven cost increase (MF3.12.12). **Confidence Level:** Moderate until value-tracking integrity is verified first. **Recommended Fixes:** Audit value tracking before any Bid Strategy/Budget response. **Priority:** Critical. **Recovery Time:** Immediate once a measurement-integrity cause is corrected; otherwise follows the underlying CVR/CPA cause's timeline.

### MF3.12.12 CPA Increase

- Fully treated at MF2.10.2; cross-referenced here at Ad-Set grain. **Symptoms:** Cost per Action (MF1.4.5) rising. **Metrics:** CPA decomposed into CPM, CTR, and CVR constituents. **Root Causes:** Auction Competition (CPM-driven), Creative Fatigue/Audience Saturation (CTR-driven), or landing-page/offer issue (CVR-driven). **Confidence Level:** High once decomposed. **Recommended Fixes:** Match to the specific decomposed cause (MF3.12.6, MF3.12.8, or MF3.12.10). **Priority:** High. **Recovery Time:** Inherits the recovery time of the decomposed root cause.

### MF3.12.13 Budget Restriction

- **Symptoms:** Ad Set fails to spend its full configured Budget; Delivery Status shows Limited (MF3.9.8).
- **Metrics:** Actual Spend versus configured Daily/Lifetime Budget (MF3.7.2–3); Bid Strategy configuration cross-check.
- **Root Causes:** Bid Strategy (Cost Cap/Bid Cap, MF2.6.2–3) too restrictive relative to current Auction conditions, preventing full Budget utilization even though Budget itself is not the binding constraint.
- **Confidence Level:** High once Budget-vs-Spend and Bid Strategy are jointly reviewed (MF3.7.6).
- **Recommended Fixes:** Relax the Bid Strategy (move toward Cost Cap or Highest Volume, MF2.6) rather than increasing Budget, which would not resolve a Bid-driven restriction.
- **Priority:** Moderate-to-High, since unspent Budget represents a missed-opportunity cost.
- **Recovery Time:** Fast — often visible within days of the Bid Strategy adjustment.

### MF3.12.14 Bid Restriction

- **Symptoms:** Severely constrained or halted Delivery specifically under a Bid Cap (MF2.6.3) configuration.
- **Metrics:** Auction win-rate proxy signals (inferred from Delivery volume relative to Reach/Audience size); Bid Cap value versus current Auction Competition Benchmarks.
- **Root Causes:** A Bid Cap set below the level needed to competitively win Auctions under current conditions — the single most common cause of severe, hard-to-diagnose Delivery restriction per MF2.6.3's Risk Level assessment.
- **Confidence Level:** High once a Bid Cap is confirmed in use and current Auction cost data is reviewed.
- **Recommended Fixes:** Raise the Bid Cap toward current competitive levels, or migrate to Cost Cap (MF2.6.2) for a less brittle, average-based constraint.
- **Priority:** High — this is the highest-expertise-requirement, least-forgiving configuration in the entire Bid Strategy catalog (MF2.6.3).
- **Recovery Time:** Fast once recalibrated, typically visible within the next Learning Phase cycle.

### MF3.12.15 Auction Competition

- Fully treated at MF2.10.9 and MF3.9.10; cross-referenced here. **Symptoms:** CPM/CPA rising with no internal configuration change. **Metrics:** CPM/CPA trend against historical same-period Benchmarks (MF1.11.5). **Root Causes:** Seasonal or competitive demand increase; alternatively, a relative Ranking-signal decline (MF1.3.44–46). **Confidence Level:** Moderate, requiring Benchmark cross-check to distinguish external from internal cause. **Recommended Fixes:** Adjust expectations if genuinely external/seasonal; investigate and remediate Ranking signals if not. **Priority:** Moderate, escalating if breakeven thresholds are threatened. **Recovery Time:** External causes resolve on their own timeline; Ranking-driven causes follow the standard Creative Refresh/landing-page-fix timeline.

### MF3.12.16 Diagnostics Summary Table

| Issue | Typical Priority | Typical Recovery Time |
|---|---|---|
| Audience Too Small | High | ~1 Learning Phase cycle |
| Audience Too Broad | Moderate | 1–2 Learning Phase cycles |
| Audience Overlap | Moderate-High | Immediate–1 cycle |
| Learning Limited | High | 1 cycle per sequential fix |
| No Delivery | Critical | Immediate |
| High CPM | Moderate–High | Variable |
| High Frequency | Moderate-High | ~1 cycle |
| Low CTR | Moderate-High | ~1 cycle |
| High CPC | Inherits root cause | Inherits root cause |
| Poor CVR | High | Variable |
| Poor ROAS | Critical | Immediate–variable |
| CPA Increase | High | Inherits root cause |
| Budget Restriction | Moderate-High | Fast |
| Bid Restriction | High | Fast |
| Auction Competition | Moderate | Variable |

---

## MF3.13 Scaling Framework

Extends MF2.9's Campaign-layer Scaling Framework with Ad-Set-specific mechanics — the levers below are the concrete, Ad-Set-configuration-level actions that Campaign-layer Vertical/Horizontal Scaling decisions are actually implemented through.

### MF3.13.1 Horizontal Scaling (Ad-Set-Layer Mechanics)

Implemented by creating new Ad Sets — via Duplication (MF2.9.3) or fresh configuration — targeting adjacent Audience segments, Placements, or a variant Optimization Event, within the same or a new Campaign. Ad-Set-layer guidance: verify Audience differentiation (MF3.3.9) before launching, and expect the new Ad Set to require its own full Learning Phase (MF3.10.7's Scaling Effects).

### MF3.13.2 Vertical Scaling (Ad-Set-Layer Mechanics)

Implemented by increasing an existing, stabilized Ad Set's Budget (MF3.7) incrementally (MF2.9.5's 15–20% guidance). Ad-Set-layer guidance: confirm Optimization Stability (MF3.10.6), not merely Learning Exit, before the first increase, and allow a full Required Events (MF3.10.5) observation window between successive increases.

### MF3.13.3 Audience Expansion (Scaling Context)

Cross-referenced from MF3.3.5 — in a Scaling context specifically, Audience Expansion is applied proactively, ahead of a Budget increase, to ensure the Audience pool is large enough to absorb the additional Delivery volume the Budget increase will attempt to generate; applying a Budget increase to an unexpanded, already-near-saturated Audience risks immediate Frequency spikes rather than genuine incremental Reach.

### MF3.13.4 Lookalike Expansion

- **Definition:** Specifically widening a Lookalike Audience's (MF1.8.3) similarity percentage (e.g., moving from 1% to 2–3%) as a Scaling lever.
- **Role in Scaling:** A more measured alternative to full Advantage+ Audience conversion, useful when the advertiser wants continued source-Audience-anchored targeting but a larger eligible pool.
- **Common Mistakes:** Jumping directly from a 1% to a 10% Lookalike in one step — a large single jump both constitutes a significant edit (MF3.10.4) and meaningfully changes targeting precision, better done incrementally.

### MF3.13.5 Budget Scaling (Ad-Set-Layer Mechanics)

The direct implementation of Vertical Scaling (MF3.13.2) via the Budget Control Framework (MF3.7) — apply MF3.7.9's Budget Decision Rules and MF2.9.5's incremental discipline together whenever a Scaling-driven Budget change is being made.

### MF3.13.6 Placement Expansion

- **Definition:** Broadening an Ad Set's Placement configuration — moving from Manual Placements toward Advantage+ Placements (MF3.4.1–2), or adding previously-excluded surfaces back in — as a Scaling lever distinct from Audience or Budget changes.
- **Role in Scaling:** Addresses Budget Saturation (MF2.10.8) specifically when the binding constraint is eligible-impression-pool size rather than Audience size — broadening Placement increases available inventory without touching who is eligible to be shown the Ad.
- **Common Mistakes:** Defaulting to Audience Expansion for every Budget Saturation symptom without checking whether Placement restriction is the actual binding constraint (MF2.10.8's Decision Tree explicitly branches on this).

### MF3.13.7 Optimization Changes (Scaling Context)

- **Definition:** Migrating an Ad Set's Optimization Event as part of a Scaling strategy — e.g., moving from Link Clicks to Landing Page Views once sufficient Pixel data exists, or from Conversions (count) to Value optimization (MF3.5.12) once sufficient Purchase-value history exists.
- **Role in Scaling:** Represents a maturity-driven Scaling dimension distinct from Audience/Budget/Placement — scaling the *precision* of what is being optimized for, not just the *volume*.
- **Common Mistakes:** Migrating to a more data-hungry Optimization Event (e.g., Value optimization) before adequate historical volume exists to support its own Learning Phase, effectively downgrading a stable, scaled Ad Set back into an unstable one.

### MF3.13.8 Scaling Risks (Ad-Set-Layer Consolidated)

| Risk | Cause | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Learning Reset mid-scale | Any Scaling lever applied as a single large edit | MF3.10.4, MF2.9.5 |
| Frequency spike from Vertical Scaling | Budget increased without prior Audience Expansion | MF3.13.3 |
| New Ad Set cannibalizing original via Horizontal Scaling | Insufficient Audience differentiation in Duplication | MF3.13.1, MF1.8.7 |
| Premature Optimization Event migration | Insufficient historical volume for the new, more data-hungry Event | MF3.13.7 |
| Masked root cause from bundled changes | Multiple Scaling levers (Budget + Audience + Placement) changed simultaneously | MF2.9.6 rule 3 |

### MF3.13.9 Scaling Decision Trees

```
Is the current binding constraint on further growth Audience size, Placement/inventory, or Budget itself?
 ├─ Audience-bound (Frequency high, Reach plateaued) → Audience/Lookalike Expansion (MF3.13.3-4) first, then Vertical Scaling
 ├─ Placement/inventory-bound (Budget Saturation despite adequate Audience) → Placement Expansion (MF3.13.6)
 └─ Genuinely Budget-bound (Audience and Placement both have headroom) → Vertical Scaling (MF3.13.2), incremental

Is the Ad Set's current Optimization Event mature enough (sufficient historical volume) for the next-precision Event?
 ├─ Yes → Optimization Changes (MF3.13.7) — migrate deliberately, expect a fresh Learning cycle
 └─ No  → Continue accumulating volume at the current Optimization Event before migrating
```

---

## MF3.14 AI Decision Framework

AI-consumable decision rules for the most common Ad-Set-layer situations, specializing the Generic Framework Series' Decision concept (`F4.5`) exactly as MF2.12 does at Campaign grain, applied here at native Ad Set grain with Audience/Placement/Bid Strategy-specific content MF2.12 does not cover.

### MF3.14.1 Rule: Bid Cap Severely Restricting Delivery

- **Observed Metrics:** Delivery Status "Limited"; Spend far below configured Budget; Bid Cap Bid Strategy (MF2.6.3) in use.
- **Likely Cause:** Bid Restriction (MF3.12.14) — Bid Cap set below competitive Auction levels.
- **Confidence Level:** High.
- **Recommended Action:** Raise the Bid Cap toward current Auction Benchmarks, or migrate to Cost Cap (MF2.6.2).
- **Priority:** High.
- **Expected Outcome:** Delivery volume increases toward full Budget utilization within days.
- **Risk:** Raising the Bid Cap too aggressively in one step could raise realized CPA faster than expected — adjust incrementally and monitor.

### MF3.14.2 Rule: Unspent Budget Despite Adequate Audience Size

- **Observed Metrics:** Spend below configured Daily/Lifetime Budget; Audience size confirmed adequate (no Learning Limited signal); Cost Cap Bid Strategy in use.
- **Likely Cause:** Budget Restriction (MF3.12.13) from an overly conservative Cost Cap.
- **Confidence Level:** High, once Audience adequacy is confirmed to rule out MF3.12.1.
- **Recommended Action:** Relax the Cost Cap or migrate to Highest Volume (MF2.6.1).
- **Priority:** Moderate-to-High.
- **Expected Outcome:** Increased Budget utilization and Delivery volume, with average cost per Event likely rising somewhat as the constraint loosens.
- **Risk:** Fully removing cost control (Highest Volume) without any ceiling may raise average cost beyond acceptable levels for cost-sensitive Objectives — prefer a relaxed Cost Cap over full removal where Financial KPIs (MF3.11.6) are tightly constrained.

### MF3.14.3 Rule: Sibling Ad Sets Showing Mutual Delivery Decline

- **Observed Metrics:** Two or more Ad Sets in the same Campaign, sharing similar Custom/Lookalike sources, both showing declining efficiency since the second was launched.
- **Likely Cause:** Audience Overlap (MF3.12.3).
- **Confidence Level:** High once confirmed via Meta's Overlap reporting tool.
- **Recommended Action:** Apply Exclusions between the Ad Sets, or consolidate under Campaign Budget (MF1.3.22) to let dynamic allocation arbitrate.
- **Priority:** Moderate-to-High under Ad Set Budget; Low-Moderate under Campaign Budget (the system already partially self-corrects).
- **Expected Outcome:** Combined efficiency improves as self-competition is removed.
- **Risk:** Over-applying Exclusions can shrink both Ad Sets' eligible pools enough to risk Learning Limited (MF3.12.1) — balance against that risk.

### MF3.14.4 Rule: Strong Delivery Efficiency, Placement Configuration Still Manual

- **Observed Metrics:** Healthy Primary KPI (MF3.11.1); Placement Breakdown shows Manual Placements (MF3.4.1) in use with no documented brand-safety justification on file.
- **Likely Cause:** A legacy or default Manual Placement configuration never revisited (MF3.4.1's Common Mistakes).
- **Confidence Level:** Moderate — requires confirming no genuine, current justification exists before recommending a change.
- **Recommended Action:** Test migration to Advantage+ Placements (MF3.4.2), monitoring for any efficiency or brand-safety regression.
- **Priority:** Low-Moderate — an efficiency opportunity, not a failure state.
- **Expected Outcome:** Likely CPM reduction and improved Learning Phase resilience given expanded inventory.
- **Risk:** If the Manual restriction was in fact justified (e.g., a genuine brand-safety requirement not documented), migrating could introduce a real, if undocumented, business risk — verify with the Ad Set owner before applying.

### MF3.14.5 Rule: Video Optimization Event with Weak Early Watch Percentage

- **Observed Metrics:** ThruPlay Rate (MF1.4.9) low; steep drop-off between 25% and 50% watch-percentage breakpoints.
- **Likely Cause:** Creative hook weakness in the video's first few seconds, per MF3.4.7's Reels/video optimization rules.
- **Confidence Level:** High, given the specific early-drop-off pattern.
- **Recommended Action:** Creative Refresh (MF1.10.9) specifically targeting the opening hook, rather than a full Creative replacement.
- **Priority:** Moderate.
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved ThruPlay Rate and downstream Video Views Objective efficiency (MF3.5.6) within one Learning Phase cycle.
- **Risk:** A Creative change constitutes a significant edit (MF3.10.4) — expect a temporary Learning Reset.

### MF3.14.6 Rule: Ad Set Approaching Budget Saturation During Vertical Scaling

- **Observed Metrics:** Marginal Delivery growth declining across two or more successive Budget increases; Audience Overlap ruled out; Frequency within healthy range.
- **Likely Cause:** Budget Saturation (MF2.10.8) from Placement/inventory exhaustion rather than Audience exhaustion.
- **Confidence Level:** High once Audience health is confirmed adequate via MF3.13.9's Decision Tree.
- **Recommended Action:** Shift to Placement Expansion (MF3.13.6) or Horizontal Scaling (MF3.13.1) rather than continuing Vertical Scaling.
- **Priority:** Moderate.
- **Expected Outcome:** Renewed proportional Delivery growth once new inventory or Audience capacity is introduced.
- **Risk:** Horizontal Scaling via Duplication risks Audience Overlap if not adequately differentiated (MF3.13.8).

### MF3.14.7 Rule: Calls Optimization Event Delivering Outside Business Hours

- **Observed Metrics:** Call initiation volume recorded outside the Business's documented staffed hours; no Business Hours Dayparting (MF3.8.5) configured.
- **Likely Cause:** Missing Call Campaign Scheduling constraint (MF3.8.6).
- **Confidence Level:** High — directly verifiable from Schedule configuration and call-timestamp data.
- **Recommended Action:** Apply Business Hours Dayparting aligned to actual staffed hours, with a closing-time buffer (MF3.8.6's Common Mistakes).
- **Priority:** High — unanswered calls represent both wasted Spend and a poor prospective-customer experience.
- **Expected Outcome:** Call initiation volume concentrates within staffed hours; effective cost per answered call improves.
- **Risk:** Overly restrictive Dayparting could meaningfully reduce total eligible Delivery hours, slowing Learning Phase (MF3.8.9) — balance staffing alignment against Learning volume needs.

---

## MF3.15 Governance Framework

Extends MF2.13's Campaign Governance and MF1.13.2's Ad Set Naming standard with Ad-Set-layer-specific governance, using the same domain-content-governance framing established at MF2.13's own opening disambiguation (distinct from EIFS/MAIFS's document-governance meaning).

### MF3.15.1 Naming Standards (Cross-Reference)

Fully defined at MF1.13.2. Ad Set Governance requires this standard be enforced at configuration time — an Ad Set name failing the standard (missing Audience type/source or Optimization Goal encoding) should block the Approval Workflow (MF3.15.4), consistent with MF2.13.1's equivalent Campaign-layer enforcement requirement.

### MF3.15.2 Documentation

Every Ad Set should carry, at minimum: its Audience configuration rationale (source, type, and any Signal supplied, MF3.3), its Placement configuration rationale (Manual vs. Advantage+, and if Manual, the specific justification, MF3.4.1), its Optimization Event selection rationale (MF3.5), its current Learning/Delivery status (MF3.9.7/MF3.10), and a reference to its most recent Diagnostics Framework (MF3.12) finding if one is active. This is the Ad-Set-layer specialization of MF2.13.5's Campaign Documentation requirement.

### MF3.15.3 Version Control

Every Ad-Set-level configuration change constituting or risking a significant edit (Audience, Placement, Optimization Goal, Bid Strategy, Budget mode) should be logged with timestamp, changed-by, prior value, new value, and rationale — extending MF2.13.2's Campaign-layer Version Control discipline to the layer where Learning Reset (MF3.10.4) actually occurs, making this log the primary tool for MF3.10.4's "expected vs. unexpected reset" diagnosis.

### MF3.15.4 Approval Rules (Approval Workflow)

Ad-Set-level changes exceeding the Business's defined "significant" threshold — a Bid Strategy change to/from Bid Cap, a Budget change exceeding the incremental Scaling guidance (MF2.9.5/MF3.13.2), or an Audience/Placement change materially widening or narrowing eligibility — should pass through the same Proposed → Reviewed → Approved → Applied → Monitored sequence established at MF2.13.4, evaluated here specifically against this Framework's own rules (MF3.3's Audience Decision Matrix, MF2.6.6's Bid Strategy Selection Summary, MF3.7.9's Budget Decision Rules).

### MF3.15.5 Configuration Standards

- Every new Ad Set should default toward Advantage+ Audience (MF3.3.4) and Advantage+ Placements (MF3.4.2) unless a specific, documented justification supports a more restrictive Manual configuration.
- Every Ad Set's Budget should be sized against its specific Optimization Event's typical cost (MF3.7.9 rule 1) before launch, not adjusted reactively only after Learning Limited is observed.
- Every Ad Set optimizing for Calls or Messaging should carry an explicit Business Hours Dayparting configuration (MF3.8.5) before launch.

### MF3.15.6 Quality Standards

- No Ad Set may launch without a documented Optimization Event selection rationale cross-checked against MF3.5's Optimization Selection Summary Table.
- No Ad Set may launch with an Audience/Placement combination pre-estimated (via historical Benchmark data, MF1.11.5) to fall below the Required Events threshold (MF3.10.5) for its configured Optimization Event.
- Every Ad Set's Attribution Setting (MF3.6.1) must be explicitly reviewed, not left at an unexamined default, before launch.

### MF3.15.7 Audit Checklist

- [ ] Naming complies with MF1.13.2/MF3.15.1.
- [ ] Documentation (MF3.15.2) is complete and current.
- [ ] Version Control log (MF3.15.3) is complete for every significant edit since the last audit.
- [ ] No Ad Set has been silently stuck in Learning Limited (MF3.10.2/MF3.12.4) beyond the Business's defined grace period.
- [ ] Any Manual Placement restriction (MF3.4.1) still in use carries a current, documented justification.
- [ ] Any Bid Cap (MF2.6.3) in use has been recalibrated against current Auction conditions within the Business's defined review cadence.
- [ ] Calls/Messaging-optimized Ad Sets carry a current Business Hours Dayparting configuration (MF3.8.5–6).

### MF3.15.8 Compliance Rules

- No Ad Set may be created without a documented Audience and Optimization Event rationale, enforced at the Approval Workflow's Reviewed stage (MF3.15.4).
- No Bid Strategy change to/from Bid Cap, and no Budget change exceeding the incremental Scaling threshold, may be applied without passing through the full Approval Workflow.
- No Ad Set may remain in Learning Limited (MF3.12.4) or severely Limited Delivery (MF3.9.8) past the Business's own defined maximum grace period without an active Recovery Strategy (MF3.10.11) applied and logged.
- Every Manual Placement restriction (MF3.4.1) must be re-justified at each Audit cycle (MF3.15.7) or reverted toward Advantage+ Placements (MF3.4.2).

---

## MF3.16 Knowledge Graph

A semantic graph connecting Ad-Set-layer concepts to each other and, by citation, back to MF1's domain vocabulary and MF2's Campaign-layer content — using the same relationship-type legend established at MF1.15.1 and applied at Campaign grain in MF2.14, applied here at native Ad Set grain.

### MF3.16.1 Structural & Configuration Edges

```
Ad Set --Contains--> Ad(s) [MF3.2.1]
Ad Set --Requires--> parent Campaign (Objective-bounded) [MF3.1.5, MF2.1.3]
Ad Set --Governs--> Audience, Placement, Optimization Goal, Bid Strategy, Budget, Schedule [MF3.2.1]
Audience (Core/Custom/Lookalike/Advantage+) --Governs--> Auction eligible population [MF3.3, MF1.3.18]
Placement (Manual/Advantage+) --Governs--> Auction eligible impression pool [MF3.4]
```

### MF3.16.2 Delivery & Auction Edges

```
Ad Set --Produces (via Auction Entry/Eligibility)--> Delivery [MF3.9.1-2, MF1.3.25]
Bid + Estimated Action Rate + Ad Quality --Produces--> Auction Total Value --Produces--> win/loss per impression [MF3.9.3-5]
Delivery --Diagnoses (via Ranking signals)--> Auction competitiveness [MF3.9.10, MF2.10.9]
Budget + Bid Strategy --Governs--> Delivery Status (Active/Learning/Limited/Not Delivering) [MF3.7.6, MF3.9.7]
```

### MF3.16.3 Learning & Diagnostic Edges

```
Significant edit (Audience/Placement/Optimization Goal/Bid Strategy/Creative) --Produces--> Learning Reset [MF3.10.4]
Optimization Event volume < Required Events --Diagnoses--> Learning Limited --Remedies via--> ordered Recovery Strategy [MF3.10.2, MF3.10.11]
Audience Too Small --Diagnoses (via low Reach)--> Learning Limited [MF3.12.1, MF3.12.4]
Rising Frequency + plateaued Reach --Diagnoses--> Audience Saturation --Remedies via--> Audience/Lookalike Expansion [MF3.12.7, MF3.13.3-4]
Declining CTR + stable Frequency --Diagnoses--> Creative Fatigue --Remedies via--> Creative Refresh [MF3.12.8]
Bid Cap below competitive level --Diagnoses--> Bid Restriction --Remedies via--> Bid Cap increase or Cost Cap migration [MF3.12.14, MF3.14.1]
```

### MF3.16.4 Scaling & Measurement Edges

```
Optimization Stability --Requires--> Learning Exit + observation period [MF3.10.6]
Vertical Scaling --Requires--> Optimization Stability, incremental Budget increase [MF3.13.2]
Horizontal Scaling --Requires--> Audience differentiation (no Overlap) [MF3.13.1, MF1.8.7]
Placement Expansion --Remedies--> Budget Saturation when inventory-bound [MF3.13.6, MF2.10.8]
Ad Set KPI Framework (Primary/Secondary/Efficiency/Audience/Delivery/Financial/Learning/Auction) --Specializes--> MF1.4 Metrics Dictionary [MF3.11]
Diagnostics Framework (MF3.12) --Requires--> KPI Framework (MF3.11) as its evidence base
AI Decision Framework (MF3.14) --Requires--> Diagnostics Framework findings as Observed Metrics input
```

### MF3.16.5 Graph Completeness Statement

Every section of this Framework (MF3.1–MF3.15, MF3.17) appears as a node or edge participant above: Ad Set Purpose and Architecture (MF3.1–2) as root structural nodes; Audience, Placement, Optimization, Attribution, Budget, Scheduling (MF3.3–8) as configuration/governance edges; Delivery and Learning (MF3.9–10) as production and state-transition edges; KPI, Diagnostics, Scaling, AI Decision, Governance (MF3.11–15) as the evidentiary and decision layer connecting back to every structural and state node — mirroring MF2.14's Campaign-layer graph structure at this Framework's own grain.

---

## MF3.17 Enterprise Best Practices

### MF3.17.1 Golden Rules

1. **Default toward system-driven breadth (Advantage+ Audience and Placements) unless a specific, documented reason justifies restriction** (MF3.3.4, MF3.4.2, MF3.15.5).
2. **Size Budget against the configured Optimization Event's actual typical cost, never an arbitrary figure** (MF3.7.9).
3. **Isolate variables in every deliberate Ad-Set-level change** — Audience, Placement, Optimization Goal, and Bid Strategy changes should never be bundled when diagnosability matters (MF3.10.11, MF2.9.6 rule 3).
4. **Diagnose before remedying** — match the Recommended Fix to the actually-confirmed root cause (MF3.12), never the most familiar lever.
5. **Respect Learning Phase as the precondition for every downstream decision** — no Scaling, no KPI trust, no Diagnostics conclusion is valid before Learning Exit and Optimization Stability (MF3.10.3, MF3.10.6).

### MF3.17.2 Optimization Rules

1. Select the Optimization Event that most precisely matches the true desired outcome, using MF3.5's Optimization Selection Summary Table, not the highest-volume option by default.
2. Review Budget and Bid Strategy jointly, never independently (MF3.7.6).
3. Apply Audience/Placement Expansion before Vertical Scaling when the binding constraint is population/inventory rather than Budget itself (MF3.13.9's Decision Tree).

### MF3.17.3 Anti-Patterns

- **The Narrow Trap:** Layering excessive Audience criteria (MF3.3.7) until the eligible population cannot support Learning Phase exit.
- **The Silent Bid Cap:** Setting a Bid Cap once and never recalibrating it as Auction conditions shift, quietly throttling Delivery over time (MF3.12.14).
- **The Overlap Blind Spot:** Launching sibling Ad Sets with near-identical Audience sources under Ad Set Budget without checking Overlap first (MF3.12.3).
- **The Unscheduled Call Campaign:** Optimizing for Calls or Messaging with no Business Hours Dayparting, generating unanswerable contact attempts (MF3.14.7).
- **The Premature Value Migration:** Moving to Value optimization (MF3.5.12) before sufficient Purchase-value history exists, destabilizing an otherwise-mature Ad Set (MF3.13.7's Common Mistakes).

### MF3.17.4 Ad Set Quality Checklist

- [ ] Audience configuration rationale is documented and matches MF3.3.14's Decision Matrix guidance.
- [ ] Placement configuration is Advantage+ by default, or carries a documented Manual justification.
- [ ] Optimization Event selection matches MF3.5's Selection Summary Table guidance for the parent Campaign's Objective.
- [ ] Attribution Setting has been explicitly reviewed, not left at an unexamined default (MF3.15.6).
- [ ] Budget is sized against the Optimization Event's typical cost (MF3.7.9).
- [ ] Bid Strategy and Budget have been reviewed jointly (MF3.7.6).
- [ ] Schedule/Dayparting is configured appropriately for Calls/Messaging Optimization Events (MF3.8.5–6).

### MF3.17.5 Deployment Checklist

- [ ] Naming complies with MF1.13.2/MF3.15.1.
- [ ] Pre-launch Required Events feasibility check completed against historical Benchmark data (MF3.15.6).
- [ ] Documentation (MF3.15.2) is complete before Approval Workflow submission.
- [ ] No un-reviewed default settings remain (Attribution, Placement, Audience Signal quality).

### MF3.17.6 Audit Checklist (Cross-Reference)

Fully specified at MF3.15.7 — repeated here only as a pointer, per this Framework's own no-duplicate-content discipline (mirroring MF3.9.9's Delivery/Diagnostics cross-reference pattern).

### MF3.17.7 Operational Standards

1. Maintain a standing review cadence (aligned to the Campaign Lifecycle's Maintenance stage, MF2.7.6) for every Always On Ad Set (MF3.8.1), checking Frequency, Ranking signals, and Bid Cap calibration at each review.
2. Never apply more than one Scaling lever (Budget, Audience, Placement, Optimization Event) in a single action (MF3.13.8).
3. Treat every Recovery Strategy or Scaling action as a hypothesis with a stated Expected Outcome (MF3.14's format), monitored and recorded to build calibrated institutional knowledge over time, mirroring MF2.15.5's Campaign-layer Optimization Principle.

## Meta Framework 3 Closing Statement

**Meta Framework 3 — Ad Set Framework is now complete, validated, and frozen.** It establishes the definitive Ad-Set-layer reference — Ad Set Purpose and Architecture, a full Audience Configuration Framework, a complete per-Placement analysis (14 Placements/strategies), a complete per-Optimization-option analysis (14 options), Attribution, Budget Control, and Scheduling Frameworks, the Delivery and Learning Frameworks in full operational depth, an Ad Set KPI Framework, a complete per-issue Diagnostics Framework (15 issues), a Scaling Framework, AI Decision Framework rules, Governance, a Knowledge Graph, and Enterprise Best Practices — governed throughout by MAIFS, built entirely on Meta Framework 1 and Meta Framework 2's vocabulary by citation only, and without modifying the Enterprise Architecture Baseline (§0–§60), EIFS, the Generic Enterprise Intelligence Framework Series (Frameworks 1–10), MAIFS, Meta Framework 1, or Meta Framework 2. Any future change to Meta Framework 3 proceeds only through the Revision mechanism established at EIFS.7 and specialized for Meta Ads Frameworks at MAIFS.11.

# Meta Framework 4 — Creative Framework

**Status:** Complete, Validated, and Frozen. All 18 planned sections (MF4.1–MF4.18), plus the MF4.19 Final Validation Checklist and Closing Statement, are present in full per MAIFS.8's lifecycle policy. Any future change proceeds only through the Revision mechanism (EIFS.7, MAIFS.11).
**Audience:** Enterprise advertisers, creative directors, performance marketers, media buyers, brand teams, agencies, AI systems, creative analysts, marketing scientists.
**Scope:** This Framework governs the Creative layer (MF1.3.17/MF1.3.37) only — Creative Architecture, Types, Components, Psychology, Hooks, CTAs, Quality, Testing, KPIs, Video/Image production, Fatigue, Diagnostics, Optimization, AI Decision rules, Governance, and Best Practices. It does not explain Campaign creation (MF2), Audience creation (MF3.3), Budget setup (MF2.5/MF3.7), or Reporting in depth (reserved for a future Reporting Framework).
**Vocabulary Rule:** No Core Domain Object, Metric, Objective, Placement, or Optimization Event term already defined in Meta Frameworks 1–3 is redefined here. Every such term is cited as `MF1.x`/`MF2.x`/`MF3.x` and extended with Creative-layer content neither prior Framework contains, per this document's Meta Framework 4 Vocabulary Rule stated in the banner above and per MAIFS.19.

---

## MF4.1 Creative Architecture

### MF4.1.1 What Is a Meta Creative

A Meta Creative (MF1.3.17) is the complete visual, textual, and interactive content package rendered to a user as an Ad (MF1.3.24) — combining one or more Creative Assets (MF1.3.37), Headline, Primary Text, Description, and a Call to Action (MF1.9.3–5) into the single unit a user actually experiences. Where the Campaign (MF2) commits to *what business outcome* is pursued and the Ad Set (MF3) commits to *who/where/when* an Ad may be shown, the Creative is the unit that determines *whether a specific user, once eligible to see it, actually responds* — it is the final and, in aggregate Auction terms (MF1.3.18, MF3.9.3–5), often the single highest-leverage layer for influencing Estimated Action Rate and Ad Quality among the three.

### MF4.1.2 Creative Layer

The Creative layer sits beneath the Ad (MF1.3.24), which itself sits beneath the Ad Set (MF1.3.14) beneath the Campaign (MF1.3.11) — the bottom of the Primary Structural Chain (MF1.2.1). Unlike the Campaign and Ad Set layers, which are configuration containers, the Creative layer is content: it is authored, tested, refreshed, and retired on its own cadence, often faster than the Ad Set configuration that hosts it, per MF1.9.12's Creative Fatigue and MF3.10.10's Creative Effects on Learning.

### MF4.1.3 Creative Components

A Creative decomposes into Media (MF1.3.37's Creative Asset), Headline, Primary Text, Description, and Call to Action (MF1.9.3–5) at minimum, extended for specific formats (MF4.2) with additional components (e.g., Carousel cards, Instant Experience blocks). The full, individually-analyzed component catalog — including psychological and production-level components MF1 never catalogued (Hook, Offer, Trust Elements, Social Proof, Branding, and 20+ others) — is delivered in MF4.3, per this Framework's own required structure.

### MF4.1.4 Creative Lifecycle

```
Ideation → Production → Quality Assurance (MF4.17.5) → Approval (MF4.17.4) → Launch → Testing (MF4.8) → Learning (MF3.10, at the Ad level) → Active Delivery → Fatigue Monitoring (MF4.12) → Refresh or Retirement (MF4.14.1) → Archival
```
This mirrors the Campaign Lifecycle's (MF2.7) and Ad Set Lifecycle's (MF3.2.4) stage-based structure, applied here at Creative grain — the fastest-cycling lifecycle of the three layers, since Creative Fatigue (MF1.9.12) typically forces iteration well before a Campaign or Ad Set's own lifecycle would otherwise demand it.

### MF4.1.5 Creative Governance (Overview)

Every Creative is subject to naming, version-control, approval, QA, and brand-consistency rules, given full treatment in MF4.17. This entry establishes only that Creative Architecture and Creative Governance are related but distinct concerns, mirroring the same distinction drawn between Architecture and Governance at both the Campaign (MF2.2/MF2.13) and Ad Set (MF3.2/MF3.15) layers.

### MF4.1.6 Creative Production Pipeline

```
Brief (business goal, Objective, Audience context cited from MF2/MF3, never re-derived here) 
 → Concept (Hook + Offer + Value Proposition selection, MF4.5/MF4.3)
   → Asset Production (video/image capture or design, MF4.10/MF4.11)
     → Copywriting (Headline/Primary Text/Description/CTA, MF4.3/MF4.6)
       → Assembly (format-specific packaging, MF4.2)
         → QA (MF4.17.5) → Approval (MF4.17.4) → Publish
```

### MF4.1.7 Creative Decision Flow

```
Is the Creative addressing a diagnosed issue (MF4.13) or a new concept/test (MF4.8)?
 ├─ Diagnosed issue → route to MF4.13's specific issue entry → apply its Recommended Solution
 └─ New concept/test → select Format (MF4.2) + Hook (MF4.5) + CTA (MF4.6) per the parent Ad Set's Optimization Event (MF3.5) → produce → test (MF4.8)
```

### MF4.1.8 Creative Testing Flow

```
Define test hypothesis (single-variable, per MF4.8.5 Variable Control) → produce variant(s) → launch under Creative Isolation (MF4.8.4) or Dynamic Creative (MF1.9.11) →
  accumulate data → reach Statistical Confidence (MF4.8.6/MF1.11.7) → select winner (MF4.8.8) → retire loser(s) (MF4.8.9) → log outcome (MF4.17.3)
```

### MF4.1.9 Creative Optimization Flow

```
Monitor Creative KPIs (MF4.9) → Diagnostics Framework (MF4.13) identifies root cause if underperforming →
  apply targeted Optimization action (MF4.14: Refresh, Iteration, Hook Replacement, CTA/Headline/Copy/Offer/Visual/Video Optimization) →
    re-test (MF4.8) → monitor recovery against Expected Outcome (MF4.15's AI Decision format)
```

---

## MF4.2 Creative Types Framework

Every Meta Creative format, each analyzed for: Business Purpose, Strengths, Weaknesses, Ideal Objectives, Best Placements, Technical Requirements, Typical Performance, Common Mistakes. Placement citations reference MF3.4's already-established Placement analysis; this section does not re-derive Placement-level delivery characteristics, only Creative-format fit to them.

### MF4.2.1 Single Image

- **Business Purpose:** The simplest, fastest-to-produce Creative format, communicating a single visual message and offer.
- **Strengths:** Low production cost and time; easy to iterate rapidly; works reliably across nearly every Placement.
- **Weaknesses:** Limited storytelling capacity relative to video; more susceptible to being scrolled past without a strong visual Hook (MF4.5).
- **Ideal Objectives:** All Objectives (MF1.5), particularly effective for Sales/Catalog-driven Creative with a clear product focus.
- **Best Placements:** Feed (MF3.4.9), Profile (MF3.4.13), Explore (MF3.4.12).
- **Technical Requirements:** High-resolution source image; format-appropriate aspect ratio per Placement (square/portrait for Feed; vertical for Stories/Reels, though a static image is a weaker fit there per MF4.2.7/4.2.8).
- **Typical Performance:** Lower average CTR than video formats for Awareness/Engagement Objectives; comparable or stronger CVR for Sales when the product itself is highly visual and self-explanatory.
- **Common Mistakes:** Using a single low-production-value image across all Placements without format-specific cropping (MF1.9's Common Mistakes), degrading Quality Ranking (MF1.3.44) on vertical-format Placements.

### MF4.2.2 Single Video

- **Business Purpose:** Communicates a fuller narrative or demonstration than a static image allows, within a single self-contained Ad.
- **Strengths:** Strong for storytelling, product demonstration, and emotional engagement (MF4.4); supports ThruPlay/Video Views Optimization Events (MF3.5.6).
- **Weaknesses:** Higher production cost and time; performance highly sensitive to Hook strength in the first 3 seconds (MF4.10.6); more susceptible to Creative Fatigue if not refreshed given higher production investment discouraging frequent iteration.
- **Ideal Objectives:** Awareness (Video Views sub-type), Engagement, Sales (demonstration-dependent products).
- **Best Placements:** Reels (MF3.4.7), Stories (MF3.4.8), In-Stream (MF3.4.10), Video Feeds (MF3.4.14), Feed.
- **Technical Requirements:** Placement-appropriate aspect ratio (9:16 for Reels/Stories, 1:1/4:5/16:9 for Feed); captions recommended given sound-off default viewing norms (MF4.10.9).
- **Typical Performance:** Strong ThruPlay Rate and watch-percentage Metrics (MF1.4.9) when Hook and pacing are well-executed; CTR/CVR performance highly variable by execution quality.
- **Common Mistakes:** Front-loading brand/logo before the Hook, causing early drop-off (MF1.4.9's Warning Range pattern) before the actual message is delivered.

### MF4.2.3 Carousel

- See MF1.9.6 for the base Creative-format definition. **Business Purpose:** Show multiple products, features, or sequential narrative steps within one Ad. **Strengths:** Higher engagement surface area than a single image/video; well-suited to product-line or multi-benefit storytelling. **Weaknesses:** Later cards receive systematically less attention (MF1.9.6's Common Mistakes); more production effort than a single asset. **Ideal Objectives:** Sales (multi-product), Traffic (sequential narrative). **Best Placements:** Feed, Explore. **Technical Requirements:** Consistent aspect ratio across all cards; each card requires its own headline/destination link. **Typical Performance:** Often stronger CTR than single image for multi-product catalogs given increased content surface. **Common Mistakes:** Placing the weakest product/message first, undermining the format's strongest-first sequencing principle (MF1.9.6's Professional Notes).

### MF4.2.4 Collection

- See MF1.9.7 for the base Creative-format definition. **Business Purpose:** Bridge a primary discovery visual with an immersive, Catalog-driven (MF1.3.35) shopping experience. **Strengths:** Combines storytelling (primary video/image) with product browsing depth (Instant Experience-powered grid) in one Ad. **Weaknesses:** Requires a connected Catalog; mobile-only, full-screen-dependent experience. **Ideal Objectives:** Sales (Catalog Sales sub-type, MF3.5.13). **Best Placements:** Mobile Feed-based Placements. **Technical Requirements:** Connected Catalog and Product Set (MF1.3.36); primary video/image plus product grid assets. **Typical Performance:** Strong for Businesses with a browsable, multi-SKU Catalog; weak fit for single-product Businesses. **Common Mistakes:** Populating the product grid with a stale or thematically mismatched Product Set relative to the primary visual (MF1.9.7's Common Mistakes).

### MF4.2.5 Instant Experience

- **Business Purpose:** A full-screen, immersive, app-like experience opening after a user taps an Ad, combining multiple content blocks (video, image, Carousel, text) into one expanded post-click canvas.
- **Strengths:** Highest content depth of any Meta Creative format; strong for storytelling-heavy or multi-product browsing needs without leaving the platform.
- **Weaknesses:** Highest production complexity; mobile-only; requires the user to tap through, adding a friction step versus a direct-to-destination format.
- **Ideal Objectives:** Sales, Leads, Traffic (deep-engagement variants).
- **Best Placements:** Feed, Collection (MF4.2.4, as its expansion canvas), Instant Forms (MF1.3.34, as an alternative expansion target).
- **Technical Requirements:** Multiple content blocks authored specifically for the Instant Experience canvas, distinct from standard single-asset Creative.
- **Typical Performance:** Strong engagement depth (time spent) but requires careful measurement of the tap-through step itself as an additional funnel stage.
- **Common Mistakes:** Building an Instant Experience so content-heavy that the core offer/CTA is buried several screens deep, losing users before they reach the actual conversion action.

### MF4.2.6 Stories (Creative Format)

- Placement-level treatment at MF3.4.8. **Business Purpose (Creative-format view):** Full-screen, vertical, ephemeral-feeling Creative designed for fast, immersive consumption. **Strengths:** High immersion; supports sequential/narrative Creative concepts across multiple Stories cards. **Weaknesses:** No interactive overlay elements available to standard Ads, unlike organic Stories (MF1.9.9's Common Mistakes); fast consumption leaves little room for lengthy copy. **Ideal Objectives:** Awareness, Engagement, Traffic. **Best Placements:** Stories specifically (by definition). **Technical Requirements:** 9:16 vertical, format-native production (not repurposed Feed assets). **Typical Performance:** Strong immersion-driven attention; CTR variable by CTA/destination clarity within the fast-consumption format. **Common Mistakes:** Treating Stories and Reels Creative as interchangeable despite differing surrounding organic context (MF1.9.9's Common Mistakes).

### MF4.2.7 Reels (Creative Format)

- Placement-level treatment at MF3.4.7. **Business Purpose (Creative-format view):** Short-form, hook-driven, sound-on-by-default vertical video Creative. **Strengths:** Large, growing inventory; strong native-feeling engagement when well-executed. **Weaknesses:** Requires purpose-built vertical video; underperforms badly with repurposed Feed assets (MF1.9.8's Common Mistakes). **Ideal Objectives:** Awareness (Video Views), Engagement. **Best Placements:** Reels specifically. **Technical Requirements:** 9:16 vertical, hook within the first 1–2 seconds (MF4.10.6). **Typical Performance:** Strong ThruPlay/watch-percentage Metrics with a well-executed hook; steep drop-off without one. **Common Mistakes:** Same Feed-asset-reuse mismatch flagged at MF1.9.8.

### MF4.2.8 Feed (Creative Format)

- Placement-level treatment at MF3.4.9. **Business Purpose (Creative-format view):** The most format-flexible Creative context, supporting single image/video, Carousel, or Collection. **Strengths:** Broadest format flexibility; most mature Benchmark data (MF1.11.5). **Weaknesses:** Most susceptible to Creative Fatigue given user familiarity with the format (MF1.9.10). **Ideal Objectives:** All. **Best Placements:** Feed specifically. **Technical Requirements:** Square (1:1) or portrait (4:5) as current standard. **Typical Performance:** Well-benchmarked across every Objective. **Common Mistakes:** Insufficient Creative Refresh cadence (MF1.10.9) given the format's fatigue susceptibility.

### MF4.2.9 Playable Ads

- **Business Purpose:** An interactive, demo-like Creative format allowing a user to briefly try an app or game experience directly within the Ad before installing.
- **Strengths:** Extremely high-intent signal — a user who plays through a Playable Ad and then installs is typically a higher-value, better-retained user than one acquired via a passive format.
- **Weaknesses:** High production complexity, specific to app/game categories; not applicable to non-app Objectives.
