Cross-referenced from MF5.7.14 — as a Funnel Audience stage specifically, Advocacy-stage Audiences are both a targeting destination (encouraging continued promotional behavior) and, per MF5.7.14's Audience/Targeting Implication, a valuable Lookalike Seed (MF5.5.1) source for reaching new, TOF-stage prospects who resemble the Business's most vocal existing supporters.

### MF5.8.11 Funnel Audience Progression Map

```
TOF (Core/Advantage+/Broad Lookalike) 
 → MOF (Engaged/Website-Intent/Tight Lookalike) 
   → BOF (Shopping Activity/Catalog) 
     → [Purchase] 
       → Retention → Upsell / Cross-sell (parallel, product-dependent)
                   → Win-back (if lapsed)
       → Loyalty → Referral → Advocacy → [feeds back into TOF via Lookalike Seed, MF5.5.1]
```
This progression map is the Audience-layer specialization of the Generic Framework Series' Sequencing relationship concept (Architecture §8, as specialized throughout this document lineage), showing how a single customer's Audience-segment membership evolves over their full relationship with the Business.

---

## MF5.9 Audience Segmentation Framework

The strategic frameworks for dividing a Business's total addressable Audience into actionable segments, synthesizing MF5.2's Types and MF5.3's targeting options into named segmentation strategies. Each is documented with: Definition, Construction Approach, Best Fit Use Case.

### MF5.9.1 Demographic Segmentation

- **Definition:** Dividing an Audience by Age, Gender, Income, Education, or Job Title (MF5.3.1–2/13–17).
- **Construction Approach:** Apply Core Audience Demographic criteria (MF5.3.13) directly, or segment an existing Custom Audience by available Demographic overlay data.
- **Best Fit Use Case:** Categories with genuine, validated Demographic-correlated purchase patterns (age-restricted products, gender-specific apparel, income-tier financial products).

### MF5.9.2 Behavioral Segmentation

- **Definition:** Dividing an Audience by observed or inferred actions — Purchase history, Website Event depth, Video watch percentage.
- **Construction Approach:** Segment Custom Audiences (MF5.4) by specific Event type/recency/depth rather than a single undifferentiated rule.
- **Best Fit Use Case:** Any Objective where actual demonstrated behavior is a stronger predictor than inferred characteristics — generally the highest-value segmentation approach when sufficient first-party data exists.

### MF5.9.3 Psychographic Segmentation

- **Definition:** Dividing an Audience by inferred values, interests, or lifestyle (Interests/Behaviors Detailed Targeting, MF5.3.11–12, or Identity/Motivation states, MF5.7.7/9).
- **Construction Approach:** Apply Interest/Behavior Core Audience criteria, or infer Psychographic alignment from engagement patterns with specific Creative themes.
- **Best Fit Use Case:** Lifestyle, fashion, hobbyist, and Identity-driven brand categories (MF4.4.15).

### MF5.9.4 Geographic Segmentation

- **Definition:** Dividing an Audience by Location criteria (Country/Region/City/Postal Code/Radius, MF5.3.4–9).
- **Construction Approach:** Apply Location-based Core Audience criteria, optionally combined with regionally-varying Creative localization (MF4.3.29's Subtitles/localization guidance).
- **Best Fit Use Case:** Multi-market Businesses with genuinely varying regional demand, regulatory requirements, or service-area constraints.

### MF5.9.5 Purchase Segmentation

- **Definition:** Dividing an existing-customer Audience by specific purchased product/category, distinct from general Behavioral Segmentation's broader Event-based approach.
- **Construction Approach:** Segment Customer List or Website Purchase Event data (MF1.6.8) by specific Product Set (MF1.3.36) purchased.
- **Best Fit Use Case:** Cross-sell (MF5.8.6) and Upsell (MF5.8.5) Funnel Audience stages specifically, where the exact prior purchase determines the most relevant next offer.

### MF5.9.6 Value Segmentation

- **Definition:** Dividing an Audience by economic value to the Business — lifetime value, average order value, or purchase frequency.
- **Construction Approach:** Segment Customer List data by attached value metadata; construct Value-Based Lookalikes (MF5.5.12) from the highest-value tier specifically.
- **Best Fit Use Case:** Prioritizing Retention/Loyalty (MF5.8.4/8) investment toward the highest-value existing customers, and seeding Lookalike prospecting from the same tier.

### MF5.9.7 Lifecycle Segmentation

- **Definition:** Dividing an Audience by their stage in the overall customer relationship — new prospect, active customer, lapsed customer, advocate — synthesizing the full Funnel Audience Progression Map (MF5.8.11).
- **Construction Approach:** Combine recency/frequency Behavioral signals with the Funnel Audience stage definitions (MF5.8.1–10) to classify each segment.
- **Best Fit Use Case:** Coordinating a full-funnel Audience strategy across many simultaneous Ad Sets, ensuring every customer relationship stage has an appropriately-targeted, non-overlapping segment.

### MF5.9.8 Intent Segmentation

- **Definition:** Dividing an Audience by demonstrated purchase-intent depth, synthesizing the Intent/Purchase Readiness/Decision Stage states (MF5.7.3–4/12) into actionable segment boundaries.
- **Construction Approach:** Segment Website/Shopping Activity Custom Audiences (MF5.4.1/14) by specific funnel-Event depth (View Content vs. Add To Cart vs. Initiate Checkout).
- **Best Fit Use Case:** Sales retargeting Ad Set structuring, where Bid Strategy (MF2.6) and Creative Urgency intensity (MF4.4.6) should scale with Intent depth.

### MF5.9.9 Segmentation Strategy Selection Rule

Combine segmentation approaches rather than relying on a single dimension exclusively — a mature Audience strategy typically layers Lifecycle Segmentation (MF5.9.7) as the primary organizing structure, with Value Segmentation (MF5.9.6) prioritizing investment within each Lifecycle stage, and Behavioral/Intent Segmentation (MF5.9.2/8) providing the specific targeting-rule granularity within each combined stage/value cell.

---

## MF5.10 Audience Overlap Framework

Extends MF1.8.7/MF3.3.9/MF3.12.3 (Audience Overlap) with complete diagnostic and remediation depth.

### MF5.10.1 Audience Overlap (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.8.7. This Framework's contribution is the complete diagnostic-to-remediation pipeline (MF5.10.4–8) the prior, briefer treatments at MF3.3.9/MF3.12.3 pointed toward but did not themselves fully develop.

### MF5.10.2 Internal Competition

- **Definition:** The specific Auction-level consequence of Audience Overlap — two or more of the Business's own Ad Sets competing against each other for the same impression opportunity, rather than against external competitors.
- **Role:** The mechanism by which Audience Overlap translates into actual wasted efficiency, distinct from Overlap being merely a structural/reporting curiosity.
- **Diagnostic Signal:** Unexpectedly high combined CPM across sibling Ad Sets despite no external Auction Competition (MF2.10.9) indication.

### MF5.10.3 Auction Cannibalization

- **Definition:** The outcome of sustained Internal Competition (MF5.10.2) — one or both overlapping Ad Sets showing degraded Delivery efficiency specifically attributable to the Business's own competing Ad Sets, rather than genuine external market competition.
- **Role:** The most severe practical consequence of unmanaged Audience Overlap, directly wasting Budget that would otherwise fund incremental Reach.
- **Diagnostic Signal:** Declining Delivery efficiency in one Ad Set that improves immediately upon pausing or excluding against a specific sibling Ad Set — the clearest possible confirmation of Cannibalization as distinct from external Auction Competition.

### MF5.10.4 Overlap Diagnostics

1. Use Meta's Audience Overlap reporting tool directly to quantify the overlap percentage between specific named Ad Set pairs.
2. Cross-check against Frequency (MF1.4.7) — genuinely overlapping Ad Sets often show elevated combined Frequency at the individual-user level even if each Ad Set's own reported Frequency looks moderate.
3. Run a controlled pause test (temporarily pausing one Ad Set) if Overlap reporting is ambiguous, observing whether the sibling Ad Set's efficiency improves — the direct confirmation method for suspected Cannibalization (MF5.10.3).

### MF5.10.5 Overlap Thresholds

- **Guidance:** There is no universal "acceptable" Overlap percentage — the threshold worth acting on depends on whether the Overlap is actually producing measurable Cannibalization (MF5.10.3), not the raw percentage alone.
- **Practical Application:** A high Overlap percentage between two Ad Sets under Campaign Budget (MF1.3.22) is often benign, since the system's own dynamic allocation already arbitrates between them; the same Overlap percentage under Ad Set Budget (MF1.3.23) is more likely to produce genuine, unmanaged Cannibalization, since no dynamic arbitration exists.
- **Common Mistake:** Treating Overlap percentage as an context-free red flag requiring action regardless of Budget mode or actual efficiency impact.

### MF5.10.6 Merge Strategy

- **Definition:** Consolidating two overlapping Ad Sets into a single Ad Set with a combined Audience definition, eliminating the overlap by eliminating the separate structures entirely.
- **When to Apply:** When the two overlapping Ad Sets serve no genuinely distinct strategic purpose (e.g., two nearly-identical Lookalike tiers from the same Seed, MF5.5.11's Common Mistake).
- **Risk:** Merging loses any distinct reporting/Bid Strategy granularity the separate Ad Sets provided — apply only when that granularity was not actually being used for a genuine strategic decision.

### MF5.10.7 Split Strategy

- **Definition:** The inverse of Merge (MF5.10.6) — deliberately dividing a single, overly broad Audience into genuinely distinct, non-overlapping segments across multiple Ad Sets.
- **When to Apply:** When a single broad Audience is masking meaningfully different sub-segment performance (per Breakdown analysis, MF1.3.39) that would benefit from differentiated Bid Strategy or Creative treatment.
- **Risk:** Over-splitting thins Learning Phase data per resulting Ad Set (MF3.10.5) — apply only when each resulting segment retains sufficient volume to support Learning Phase exit independently.

### MF5.10.8 Exclusion Strategy

- Extends MF3.3.8/MF1.8.6. **When to Apply:** The most common and lowest-disruption remediation for confirmed Cannibalization (MF5.10.3) between sibling Ad Sets, or between a prospecting Ad Set and an existing-customer segment (the Retargeting/prospecting separation pattern). **Method:** Apply the narrower or lower-priority Ad Set's Audience as an Exclusion on the broader or higher-priority sibling, rather than excluding bidirectionally, which can needlessly shrink both pools. **Risk:** Over-applying Exclusions across many sibling Ad Sets can cumulatively shrink each one's eligible pool enough to risk Learning Limited (MF3.12.1) — balance against that risk per MF3.3.8's own guidance.

### MF5.10.9 Overlap Remediation Decision Tree

```
Overlap confirmed via reporting tool or pause test (MF5.10.4)?
 ├─ No genuine Cannibalization detected → No action needed (MF5.10.5's threshold guidance)
 └─ Cannibalization confirmed
      ├─ Ad Sets serve no distinct strategic purpose → Merge (MF5.10.6)
      ├─ Ad Sets serve genuinely distinct purposes, one is clearly lower-priority → Exclusion (MF5.10.8)
      └─ A single broad Audience is masking valuable sub-segment differences → Split (MF5.10.7)
```

---

## MF5.11 Audience Saturation Framework

Extends MF1.8.8/MF3.3.10 (Audience Saturation) and MF4.12.5 (Audience Fatigue, the Creative-Fatigue-taxonomy's Audience-root-cause sub-type) with complete Audience-layer treatment.

### MF5.11.1 Audience Fatigue (cross-reference)

Fully defined as the diagnosis-of-last-resort sub-type at MF4.12.5, reached only after Frequency/Visual/Offer Fatigue (MF4.12.2–4) are individually ruled out. This Framework's contribution is the full Audience-construction-level treatment of why and how a specific Audience pool becomes genuinely exhausted, which MF4.12.5 cited but did not itself develop.

### MF5.11.2 Frequency (Saturation-Diagnostic View)

Cross-referenced from MF1.4.7/MF3.3.10. The primary leading indicator for Audience Saturation across every layer of this document lineage — rising Frequency at a plateaued Reach is the consistent signature this Framework, MF3, and MF4 all key off of, applied here at the Audience-construction-strategy level specifically (i.e., which Audience Type/Segmentation choice is responsible, not merely that Frequency is rising).

### MF5.11.3 Reach Exhaustion

- **Definition:** The state in which an Audience's total addressable population (given current Location/Detailed Targeting/Custom Audience/Lookalike-tier boundaries) has been effectively fully reached, such that further Delivery necessarily means re-showing existing reached users rather than finding new ones.
- **Diagnostic Signal:** Reach growth rate approaching zero despite continued or increased Budget, the specific pattern distinguishing Reach Exhaustion from a merely slowing-but-still-growing Reach trajectory.
- **Relationship to Other Concepts:** The root structural cause underlying Frequency Fatigue (MF4.12.2) at the Creative layer and Budget Saturation (MF2.10.8) at the Campaign layer — Reach Exhaustion is the Audience-construction-level explanation for why both of those symptoms occur.

### MF5.11.4 Delivery Slowdown

- **Definition:** A decline in the rate of new Impressions/Reach accrual for a given Ad Set, observable before full Reach Exhaustion (MF5.11.3) is reached — an early-warning signal rather than the terminal state itself.
- **Diagnostic Signal:** A decelerating (but not yet zero) Reach growth rate trend over successive periods.
- **Relationship to Other Concepts:** The appropriate trigger point for proactive Audience Refresh/Expansion (MF5.11.6–7) — waiting until full Reach Exhaustion (MF5.11.3) is confirmed means the Ad Set has already been operating inefficiently for some time.

### MF5.11.5 Creative Saturation

- **Definition:** The specific interaction between Audience Saturation and Creative Fatigue (MF1.9.12) — a fixed Audience pool exposed to a fixed Creative for an extended period, compounding both Frequency Fatigue and Visual Fatigue (MF4.12.2–3) simultaneously.
- **Diagnostic Signal:** Both Audience Fatigue and Visual Fatigue diagnostic patterns (MF4.12.3/5) present concurrently, rather than either occurring in isolation.
- **Relationship to Other Concepts:** The compounding case explicitly flagged at MF1.8.8's Common Mistakes and MF4.12.1's taxonomy overview — Creative Saturation requires addressing both dimensions (Audience Expansion AND Creative Refresh) rather than either alone, since fixing only one leaves the other's contribution to the decline unaddressed.

### MF5.11.6 Audience Refresh (Full Strategic Treatment)

Extends MF3.3.11's Ad-Set-configuration-scoped treatment with full strategy: (1) re-run Lookalike generation from an updated, current Seed (MF5.5.1) rather than an aging one; (2) re-evaluate Custom Audience source Retention Windows (MF5.4) for continued relevance; (3) rotate between multiple pre-built, genuinely distinct segments (MF1.10.10) on a planned cadence rather than reactively.

### MF5.11.7 Expansion Strategy (Saturation-Response View)

Cross-referenced from MF3.3.5/MF5.5.4/MF5.6.2 — in a Saturation-response context specifically, the appropriate Expansion mechanism depends on which Audience Type is Saturated: widen Lookalike Similarity (MF5.5.4) for a Lookalike-based Ad Set; broaden Advantage+ Audience Signal breadth or loosen the Signal itself (MF5.6.2) for an Advantage+-based Ad Set; relax Detailed Targeting Layering (MF5.3.22) for a Core-Audience-based Ad Set.

### MF5.11.8 Replacement Strategy

- **Definition:** Fully retiring a Saturated Audience configuration and sourcing an entirely new one, as distinct from an incremental Refresh/Expansion (MF5.11.6–7) of the existing configuration.
- **When to Apply:** When successive Refresh/Expansion attempts fail to resolve recurring Saturation symptoms, indicating the underlying Audience pool itself (not just its current configuration) is exhausted — mirroring MF4.12.9's Creative Replacement logic applied to the Audience layer.
- **Method:** Source a new Seed (for Lookalike-based Ad Sets), a new Detailed Targeting combination (for Core-Audience-based Ad Sets), or pivot to a different Audience Type entirely (e.g., from Lookalike to Advantage+ Audience) — document the retired configuration's history (MF5.18.2's Version Control) before replacement.

### MF5.11.9 Audience Saturation Response Decision Tree

```
Rising Frequency at plateaued/slowing Reach confirmed (MF5.11.2-4)?
 ├─ Creative Fatigue symptoms also present (MF4.12.3) → Creative Saturation (MF5.11.5) → address both Audience Expansion AND Creative Refresh
 └─ Audience-only symptoms → Delivery Slowdown stage → Audience Refresh/Expansion (MF5.11.6-7)
      └─ Refresh/Expansion fails across multiple attempts → Replacement Strategy (MF5.11.8)
```

---

## MF5.12 Audience KPI Framework

The official Audience-layer KPI system. Metrics already defined at MF1.4/MF4.9 are cited, not restated; every remaining KPI is new Audience-specific content.

### MF5.12.1 Audience Size

- **Definition:** The total estimated eligible population of an Audience configuration before any Delivery has occurred, shown at construction time as a sizing estimate.
- **Diagnostic Role:** The first-pass check against Learning Phase feasibility (MF3.10.5) — an Audience Size estimate far below the volume needed to support the intended Optimization Event's typical cost/frequency is a pre-launch warning sign, addressed in full at MF5.13.1.

### MF5.12.2 Reach (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.3.27. Audience-layer role: the realized-Delivery counterpart to the pre-launch Audience Size estimate (MF5.12.1) — a large gap between the two over time can itself be diagnostic (see MF5.13's Diagnostics Framework).

### MF5.12.3 Frequency (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.4.7. Audience-layer role: the primary Saturation-diagnostic KPI throughout MF5.11.

### MF5.12.4 Unique Reach (cross-reference)

Reach (MF1.3.27) is itself already a unique-user Metric by definition; "Unique Reach" as a distinct Audience-layer KPI term refers specifically to cross-Ad-Set or cross-Campaign unique reach (requiring Meta's dedicated reach-and-frequency/overlap tooling, per MF1.3.27's Professional Notes) — not summable from individual Ad Sets' own Reach figures, a distinction this Framework treats as essential given the Audience Overlap risk (MF5.10) inherent in any multi-Ad-Set structure.

### MF5.12.5 Audience Growth

- **Definition:** The rate of change in an Audience's realized Reach (MF5.12.2) or, for Custom Audiences, its underlying population size, over successive periods.
- **Diagnostic Role:** The primary input to distinguishing Delivery Slowdown (MF5.11.4) from healthy, still-expanding Delivery — a Trend-based (MF1.11.8) KPI requiring multi-period observation, never a single-period reading.

### MF5.12.6 Overlap %

- **Definition:** The percentage of a given Ad Set's Audience that also falls within a specified sibling Ad Set's Audience, as reported by Meta's Audience Overlap tool.
- **Diagnostic Role:** The primary quantitative input to the Overlap Diagnostics process (MF5.10.4), interpreted per MF5.10.5's threshold guidance (context-dependent, not a fixed universal cutoff).

### MF5.12.7 CPM (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.4.1. Audience-layer role: directly shaped by Audience breadth and Signal quality (MF3.3.6/MF5.6.1) — a primary Efficiency KPI for evaluating Audience configuration choices against each other.

### MF5.12.8 CTR (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.4.2. Audience-layer role: a Relevance-diagnostic KPI (MF4.7.6) reflecting Audience-Creative match quality specifically when held constant against Creative variation.

### MF5.12.9 CVR (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.4.12. Audience-layer role: the primary KPI for evaluating Intent/Purchase Readiness (MF5.7.3–4) segmentation accuracy — a well-segmented BOF Audience (MF5.8.3) should show materially higher CVR than an undifferentiated broader pool.

### MF5.12.10 CPA (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.4.5. Audience-layer role: the primary Financial KPI for comparing Audience Type/source cost-efficiency against each other (e.g., comparing CPA across Lookalike tiers, MF5.5.5–9).

### MF5.12.11 ROAS (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.4.6. Audience-layer role: the primary Financial KPI for evaluating Value-Based Lookalike (MF5.5.12) and Value Segmentation (MF5.9.6) effectiveness specifically.

### MF5.12.12 Quality Score (Audience-Specific)

- **Definition:** A composite diagnostic assessment of an Audience configuration's overall health — combining Seed Quality (MF5.5.2, for Lookalikes), Signal quality (MF3.3.6/MF5.6.1, for Advantage+ Audience), Overlap % (MF5.12.6), and Saturation status (MF5.11) into a single directional read on whether a given Audience configuration is genuinely well-constructed.
- **Diagnostic Role:** Not a single fixed Meta-native Metric but a composite of the constituent inputs named above, deliberately left without a fixed implementation-level formula, consistent with MAIFS.14 and this document lineage's prior treatment of composite scores (MF4.9.18–19's Creative Fatigue/Efficiency Score precedent).

### MF5.12.13 Engagement Rate (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF4.9.10. Audience-layer role: a Relevance-diagnostic KPI for Engaged/Instagram/Page Audience (MF5.2.6/9–10) construction quality specifically.

### MF5.12.14 Purchase Rate (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF4.9.14. Audience-layer role: the Audience-attributed view of CVR (MF5.12.9), useful for comparing Audience segments directly rather than Creative variants (MF4.9.14's original framing).

### MF5.12.15 Lifetime Value

- **Definition:** The total economic value a given customer or Audience segment is expected to generate over their full relationship with the Business, extending beyond any single Purchase's immediate ROAS contribution.
- **Diagnostic Role:** The correct evaluation basis for Value Segmentation (MF5.9.6) and Value-Based Lookalike (MF5.5.12) seeding decisions — a segment with modest immediate ROAS but strong Lifetime Value may still be a superior Seed/investment target compared to a segment with strong immediate ROAS but weak retention.

### MF5.12.16 Retention (Audience-Specific)

- **Definition:** The rate at which a given Audience segment (particularly a Loyalty-stage segment, MF5.7.13) continues to engage/purchase over successive periods, rather than lapsing into the Win-back stage (MF5.8.7).
- **Diagnostic Role:** The primary success KPI for the Retention and Loyalty Funnel Audience stages (MF5.8.4/8) specifically, distinct from acquisition-focused KPIs (CPA, CPM) that dominate TOF/MOF/BOF stage evaluation.

### MF5.12.17 Audience KPI Selection Rule

Match the KPI set to the Funnel Audience stage (MF5.8) being evaluated: Audience Size/CPM/CTR for TOF; CTR/CVR/Overlap % for MOF; CVR/CPA/ROAS for BOF; Lifetime Value/Retention for Retention/Loyalty/Referral/Advocacy stages — extending the same funnel-sequenced KPI-prioritization principle established at MF2.11.10, MF3.11.9, and MF4.9.20 to this Framework's own Audience-lifecycle-aware KPI set.

---

## MF5.13 Audience Diagnostics Framework

Enterprise diagnostic models for the 12 most common Audience-layer issues. Issues already treated at MF3.12 (Ad-Set-layer Diagnostics) are cited and extended with Audience-construction-specific root-cause depth, not redefined. Each model: Symptoms, Metrics, Root Causes, Confidence Level, Priority, Solutions, Expected Recovery.

### MF5.13.1 Audience Too Small

- Base treatment at MF3.12.1. **Symptoms:** Learning status persists beyond typical window; Audience Size estimate (MF5.12.1) low relative to Budget. **Metrics:** Reach vs. Audience Size estimate; Required Events threshold (MF3.10.5). **Root Causes (Audience-construction depth):** Over-layered Detailed Targeting (MF5.3.22); over-narrow Lookalike Similarity tier (MF5.5.3); an Exclusion list (MF5.3.24) inadvertently removing most of the intended population; a Custom Audience source (MF5.4) with genuinely low underlying volume. **Confidence Level:** High. **Priority:** High. **Solutions:** Reduce Detailed Targeting layers; widen Lookalike Similarity incrementally (MF5.5.4); audit Exclusion lists for unintended over-application. **Expected Recovery:** One Learning Phase cycle post-fix.

### MF5.13.2 Audience Too Broad

- Base treatment at MF3.12.2. **Symptoms:** Adequate Delivery volume, weak CVR/Conversion Rate Ranking. **Metrics:** CVR relative to Benchmark; Breakdown by Detailed Targeting sub-segment if available. **Root Causes (Audience-construction depth):** Advantage+ Audience with a weak/absent Signal (MF5.6.1/8); an overly-wide Lookalike tier (MF5.5.8–10) applied without validating tighter tiers first; Core Audience criteria too generic for the actual Optimization Event. **Confidence Level:** Moderate — rule out a landing-page/offer-side cause (MF2.10.2) first. **Priority:** Moderate. **Solutions:** Supply/strengthen the Advantage+ Signal (MF5.6.1); narrow Lookalike tier per MF5.5.3's incremental discipline; add validated Detailed Targeting criteria. **Expected Recovery:** One to two Learning Phase cycles.

### MF5.13.3 Poor Quality

- **Symptoms:** Consistently weak performance across multiple Objectives/Ad Sets all drawing on the same underlying Audience source, despite otherwise-sound Campaign/Ad Set/Creative configuration.
- **Metrics:** Quality Score (MF5.12.12) trend; comparison against a known-strong alternative source's performance.
- **Root Causes:** Poor Seed Quality (MF5.5.2) propagating into every downstream Lookalike; a Custom Audience source (MF5.4) with low Matching Quality (MF1.6.17) or stale data; a fundamentally mismatched Detailed Targeting hypothesis never validated against real performance.
- **Confidence Level:** Moderate — requires comparison against an alternative source to confirm the issue is source-specific rather than a broader Campaign/Creative issue.
- **Priority:** High, given the propagating, multi-Ad-Set impact.
- **Solutions:** Replace the underlying source (MF5.11.8's Replacement Strategy) rather than iterating on Ad Set-level configuration alone, since the root cause sits upstream of any single Ad Set.
- **Expected Recovery:** Variable, dependent on how quickly an improved-quality source can be identified/constructed.

### MF5.13.4 High CPM

- Base treatment at MF3.12.6/MF2.10.9. **Root Causes (Audience-construction depth):** An Audience too narrow, forcing the Auction to compete harder for a small eligible pool (distinct from genuine external Auction Competition); Placement restriction interacting with a narrow Audience compounding the eligible-pool constraint (MF3.4.1). **Confidence Level:** Moderate, requiring Placement Breakdown cross-check. **Priority:** Moderate-High. **Solutions:** Broaden the Audience (MF5.11.7's Expansion Strategy) before assuming pure external Auction Competition. **Expected Recovery:** One Learning Phase cycle post-broadening.

### MF5.13.5 Low CTR

- Base treatment at MF3.12.8. **Root Causes (Audience-construction depth):** Audience-Creative Relevance mismatch (MF4.7.6) — the Audience segment's actual Interest/Behavior profile does not align with the Creative's Hook/message; an Advantage+ Audience Signal misaligned with the actual Optimization Event (MF5.6.4). **Confidence Level:** Moderate, requiring Creative-side causes (MF4.13.1) to be ruled out first. **Priority:** Moderate-High. **Solutions:** Re-evaluate Signal/Segmentation alignment with the actual Creative concept and Optimization Event before assuming a pure Creative-layer cause. **Expected Recovery:** One Learning Phase cycle post-fix.

### MF5.13.6 High CPA

- Base treatment at MF3.12.12/MF2.10.2. **Root Causes (Audience-construction depth):** A Value Segmentation (MF5.9.6) failure — targeting a genuinely lower-value segment at the same Bid Strategy intensity as a higher-value one; an over-broad Audience (MF5.13.2) diluting overall efficiency. **Confidence Level:** Moderate. **Priority:** High. **Solutions:** Apply Value Segmentation to differentiate Bid Strategy/Budget allocation by segment value rather than treating the Audience as uniform. **Expected Recovery:** Variable, dependent on segmentation implementation.

### MF5.13.7 Low ROAS

- Base treatment at MF3.12.11/MF2.10.3. **Root Causes (Audience-construction depth):** Seeding a Value-Based Lookalike (MF5.5.12) from a non-value-weighted or low-value source; targeting a segment with low Purchase Readiness (MF5.7.4) under a Sales Objective expecting immediate Conversion. **Confidence Level:** Moderate, requiring measurement-integrity verification first (MF2.10.3). **Priority:** Critical. **Solutions:** Rebuild the Lookalike Seed with Value weighting (MF5.5.12); re-align Audience Buying Stage (MF5.7.11) with the actual Optimization Event being pursued. **Expected Recovery:** Variable.

### MF5.13.8 High Frequency

- Base treatment at MF3.12.7/MF1.4.7. **Root Causes (Audience-construction depth):** Fully treated as Audience Saturation at MF5.11 — Reach Exhaustion (MF5.11.3) is the specific Audience-construction-level explanation. **Confidence Level:** High. **Priority:** Moderate-High. **Solutions:** Apply MF5.11.9's Saturation Response Decision Tree. **Expected Recovery:** One Learning Phase cycle post-Expansion/Refresh.

### MF5.13.9 Audience Saturation (cross-reference)

Fully treated at MF5.11. Cross-referenced here as a Diagnostics Framework entry point, consistent with this document lineage's established no-duplicate-content discipline (mirroring MF3.9.9, MF3.17.6, MF4.18.9's identical cross-reference pattern).

### MF5.13.10 Audience Overlap (cross-reference)

Fully treated at MF5.10. Cross-referenced here as a Diagnostics Framework entry point, per the same no-duplicate-content discipline.

### MF5.13.11 Weak Purchase Intent

- **Symptoms:** Adequate Delivery/Engagement but a low proportion of the Audience reaching Intent/Purchase Readiness states (MF5.7.3–4) despite Sales-objective targeting.
- **Metrics:** Funnel-stage progression rate (TOF-to-MOF-to-BOF conversion within the Audience, per MF5.8.11's Progression Map) rather than a single Ad-Set-level CVR reading.
- **Root Causes:** Targeting an Audience genuinely earlier in Buying Stage (MF5.7.11) than the Optimization Event assumes (e.g., a cold Lookalike targeted directly with a Purchase Optimization Event, skipping MOF nurture entirely); a Lookalike/Advantage+ Signal seeded from a low-Intent source (e.g., Page Engagement rather than Purchase, MF5.2.9 vs. MF1.6.8).
- **Confidence Level:** Moderate — requires funnel-stage-aware analysis rather than a single aggregate Metric.
- **Priority:** High for Sales/Leads Objectives specifically.
- **Solutions:** Introduce an intermediate MOF stage (MF5.8.2) rather than jumping directly from TOF Audience to a BOF Optimization Event; reseed Lookalikes/Signals from a higher-Intent source.
- **Expected Recovery:** Multiple Learning Phase cycles, given the fix often requires restructuring the funnel stage sequence itself, not a single-variable Ad Set edit.

### MF5.13.12 Weak Engagement

- **Symptoms:** Low Engagement Rate/Share Rate/Save Rate (MF4.9.10–12) specifically attributable to Audience mismatch rather than Creative quality.
- **Metrics:** Engagement Rate compared across otherwise-identical Creative run against different Audience segments (Creative Isolation, MF4.8.4, applied to isolate the Audience variable instead).
- **Root Causes:** Psychographic/Identity mismatch (MF5.9.3, MF5.7.9) between the targeted segment and the Creative's underlying appeal; an Engaged/Page/Instagram Audience (MF5.2.6/9–10) source that is stale or over-broad.
- **Confidence Level:** Moderate — requires the Creative-Isolation cross-check to confirm the issue is Audience-side rather than Creative-side (MF4.13.5's Poor Engagement diagnostic, approached from the opposite direction).
- **Priority:** Moderate.
- **Solutions:** Re-align Psychographic/Identity Segmentation (MF5.9.3) with the actual Creative concept; refresh stale Engaged Audience sources (MF5.11.6).
- **Expected Recovery:** One to two Learning Phase cycles.

### MF5.13.13 Diagnostics Summary Table

| Issue | Typical Priority | Typical Recovery |
|---|---|---|
| Audience Too Small | High | ~1 cycle |
| Audience Too Broad | Moderate | 1–2 cycles |
| Poor Quality | High | Variable |
| High CPM | Moderate-High | ~1 cycle |
| Low CTR | Moderate-High | ~1 cycle |
| High CPA | High | Variable |
| Low ROAS | Critical | Variable |
| High Frequency | Moderate-High | ~1 cycle |
| Audience Saturation | Moderate-High | ~1 cycle |
| Audience Overlap | Moderate-High | Immediate–1 cycle |
| Weak Purchase Intent | High | Multiple cycles |
| Weak Engagement | Moderate | 1–2 cycles |

---

## MF5.14 Audience Optimization Framework

The concrete optimization actions this Framework's Diagnostics (MF5.13) route into. Each documented with Definition, When to Apply, Method — citing prior sections rather than restating them.

### MF5.14.1 Expansion (cross-reference)

Fully treated at MF3.3.5/MF5.11.7. **When to Apply:** Audience Too Small (MF5.13.1) or Audience Saturation (MF5.13.9) diagnoses.

### MF5.14.2 Narrowing (cross-reference)

Fully treated at MF5.3.23. **When to Apply:** Audience Too Broad (MF5.13.2) diagnoses, applied incrementally per MF5.3.23's guidance.

### MF5.14.3 Layering (cross-reference)

Fully treated at MF5.3.22. **When to Apply:** As a component of Narrowing (MF5.14.2) when a specific additional relevance hypothesis is validated, never as a default practice.

### MF5.14.4 Exclusions (cross-reference)

Fully treated at MF5.3.24/MF5.10.8. **When to Apply:** Audience Overlap (MF5.13.10) diagnoses, or removing existing customers from prospecting Ad Sets.

### MF5.14.5 Segmentation (cross-reference)

Fully treated at MF5.9. **When to Apply:** Weak Purchase Intent (MF5.13.11) or High CPA (MF5.13.6) diagnoses traceable to an undifferentiated, one-size-fits-all Audience treatment.

### MF5.14.6 Refresh (cross-reference)

Fully treated at MF5.11.6. **When to Apply:** Audience Saturation (MF5.13.9) or Weak Engagement (MF5.13.12) diagnoses traceable to source staleness.

### MF5.14.7 Scaling (cross-reference)

The complete treatment is delivered at MF5.15, per this Framework's own required structure. **When to Apply:** A validated, stable Audience configuration ready for growth per MF3.13's Ad-Set-layer Scaling Framework preconditions (Optimization Stability, MF3.10.6).

### MF5.14.8 Rotation (cross-reference)

Fully treated at MF1.10.10/MF4.12.10. **When to Apply:** Proactive Saturation prevention (MF5.11.4's Delivery Slowdown stage) via the Testing Calendar (MF4.8.10) applied to Audience segments specifically.

### MF5.14.9 Lookalike Optimization

- **Definition:** The consolidated application of MF5.5's full Lookalike methodology (Seed Quality, Similarity tier progression, Value-Based weighting) as an ongoing optimization discipline rather than a one-time construction decision.
- **When to Apply:** Continuously, as a scheduled review (MF5.18.10's Audit Standards) confirming Seed currency and Similarity tier appropriateness given current Delivery/Saturation data.
- **Method:** Apply MF5.5.14's Scaling Strategy in reverse when contracting (e.g., stepping back from a 5% to a 2% tier if quality has degraded) as well as forward when expanding.

### MF5.14.10 Custom Audience Optimization

- **Definition:** The consolidated application of MF5.4's source-selection and Retention Window/Refresh Strategy guidance as an ongoing optimization discipline.
- **When to Apply:** Continuously, particularly whenever a new, higher-quality data source becomes available (e.g., a Business newly implementing Conversions API, MF5.4.3) that should supersede a lower-quality existing source.
- **Method:** Apply MF5.4.18's Source Selection Rule on an ongoing basis, retiring lower-maturity sources as higher-maturity ones become available rather than accumulating an ever-growing, unmanaged set of overlapping Custom Audiences.

### MF5.14.11 Advantage+ Optimization

- **Definition:** The consolidated application of MF5.6's Signal-quality and Restriction-awareness guidance as an ongoing optimization discipline.
- **When to Apply:** Whenever the Optimization Event changes (MF3.13.7) or a stronger candidate Signal (e.g., a newly-available Value-Based Customer List, MF5.5.12/5.2.14) becomes available.
- **Method:** Apply MF5.6.10's Governance guidance on a recurring basis, re-validating Signal relevance rather than setting it once at Ad Set creation and never revisiting it.

### MF5.14.12 Audience Optimization Priority Sequence

When multiple Optimization actions are candidates simultaneously, apply in this general order: (1) verify Segmentation (MF5.14.5) and Exclusions (MF5.14.4) are correctly configured as the structural foundation; (2) apply source-specific Optimization (Lookalike/Custom Audience/Advantage+, MF5.14.9–11) to improve the quality of each Audience component; (3) apply Expansion/Narrowing (MF5.14.1–2) to adjust breadth; (4) apply Refresh/Rotation (MF5.14.6/8) as ongoing maintenance; (5) apply Scaling (MF5.14.7/MF5.15) only once the above are validated stable.

---

## MF5.15 Audience Scaling Framework

Extends MF3.13's Ad-Set-layer Scaling Framework with Audience-construction-specific scaling mechanics.

### MF5.15.1 Horizontal Scaling (Audience-Layer Mechanics)

Cross-referenced from MF3.13.1. At the Audience-construction layer specifically, implemented via Multiple Lookalikes from differentiated Seeds (MF5.5.11), distinct Custom Audience sources run in parallel (MF5.4.18's source-diversification guidance), or genuinely distinct Segmentation cells (MF5.9) run as separate Ad Sets.

### MF5.15.2 Vertical Scaling (Audience-Layer Mechanics)

Cross-referenced from MF3.13.2. At the Audience-construction layer, Vertical Scaling is bounded by Reach Exhaustion (MF5.11.3) — Budget increases alone cannot scale Delivery past a genuinely exhausted Audience pool, making Audience-side Expansion (MF5.14.1) a frequent prerequisite to further Vertical Scaling, not merely an alternative to it.

### MF5.15.3 Country Expansion

- **Definition:** Extending an Audience configuration's geographic Location criteria (MF5.3.4–6) to include additional countries.
- **Guidance:** Given Country Dependency (MF5.5.13), a Lookalike-based Audience requires a fresh, country-specific Lookalike build rather than assuming the existing one generalizes; a Core/Advantage+ Audience can extend more directly, subject to Language (MF5.3.3) and Creative localization (MF4.3.29) readiness.
- **Risk:** Expanding into a new Country without corresponding Creative localization or fulfillment/service capability, producing poor Conversion Quality (MF4.7.3) despite adequate Delivery volume.

### MF5.15.4 Region Expansion

- **Definition:** Extending an Audience configuration's Location criteria to additional Regions (MF5.3.7) within an already-served Country.
- **Guidance:** Lower-risk than full Country Expansion (MF5.15.3) given shared Language/regulatory context typically remains constant within a single Country; still validate genuine service/fulfillment capability in the new Region before scaling Budget into it.
- **Risk:** Assuming uniform performance across all Regions of a large, diverse Country without validating per-Region Breakdown data (MF1.3.39) first.

### MF5.15.5 Lookalike Expansion (Scaling Context)

Cross-referenced from MF3.13.4/MF5.5.4/MF5.5.14. In an Audience-Scaling context specifically, apply the full MF5.5.14 Scaling Strategy sequence (1% → validate → 2–3% → Multiple Lookalikes from differentiated Seeds → Broad tiers) rather than jumping directly to a wide tier for scale.

### MF5.15.6 Broad Audience Scaling

- **Definition:** Scaling Delivery volume via Broad Lookalike tiers (MF5.5.10) or full Advantage+ Audience (MF5.6) Automation, deliberately favoring system-driven breadth over continued manual, tightly-tiered management.
- **Guidance:** The appropriate strategy once Multiple Lookalikes and Horizontal Scaling (MF5.15.1) options are largely exhausted, or when a Business's operational capacity does not support ongoing granular manual Audience management at scale.
- **Risk:** Applied prematurely (before tighter tiers are validated), forfeiting the opportunity to learn the Business's true similarity-vs-quality tradeoff, per MF5.5.10's Common Mistake.

### MF5.15.7 AI Scaling

- **Definition:** Scaling Delivery primarily through Advantage+ Audience's AI Expansion mechanism (MF5.6.2) rather than manual Audience-construction levers, representing the fullest expression of the Automation spectrum (MF5.6.3) applied to a Scaling goal specifically.
- **Guidance:** Particularly effective once sufficient Optimization Event history exists for the system's own inference to be well-calibrated (MF5.6.5's Learning Behavior advantage); less effective as a Scaling strategy for a brand-new Ad Set with no performance history to inform the AI's inference.
- **Risk:** Over-relying on AI Scaling without maintaining Signal quality (MF5.15.8) monitoring, since AI Expansion's effectiveness remains bounded by the quality of its guiding Signal even at scale.

### MF5.15.8 Signals Improvement

- **Definition:** Proactively strengthening the Audience Signal(s) (MF3.3.6/MF5.6.1) feeding Advantage+ Audience or Lookalike Seed selection, as a Scaling enabler distinct from any single Expansion/Scaling action itself.
- **Guidance:** Prioritize sourcing higher-quality Signals (Value-weighted Customer Lists, MF5.5.12; deeper-Intent Website Events, MF5.7.3) over simply widening existing Signal-agnostic breadth — a stronger Signal often enables more efficient Scaling than breadth expansion alone.
- **Risk:** Neglecting Signal quality entirely while pursuing Broad Audience Scaling (MF5.15.6) or AI Scaling (MF5.15.7), leaving genuine efficiency gains unrealized.

### MF5.15.9 Audience Scaling Decision Tree

```
Is the current binding constraint on further growth genuine Reach Exhaustion (MF5.11.3) of the current Audience, or Budget/Bid Strategy itself?
 ├─ Reach Exhaustion confirmed
 │    ├─ Lookalike-based → Lookalike Expansion (MF5.15.5) or Multiple Lookalikes (Horizontal, MF5.15.1)
 │    ├─ Advantage+-based → Signals Improvement (MF5.15.8) or AI Scaling (MF5.15.7)
 │    └─ Core-Audience-based → reduce Layering (MF5.14.3) or migrate toward Advantage+ Audience (MF5.6)
 └─ Not yet Exhausted → proceed with Vertical Scaling (MF5.15.2) per MF2.9.5's incremental discipline
```

---

## MF5.16 AI Audience Decision Framework

AI-consumable decision rules for the most common Audience scenarios, specializing the Generic Framework Series' Decision concept (`F4.5`) exactly as MF2.12, MF3.14, and MF4.15 do at their respective layers, applied here at Audience grain.

### MF5.16.1 Rule: Rising Frequency with Plateaued Reach on a Lookalike-Based Ad Set

- **Observed Metrics:** Frequency (MF1.4.7) rising; Reach growth rate approaching zero (MF5.11.3); Ad Set uses a 1% Lookalike (MF5.5.5).
- **Likely Cause:** Reach Exhaustion (MF5.11.3) of the current tight Lookalike tier.
- **Confidence Level:** High.
- **Recommended Action:** Lookalike Expansion (MF5.5.4) to the next tier (2%), applied incrementally.
- **Priority:** Moderate-High.
- **Risk:** Jumping directly to a Broad tier (5–10%) rather than the adjacent tier forfeits the ability to learn the true similarity-vs-quality tradeoff at this Seed.
- **Expected Outcome:** Renewed Reach growth and stabilized Frequency within one Learning Phase cycle.

### MF5.16.2 Rule: Strong Delivery, Weak Conversion on an Advantage+ Audience Ad Set

- **Observed Metrics:** Healthy Reach/CTR; weak CVR/Conversion Rate Ranking; Advantage+ Audience enabled with no supplied Signal.
- **Likely Cause:** Audience Too Broad (MF5.13.2) — Advantage+ Audience operating without Signal guidance.
- **Confidence Level:** Moderate-High.
- **Recommended Action:** Supply a high-quality Audience Signal (MF5.6.1), ideally a Value-Based Customer List (MF5.5.12/5.2.14) or a deep-Intent Website Audience (MF5.7.3).
- **Priority:** Moderate-High.
- **Risk:** Adding an overly restrictive Signal (treated as a hard filter rather than a soft suggestion) could unintentionally shrink Delivery volume — verify Signal is applied as guidance, not a hard restriction, per MF1.8.4's own definition.
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved CVR within one to two Learning Phase cycles as the system's inference sharpens around the supplied Signal.

### MF5.16.3 Rule: Two Sibling Ad Sets Showing Declining Combined Efficiency

- **Observed Metrics:** Two Ad Sets under Ad Set Budget (MF1.3.23), sharing a similar Custom Audience source, both showing declining CPM/CPA since the second launched.
- **Likely Cause:** Audience Overlap (MF5.13.10) producing Auction Cannibalization (MF5.10.3).
- **Confidence Level:** High once confirmed via the Overlap reporting tool or a pause test (MF5.10.4).
- **Recommended Action:** Apply Exclusion Strategy (MF5.10.8) — exclude the lower-priority Ad Set's Audience from the higher-priority one, or migrate both under Campaign Budget (MF1.3.22) to let dynamic allocation arbitrate.
- **Priority:** Moderate-High.
- **Risk:** Bidirectional Exclusion could shrink both pools enough to risk Learning Limited (MF3.10.2) — apply unidirectionally per MF5.10.8's Method guidance.
- **Expected Outcome:** Combined efficiency improves within one Learning Phase cycle post-Exclusion.

### MF5.16.4 Rule: New Lookalike Seed Available from Recently-Matured CRM Data

- **Observed Metrics:** A Business has newly integrated CRM-fed Customer List data (MF5.2.14) with attached Lifetime Value (MF5.12.15) metadata; current prospecting relies on a Page-Engagement-seeded Lookalike (MF5.2.9-adjacent Seed).
- **Likely Cause:** Not a diagnosed failure — a proactive Signals Improvement (MF5.15.8) opportunity.
- **Confidence Level:** Moderate, pending validation of the new Seed's actual size/quality (MF5.5.2).
- **Recommended Action:** Build a Value-Based Lookalike (MF5.5.12) from the new high-Lifetime-Value Customer List segment, testing it against the existing Page-Engagement-seeded Lookalike under Creative Isolation-equivalent Audience testing (holding Creative/Ad Set configuration constant).
- **Priority:** Moderate (opportunity-driven, not corrective).
- **Risk:** Switching Seeds entirely without a parallel test could mask whether the new Seed genuinely outperforms, rather than merely being newer.
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved ROAS/CPA if the new Seed's higher inherent Seed Quality (MF5.5.2) translates into better prospecting, confirmed via direct comparison rather than assumption.

### MF5.16.5 Rule: TOF Audience Segment Directly Targeted with a BOF Optimization Event

- **Observed Metrics:** A cold Core Audience or broad Lookalike (TOF, MF5.8.1) Ad Set configured with a Purchase Optimization Event (MF3.5.11); Learning Limited or very low Conversion volume.
- **Likely Cause:** Weak Purchase Intent (MF5.13.11) — Buying Stage (MF5.7.11) mismatch between the Audience and the Optimization Event.
- **Confidence Level:** High.
- **Recommended Action:** Introduce an intermediate MOF stage (MF5.8.2) — retarget engaged TOF users with a lower-friction Optimization Event (Add To Cart, MF1.6.5) before a dedicated BOF Ad Set pursues Purchase directly.
- **Priority:** High.
- **Risk:** Restructuring the funnel sequence requires additional Ad Sets/Budget coordination (MF2.5) — a structural change, not a single-variable edit, and should be planned accordingly.
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved overall funnel efficiency (Lifetime Value-adjusted) even if any single stage's individual CPA looks less impressive in isolation.

### MF5.16.6 Rule: Audience Approaching Estimated Saturation During a Scaling Push

- **Observed Metrics:** Reach growth rate decelerating (Delivery Slowdown, MF5.11.4) during an active Vertical Scaling (MF5.15.2) sequence; Budget increases showing diminishing marginal Delivery.
- **Likely Cause:** Approaching Reach Exhaustion (MF5.11.3) before the Scaling plan's target Budget is reached.
- **Confidence Level:** Moderate-High, based on the Trend (MF1.11.8) of decelerating Reach growth across successive increases.
- **Recommended Action:** Pause further Vertical Scaling; apply Audience Expansion or Horizontal Scaling (MF5.15.1) to create additional headroom before resuming Budget increases.
- **Priority:** Moderate-High (preventive, avoiding a predictable near-term Saturation failure).
- **Risk:** Continuing Vertical Scaling without addressing the Audience-side constraint wastes incremental Budget efficiency and risks triggering full Reach Exhaustion mid-Campaign.
- **Expected Outcome:** Sustained proportional Delivery growth once Audience headroom is restored, rather than a Scaling plateau followed by efficiency decline.

---

## MF5.17 Audience Knowledge Graph

A semantic graph connecting Audience-layer concepts to each other and, by citation, back to MF1–MF4's vocabulary — using the same relationship-type legend established at MF1.15.1 and applied at Campaign (MF2.14), Ad Set (MF3.16), and Creative (MF4.16) grain, applied here at Audience grain.

### MF5.17.1 Structural & Construction Edges

```
Audience --Specializes into--> Core, Custom, Lookalike, Advantage+ (and 13 further sub-types) [MF5.2]
Custom Audience --Requires--> a data source (Pixel, Customer List, Engagement, Offline, App, Catalog) [MF5.4]
Lookalike --Requires--> Seed Audience (a Custom Audience) + Similarity tier [MF5.5]
Advantage+ Audience --Requires (optionally)--> a Signal [MF5.6.1]
Behavior, Interest, Intent, Purchase --Produces (via Event tracking)--> Custom Audience membership [MF5.3.11-12, MF5.7.3, MF1.6.8]
Pixel --Produces--> Website/Shopping Activity Custom Audience data [MF5.4.1-2/14]
```

### MF5.17.2 Psychology & Funnel Edges

```
Audience Psychology states (Awareness, Interest, Intent, Purchase Readiness, Trust, Risk, ...) --Governs--> Funnel Audience stage assignment [MF5.7, MF5.8]
Buying Stage + Decision Stage --Governs--> Optimization Event and Creative Psychology selection [MF5.7.11-12, MF3.5, MF4.4]
Funnel Audience Progression (TOF→MOF→BOF→Retention→Loyalty→Referral→Advocacy) --Produces--> Lookalike Seed candidates (Advocacy feeds back to TOF) [MF5.8.11]
```

### MF5.17.3 Learning, Optimization & Delivery Edges

```
Audience size/specificity --Governs--> Learning Phase feasibility [MF5.1.7, MF3.10.5]
Signals quality --Governs--> Advantage+ AI Expansion effectiveness [MF5.6.1-2]
Audience --Governs--> Auction eligible population and Delivery Reach/Frequency [MF5.1.5-6]
Segmentation (MF5.9) --Requires--> Behavioral/Value/Intent data as input
Diagnostics Framework (MF5.13) --Requires--> KPI Framework (MF5.12) as its evidence base
Optimization Framework (MF5.14) --Remedies--> Diagnostics Framework findings
AI Audience Decision Framework (MF5.16) --Requires--> Diagnostics findings as Observed Metrics input
```

### MF5.17.4 Overlap, Saturation & Scaling Edges

```
Audience Overlap --Produces--> Internal Competition --Produces--> Auction Cannibalization [MF5.10]
Audience Overlap --Remedies via--> Merge, Split, or Exclusion Strategy [MF5.10.6-8]
Reach Exhaustion --Produces--> Audience Saturation / Frequency Fatigue (Creative-layer manifestation) [MF5.11.3, MF4.12.2]
Audience Saturation --Remedies via--> Refresh, Expansion, or Replacement Strategy [MF5.11.6-8]
Scaling (Horizontal/Vertical/Country/Region/AI) --Requires--> Reach Exhaustion status check first [MF5.15.9]
Retention (KPI) --Measures--> Loyalty/Advocacy Funnel Audience stage health [MF5.12.16, MF5.8.8/10]
```

### MF5.17.5 Graph Completeness Statement

Every section of this Framework (MF5.1–MF5.16, MF5.18) appears as a node or edge participant above: Audience Architecture and Types (MF5.1–2) as root structural nodes; Core/Custom/Lookalike/Advantage+ construction (MF5.3–6) as construction-lever edges; Psychology, Funnel, Segmentation (MF5.7–9) as behavioral-classification edges; Overlap, Saturation, KPIs (MF5.10–12) as diagnostic-evidence edges; Diagnostics, Optimization, Scaling, AI Decision, Governance (MF5.13–18) as the decision and compliance layer connecting back to every structural and behavioral node — mirroring MF2.14's, MF3.16's, and MF4.16's graph structure at this Framework's own grain.

---

## MF5.18 Audience Governance

Extends MF2.13's, MF3.15's, and MF4.17's Campaign/Ad-Set/Creative-layer governance patterns to the Audience layer, using the same domain-content-governance framing (distinct from EIFS/MAIFS's document-governance meaning) — with the important addition that Audience Governance uniquely carries genuine data-privacy/regulatory weight, per MF5.1.10's Architecture-section framing.

### MF5.18.1 Naming Standards

Extends MF1.13.4 (Audience Naming). Audience Governance requires this standard be enforced at construction time — an Audience name failing the standard (missing type/source/lookback-window encoding) should block the Approval Workflow (MF5.18.4).

### MF5.18.2 Version Control

Every Audience construction change (Seed swap, Similarity tier change, Signal update, Detailed Targeting modification) should be logged with timestamp, changed-by, prior/new configuration, and rationale — extending MF2.13.2/MF3.15.3/MF4.17.3's Version Control discipline to the standing-data-object layer, where a single Audience's history typically spans many more redeployments than any single Ad Set/Creative's own Version Control log.

### MF5.18.3 Documentation

Every Audience should carry, at minimum: its Type and source (MF5.2/5.4), its construction rationale (Seed choice for Lookalikes, Signal choice for Advantage+), its current Funnel Audience stage assignment (MF5.8), and a reference to its most recent Diagnostics Framework (MF5.13) finding if one is active.

### MF5.18.4 Approval Workflow

Audience changes should pass through the same Proposed → Reviewed → Approved → Applied → Monitored sequence established at MF2.13.4/MF3.15.4/MF4.17.4, evaluated here against this Framework's own standards: Naming (MF5.18.1), Privacy Compliance (MF5.18.6), and QA (MF5.18.5) sign-off — with Customer List and Offline Audience sources (MF5.2.14–15) specifically requiring Privacy Compliance sign-off before any deployment, never after.

### MF5.18.5 Audience QA

A pre-deployment review confirming: Audience Size (MF5.12.1) estimate supports Learning Phase feasibility (MF3.10.5) for the intended Optimization Event; Seed Quality (MF5.5.2) or Signal quality (MF5.6.1) is validated, not assumed; no unintended Audience Overlap (MF5.10) exists with an already-deployed sibling Ad Set's Audience; special-category Restrictions (MF5.6.9) are confirmed not applicable or are correctly applied.

### MF5.18.6 Privacy Compliance

Every Customer List, Email, Phone, or Offline Event upload (MF5.4.4–6/16) must be verified against the Business's own legal basis for processing that data (consent, legitimate interest, or contractual necessity, as applicable in the relevant jurisdiction) before upload — a hard gate at the Approval Workflow (MF5.18.4), not an optional post-hoc check. Hashing and upload must follow current platform-required technical specifications for PII handling.

### MF5.18.7 GDPR Considerations

For any Business operating in or targeting users within the European Economic Area (or other jurisdictions with substantively similar data-protection regimes), Customer List and Offline Audience construction (MF5.2.14–15) must additionally confirm: a documented lawful basis for processing exists specifically covering use for advertising Audience matching (not merely for the original data-collection purpose); a mechanism exists for honoring data-subject deletion/opt-out requests that propagates to removal from any constructed Custom Audience; Lookalike Audiences seeded from EEA-subject data inherit the same lawful-basis requirement as their Seed. This Framework does not itself constitute legal advice — Businesses must consult qualified counsel for jurisdiction-specific compliance obligations; this section establishes only that GDPR-relevant considerations must be reviewed at the Approval Workflow (MF5.18.4), not that this document supplies the compliance determination itself.

### MF5.18.8 Customer Data Governance

Beyond GDPR-specific considerations (MF5.18.7), general Customer Data Governance requires: a documented data-retention policy for uploaded Customer Lists/Offline Events (MF5.18.9); a designated accountable owner for the Business's overall Custom Audience data-handling practices (extending MF2.13.6/MF3.15's Ownership pattern to data-governance specifically); a process for responding to platform-level data-matching or compliance inquiries.

### MF5.18.9 Retention Rules

- Customer List and Offline Audiences (MF5.2.14–15) should be re-uploaded/refreshed on a defined cadence (MF5.4.4/16's Refresh Strategy) rather than persisting indefinitely without review.
- Event-based Custom Audiences (Website, Engagement, Video, App, MF5.4.1/8-9/12/17) should use a Retention Window matched to genuine behavioral relevance (MF5.4's per-source guidance), not a maximal default applied unreflectively.
- Audiences no longer in active use (superseded by Replacement, MF5.11.8, or belonging to a retired Campaign, MF2.7.11) should be formally retired/deleted rather than left dormant indefinitely, consistent with data-minimization principles underlying most data-protection regimes (MF5.18.7).

### MF5.18.10 Audit Standards

- [ ] Naming complies with MF1.13.4/MF5.18.1.
- [ ] Documentation (MF5.18.3) is complete and current for every active Audience.
- [ ] Version Control log (MF5.18.2) is complete for every construction change since the last audit.
- [ ] No Saved Audience (MF5.2.2) has gone unreviewed past the Business's defined staleness threshold.
- [ ] Privacy Compliance and GDPR sign-off (MF5.18.6–7) is on file for every Customer List/Offline Audience in active use.
- [ ] Retention Rules (MF5.18.9) are being followed — no indefinitely-persisting, unrefreshed Customer List/Offline Audience beyond its defined review cadence.
- [ ] No confirmed Audience Overlap/Cannibalization (MF5.10) remains unaddressed past the Business's defined grace period.
- [ ] Every retired Audience's rationale is logged for institutional-knowledge purposes (mirroring MF4.8.7's Creative Learning repository pattern applied to Audience).

---

## MF5.19 Enterprise Best Practices

### MF5.19.1 Golden Rules

1. **Audience quality begins upstream of any single Ad Set** — Seed Quality, Signal quality, and Custom Audience source maturity determine the ceiling for everything the Ad Set layer (MF3.3) can achieve, no Bid Strategy or Budget adjustment compensates for a poorly-constructed Audience.
2. **Default toward breadth and AI delegation, narrow only with validated reason** — Core Audience over-narrowing, excessive Layering, and premature Broad Lookalike/Advantage+ avoidance are the most common, most avoidable Audience failure patterns across this entire Framework.
3. **Match Audience Buying Stage to Optimization Event** — the single highest-leverage Audience-Optimization alignment decision (MF5.7.11, MF5.13.11).
4. **Never fabricate or assume data-privacy compliance** — Customer List, Email, Phone, and Offline Audience construction require verified, documented lawful basis before every deployment (MF5.18.6–7).
5. **Diagnose Overlap and Saturation before scaling** — confirm Reach Exhaustion status (MF5.11.3) is not already the binding constraint before applying further Budget or Horizontal Scaling.
6. **Segment by value, not just by stage** — Lifetime Value-aware Segmentation (MF5.9.6) consistently outperforms funnel-stage-only Segmentation for mature Businesses with sufficient data.

### MF5.19.2 Audience Checklist

- [ ] Audience Type and source selected match the Business's actual data infrastructure maturity (MF5.4.18).
- [ ] Audience Size estimate (MF5.12.1) supports Learning Phase feasibility for the intended Optimization Event.
- [ ] No unintended Overlap exists with an already-deployed sibling Audience (MF5.10.4).
- [ ] Buying Stage/Funnel Audience assignment (MF5.7.11, MF5.8) matches the parent Ad Set's Optimization Event.
- [ ] Privacy Compliance sign-off obtained for any Customer List/Offline source in use (MF5.18.6).

### MF5.19.3 Targeting Checklist

- [ ] Core Audience Detailed Targeting criteria (MF5.3.10–17) are each individually validated, not assumed.
- [ ] Expansion (MF5.3.21) is enabled by default unless a specific, tested reason justifies disabling it.
- [ ] Layering (MF5.3.22) is minimal and each criterion independently justified.
- [ ] Exclusions (MF5.3.24) are applied deliberately (Connection, existing customers, known Overlap sources), not omitted or over-applied.

### MF5.19.4 Lookalike Checklist

- [ ] Seed Audience (MF5.5.1) represents genuinely high-value users, not the broadest available pool.
- [ ] Similarity tier starts at 1% and expands incrementally per validated performance (MF5.5.3–4).
- [ ] Value-Based weighting (MF5.5.12) is applied wherever value-differentiated Seed data is available.
- [ ] Country Dependency (MF5.5.13) is accounted for — a separate Lookalike is built per target market, not assumed to generalize.

### MF5.19.5 Custom Audience Checklist

- [ ] Source Event/lookback window relevance is verified before every deployment (MF5.4's per-source Retention Window guidance).
- [ ] Refresh Strategy (MF5.4) is scheduled, not left to indefinite staleness.
- [ ] Matching Quality (MF1.6.17) is monitored for Pixel/Conversions API-sourced Audiences.
- [ ] Privacy Compliance (MF5.18.6–7) sign-off is on file for any PII-based source (Customer Lists, Email, Phone, Offline Events).

### MF5.19.6 Scaling Checklist

- [ ] Reach Exhaustion status (MF5.11.3) is confirmed not yet binding before further Vertical Scaling.
- [ ] Scaling increments follow the incremental discipline (MF2.9.5) rather than large single-step jumps.
- [ ] Horizontal Scaling candidates (MF5.15.1) are validated for genuine Audience differentiation, avoiding Overlap (MF5.10).
- [ ] Signals Improvement (MF5.15.8) is considered alongside pure breadth expansion, not overlooked in favor of Broad Audience/AI Scaling alone.

### MF5.19.7 Optimization Checklist

- [ ] Diagnostics Framework (MF5.13) finding confirmed before selecting an Optimization action.
- [ ] Optimization action matched to the specific diagnosed cause, per MF5.14's per-action "When to Apply" guidance.
- [ ] Only one Optimization variable applied per iteration cycle (MF5.14.12's Priority Sequence discipline).
- [ ] Expected Outcome stated before applying the fix, per MF5.16's AI Decision format, enabling honest post-hoc evaluation.

### MF5.19.8 Audit Checklist (Cross-Reference)

Fully specified at MF5.18.10 — repeated here only as a pointer, per this Framework's own no-duplicate-content discipline (mirroring MF3.17.6's and MF4.18.9's identical cross-reference pattern at prior layers).

## Meta Framework 5 Closing Statement

**Meta Framework 5 — Audience Framework is now complete, validated, and frozen.** It establishes the definitive Audience-layer reference — Audience Architecture, a complete per-Type analysis (17 Audience Types), a complete Core Audience targeting catalog (24 options), a complete Custom Audience source catalog (17 sources), full Lookalike and Advantage+ Audience methodologies, an Audience Psychology Framework (14 states), a Funnel Audience Framework (10 stages), an Audience Segmentation Framework (8 approaches), Overlap and Saturation Frameworks, an Audience KPI Framework (16 KPIs), a complete per-Issue Diagnostics Framework (12 issues), Optimization and Scaling Frameworks, AI Audience Decision rules, a Knowledge Graph, Governance (including genuine Privacy Compliance and GDPR treatment), and Enterprise Best Practices — governed throughout by MAIFS, built entirely on Meta Frameworks 1–4's vocabulary by citation only, and without modifying the Enterprise Architecture Baseline (§0–§60), EIFS, the Generic Enterprise Intelligence Framework Series (Frameworks 1–10), MAIFS, or Meta Frameworks 1–4. Any future change to Meta Framework 5 proceeds only through the Revision mechanism established at EIFS.7 and specialized for Meta Ads Frameworks at MAIFS.11.

# Meta Framework 6 — Delivery Framework

**Status:** Complete, Validated, and Frozen. All 18 planned sections (MF6.1–MF6.18), plus the MF6.19 Final Validation Checklist and Closing Statement, are present in full per MAIFS.8's lifecycle policy. Any future change proceeds only through the Revision mechanism (EIFS.7, MAIFS.11).
**Audience:** Performance engineers, enterprise advertisers, growth teams, marketing scientists, data scientists, AI systems, Meta specialists, business intelligence teams.
**Scope:** This Framework governs Delivery only — Delivery Architecture, the full Delivery Pipeline, Auction mechanics, Auction Ranking factors, Learning, Delivery Status, Budget Pacing, Delivery Optimization, Delivery Signals, Frequency, Inventory, KPIs, Diagnostics, AI Delivery Intelligence, Knowledge Graph, Governance, and Best Practices. It does not explain Creative production (MF4), Audience creation (MF5), or Reporting in depth (reserved for a future Reporting Framework).
**Vocabulary Rule:** No Core Domain Object, Metric, Objective, Audience, Creative, or Optimization term already defined in Meta Frameworks 1–5 is redefined here. Every such term (especially MF1.3.18's Auction, MF1.3.25's Delivery, MF3.9's Ad-Set-layer Delivery Framework, and MF3.10's Learning Framework) is cited as `MF1.x`–`MF5.x` and extended with the full technical Delivery/Auction depth those Frameworks deliberately deferred to this one, per this document's Meta Framework 6 Vocabulary Rule stated in the banner above and per MAIFS.19.

---

## MF6.1 Delivery Architecture

### MF6.1.1 What Delivery Is

Delivery (MF1.3.25) is the process — and its resulting observable status — by which an Ad Set's Ads are actually served to users through the Auction (MF1.3.18). Where Meta Framework 3 introduced Delivery from the Ad Set's own operational perspective (MF3.9), this Framework treats Delivery as its own complete technical subject: the full pipeline from business intent to served impression, the Auction mechanics that govern every individual impression decision, and the Learning/pacing/signal systems that make Delivery predictable at scale.

### MF6.1.2 Business Role

Delivery is the point at which every upstream business decision — Objective (MF2), Optimization Goal (MF3.5), Audience (MF5), Creative (MF4), Budget (MF2.5/MF3.7) — converts into an actual, measurable outcome. A Business's entire Campaign strategy is only as good as its Delivery outcome; this Framework exists because Delivery behavior is frequently the least understood and most consequential layer in the entire document lineage, despite (or because of) being the layer no single upstream configuration decision fully controls on its own.

### MF6.1.3 Technical Role

Technically, Delivery is the output of a continuous, per-impression decision process (the Delivery Pipeline, MF6.2) executed by Meta's Auction system, informed by Learning-accumulated data (MF6.5), governed by Budget Pacing (MF6.7), and constrained by Inventory (MF6.11). Every configuration layer in MF2–MF5 is, from this Framework's perspective, an input to this single continuous technical process.

### MF6.1.4 Delivery Pipeline (Overview)

The complete 14-stage sequence from Business Goal to Optimization Feedback is delivered in full at MF6.2, per this Framework's own required structure. This entry establishes only that the Pipeline is the organizing structure the remainder of this Framework elaborates stage-by-stage (Auction mechanics elaborate the Auction Entry-through-Winning Probability stages specifically; Learning elaborates the Learning stage; Budget Pacing elaborates how the Pipeline's Auction Entry stage is gated over time).

### MF6.1.5 Delivery Lifecycle

```
Pre-Delivery (Campaign/Ad Set/Audience/Creative configured, not yet submitted) → Auction Entry Eligibility Established (MF6.2) 
 → Learning (MF6.5) → Learning Complete / Learning Limited → Stable Delivery → Status Transitions (MF6.6, e.g., Paused/Scheduled) 
   → Decline (Frequency Saturation, MF6.10, or Auction Competition shift) → Recovery or Retirement (at the parent Ad Set's own Lifecycle, MF3.2.4)
```
This Delivery-specific lifecycle view is narrower than, and nested within, the Ad Set Lifecycle (MF3.2.4) — a single Ad Set's Delivery can cycle through Learning→Stable→Decline→Recovery multiple times over that Ad Set's own longer lifespan.

### MF6.1.6 Delivery Dependencies

Delivery depends on: a valid, Auction-Entry-eligible Ad Set configuration (Campaign Objective, Optimization Goal, Audience, Placement, Budget, Bid Strategy — MF2/MF3); Creative meeting compliance/QA standards (MF4.17.5–6); available Inventory (MF6.11) within the configured Audience/Placement; and sufficient Budget Pacing headroom (MF6.7) for the current period. This is a Delivery-scoped restatement of MF1.14's Dependency Map and MF3.2.4's Ad Set Dependencies, not a new dependency system.

### MF6.1.7 Delivery Governance (Overview)

Every Delivery-affecting configuration change is subject to the same naming/documentation/version-control/approval discipline established at every prior layer (MF2.13/MF3.15/MF4.17/MF5.18), given Delivery-specific treatment in MF6.17. This entry establishes only that Delivery Architecture and Delivery Governance are related but distinct concerns, consistent with the pattern established at every prior layer.

### MF6.1.8 Relationship with Campaign

Cross-referenced from MF2.1.7/MF2.2.7 — the Campaign's Objective (MF1.3.12) and, where used, Campaign Budget (MF1.3.22) bound and shape every downstream Delivery Pipeline stage without being a Pipeline stage themselves; Campaign Budget's dynamic allocation (MF1.3.22) is itself implemented through the Budget Pacing mechanisms detailed at MF6.7.

### MF6.1.9 Relationship with Ad Set

Cross-referenced from MF3.1.8/MF3.9 — the Ad Set is the unit of Auction participation (MF3.1.10) and the level at which Learning Phase (MF3.10) is evaluated; this Framework's MF6.2–6.6 provide the complete technical depth behind MF3.9's Ad-Set-perspective summary.

### MF6.1.10 Relationship with Audience

Cross-referenced from MF5.1.5–6 — Audience bounds Auction Entry eligibility and Inventory availability (MF6.11.2); Audience Signal quality (MF5.6.1) directly feeds the Estimated Action Rate stage of the Delivery Pipeline (MF6.2.6).

### MF6.1.11 Relationship with Creative

Cross-referenced from MF4.7.1 — Creative Ad Quality (Quality/Engagement Rate/Conversion Rate Ranking, MF1.3.44–46) is a direct input to the Auction Ranking Formula (MF6.4); Creative Fatigue (MF1.9.12/MF4.12) is a primary driver of Delivery decline over an Ad Set's Delivery Lifecycle (MF6.1.5).

### MF6.1.12 Relationship with Auction

The Auction (MF1.3.18) is the mechanism at the center of this entire Framework — full architectural treatment at MF6.3, full ranking-factor treatment at MF6.4.

### MF6.1.13 Relationship with Learning

Learning (MF1.3.19/MF3.10) is both a Delivery Pipeline stage (MF6.2.12) and a precondition for every other Pipeline stage's outputs to be considered reliable — full Delivery-layer treatment at MF6.5.

### MF6.1.14 Relationship with Measurement

Delivery produces the raw Impressions/Reach/Clicks/Conversions data (MF1.3.25) that Measurement (MF1.4, cited but not re-explained per this Framework's Scope) reports on; this Framework treats Measurement only as the Delivery Pipeline's terminal output stage (MF6.2.13), not as a subject of its own depth.

### MF6.1.15 Relationship with Optimization

Delivery Optimization (MF6.8) is both a Pipeline stage (Optimization Feedback, MF6.2.14) and a continuous background process feeding back into every earlier stage — the Auction's own Estimated Action Rate predictions (MF6.2.6) improve as Optimization Feedback accumulates, closing the loop the full Pipeline diagram (MF6.2) depicts.

---

## MF6.2 Delivery Pipeline

The complete 14-stage sequence from business intent to Optimization Feedback, extending MF3.2.6–7's Ad-Set-perspective Optimization/Delivery Flow diagrams with full stage-by-stage technical depth.

```
Business Goal
  ↓
Campaign Eligibility
  ↓
Ad Set Eligibility
  ↓
Audience Eligibility
  ↓
Auction Entry
  ↓
Estimated Action Rate
  ↓
Bid
  ↓
Ad Quality
  ↓
Auction Ranking
  ↓
Winning Probability
  ↓
Delivery
  ↓
Learning
  ↓
Measurement
  ↓
Optimization Feedback (loops back to Estimated Action Rate)
```

### MF6.2.1 Business Goal

The originating business intent (MF2.1.5's translation-layer treatment) that determines Objective selection (MF2.4) — the Pipeline's true starting point, upstream of any platform-technical stage, establishing why every subsequent stage's outcome matters at all.

### MF6.2.2 Campaign Eligibility

The Campaign-level gate confirming the Campaign is Active (not Paused/Completed/Archived, MF2.2.6), within its Schedule, and has available Budget (Campaign Budget, MF1.3.22, or sufficient Account-level Spend Limit, MF3.7.5) before any contained Ad Set may proceed further in the Pipeline.

### MF6.2.3 Ad Set Eligibility

The Ad Set-level gate confirming the Ad Set is Active, within its own Schedule (MF3.8), has available Budget (Ad Set Budget, MF1.3.23, if applicable), and is not subject to a Delivery restriction (MF6.6's Delivery Status Framework) — this stage is the Pipeline's full elaboration of MF3.9.1's Auction Entry gating summary.

### MF6.2.4 Audience Eligibility

The confirmation that a specific user falls within the Ad Set's configured Audience (MF5.2) and eligible Placement (MF3.4) boundaries — the point at which Audience construction quality (MF5.3–6) becomes Pipeline-relevant for a given impression opportunity.

### MF6.2.5 Auction Entry

Cross-referenced from MF3.9.1. The point at which an eligible Ad (having passed Campaign/Ad Set/Audience Eligibility) is actually submitted into the Auction (MF1.3.18) for a specific impression opportunity, alongside every other eligible advertiser's Ads competing for the same opportunity.

### MF6.2.6 Estimated Action Rate

Cross-referenced from MF3.9.3. The Auction's prediction of how likely the specific user is to complete the Ad Set's configured Optimization Event (MF3.5) if shown this specific Ad — informed by Delivery Signals (MF6.9), sharpened over time by Learning (MF6.5) and Optimization Feedback (MF6.2.14).

### MF6.2.7 Bid

Cross-referenced from MF3.9.4/MF2.6. The Ad Set's Bid input, governed by its configured Bid Strategy, submitted alongside Estimated Action Rate and Ad Quality into the Auction Ranking calculation (MF6.4).

### MF6.2.8 Ad Quality

Cross-referenced from MF3.9.5/MF4.7.1. The Ranking-signal-informed quality input (Quality/Engagement Rate/Conversion Rate Ranking, MF1.3.44–46) reflecting the specific Ad's Creative and post-click experience quality relative to competing Ads.

### MF6.2.9 Auction Ranking

The composite calculation combining Bid (MF6.2.7), Estimated Action Rate (MF6.2.6), and Ad Quality (MF6.2.8) — plus the further factors (User Value, Context, Competition, Inventory, Placement, Optimization Goal) this Framework introduces at MF6.4 — into a single Total Value figure determining relative standing among competing Ads for a given impression.

### MF6.2.10 Winning Probability

The likelihood, derived from an Ad's Auction Ranking (MF6.2.9) relative to competing Ads', that it will actually be selected to fill a given impression opportunity — not a guarantee, since Auction Ranking determines relative standing probabilistically across the volume of similar impression opportunities rather than deterministically for any single one.

### MF6.2.11 Delivery (Pipeline Stage)

Cross-referenced from MF1.3.25/MF3.9. The realized outcome of won impression opportunities accruing into Impressions, Reach, Clicks, and Conversions — the Pipeline's central, most visible output stage.

### MF6.2.12 Learning (Pipeline Stage)

Cross-referenced from MF1.3.19/MF3.10, with full Delivery-layer depth at MF6.5. The stage at which accumulating Delivery outcomes are used to stabilize the Estimated Action Rate (MF6.2.6) predictions for the Ad Set's specific configuration.

### MF6.2.13 Measurement (Pipeline Stage)

The stage at which raw Delivery outcomes are aggregated into reportable Metrics (MF1.4) — this Framework treats Measurement only as this Pipeline stage per its stated Scope, deferring Reporting depth to a future Framework.

### MF6.2.14 Optimization Feedback

The stage at which Measurement outcomes (MF6.2.13) feed back into the Auction's own Estimated Action Rate model (MF6.2.6), closing the Pipeline's loop — this feedback is what allows Learning (MF6.2.12) to actually improve predictions over time rather than merely accumulating static historical data. Full Delivery Optimization treatment at MF6.8.

---

## MF6.3 Meta Auction Framework

Extends MF1.3.18 (Auction) with complete architectural depth.

### MF6.3.1 Auction Architecture

The Auction is not a single, periodic event but a continuous, per-impression-opportunity evaluation process running at massive scale — every time any user is about to be shown any content with an available ad slot, an independent Auction instance evaluates every currently-eligible advertiser's Ads (per MF6.2.3–4's Eligibility gates) for that specific opportunity.

### MF6.3.2 Auction Flow

```
Impression opportunity arises (a user opens a Placement surface, MF3.4)
 → Eligible Ads identified (Campaign/Ad Set/Audience Eligibility, MF6.2.2-4)
   → Each eligible Ad's Estimated Action Rate, Bid, Ad Quality computed (MF6.2.6-8)
     → Auction Ranking calculated per Ad (MF6.4)
       → Highest-Ranking Ad(s) win the opportunity (Winning Probability realized, MF6.2.10)
         → Delivery accrues to the winner (MF6.2.11)
```

### MF6.3.3 Auction Ranking (cross-reference)

Fully defined as a Pipeline stage at MF6.2.9, with the complete per-factor treatment delivered at MF6.4, per this Framework's own required structure.

### MF6.3.4 Auction Entry (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF3.9.1/MF6.2.5. Auction Architecture significance: Auction Entry is the gate determining the *set* of Ads competing in a given Auction Flow (MF6.3.2) instance — the Auction itself only ever evaluates Ads that have already cleared this gate.

### MF6.3.5 Auction Exit

- **Definition:** The point at which an Ad Set or Ad ceases to be eligible for further Auction Entry — via Budget exhaustion (MF6.7.6), Schedule end (MF3.8.2), manual pause, compliance rejection, or Campaign/Ad Set Retirement (MF2.7.11/MF3.2.4).
- **Role:** The inverse boundary condition to Auction Entry, together fully bounding the window during which a given Ad Set's Ads can ever compete.
- **Diagnostic Significance:** An unexpected Auction Exit (e.g., a Delivery Status Framework transition to "Limited" or "Rejected," MF6.6) is a primary Delivery Diagnostics (MF6.13) starting point.

### MF6.3.6 Auction Competition (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF2.10.9/MF3.9.10.
