Auction Architecture significance: Competition is not a fixed property of a market but a per-Auction-instance, per-impression-opportunity dynamic — the same Ad Set can face meaningfully different Competition levels across different times of day, Placements, or Audience segments simultaneously.

### MF6.3.7 Second Price Concepts

- **Definition:** A characterization of the general pricing principle underlying Meta's Auction — an advertiser's actual realized cost for a won impression is generally influenced by the Auction Ranking of the next-closest competing Ad, not simply their own submitted Bid, meaning advertisers are typically not charged their full Bid amount but closer to the minimum needed to win against genuine competition.
- **Role:** Explains why realized CPM/CPC (MF1.4.1/3) is often lower than an advertiser's configured Bid Cap (MF2.6.3), a source of common confusion addressed directly in MF6.4.10's Common Misconceptions.
- **Professional Notes:** This Framework describes the general pricing principle only, consistent with MAIFS.14's prohibition on prescribing implementation-level algorithmic detail — the precise, proprietary mechanics of Meta's own pricing calculation are outside this Framework's scope and, per this document lineage's Genericity/Agnosticism precedent, are not the kind of content any Framework in this Series attempts to specify.

### MF6.3.8 Value Optimization

- **Definition:** The Auction's overall objective — maximizing total ecosystem value (advertiser outcomes, user experience, and Meta's own marketplace health) rather than purely maximizing short-term Bid revenue, which is why Ad Quality (MF6.2.8) and Estimated Action Rate (MF6.2.6) are weighted alongside Bid rather than Bid alone determining outcomes.
- **Role:** The conceptual justification for why a lower-Bid, higher-Quality Ad can out-rank a higher-Bid, lower-Quality one (MF6.4's full Ranking Formula treatment) — Value Optimization at the Auction-design level, not to be confused with the Value optimization Optimization Event (MF3.5.12) at the Ad-Set-configuration level, a specific terminology-collision risk this Framework flags explicitly.
- **Common Mistake:** Conflating Auction-level Value Optimization (this entry) with Ad-Set-level Value Optimization Event selection (MF3.5.12) — the two share a name but operate at entirely different levels of this document lineage's Domain Architecture (MF1.2.1).

### MF6.3.9 Auction Dynamics

- **Definition:** The observation that Auction outcomes shift continuously as the population of competing advertisers, their Bids, their Ad Quality, and available Inventory (MF6.11) all change simultaneously and independently — no single advertiser's configuration exists in a static competitive environment.
- **Role:** The reason Benchmark data (MF1.11.5) must be continuously refreshed rather than treated as a fixed reference, and the reason Auction Competition (MF6.3.6) diagnostics (MF6.13) must always cross-check against current, not historical, comparison periods.
- **Practical Implication:** An Ad Set's performance can shift meaningfully with zero configuration change on the advertiser's own part, purely from Auction Dynamics — this is the single most important interpretive caveat this Framework introduces for every downstream Diagnostics (MF6.13) and Decision Framework (MF6.14) conclusion.

### MF6.3.10 Auction Intelligence

- **Definition:** The overarching term for how Meta's system continuously refines its Estimated Action Rate predictions (MF6.2.6) and Ranking calculations (MF6.4) using accumulated Learning (MF6.5) and Optimization Feedback (MF6.2.14) data, at both the individual-Ad-Set and platform-wide levels.
- **Role:** The conceptual umbrella under which this Framework's AI Delivery Intelligence section (MF6.15) provides its own dedicated depth — Auction Intelligence is the general capability; MF6.15 addresses specifically how an AI system (whether Meta's own or a Business's analytical layer) should interpret and reason about it.

---

## MF6.4 Auction Ranking Formula

Every factor feeding the Auction Ranking calculation (MF6.2.9), each analyzed for: How It Affects Delivery, Interaction With Other Factors. A consolidated How Meta Balances Them and Common Misconceptions treatment follows at MF6.4.10–11.

### MF6.4.1 Bid

- **How It Affects Delivery:** A higher Bid (MF6.2.7/MF2.6) generally improves Auction Ranking, all else equal, but is only one of multiple weighted factors, not the sole determinant (MF6.3.8's Value Optimization principle).
- **Interaction With Other Factors:** Under Second Price-style pricing dynamics (MF6.3.7), realized cost is influenced by competing Ads' Ranking, not Bid alone — a high Bid does not guarantee proportionally high realized cost.

### MF6.4.2 Estimated Action Rate

- **How It Affects Delivery:** A higher predicted likelihood of completing the configured Optimization Event (MF6.2.6) improves Ranking — this factor is why Optimization Event selection (MF3.5) and Audience Signal quality (MF5.6.1) matter as much as Bid for Auction success.
- **Interaction With Other Factors:** Improves over an Ad Set's life via Learning (MF6.5) and Optimization Feedback (MF6.2.14) — an identical Bid/Ad Quality combination can Rank differently for the same Ad Set at different points in its Learning trajectory.

### MF6.4.3 Ad Quality

- **How It Affects Delivery:** Higher Quality/Engagement Rate/Conversion Rate Ranking (MF1.3.44–46) improves Auction Ranking independent of Bid — this is the mechanism by which strong Creative (MF4) can outcompete a higher-Bid, lower-quality competitor.
- **Interaction With Other Factors:** Directly shaped by Creative production quality (MF4.10–11) and Audience-Creative Relevance (MF4.7.6) — a Quality issue should be diagnosed and remedied at the Creative layer (MF4.13), not compensated for by raising Bid.

### MF6.4.4 User Value

- **Definition:** Meta's own assessment of how valuable a given ad experience is likely to be to the specific user being shown it, incorporating signals beyond the advertiser's own Estimated Action Rate prediction — informed by the user's own historical interaction patterns, ad-load context, and platform-wide relevance signals.
- **How It Affects Delivery:** Higher User Value assessments improve Ranking for that specific user, independent of the advertiser's own configuration — this factor is the Auction's mechanism for protecting overall user experience alongside advertiser Value Optimization (MF6.3.8).
- **Interaction With Other Factors:** Correlates with, but is distinct from, Ad Quality (MF6.4.3) — Ad Quality reflects the specific Ad's own signals, while User Value reflects the receiving user's own context and likely receptiveness.

### MF6.4.5 Context

- **Definition:** The situational factors surrounding a specific impression opportunity — time of day, device, Placement surface (MF3.4), surrounding content — that inform how an Ad's relevance and likely performance should be assessed for that specific opportunity.
- **How It Affects Delivery:** The same Ad can Rank differently across different Contexts even for the identical user, since Estimated Action Rate (MF6.4.2) itself is Context-conditional (e.g., a Calls-optimized Ad's Estimated Action Rate is Context-dependent on whether it is currently within Business Hours, MF3.8.5).
- **Interaction With Other Factors:** Directly interacts with Scheduling (MF3.8) and Placement (MF3.4) configuration — advertiser-side Context-awareness (Dayparting, Placement selection) is how a Business can deliberately shape which Contexts its Ads are evaluated within.

### MF6.4.6 Competition

- Cross-referenced from MF6.3.6/MF2.10.9. **How It Affects Delivery:** Higher Competition for a given impression opportunity (more eligible advertisers with strong Ranking factors) raises the effective Ranking threshold needed to win, independent of any change to the advertiser's own configuration. **Interaction With Other Factors:** The primary source of Auction Dynamics (MF6.3.9) volatility — an advertiser's own Bid/Quality/Estimated Action Rate can remain constant while realized outcomes shift purely from Competition-side changes.

### MF6.4.7 Inventory

- Cross-referenced from the full treatment at MF6.11. **How It Affects Delivery:** The total available impression opportunities within an Ad Set's configured Audience/Placement bounds caps the ceiling on possible Delivery volume regardless of how favorably an Ad Ranks within each individual Auction instance. **Interaction With Other Factors:** Interacts directly with Placement breadth (MF3.4.2's Advantage+ Placements) and Audience breadth (MF5.6's Advantage+ Audience) — both expand available Inventory, a distinct lever from improving Ranking factors within a fixed Inventory pool.

### MF6.4.8 Placement

- Cross-referenced from MF3.4. **How It Affects Delivery:** Different Placements carry different baseline Competition levels, User Value patterns, and Ad Quality evaluation criteria (e.g., video-specific Ranking signals for Reels/In-Stream, MF3.4.7/10) — the same Ad's Ranking can differ meaningfully by Placement even holding Bid/Audience constant. **Interaction With Other Factors:** The primary lever, alongside Audience breadth, for expanding available Inventory (MF6.4.7) — Advantage+ Placements' (MF3.4.2) Ranking advantage stems partly from the larger Inventory pool it draws from.

### MF6.4.9 Optimization Goal

- Cross-referenced from MF1.3.13/MF3.5. **How It Affects Delivery:** Directly determines what Estimated Action Rate (MF6.4.2) is even predicting — the single highest-leverage Ranking-adjacent decision an advertiser controls, since a mismatched Optimization Goal produces a well-calibrated prediction for the wrong target behavior. **Interaction With Other Factors:** Every other factor in this Formula (Bid, Ad Quality, User Value, Context) is evaluated *relative to* the configured Optimization Goal — changing the Optimization Goal effectively changes what all other factors are being optimized to predict.

### MF6.4.10 Common Misconceptions

1. **"Highest Bid always wins"** — false; Bid is one weighted factor among several (MF6.3.8's Value Optimization), and a lower-Bid, higher-Quality Ad can out-rank a higher-Bid, lower-Quality one.
2. **"I pay exactly what I bid"** — generally false under Second Price-style dynamics (MF6.3.7); realized cost is typically influenced by the competitive Ranking landscape, not simply the advertiser's own submitted Bid.
3. **"A Ranking factor is static once configured"** — false; Estimated Action Rate improves with Learning (MF6.5), Competition shifts continuously (MF6.3.9), and User Value/Context are evaluated fresh per impression opportunity.
4. **"More Budget directly improves Ranking"** — false; Budget (MF6.7) governs how many Auction opportunities an Ad Set can pursue and its pacing, not its Ranking within any single Auction instance.
5. **"Ad Quality is purely subjective/unmeasurable"** — false; Ad Quality is expressed through the specific, defined Ranking signals (Quality/Engagement Rate/Conversion Rate Ranking, MF1.3.44–46), each independently diagnosable (MF4.13).

### MF6.4.11 How Meta Balances the Factors

This Framework does not specify the precise proprietary weighting Meta applies across Bid, Estimated Action Rate, Ad Quality, User Value, Context, Competition, Inventory, Placement, and Optimization Goal — doing so would constitute exactly the kind of implementation-level algorithmic detail MAIFS.14 and this document lineage's Genericity/Agnosticism precedent (EIFS.15, applied here to a Meta Ads Framework's own technical-mechanics content) exclude. What this Framework establishes, and what is professionally actionable, is the *qualitative* balancing principle already stated at MF6.3.8: no single factor deterministically dominates, Bid and Quality are jointly weighted (Value Optimization), and every factor is Context- and Competition-conditional rather than fixed. Advertiser-side professional practice should therefore treat every factor in this section as a lever to *improve*, never as a guaranteed, precisely-predictable input to a known formula.

---

## MF6.5 Learning Framework

Extends MF1.3.19 and MF3.10's Ad-Set-layer Learning Framework with Delivery-Pipeline-level depth — specifically, how Learning interacts with the Auction Ranking Formula (MF6.4) rather than repeating MF3.10's Ad-Set-configuration-scoped treatment.

### MF6.5.1 Learning Phase (Pipeline-Level View)

Cross-referenced from MF1.3.19/MF3.10.1/MF6.2.12. Pipeline-level significance: during Learning Phase, the Estimated Action Rate (MF6.4.2) input to Auction Ranking is itself unstable — every other Ranking factor may be held constant while Auction outcomes still vary, since the Ranking Formula's own input is still converging.

### MF6.5.2 Learning Limited (Pipeline-Level View)

Cross-referenced from MF1.10.2/MF3.10.2. Pipeline-level significance: an Ad Set in Learning Limited effectively never reaches a stable Estimated Action Rate (MF6.4.2) input, meaning its Auction Ranking remains persistently volatile or systematically conservative, not merely temporarily unstable as in ordinary Learning Phase.

### MF6.5.3 Learning Complete

Cross-referenced from MF3.10.3's "Learning Exit" terminology — Learning Complete is the equivalent state described in this Framework's own vocabulary (matching the Delivery Status Framework's naming convention, MF6.6), never a redefinition of MF3.10.3's concept. Pipeline-level significance: Learning Complete is the point at which Estimated Action Rate (MF6.4.2) is considered a stable Auction Ranking input, making Delivery outcomes attributable to genuine configuration/market factors rather than residual prediction convergence.

### MF6.5.4 Learning Reset (Pipeline-Level View)

Cross-referenced from MF3.10.4. Pipeline-level significance: every significant edit at any configuration layer (Audience, MF5; Creative, MF4; Optimization Goal/Bid Strategy, MF3.5/MF2.6) that triggers Learning Reset does so specifically because it invalidates the Estimated Action Rate (MF6.4.2) model that had been converging — the Reset is not an arbitrary penalty but a direct, mechanical consequence of the Ranking Formula's input changing.

### MF6.5.5 Optimization Window

- **Definition:** The rolling time period (commonly the 7-day window referenced throughout MF1.3.19) over which Meta's system evaluates accumulated Optimization Event volume to judge Learning Phase progress — distinct from the Attribution Window (MF1.7.3), which governs Conversion crediting rather than Learning evaluation.
- **Role:** The temporal boundary within which Required Events (MF6.5.6) must accumulate for Learning Complete (MF6.5.3) to be reached; an Ad Set accumulating events too slowly relative to this window (rather than too few in absolute terms) is functionally in the same Learning Limited (MF6.5.2) state.
- **Common Mistake:** Conflating the Optimization Window with the Attribution Window (MF1.7.3) — the two govern entirely different aspects of Delivery/Measurement despite both being time-bound concepts, a specific terminology-collision risk flagged explicitly, mirroring MF6.3.8's Value Optimization naming-collision flag.

### MF6.5.6 Required Events (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF3.10.5 (the ~50-events-in-7-days reference point). Pipeline-level significance: Required Events is the specific numeric threshold governing the Optimization Window (MF6.5.5) evaluation — the two concepts are companions, not synonyms (window = the time boundary, Required Events = the volume threshold within it).

### MF6.5.7 Learning Stability

Cross-referenced from MF3.10.6's "Optimization Stability" terminology — Learning Stability is this Framework's equivalent naming for the same underlying state (the state following Learning Complete/Learning Exit in which Estimated Action Rate has genuinely converged, not merely crossed the Required Events threshold once). Pipeline-level significance: Learning Stability, not the mere crossing of the Required Events threshold, is the correct precondition for treating Auction Ranking outcomes as a trustworthy baseline for Delivery Optimization Decision-making (MF6.14).

### MF6.5.8 Learning Signals

- **Definition:** The specific Delivery Signals (MF6.9) that most directly feed the Learning process's Estimated Action Rate refinement — primarily Conversion Signals and Behavioral Signals (MF6.9.4/7) at the individual-Ad-Set level, supplemented by Historical Signals (MF6.9.3) drawn from the Business's own prior Delivery history where available.
- **Role:** Explains why a Business with substantial prior Delivery history (strong Historical Signals) often reaches Learning Complete (MF6.5.3) faster on a new, similar Ad Set than a Business launching its first-ever Campaign — the Learning process is not purely Ad-Set-isolated but can draw on broader account-level signal.
- **Common Mistake:** Assuming every new Ad Set starts Learning from an identical blank state — Historical Signals (MF6.9.3) genuinely differentiate Learning speed across Businesses with different account maturity.

### MF6.5.9 Learning Risks

- **Premature Scaling Risk:** Scaling (MF3.13/MF5.15) before Learning Stability (MF6.5.7), not merely Learning Complete (MF6.5.3), is confirmed — mirroring MF2.7.4/MF3.10.6's existing caution, restated here as a Pipeline-level risk category.
- **Repeated Reset Risk:** Serial significant edits across multiple configuration layers preventing the Ad Set from ever reaching Learning Complete, per MF3.10.4's Common Mistakes.
- **False Stability Risk:** Mistaking a temporary lull in Auction Competition (MF6.3.6) for genuine Learning Stability, when the apparent stability is actually a Competition-driven, temporary Auction Dynamics (MF6.3.9) artifact rather than true Estimated Action Rate convergence.
- **Signal Starvation Risk:** An Ad Set with a genuinely rare Optimization Event (MF3.5.11's high-AOV Purchase example) structurally unable to accumulate Required Events (MF6.5.6) within the Optimization Window (MF6.5.5) regardless of Budget/Audience adjustments, absent a proxy-Event strategy (MF1.6.5).

### MF6.5.10 Recovery Strategy (Pipeline-Level View)

Cross-referenced from MF3.10.11's full ordered Recovery Method sequence (Audience Expansion → Bid Strategy relaxation → Budget increase → temporary proxy Optimization Event). Pipeline-level significance: every Recovery Method in that sequence works by increasing the rate of Auction Entry (MF6.2.5) or broadening Estimated Action Rate (MF6.2.6) signal availability — this Framework's contribution is making explicit that all four methods share this single underlying mechanism, rather than being four unrelated tactics.

---

## MF6.6 Delivery Status Framework

Expands MF3.9.7's 4-category Delivery Status summary (Active/Learning/Limited/Not Delivering) into the complete 10-status taxonomy, each analyzed for: Meaning, Causes, Business Impact, Technical Impact, Recovery Steps.

### MF6.6.1 Active

- **Meaning:** The Ad Set/Ad is fully eligible for Auction Entry and delivering normally, having passed Learning Complete (MF6.5.3) or otherwise showing stable Delivery.
- **Causes:** All Eligibility gates (MF6.2.2–4) satisfied; no restriction in force.
- **Business Impact:** The expected, healthy operating state — Metrics can be trusted per this stage's stability.
- **Technical Impact:** Full Auction Entry (MF6.2.5) participation across the configured Audience/Placement.
- **Recovery Steps:** Not applicable — this is the target state every other status either leads toward or represents a deviation from.

### MF6.6.2 Learning

- Cross-referenced from MF6.5.1. **Meaning:** Accumulating Optimization Event data toward Learning Complete (MF6.5.3); Estimated Action Rate not yet stable. **Causes:** Recent Ad Set creation or a significant edit (MF6.5.4). **Business Impact:** Metrics during this status should not be treated as a reliable performance baseline (MF1.3.19's Professional Notes). **Technical Impact:** Auction Ranking (MF6.2.9) outcomes remain volatile as Estimated Action Rate converges. **Recovery Steps:** No action needed if progressing normally; monitor Required Events accumulation rate (MF6.5.6) against the Optimization Window (MF6.5.5).

### MF6.6.3 Learning Limited

- Cross-referenced from MF6.5.2. **Meaning:** Insufficient Optimization Event volume/rate to ever reach Learning Complete under current configuration. **Causes:** Audience too narrow, Bid Strategy too restrictive, Budget too low, or a genuinely rare Optimization Event (MF6.5.9's Signal Starvation Risk). **Business Impact:** The Ad Set spends inefficiently indefinitely until addressed — a genuine failure state, not a temporary one. **Technical Impact:** Estimated Action Rate never stabilizes; Auction Ranking remains persistently unreliable. **Recovery Steps:** Apply MF6.5.10's Recovery Strategy sequence in order.

### MF6.6.4 In Review

- **Meaning:** The Ad/Ad Set is undergoing Meta's compliance/policy review process before being permitted to enter the Auction at all.
- **Causes:** Standard review triggered at creation or after a significant Creative/targeting edit; may also be triggered by automated policy-flag detection.
- **Business Impact:** Zero Delivery during this status — a launch-timeline risk that should be planned for (typically resolving within a short standard window, though special-category content, MF5.6.9, may take longer).
- **Technical Impact:** The Ad has not yet cleared Auction Entry Eligibility (MF6.2.3) at all; no Auction participation occurs.
- **Recovery Steps:** No advertiser action typically needed for standard review; verify Creative/Copy compliance proactively (MF4.17.6) to minimize the likelihood of an extended or failed review.

### MF6.6.5 Rejected

- **Meaning:** The Ad/Ad Set has failed Meta's compliance/policy review and is not eligible for Delivery.
- **Causes:** Policy violation in Creative content, Copy, landing-page experience, or targeting configuration (e.g., a special-category violation, MF5.6.9).
- **Business Impact:** Zero Delivery, requiring a Creative/configuration correction and resubmission before any Delivery can occur — a potentially significant timeline risk if discovered late.
- **Technical Impact:** Permanently blocked from Auction Entry (MF6.2.3) in its current form.
- **Recovery Steps:** Review the specific rejection reason provided, correct the identified Creative/Copy/targeting issue (cross-referencing MF4.17.6's Compliance guidance), and resubmit for review (returning to In Review, MF6.6.4).

### MF6.6.6 Limited

- Cross-referenced from MF3.9.8. **Meaning:** Auction-eligible but experiencing constrained Delivery due to a binding configuration restriction. **Causes:** Overly restrictive Bid Strategy (Bid/Cost Cap, MF2.6.2–3), Audience too narrow, or Budget insufficient relative to the Optimization Event's typical cost — the Delivery Status manifestation of the same root causes as Learning Limited (MF6.6.3), but can occur even post-Learning-Complete if a constraint tightens later. **Business Impact:** Suboptimal but non-zero Delivery; represents unrealized efficiency/volume. **Technical Impact:** Frequent loss at the Auction Ranking (MF6.2.9) stage due to the binding constraint, rather than complete Auction Entry exclusion. **Recovery Steps:** Diagnose the specific binding constraint (MF6.13's Diagnostics Framework) and apply the matched Delivery Optimization action (MF6.8).

### MF6.6.7 Scheduled

- **Meaning:** The Ad Set is configured with a future Start Date (MF3.8.2) and is not yet eligible for Auction Entry because that date has not yet arrived.
- **Causes:** A deliberate future-dated launch configuration.
- **Business Impact:** Expected, planned zero Delivery until the Start Date — not a failure state.
- **Technical Impact:** Excluded from Auction Entry Eligibility (MF6.2.3) purely on Schedule grounds.
- **Recovery Steps:** Not applicable — resolves automatically at the configured Start Date, assuming all other Eligibility gates are otherwise satisfied at that time.

### MF6.6.8 Paused

- **Meaning:** The Ad Set has been manually or programmatically paused, halting Delivery while retaining its full configuration and accumulated Learning/reporting history.
- **Causes:** Deliberate advertiser action (Campaign Lifecycle Maintenance decision, MF2.7.6, or a Diagnostics-driven pause, MF6.13) or an automated rule.
- **Business Impact:** Zero Delivery, fully reversible; no configuration or Learning history is lost.
- **Technical Impact:** Excluded from Auction Entry Eligibility (MF6.2.3) by explicit status, not by any underlying constraint.
- **Recovery Steps:** Resume (unpause) when ready; whether Learning Phase resumes from its prior state or restarts depends on the duration paused and whether any edits were made during the pause (an extended pause can itself function similarly to a significant edit for Learning purposes).

### MF6.6.9 Completed

- **Meaning:** The Ad Set has reached its configured End Date (MF3.8.2) or Lifetime Budget (MF3.7.3) exhaustion and has concluded its scheduled Delivery run.
- **Causes:** A fixed-duration or fixed-budget configuration reaching its natural endpoint.
- **Business Impact:** Expected, planned conclusion — the terminal state for a deliberately time-bound or budget-bound Ad Set, feeding into the Campaign/Ad Set Retirement stage (MF2.7.11/MF3.2.4).
- **Technical Impact:** Permanently excluded from further Auction Entry (MF6.2.3) under this configuration; a new Ad Set/Campaign would be required to resume similar Delivery.
- **Recovery Steps:** Not applicable — Completed is a terminal status; create a new Ad Set/Campaign if continued Delivery of a similar kind is desired.

### MF6.6.10 No Delivery

- **Meaning:** The Ad Set is nominally Active but showing zero or near-zero Impressions despite no explicit Paused/Scheduled/Rejected/Completed status.
- **Causes:** The most diagnostically ambiguous status in this catalog — potential causes span Budget exhaustion for the period, an extremely restrictive Bid Strategy, a near-zero eligible Audience/Placement combination, or an underlying technical/compliance issue not yet surfaced as an explicit Rejected status.
- **Business Impact:** Zero Delivery with no self-evident cause, requiring active Diagnostics investigation rather than a status-implied remedy.
- **Technical Impact:** Effectively failing Auction Entry (MF6.2.5) or Auction Ranking (MF6.2.9) so completely that no measurable Delivery results, without a specific status code explaining why.
- **Recovery Steps:** Apply MF6.13.1's No Delivery diagnostic model directly — verify Schedule and Budget status first (lowest-effort checks), then escalate to Bid Strategy, Audience/Placement, and compliance review in that order.

### MF6.6.11 Delivery Status Summary Table

| Status | Auction Entry? | Business Impact | Typical Resolution Path |
|---|---|---|---|
| Active | Yes | Healthy | N/A |
| Learning | Yes | Metrics not yet trustworthy | Monitor progress |
| Learning Limited | Yes (ineffectively) | Persistent inefficiency | MF6.5.10 Recovery Strategy |
| In Review | No | Launch delay | Wait / verify compliance |
| Rejected | No | Blocked | Correct and resubmit |
| Limited | Yes (constrained) | Suboptimal volume | MF6.13 Diagnostics → MF6.8 Optimization |
| Scheduled | No (not yet) | Planned delay | Automatic at Start Date |
| Paused | No | Reversible pause | Resume when ready |
| Completed | No (terminal) | Planned conclusion | New Ad Set/Campaign if continuing |
| No Delivery | Ambiguous | Requires investigation | MF6.13.1 diagnostic model |

---

## MF6.7 Budget Pacing Framework

Extends MF2.5/MF3.7's Budget Control Frameworks with the Delivery-mechanics-level treatment of how Budget is actually spent over time.

### MF6.7.1 Budget Pacing

- **Definition:** The rate and pattern at which a configured Budget (MF1.3.21) is actually consumed over its Daily or Lifetime period, governed by Meta's system to avoid exhausting Budget too early or too unevenly within the period.
- **Role:** The Delivery-mechanics-level implementation of what MF2.5/MF3.7 describe at the configuration level — Budget Pacing is what actually happens between "Budget is set" and "Budget is spent."
- **Relationship to Auction:** Budget Pacing governs Auction Entry (MF6.2.5) rate over time — an Ad Set does not necessarily enter every eligible Auction opportunity immediately upon eligibility if doing so would exhaust Budget too early in the period.

### MF6.7.2 Daily Budget Distribution

- Extends MF3.7.2. **Mechanics:** Spend is distributed across the day's Auction opportunities with some deliberate variance (commonly up to double the average on any single day, per MF3.7.2) rather than a perfectly even hourly split, allowing the system to pursue efficient opportunities as they arise rather than artificially rationing spend hour-by-hour. **Common Mistake:** Interpreting a single hour's or day's spend spike as anomalous rather than expected Daily Budget Distribution variance.

### MF6.7.3 Lifetime Budget Distribution

- Extends MF3.7.3. **Mechanics:** Spend is paced across the entire configured Start-to-End Date window, optionally weighted by Budget Scheduling/Dayparting (MF3.7.4, MF3.8.3) — the system aims to avoid exhausting a Lifetime Budget significantly before the End Date arrives, which would leave later-period Auction opportunities unpursued. **Common Mistake:** Setting a Lifetime Budget so large relative to achievable Delivery volume that meaningful underspend occurs, or so small relative to the window that Budget Exhaustion (MF6.7.6) occurs well before the End Date.

### MF6.7.4 Spend Acceleration

- **Definition:** A deliberate or system-driven increase in the rate of Budget consumption relative to the default pacing pattern, pursued when strong Auction opportunities are available and the system predicts spending faster will not meaningfully harm efficiency.
- **Role:** The mechanism underlying favorable Auction Dynamics (MF6.3.9) periods (e.g., unusually low Competition) translating into faster-than-average Delivery, rather than the system rigidly adhering to a fixed pacing curve regardless of opportunity quality.
- **Risk:** Uncontrolled Spend Acceleration during a temporary favorable window could exhaust a Daily/Lifetime Budget faster than intended, foreclosing participation in later, potentially equally-good opportunities.

### MF6.7.5 Spend Limitation

- **Definition:** A deliberate constraint on the rate of Budget consumption, applied when the system predicts spending at the default pace would exhaust Budget before capturing an adequate spread of Auction opportunities across the full period.
- **Role:** The counterbalancing mechanism to Spend Acceleration (MF6.7.4) — Budget Pacing (MF6.7.1) is fundamentally a balance between these two opposing pressures across the full configured period.
- **Risk:** Overly aggressive Spend Limitation (functionally similar to an overly restrictive Bid Strategy, MF2.6.2–3) can produce the Limited Delivery Status (MF6.6.6) even when total Budget is adequate, simply because the pacing constraint prevents full utilization within the period.

### MF6.7.6 Budget Exhaustion

- **Definition:** The point at which a Daily or Lifetime Budget is fully consumed, halting further Auction Entry (MF6.2.5) until the next period (Daily) or permanently for the configured run (Lifetime, transitioning to Completed Status, MF6.6.9).
- **Diagnostic Significance:** Premature Budget Exhaustion (well before the expected pacing curve would predict) can indicate Spend Acceleration (MF6.7.4) driven by unusually favorable, possibly temporary, Auction Dynamics (MF6.3.9) — worth investigating via Budget Efficiency (MF6.7.7) analysis rather than assuming it is purely positive.
- **Common Mistake:** Treating any Budget Exhaustion as inherently positive ("we used our whole Budget") without checking whether the resulting Delivery was genuinely efficient or merely fast.

### MF6.7.7 Budget Efficiency

- **Definition:** The relationship between Budget consumed and business outcome achieved (the underlying concept behind every Financial KPI, MF1.4.5–6/MF3.11.6/MF5.12.10-11), evaluated specifically through the lens of whether Pacing itself contributed to or detracted from that relationship.
- **Role:** Distinguishes "spent the full Budget" (a pacing/exhaustion fact) from "spent the Budget well" (an efficiency judgment) — the two are related but not identical, and Budget Pacing analysis should always evaluate both.
- **Diagnostic Signal:** Budget Exhaustion (MF6.7.6) occurring alongside declining marginal efficiency late in the period (the same pattern as Budget Saturation, MF2.10.8) indicates Pacing successfully used the full Budget but at declining Efficiency — a nuanced outcome neither purely positive nor negative.

### MF6.7.8 Budget Stability

- **Definition:** The consistency of Budget Pacing behavior across successive periods (days, for Daily Budget; or across the full window, for Lifetime Budget) absent any underlying configuration change.
- **Role:** A healthy, stable Ad Set should show reasonably consistent Pacing patterns period-to-period; significant, unexplained Pacing volatility (e.g., wildly different daily spend patterns with no Dayparting or Auction Competition explanation) is itself a Diagnostics-worthy signal (MF6.13.13's Poor Delivery Stability).
- **Relationship to Auction Dynamics:** Some Pacing variability is expected and healthy, reflecting genuine Auction Dynamics (MF6.3.9) responsiveness (Spend Acceleration/Limitation, MF6.7.4–5) — Budget Stability concerns instability beyond this expected, opportunity-responsive variance.

### MF6.7.9 Pacing Algorithms

This Framework does not specify the precise proprietary mechanics of Meta's own Pacing Algorithms — doing so would constitute the same kind of implementation-level detail excluded at MF6.3.7/MF6.4.11 under MAIFS.14 and this document lineage's Genericity/Agnosticism precedent. What this Framework establishes is the algorithm's *functional* behavior: balancing Spend Acceleration against Spend Limitation (MF6.7.4–5) across the configured period to maximize Budget Efficiency (MF6.7.7) while avoiding both premature Budget Exhaustion (MF6.7.6) and end-of-period underspend, informed by real-time Auction Dynamics (MF6.3.9) and the Ad Set's own Learning Stability status (MF6.5.7).

---

## MF6.8 Delivery Optimization Framework

Extends MF3.5's complete Optimization & Delivery catalog with Delivery-Pipeline-mechanics-specific depth — this section does not re-derive MF3.5's per-Event field analysis (Optimization Event, Meta Learning Behavior, Auction Impact, Scaling Readiness, Risk Level, Expected Delivery Pattern, Ideal Business Cases), only how each category interacts with the Pipeline stages this Framework introduces (MF6.2–6.7).

### MF6.8.1 Optimization Goal (Pipeline-Level View)

Cross-referenced from MF1.3.13/MF6.4.9. Pipeline-level significance: Optimization Goal is the input that defines what Estimated Action Rate (MF6.2.6) predicts — every category below (MF6.8.2–10) is a specific Optimization Goal family, and the Pipeline treats each identically in mechanism (feeding Estimated Action Rate) while differing in what specifically is being predicted.

### MF6.8.2 Event Optimization

- **Definition:** The general mechanism by which any specific Event (MF1.6) is designated as the Optimization Goal's target, of which every named category below (Conversion, Purchase, Lead, Message, Reach, Traffic Optimization) is a specific instance.
- **Pipeline Role:** The unifying concept underlying MF6.8.3–10 — Event Optimization is the general case, the remaining entries are its named specializations already catalogued in full at MF3.5.

### MF6.8.3 Delivery Optimization (General Mechanism)

- **Definition:** The overarching term for how the Auction Ranking Formula (MF6.4) and Budget Pacing (MF6.7) jointly work to maximize the configured Optimization Goal's outcome within Budget — the Pipeline-level synthesis of Auction mechanics (MF6.3–4) and pacing mechanics (MF6.7) toward a single business-relevant target.
- **Pipeline Role:** Represents the complete closed loop this Framework's Pipeline diagram (MF6.2) depicts — Delivery Optimization is not a separate stage but the emergent behavior of every stage working together toward the configured Optimization Goal.

### MF6.8.4 Value Optimization (Optimization-Goal-Level View)

Cross-referenced from MF3.5.12/MF2.6.4–5. Distinct from Auction-level Value Optimization (MF6.3.8) — this entry addresses the Optimization Goal specifically pursuing Purchase value rather than count, per the terminology-collision flag already raised at MF6.3.8's Common Mistake.

### MF6.8.5 Conversion Optimization

Cross-referenced from MF3.5.1 (Conversions). Pipeline-level note: the most Estimated-Action-Rate-data-hungry Event Optimization category, per MF6.5.6's Required Events threshold, given Conversions' typically lower volume than click- or engagement-based Events.

### MF6.8.6 Purchase Optimization

Cross-referenced from MF3.5.11 (Purchases). Pipeline-level note: directly feeds Value Optimization (MF6.8.4) when paired with a value-based Bid Strategy (MF2.6.4–5); Auction Ranking (MF6.4) for Purchase-optimized Ad Sets weighs Estimated Action Rate against a typically higher-value, lower-volume Conversion Event than most other categories.

### MF6.8.7 Lead Optimization

Cross-referenced from MF3.5.9 (Leads). Pipeline-level note: Estimated Action Rate quality for Lead Optimization is particularly sensitive to CRM-fed quality feedback (MF1.5.4's Professional Notes) — without it, the Auction's own Estimated Action Rate model optimizes toward submission likelihood only, not genuine lead quality, a Pipeline-level restatement of MF1.3.32's volume-vs-quality risk.

### MF6.8.8 Message Optimization

Cross-referenced from MF3.5.8 (Messages). Pipeline-level note: Auction Ranking for Message-optimized Ad Sets is evaluated specifically within Messenger/WhatsApp/Instagram Direct Placement contexts (MF3.4.5), where Context (MF6.4.5) plays a particularly significant role given the conversational, real-time nature of the target Event.

### MF6.8.9 Reach Optimization

Cross-referenced from MF3.5.4 (Reach). Pipeline-level note: the least Estimated-Action-Rate-dependent Event Optimization category, since Reach itself (unique-user delivery) rather than a specific user action is the target — this is why Reach Optimization shows the fastest, most reliable Learning Complete (MF6.5.3) among all categories, per MF3.5.4's own Scaling Readiness assessment.

### MF6.8.10 Traffic Optimization

Cross-referenced from MF3.5.2–3 (Landing Page Views, Link Clicks). Pipeline-level note: Auction Ranking for Traffic-optimized Ad Sets weighs a comparatively high-volume, low-friction Estimated Action Rate target, making Competition (MF6.4.6) and Inventory (MF6.4.7) the more commonly binding constraints relative to Estimated-Action-Rate data scarcity.

### MF6.8.11 Delivery Optimization Category Summary

| Category | Pipeline Data Intensity | Primary Auction Ranking Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|
| Conversion Optimization | High | Estimated Action Rate convergence speed |
| Purchase/Value Optimization | Very High | Estimated Action Rate + value-tracking accuracy |
| Lead Optimization | Moderate | Estimated Action Rate quality (CRM-feedback-dependent) |
| Message Optimization | Moderate | Context (Placement-specific) |
| Reach Optimization | Very Low | Inventory/Competition (minimal Estimated Action Rate dependency) |
| Traffic Optimization | Low | Competition/Inventory |

---

## MF6.9 Delivery Signals Framework

Every category of signal feeding the Auction's Estimated Action Rate (MF6.2.6) and broader Delivery decision-making, each analyzed for: Definition, Role in Delivery, Relationship to Other Signals.

### MF6.9.1 Audience Signals

- Cross-referenced from MF3.3.6/MF5.6.1. **Role in Delivery:** The advertiser-supplied or Audience-construction-derived input shaping who the Auction considers eligible/relevant for a given Ad Set — the most directly advertiser-controllable signal category in this catalog.

### MF6.9.2 Creative Signals

- **Definition:** The signals derived from a specific Creative's own attributes and historical performance (Quality/Engagement Rate/Conversion Rate Ranking, MF1.3.44–46; format and Hook characteristics, MF4.2/5) feeding the Ad Quality input to Auction Ranking (MF6.4.3).
- **Role in Delivery:** The Creative-layer counterpart to Audience Signals — while Audience Signals shape *who* is evaluated, Creative Signals shape how favorably *this specific Ad* is evaluated for that population.
- **Relationship to Other Signals:** Interacts directly with Behavioral Signals (MF6.9.7) — a Creative's Engagement Rate Ranking is itself derived from aggregated user behavioral response to it.

### MF6.9.3 Historical Signals

- **Definition:** Signals drawn from a Business's own prior Delivery history — past Campaign/Ad Set performance, prior Learning outcomes, established Audience/Creative combinations that have previously succeeded.
- **Role in Delivery:** Explains why an account with substantial prior Delivery history often achieves faster Learning Complete (MF6.5.3) and more favorable initial Estimated Action Rate estimates on new, similar Ad Sets than a brand-new account (MF6.5.8's Learning Signals treatment).
- **Relationship to Other Signals:** Functions as a prior/baseline that Conversion and Behavioral Signals (MF6.9.4/7) refine for each specific new Ad Set, rather than a wholly independent signal category.

### MF6.9.4 Conversion Signals

- **Definition:** Signals derived from actual Conversion Event (MF1.3.30) occurrences — the direct behavioral evidence of the Optimization Goal's target action actually occurring.
- **Role in Delivery:** The most direct, highest-value input to Estimated Action Rate (MF6.2.6) refinement for Conversion-based Optimization Goals (MF6.8.5–7) specifically — this is the signal category Learning (MF6.5) is fundamentally accumulating.
- **Relationship to Other Signals:** Depends entirely on Pixel/CAPI Signal (MF6.9.5–6) infrastructure being correctly implemented to be captured at all — a Conversion Signal cannot exist without an underlying tracking mechanism reporting it.

### MF6.9.5 Pixel Signals

- Cross-referenced from MF1.3.6/MF5.4.2. **Role in Delivery:** The browser-side data-capture mechanism producing Conversion and Behavioral Signals (MF6.9.4/7) for website-based Optimization Goals — subject to the same browser-tracking degradation concerns flagged throughout MF1/MF5 (MF1.3.31's Professional Notes).

### MF6.9.6 CAPI Signals

- Cross-referenced from MF1.3.7/MF5.4.3 (Conversions API). **Role in Delivery:** The server-side complement/substitute for Pixel Signals, generally improving signal completeness and, by extension, Estimated Action Rate reliability where browser-side tracking alone would be degraded — properly Deduplicated (MF1.6.16) against Pixel Signals to avoid double-counting the same underlying Conversion Signal.

### MF6.9.7 Behavioral Signals

- **Definition:** Signals derived from general user interaction patterns (engagement depth, video watch behavior, click patterns) beyond formally-defined Conversion Events specifically.
- **Role in Delivery:** Feeds Estimated Action Rate refinement for Engagement/Awareness-oriented Optimization Goals (MF6.8.9 Reach, and Engagement-family categories) where a formal Conversion Event is not the target.
- **Relationship to Other Signals:** The broader category Creative Signals' (MF6.9.2) Engagement Rate Ranking component is itself derived from.

### MF6.9.8 Inventory Signals

- Cross-referenced from MF6.4.7/MF6.11. **Role in Delivery:** Real-time signals about available impression opportunities within a given Audience/Placement configuration, informing both Auction Ranking (via the Inventory factor, MF6.4.7) and Budget Pacing (via how quickly opportunities are likely to arise, MF6.7.1).

### MF6.9.9 Seasonality

- **Definition:** Time-correlated signals reflecting predictable, recurring demand and Competition patterns (holiday periods, day-of-week patterns, industry-specific cycles) distinct from genuinely novel Auction Dynamics shifts (MF6.3.9).
- **Role in Delivery:** Informs Budget Pacing (MF6.7) and Auction Competition (MF6.4.6) expectations — the signal category underlying MF3.8.7–8's Seasonal/Holiday Scheduling guidance and MF2.10.9's "genuinely seasonal" branch of the Auction Competition diagnostic.
- **Relationship to Other Signals:** A predictable subset of Competition Signals (MF6.9.10) specifically, distinguishable from genuinely novel competitive shifts by its recurring, forecastable pattern.

### MF6.9.10 Competition

- Cross-referenced from MF6.3.6/MF6.4.6. **Role in Delivery:** The aggregate signal of how many other advertisers, at what Bid/Quality levels, are pursuing the same impression opportunities — the primary source of Auction Dynamics (MF6.3.9) volatility, partially predictable via Seasonality (MF6.9.9) and partially genuinely novel.

### MF6.9.11 Delivery Signals Interaction Summary

Every signal category in this catalog ultimately feeds one of two Auction Ranking inputs: Estimated Action Rate (Audience, Creative, Historical, Conversion, Pixel, CAPI, Behavioral Signals — MF6.9.1–7) or the Competition/Inventory context the Ranking is evaluated within (Inventory, Seasonality, Competition — MF6.9.8–10). Diagnosing a Delivery issue (MF6.13) at the signal-category level, rather than only at the symptom level, is what distinguishes an actionable root-cause finding from a purely descriptive one.

---

## MF6.10 Frequency Framework

Extends MF1.4.7 (Frequency) and MF5.11 (Audience Saturation) with Delivery-Pipeline-mechanics-specific depth.

### MF6.10.1 Frequency (Pipeline-Level View)

Cross-referenced from MF1.3.26/MF1.4.7. Pipeline-level significance: Frequency is a derived Delivery outcome (Impressions ÷ Reach), not itself an Auction Ranking input — but its trend is one of the most important Delivery Signals (MF6.9) for diagnosing whether continued Auction Entry into the same Audience pool remains efficient.

### MF6.10.2 Frequency Distribution

- **Definition:** The spread of Frequency across individual users within an Audience, as distinct from the single averaged Frequency figure typically reported — some users may be seeing an Ad far more or far less often than the average suggests.
- **Role:** A more granular diagnostic than average Frequency alone; a healthy-looking average Frequency can mask a subset of the Audience experiencing genuinely excessive repetition (heavy Distribution skew) while the majority sees the Ad rarely.
- **Diagnostic Significance:** Where available, Frequency Distribution analysis should precede broad Audience Expansion decisions (MF5.14.1) — if excess Frequency is concentrated in a small, identifiable sub-segment, a targeted Exclusion (MF5.3.24) may be more precise than broadening the whole Audience.

### MF6.10.3 Frequency Saturation (cross-reference)

Fully treated as Audience Saturation at MF1.8.8/MF5.11. Pipeline-level significance: Frequency Saturation is the Delivery-observable symptom; Reach Exhaustion (MF5.11.3) is its Audience-construction-level root cause — this Framework treats the two as cause-and-effect within the Delivery Pipeline, not synonyms.

### MF6.10.4 Optimal Frequency

- **Definition:** The Frequency level at which a given Ad Set achieves the best balance of Reach efficiency and message reinforcement for its specific Objective and Audience type, beyond which further Frequency produces diminishing or negative returns.
- **Role:** Not a universal number — as established at MF1.4.7's Healthy Range treatment, Optimal Frequency varies by Objective (broad Awareness prospecting tolerates lower Optimal Frequency than a small Retargeting Audience, MF1.8.5) and must be benchmarked against the Business's own historical data (MF1.11.5).
- **Relationship to Other Concepts:** The threshold Frequency Saturation (MF6.10.3) is diagnosed relative to — Saturation is specifically Frequency exceeding this Audience/Objective-specific Optimal level, not any fixed absolute number.

### MF6.10.5 Fatigue (Pipeline-Level View)

Cross-referenced from MF1.9.12/MF4.12/MF5.11. Pipeline-level significance: Fatigue (in any of its Creative/Audience sub-type forms, MF4.12.2–5) manifests in the Delivery Pipeline as declining Estimated Action Rate accuracy or realized performance at a given Frequency level that previously performed well — the Pipeline's own signal that the current Audience-Creative combination's effective lifespan is ending.

### MF6.10.6 Frequency Diagnostics

Extends MF2.10.5/MF3.12.7's existing Frequency Increase diagnostic models with the Frequency Distribution (MF6.10.2) lens: (1) confirm genuine Trend (MF1.11.8), not single-period variance; (2) check Frequency Distribution for concentration versus even spread; (3) if concentrated in an identifiable sub-segment, apply targeted Exclusion; (4) if evenly spread, diagnose as general Reach Exhaustion (MF5.11.3) and apply Audience Expansion/Refresh (MF5.14.1/6).

### MF6.10.7 Recovery (Frequency-Specific)

Cross-referenced from MF5.11.6–8's full Audience Refresh/Expansion/Replacement Strategy treatment and MF4.14.1's Creative Refresh. Pipeline-level significance: Frequency-driven Recovery actions work by either increasing Reach (Audience-side Expansion) or resetting Estimated Action Rate favorability at the existing Frequency level (Creative-side Refresh) — the correct choice depends on the MF6.10.6 diagnostic outcome (Audience-side Reach Exhaustion versus Creative-side Fatigue).

### MF6.10.8 Rotation (Frequency-Management Context)

Cross-referenced from MF1.10.10/MF4.12.10/MF5.14.8. Pipeline-level significance: proactive Rotation (scheduled via the Testing Calendar, MF4.8.10) prevents Frequency from ever reaching the Saturation threshold in the first place, avoiding the reactive Recovery cycle entirely — the Delivery Pipeline's preferred long-term Frequency-management posture for Always On (MF3.8.1) Ad Sets specifically.

---

## MF6.11 Inventory Framework

Extends MF6.4.7's Auction Ranking factor treatment of Inventory with complete Delivery-mechanics depth.

### MF6.11.1 Placements Inventory

- **Definition:** The total available impression opportunities across an Ad Set's eligible Placements (MF3.4), varying by Placement popularity, format support, and overall platform usage patterns.
- **Role:** The Placement-side determinant of Inventory (MF6.4.7) — Advantage+ Placements (MF3.4.2) draws from a larger Placements Inventory pool than a Manually-restricted (MF3.4.1) configuration, directly explaining that configuration's typically stronger Learning/scaling characteristics.

### MF6.11.2 Audience Inventory

- **Definition:** The total available impression opportunities within an Ad Set's eligible Audience (MF5.2), varying by Audience breadth/construction choice.
- **Role:** The Audience-side determinant of Inventory (MF6.4.7) — directly corresponds to Reach Exhaustion (MF5.11.3) as the point at which Audience Inventory, for practical purposes, has been fully drawn upon.

### MF6.11.3 Auction Availability

- **Definition:** The real-time presence of an actual impression opportunity matching an Ad Set's Placements Inventory (MF6.11.1) and Audience Inventory (MF6.11.2) bounds at any given moment — distinct from the theoretical total Inventory size, since Availability reflects genuine, current user activity.
- **Role:** Explains why an Ad Set with a theoretically large combined Inventory can still show inconsistent Delivery pacing if actual user activity (Auction Availability) within that Inventory fluctuates by time of day/week (Seasonality, MF6.9.9) — Inventory size and Inventory Availability are related but distinct concepts.

### MF6.11.4 Inventory Competition

- **Definition:** The degree to which other advertisers are simultaneously drawing on the same Placements/Audience Inventory pool, the Inventory-specific lens on the broader Competition concept (MF6.4.6/MF6.9.10).
- **Role:** High Inventory Competition can produce Limited Delivery (MF6.6.6) symptoms even with an adequately-sized Inventory pool in absolute terms, since the *effective* available Inventory (after competing advertisers' own claims on it) is what actually matters for a given Ad Set's realistic Winning Probability (MF6.2.10).

### MF6.11.5 Inventory Saturation

- **Definition:** The state in which an Ad Set has effectively exhausted its realistically available Inventory (Placements × Audience × Auction Availability, net of Inventory Competition) such that further Budget cannot translate into proportional additional Delivery — the Inventory-Framework-level restatement of Budget Saturation (MF2.10.8).
- **Role:** The unifying concept connecting Reach Exhaustion (MF5.11.3, Audience-side), Placements Inventory limits (MF6.11.1, Placement-side), and Inventory Competition (MF6.11.4, competitive-side) into a single diagnostic conclusion — Inventory Saturation can stem from any one or a combination of these three sources.

### MF6.11.6 Inventory Constraints

- **Definition:** Advertiser-imposed limitations on which Inventory is eligible for a given Ad Set — Manual Placement restrictions (MF3.4.1), narrow Audience construction (MF5.3), or special-category Restrictions (MF5.6.9) applied for compliance reasons.
- **Role:** Distinguishes self-imposed Inventory limitation (correctable via configuration change) from genuinely external Inventory Saturation (MF6.11.5, requiring Horizontal Scaling or market-level patience) — the first diagnostic branch point when investigating any Inventory-related Delivery symptom (MF6.13.2's Limited Delivery model).

### MF6.11.7 Inventory Diagnostic Decision Tree

```
Delivery constrained despite adequate Budget/Bid Strategy?
 ├─ Advertiser-imposed Inventory Constraint present (Manual Placement, narrow Audience, special-category Restriction)? 
 │    └─ Yes → Relax the specific Constraint (MF3.4.2, MF5.14.1) — self-correctable
 └─ No advertiser-imposed Constraint identified
      ├─ Auction Availability (MF6.11.3) genuinely low for current time/context → Seasonality-aware expectation adjustment (MF6.9.9)
      └─ Inventory Competition (MF6.11.4) high → Horizontal Scaling into different Inventory (MF3.13.1/MF5.15.1) or accept the competitive cost premium
```

---

## MF6.12 Delivery KPI Framework

The official Delivery-layer KPI library. Metrics already defined at MF1.4/MF4.9/MF5.12 are cited, not restated; every remaining KPI is new Delivery-Pipeline-specific content.

### MF6.12.1 Reach (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.3.27. Delivery-layer role: the primary Audience Inventory (MF6.11.2) utilization indicator.

### MF6.12.2 Impressions (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.3.28. Delivery-layer role: the primary Placements Inventory (MF6.11.1) utilization indicator.

### MF6.12.3 Frequency (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.4.7, full Delivery-layer treatment at MF6.10.

### MF6.12.4 CPM (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.4.1. Delivery-layer role: the primary Auction Competition (MF6.4.6) and Inventory Competition (MF6.11.4) cost indicator.

### MF6.12.5 CTR (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.4.2. Delivery-layer role: a Creative Signal (MF6.9.2) input to Estimated Action Rate for click-based Optimization Goals.

### MF6.12.6 Unique CTR (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF4.9.2. Delivery-layer role: distinguishes genuine Reach-breadth response from repeat-click concentration within a smaller Frequency Distribution (MF6.10.2) segment.

### MF6.12.7 CPC (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.4.3. Delivery-layer role: the composite outcome of CPM (Auction Competition) and CTR (Creative/Signal quality) — never diagnosed independently, per MF1.4.3's own decomposition guidance.

### MF6.12.8 CPA (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.4.5. Delivery-layer role: the ultimate cost-efficiency outcome of the full Pipeline (MF6.2) operating on a specific Optimization Goal.

### MF6.12.9 ROAS (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF1.4.6. Delivery-layer role: the ultimate value-efficiency outcome for Value/Purchase Optimization Goals (MF6.8.4/6) specifically.

### MF6.12.10 Delivery Rate

- **Definition:** The proportion of an Ad Set's theoretically available Inventory (MF6.11) actually converted into realized Impressions over a given period.
- **Diagnostic Role:** A low Delivery Rate relative to Inventory size, holding Budget/Bid Strategy adequate, is the core quantitative signature of the Limited Delivery Status (MF6.6.6) and a primary input to the Inventory Diagnostic Decision Tree (MF6.11.7).

### MF6.12.11 Auction Win Rate

- **Definition:** The proportion of Auction Entry attempts (MF6.2.5) that result in a won impression (realized Winning Probability, MF6.2.10), for the Auctions an Ad Set actually participates in.
- **Diagnostic Role:** Distinguishes an Inventory-availability problem (few Auction Entry opportunities at all, MF6.11) from an Auction-competitiveness problem (adequate opportunities, but consistently losing them, pointing to Bid/Ad Quality/Estimated Action Rate weakness, MF6.13.9–10) — a low Auction Win Rate specifically implicates the latter.

### MF6.12.12 Estimated Action Rate (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF3.9.3/MF6.2.6/MF6.4.2. Delivery-layer role: not directly exposed as a advertiser-visible number, but inferable from Delivery efficiency trends and central to nearly every diagnostic model in this Framework (MF6.13).

### MF6.12.13 Cost Efficiency

- **Definition:** A general term synthesizing CPM, CPC, CPA, and ROAS (MF6.12.4/7–9) into a single directional assessment of whether a given Ad Set's Delivery cost profile is healthy relative to its Objective and Benchmark (MF1.11.5).
- **Diagnostic Role:** The umbrella KPI category this Framework's Diagnostics section (MF6.13) most frequently references when a specific decomposed cause (CPM vs. CTR vs. CVR-driven) has not yet been isolated.

### MF6.12.14 Budget Utilization

- **Definition:** The proportion of configured Budget (Daily or Lifetime, MF3.7.2–3) actually consumed within its period, the direct KPI expression of Budget Pacing (MF6.7.1) outcomes.
- **Diagnostic Role:** Low Budget Utilization combined with adequate Inventory and Delivery Rate points toward a Bid Strategy-driven Spend Limitation (MF6.7.5) cause (MF6.13.6's Budget Not Spending model); high Budget Utilization combined with declining Cost Efficiency (MF6.12.13) points toward Inventory Saturation (MF6.11.5).

### MF6.12.15 Learning Progress

- **Definition:** The proportion of Required Events (MF6.5.6) accumulated within the current Optimization Window (MF6.5.5), expressing how close an Ad Set is to Learning Complete (MF6.5.3).
- **Diagnostic Role:** The primary KPI for the Learning Status (MF6.6.2) period specifically — a Learning Progress trend that has stalled well short of 100% within the Optimization Window is the quantitative confirmation of a Learning Limited (MF6.6.3) diagnosis.

### MF6.12.16 Delivery KPI Selection Rule

Match the KPI set to the Delivery Status (MF6.6) currently in effect: Learning Progress (MF6.12.15) during Learning; Delivery Rate/Auction Win Rate (MF6.12.10–11) during Limited; Cost Efficiency/Budget Utilization (MF6.12.13–14) during Active — extending the same status/stage-aware KPI-prioritization principle established at MF2.11.10, MF3.11.9, MF4.9.20, and MF5.12.17 to this Framework's own Delivery-Status-aware KPI set.

---

## MF6.13 Delivery Diagnostics Framework

Enterprise diagnostic models for the 13 most common Delivery-layer issues. Each model: Symptoms, Metrics, Likely Causes, Confidence Level, Priority, Recovery Strategy, Expected Outcome.

### MF6.13.1 No Delivery

- **Symptoms:** Zero or near-zero Impressions despite an ostensibly Active configuration.
- **Metrics:** Delivery Rate (MF6.12.10) at or near zero; Delivery Status (MF6.6.10).
- **Likely Causes:** Schedule not active (MF6.6.7), Budget already exhausted (MF6.7.6), a Rejected/In Review status (MF6.6.4–5) not yet surfaced to the advertiser's attention, or a near-zero Inventory Constraint (MF6.11.6).
- **Confidence Level:** High — among the most mechanically diagnosable issues via direct status inspection.
- **Priority:** Critical.
- **Recovery Strategy:** Verify Schedule and Budget status first (lowest-effort checks); escalate to compliance/Inventory review per MF6.6.10's Recovery Steps.
- **Expected Outcome:** Immediate resolution once the specific blocking cause is identified and corrected.

### MF6.13.2 Limited Delivery

- **Symptoms:** Non-zero but constrained Delivery relative to configured Budget/Audience size.
- **Metrics:** Low Delivery Rate (MF6.12.10) and/or Budget Utilization (MF6.12.14) relative to Inventory (MF6.11) size.
- **Likely Causes:** Advertiser-imposed Inventory Constraint (MF6.11.6) or a Bid Strategy-driven Spend Limitation (MF6.7.5) — apply MF6.11.7's Inventory Diagnostic Decision Tree.
- **Confidence Level:** High once decomposed via the Decision Tree.
- **Priority:** Moderate-High.
- **Recovery Strategy:** Relax the specific identified Constraint (Placement, Audience, or Bid Strategy).
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved Delivery Rate within one Learning cycle post-relaxation.

### MF6.13.3 High CPM

- Base treatment at MF2.10.9/MF3.12.6. **Symptoms:** CPM (MF6.12.4) elevated relative to Benchmark. **Metrics:** CPM trend, Inventory Competition (MF6.11.4) level. **Likely Causes:** Genuine Auction Competition increase (MF6.4.6), Seasonality (MF6.9.9), or declining Ad Quality (MF6.13.10) raising effective cost. **Confidence Level:** Moderate, requiring Benchmark and Ad Quality cross-check. **Priority:** Moderate-High. **Recovery Strategy:** Broaden Inventory (Placement/Audience Expansion) if Competition-driven; remediate Ad Quality if Ranking-driven; adjust expectations if genuinely seasonal. **Expected Outcome:** Variable, dependent on the confirmed cause.

### MF6.13.4 Learning Limited (Pipeline-Level)

- Base treatment at MF6.5.2/MF6.6.3. **Symptoms:** Learning Progress (MF6.12.15) stalled well short of Required Events (MF6.5.6) within the Optimization Window (MF6.5.5). **Metrics:** Learning Progress trend. **Likely Causes:** Audience Too Small (MF5.13.1), Bid Restriction (MF3.12.14), inadequate Budget, or genuine Signal Starvation (MF6.5.9). **Confidence Level:** High. **Priority:** High. **Recovery Strategy:** Apply MF6.5.10's ordered Recovery Strategy sequence. **Expected Outcome:** Learning Complete within one further Optimization Window once the binding constraint is resolved.

### MF6.13.5 Budget Not Spending

- **Symptoms:** Budget Utilization (MF6.12.14) significantly below 100% despite adequate remaining period.
- **Metrics:** Budget Utilization trend; Bid Strategy configuration cross-check.
- **Likely Causes:** Spend Limitation (MF6.7.5) from an overly restrictive Cost Cap/Bid Cap (MF2.6.2–3), or a genuine Inventory Constraint (MF6.11.6).
- **Confidence Level:** High once Bid Strategy and Inventory are jointly reviewed.
- **Priority:** Moderate-High (missed-opportunity cost).
- **Recovery Strategy:** Relax Bid Strategy (MF2.6) rather than increasing Budget, which would not resolve a Bid-driven Spend Limitation.
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved Budget Utilization within days of Bid Strategy adjustment.

### MF6.13.6 Budget Overspending

- **Symptoms:** Budget Exhaustion (MF6.7.6) occurring significantly earlier than the expected pacing curve would predict.
- **Metrics:** Budget Utilization rate-of-consumption trend versus historical baseline.
- **Likely Causes:** Spend Acceleration (MF6.7.4) driven by an unusually favorable, possibly temporary, Auction Dynamics (MF6.3.9) window; a recent Bid Strategy change toward a less-constrained option (e.g., Cost Cap → Highest Volume, MF2.6.1/2).
- **Confidence Level:** Moderate, requiring Budget Efficiency (MF6.7.7) cross-check to determine if the faster spend was genuinely efficient or merely fast.
- **Priority:** Moderate (a pacing/planning concern more than an efficiency failure, unless Budget Efficiency is also declining).
- **Recovery Strategy:** If genuinely a favorable, temporary window, no action needed; if driven by an unintended Bid Strategy change, revert or apply a Cost Cap.
- **Expected Outcome:** Stabilized pacing within the next period once cause is confirmed and, if needed, corrected.

### MF6.13.7 Frequency Too High

- Base treatment at MF6.10.3/MF2.10.5. **Symptoms:** Frequency (MF6.12.3) exceeding Optimal Frequency (MF6.10.4) for the Objective/Audience type. **Metrics:** Frequency trend and Frequency Distribution (MF6.10.2). **Likely Causes:** Reach Exhaustion (MF5.11.3) or a concentrated Frequency Distribution sub-segment. **Confidence Level:** High. **Priority:** Moderate-High. **Recovery Strategy:** Apply MF6.10.6's Frequency Diagnostics decision sequence (targeted Exclusion vs. general Audience Expansion). **Expected Outcome:** Frequency normalization within one Learning cycle.

### MF6.13.8 Auction Competition (Delivery-Level)

- Base treatment at MF6.3.6/MF6.4.6. **Symptoms:** Rising CPM/CPA with no internal configuration change. **Metrics:** CPM/CPA versus historical same-period Benchmark (MF1.11.5). **Likely Causes:** Genuine external demand increase (Seasonality, MF6.9.9) or a relative Ad Quality decline (MF6.13.10) making the Ad Set comparatively less competitive. **Confidence Level:** Moderate, requiring Benchmark cross-check. **Priority:** Moderate, escalating if breakeven thresholds are threatened. **Recovery Strategy:** Adjust expectations if seasonal; remediate Ad Quality if Ranking-driven. **Expected Outcome:** Resolves with the external cycle if seasonal; improves post-remediation if Ranking-driven.

### MF6.13.9 Weak Estimated Action Rate

- **Symptoms:** Persistently low Auction Win Rate (MF6.12.11) despite competitive Bid and adequate Ad Quality.
- **Metrics:** Auction Win Rate; inferred Estimated Action Rate trend (via Delivery efficiency, since not directly exposed, MF6.12.12).
- **Likely Causes:** Weak Audience/Creative Signal quality (MF6.9.1–2) providing poor prediction input; a mismatched Optimization Goal (MF6.8.1) relative to the Business's true target behavior; insufficient Historical/Conversion Signal volume (MF6.9.3–4) to support confident prediction.
- **Confidence Level:** Moderate — requires ruling out Bid/Ad Quality causes first (a low Auction Win Rate could stem from either).
- **Priority:** High, since this is a root-cause-layer diagnosis underlying many surface symptoms.
- **Recovery Strategy:** Strengthen Signal quality (MF5.6.10/MF5.15.8's Signals Improvement); verify Optimization Goal alignment (MF2.4); allow further Learning Progress accumulation (MF6.12.15) if genuinely still early in the Optimization Window.
- **Expected Outcome:** Gradual improvement as Signal quality and Learning Progress compound over successive Optimization Windows.

### MF6.13.10 Weak Ad Quality

- Base treatment at MF4.7.1/MF1.3.44. **Symptoms:** Declining or persistently low Quality/Engagement Rate/Conversion Rate Ranking (MF1.3.44–46). **Metrics:** The three Ranking signals directly. **Likely Causes:** Creative production quality issues (MF4.10–11), Negative Feedback (MF4.7.4), or Audience-Creative Relevance mismatch (MF4.7.6). **Confidence Level:** High, given direct Ranking signal visibility. **Priority:** High. **Recovery Strategy:** Route to the Creative Diagnostics Framework (MF4.13) for the specific Ranking-signal-driving root cause. **Expected Outcome:** Improved Ranking within one Learning cycle post-Creative-remediation.

### MF6.13.11 Low Reach

- **Symptoms:** Reach (MF6.12.1) below expectation relative to Budget and Audience Inventory (MF6.11.2) size.
- **Metrics:** Reach versus Audience Size estimate (MF5.12.1); Delivery Rate (MF6.12.10).
- **Likely Causes:** Audience Too Small (MF5.13.1); a Placements Inventory (MF6.11.1) restriction limiting available surfaces; Budget insufficient to reach the full available Audience Inventory within the period.
- **Confidence Level:** High once Audience Size and Delivery Rate are jointly reviewed.
- **Priority:** Moderate-High, especially for Awareness-objective Ad Sets where Reach is the Primary KPI.
- **Recovery Strategy:** Audience/Placement Expansion (MF5.14.1/MF3.4.2) if Inventory-constrained; Budget increase (per MF2.9.5's incremental discipline) if genuinely Budget-constrained.
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved Reach within one Learning cycle post-Expansion.

### MF6.13.12 Low Impressions

- **Symptoms:** Impressions (MF6.12.2) below expectation, distinct from Low Reach (MF6.13.11) in that this may occur even with adequate Reach if Frequency is also low.
- **Metrics:** Impressions versus Placements Inventory (MF6.11.1) size; Frequency (MF6.10.1) level.
- **Likely Causes:** Placements Inventory restriction (Manual Placements, MF3.4.1); low Auction Win Rate (MF6.12.11) limiting how many eligible opportunities are actually won.
- **Confidence Level:** Moderate, requiring Reach to be held constant in the comparison to isolate this from a general Low Reach (MF6.13.11) cause.
- **Priority:** Moderate.
- **Recovery Strategy:** Broaden Placements Inventory (MF3.4.2) if restriction-driven; investigate Auction Win Rate (MF6.13.9's Weak Estimated Action Rate or MF6.13.10's Weak Ad Quality) if Ranking-driven.
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved Impressions within one Learning cycle post-fix.

### MF6.13.13 Poor Delivery Stability

- **Symptoms:** Significant, unexplained period-to-period volatility in Delivery volume/cost absent any underlying configuration change or known Seasonality (MF6.9.9) pattern.
- **Metrics:** Budget Stability (MF6.7.8) trend; Delivery Rate/Cost Efficiency (MF6.12.10/13) volatility.
- **Likely Causes:** Genuinely volatile Auction Dynamics (MF6.3.9) beyond normal Seasonal patterns; an Ad Set oscillating near a Learning Limited (MF6.6.3) threshold, alternating between marginal Learning Progress and stalling; an unrecognized, undocumented significant edit causing intermittent Learning Resets (MF6.5.4).
- **Confidence Level:** Low-Moderate — the least mechanically diagnosable issue in this catalog, often requiring elimination of every other more specific diagnosis first.
- **Priority:** Moderate, escalating if the instability is severe enough to prevent any reliable Stabilization-stage baseline (MF2.7.4) from ever forming.
- **Recovery Strategy:** Audit Version Control logs (MF2.13.2/MF3.15.3) for unrecognized edits first; if none found, treat as a genuine Auction Dynamics volatility case and extend the observation period before drawing further conclusions.
- **Expected Outcome:** Variable — may resolve once an unrecognized edit is identified and controlled for, or may require simply accepting a longer Stabilization period for a genuinely volatile market.

### MF6.13.14 Diagnostics Summary Table

| Issue | Typical Priority | Typical Recovery |
|---|---|---|
| No Delivery | Critical | Immediate |
| Limited Delivery | Moderate-High | ~1 cycle |
| High CPM | Moderate-High | Variable |
| Learning Limited | High | 1 further Optimization Window |
| Budget Not Spending | Moderate-High | Fast |
| Budget Overspending | Moderate | Next period |
| Frequency Too High | Moderate-High | ~1 cycle |
| Auction Competition | Moderate | Variable |
| Weak Estimated Action Rate | High | Gradual, multi-window |
| Weak Ad Quality | High | ~1 cycle |
| Low Reach | Moderate-High | ~1 cycle |
| Low Impressions | Moderate | ~1 cycle |
| Poor Delivery Stability | Moderate | Variable |

---

## MF6.14 Delivery Optimization Decision Framework

Enterprise decision systems for the most common Delivery scenarios, specializing the Generic Framework Series' Decision concept (`F4.5`) exactly as MF2.12, MF3.14, MF4.15, and MF5.16 do at their respective layers, applied here at Delivery-Pipeline grain. Each rule: Observed Metrics, Likely Cause, Decision Priority, Recommended Action, Risk, Expected Improvement.

### MF6.14.1 Decision: Low Auction Win Rate with Competitive Bid

- **Observed Metrics:** Auction Win Rate (MF6.12.11) low; Bid Strategy confirmed competitive relative to Benchmark; Ad Quality Rankings (MF1.3.44–46) healthy.
- **Likely Cause:** Weak Estimated Action Rate (MF6.13.9) from insufficient Signal quality or Learning Progress.
- **Decision Priority:** High.
- **Recommended Action:** Strengthen Audience/Creative Signals (MF6.9.1–2) and allow further Learning Progress (MF6.12.15) accumulation before concluding the Optimization Goal itself is misconfigured.
- **Risk:** Prematurely abandoning the current configuration (e.g., switching Optimization Goal) before Learning Progress has had adequate time to compound risks discarding genuine, still-accumulating value.
- **Expected Improvement:** Gradual Auction Win Rate improvement across successive Optimization Windows (MF6.5.5).

### MF6.14.2 Decision: Budget Fully Utilized but Cost Efficiency Declining

- **Observed Metrics:** Budget Utilization (MF6.12.14) near 100%; Cost Efficiency (MF6.12.13) trending down over successive periods.
- **Likely Cause:** Inventory Saturation (MF6.11.5) — the Ad Set is spending its full Budget but increasingly against lower-quality remaining Inventory.
- **Decision Priority:** Moderate-High.
- **Recommended Action:** Shift from continued Vertical Scaling to Horizontal Scaling or Placement/Audience Expansion (MF3.13.1/MF6.11.7's Decision Tree) to access fresh Inventory rather than continuing to compete for the same saturated pool.
- **Risk:** Continuing to increase Budget into an already-saturated Inventory pool further degrades Cost Efficiency without added Delivery benefit.
- **Expected Improvement:** Restored Cost Efficiency once fresh Inventory is accessed.

### MF6.14.3 Decision: Stable Delivery Suddenly Destabilizes

- **Observed Metrics:** Delivery Stability (MF6.7.8) degrades sharply after a period of healthy, predictable Delivery; no advertiser-side edit consciously recalled.
- **Likely Cause:** An unrecognized significant edit (Learning Reset, MF6.5.4) or a genuine external Auction Dynamics (MF6.3.9) shift.
- **Decision Priority:** High (diagnostic ambiguity itself is a priority driver, per MF6.13.13).
- **Recommended Action:** Audit the Version Control log (MF2.13.2/MF3.15.3/MF4.17.3/MF5.18.2) across every configuration layer before concluding the cause is external.
- **Risk:** Misattributing an internal, correctable cause (an unlogged edit) to "just market conditions" delays a straightforward fix.
- **Expected Improvement:** Immediate stabilization once an unrecognized edit is identified and reverted/controlled for; otherwise, stabilization on the market's own timeline.

### MF6.14.4 Decision: Ad Set Approaching Learning Limited Near Optimization Window Close

- **Observed Metrics:** Learning Progress (MF6.12.15) well short of 100% with the Optimization Window (MF6.5.5) nearly elapsed.
- **Likely Cause:** Impending Learning Limited (MF6.6.3), root cause not yet isolated among Audience/Bid/Budget/Event-rarity (MF6.5.9).
- **Decision Priority:** High (time-sensitive — the Window is a rolling one, but repeated near-misses compound inefficiency).
- **Recommended Action:** Apply MF6.5.10's Recovery Strategy sequence proactively rather than waiting for a formal Learning Limited status to register.
- **Risk:** Applying multiple Recovery Methods simultaneously (Audience + Bid + Budget all at once) prevents identifying which one actually resolved the shortfall.
- **Expected Improvement:** Learning Complete within the next Optimization Window once the binding constraint is addressed.

### MF6.14.5 Decision: High-Performing Ad Set Nearing Frequency Saturation During Active Scaling

- **Observed Metrics:** Frequency (MF6.12.3) approaching Optimal Frequency (MF6.10.4) ceiling during an active Vertical Scaling sequence (MF3.13.2).
- **Likely Cause:** Approaching Frequency Saturation (MF6.10.3) before the Scaling plan's target Budget is reached.
- **Decision Priority:** Moderate-High (preventive).
- **Recommended Action:** Pause further Vertical Scaling; apply Audience Expansion or Rotation (MF6.10.7–8) to create Frequency headroom before resuming.
- **Risk:** Continuing Vertical Scaling without addressing Frequency heightens the risk of a sudden, sharp Cost Efficiency decline once Saturation is fully reached.
- **Expected Improvement:** Sustained Scaling trajectory without the efficiency cliff a fully-reached Saturation point would otherwise produce.

### MF6.14.6 Decision: Consistently Winning Auctions at Unexpectedly Low Realized Cost

- **Observed Metrics:** Auction Win Rate (MF6.12.11) high; realized CPM/CPC (MF6.12.4/7) notably below the configured Bid Cap or Benchmark expectation.
- **Likely Cause:** Not a failure — the expected outcome of Second Price-style pricing dynamics (MF6.3.7) combined with strong Ad Quality (MF6.4.3) reducing genuine required cost to win.
- **Decision Priority:** Low (opportunity confirmation, not a corrective situation).
- **Recommended Action:** Consider this a Scaling-readiness signal (MF3.13/MF6.14.2's inverse case) — the Ad Set may have headroom for Vertical Scaling before Cost Efficiency would be expected to decline.
- **Risk:** Misinterpreting strong performance as fragile/temporary and under-investing in Scaling it, forfeiting available efficient growth.
- **Expected Improvement:** Successful incremental Scaling captures additional volume at continued favorable Cost Efficiency, until Inventory Saturation (MF6.11.5) naturally emerges as the eventual ceiling.

---

## MF6.15 AI Delivery Intelligence

Explains how an AI system — whether Meta's own delivery/Auction system or a Business's own analytical/reasoning layer consuming this document lineage — should interpret and reason about Delivery data. Consistent with MAIFS.14 and this document lineage's Genericity/Agnosticism precedent, this section describes conceptual interpretive posture, never a specific algorithm, model architecture, or implementation detail. It specializes the Generic Framework Series' Knowledge, Reasoning, and Decision concepts (`F3.x`, `F5.x`, `F4.x`) for Delivery-domain content specifically.

### MF6.15.1 How AI Interprets Delivery

An AI system reasoning about Delivery data should treat every Metric (MF6.12) as conditional on the current Delivery Status (MF6.6) and Learning Stability (MF6.5.7) — the same numeric CTR value means something different during Learning than during confirmed Stable Delivery, per this Framework's repeated Status-conditional interpretation guidance (MF6.6.11, MF6.12.16). This specializes the Generic Framework Series' Knowledge concept (`F3.x`) — Delivery data is not raw fact but Knowledge only once correctly contextualized by its own Status/Stability state.

### MF6.15.2 Signal Weighting

- **Definition:** The conceptual principle that not every Delivery Signal (MF6.9) carries equal diagnostic weight in every situation — Conversion Signals (MF6.9.4) are more diagnostic for a Sales-objective Ad Set's health than Behavioral Signals (MF6.9.7), while the reverse holds for an Awareness-objective Ad Set.
- **AI Reasoning Guidance:** An AI system should weight Signal categories according to the Ad Set's actual configured Optimization Goal (MF6.8.1), never applying a single fixed Signal-weighting scheme uniformly across all Objectives.
