- **Boundary:** This Framework does not specify numeric weight values — doing so would be exactly the implementation-level detail MAIFS.14 excludes. The conceptual principle (weight by Optimization-Goal relevance) is what is professionally actionable and Framework-appropriate.

### MF6.15.3 Learning Adaptation

- **Definition:** The conceptual principle that an AI system's own confidence in its Delivery-diagnosis conclusions should adapt as Learning Progress (MF6.12.15) accumulates — a diagnosis reached during early Learning should be held more tentatively than an identical-looking diagnosis reached post-Learning-Complete (MF6.5.3).
- **AI Reasoning Guidance:** Specializes the Generic Framework Series' Reasoning concept (`F5.x`) — Learning Adaptation is Goal-Directed Reasoning (per F5.5's Taxonomy) specifically conditioned on the evolving reliability of the underlying Estimated Action Rate (MF6.2.6) input.
- **Common Mistake:** An AI system treating a Learning-Phase-derived Metric with the same confidence as a Learning-Complete-derived one, violating this Framework's central status-conditional-interpretation principle (MF6.15.1).

### MF6.15.4 Auction Prediction

- **Definition:** The conceptual task of forecasting likely Auction Ranking (MF6.2.9) outcomes for a given Ad Set configuration before or during Delivery, used for pre-launch feasibility assessment (e.g., MF3.15.6's Quality Standards Required Events feasibility check) or ongoing Scaling-readiness assessment (MF6.14.6).
- **AI Reasoning Guidance:** Any such prediction should be treated as probabilistic and Context-conditional (MF6.4.5), never as a deterministic guarantee — directly following from MF6.3.9's Auction Dynamics principle that outcomes shift continuously with Competition and Inventory conditions.
- **Boundary:** This Framework does not specify a prediction methodology — only that any prediction, however produced, must be interpreted probabilistically and re-validated against actual observed outcomes rather than trusted indefinitely.

### MF6.15.5 Probability Estimation

- **Definition:** The conceptual treatment of Winning Probability (MF6.2.10) and Estimated Action Rate (MF6.2.6) as genuinely probabilistic quantities — a given Ad has a likelihood, not a certainty, of winning a specific impression opportunity or of a specific user completing the Optimization Event.
- **AI Reasoning Guidance:** An AI system should express Delivery-related conclusions with appropriate uncertainty bounds (mirroring MF1.11.6–7's Confidence/Statistical Significance concepts, applied here to Delivery-prediction specifically) rather than false precision — this is why every AI Decision Framework rule throughout this document lineage (MF2.12, MF3.14, MF4.15, MF5.16, MF6.14) includes an explicit Confidence Level field rather than presenting conclusions as certain.

### MF6.15.6 Expected Value

- **Definition:** The conceptual synthesis of Probability Estimation (MF6.15.5) with the magnitude of the outcome being predicted — e.g., a moderate-probability, high-value Purchase Optimization outcome may warrant a different Decision Priority (MF6.14) than a high-probability, low-value one.
- **AI Reasoning Guidance:** Specializes the Generic Framework Series' Decision concept (`F4.x`) — Expected Value is the synthesis step between an Evaluation (F1.2 item 8) of likely outcomes and an actual Decision (F1.2 item 9) about which Recommended Action to prioritize, directly underlying this Framework's own Decision Priority field (MF6.14).
- **Common Mistake:** Prioritizing the highest-probability action without weighing outcome magnitude, or the highest-magnitude action without weighing its actual likelihood — Expected Value requires both factors jointly.

### MF6.15.7 Delivery Confidence

- **Definition:** The overall, synthesized degree of trust an AI system (or a human analyst) should place in a given Delivery-layer conclusion, integrating Learning Stability (MF6.5.7), Signal quality (MF6.9), and Statistical Significance (MF1.11.7) of the underlying observation into a single interpretive posture.
- **AI Reasoning Guidance:** The capstone concept this entire section builds toward — every prior entry (MF6.15.1–6) contributes one input to an overall Delivery Confidence assessment, which should in turn govern how assertively any Diagnostics (MF6.13) or Decision Framework (MF6.14) conclusion is acted upon.
- **Practical Application:** A Recommended Action (MF6.14) reached with low Delivery Confidence (e.g., during early Learning, from a single ambiguous Signal) warrants a smaller, more reversible first step than an identical-looking Recommended Action reached with high Delivery Confidence (post-Learning-Complete, from multiple corroborating Signals) — directly extending this document lineage's established incremental-change discipline (MF2.9.6, MF3.10.11) to be Confidence-calibrated rather than uniformly cautious regardless of evidence strength.

---

## MF6.16 Delivery Knowledge Graph

A semantic graph connecting Delivery-layer concepts to each other and, by citation, back to MF1–MF5's vocabulary — using the same relationship-type legend established at MF1.15.1 and applied at every prior layer (MF2.14, MF3.16, MF4.16, MF5.17), applied here at Delivery grain.

### MF6.16.1 Pipeline & Structural Edges

```
Business Goal --Produces (via Campaign/Ad Set/Audience Eligibility)--> Auction Entry [MF6.2.1-5]
Auction Entry --Requires--> Estimated Action Rate + Bid + Ad Quality [MF6.2.6-8]
Estimated Action Rate + Bid + Ad Quality + User Value + Context + Competition + Inventory + Placement + Optimization Goal --Produces--> Auction Ranking [MF6.4]
Auction Ranking --Produces--> Winning Probability --Produces--> Delivery [MF6.2.9-11]
Delivery --Produces--> Learning --Produces--> Measurement --Produces--> Optimization Feedback --Governs (loop)--> Estimated Action Rate [MF6.2.12-14]
```

### MF6.16.2 Signal & Learning Edges

```
Audience, Creative, Historical, Conversion, Pixel, CAPI, Behavioral Signals --Governs--> Estimated Action Rate accuracy [MF6.9]
Inventory, Seasonality, Competition Signals --Governs--> Auction Ranking context [MF6.9.8-10]
Learning Phase --Requires--> Required Events within Optimization Window --Produces--> Learning Complete or Learning Limited [MF6.5]
Learning Complete --Requires (further)--> Learning Stability before Decision-grade trust [MF6.5.7]
```

### MF6.16.3 Budget, Frequency & Inventory Edges

```
Budget --Governs (via Budget Pacing)--> Auction Entry rate over time [MF6.7.1]
Spend Acceleration / Spend Limitation --Governs--> Budget Exhaustion timing [MF6.7.4-6]
Frequency --Diagnoses--> Frequency Saturation --Remedies via--> Refresh/Expansion/Rotation [MF6.10]
Placements Inventory + Audience Inventory + Auction Availability + Inventory Competition --Produces--> Inventory Saturation [MF6.11]
```

### MF6.16.4 Diagnostic, Optimization & Performance Edges

```
Delivery KPI Framework (MF6.12) --Requires--> Delivery Status context for correct interpretation [MF6.6, MF6.15.1]
Diagnostics Framework (MF6.13) --Requires--> KPI Framework (MF6.12) as its evidence base
Optimization Decision Framework (MF6.14) --Requires--> Diagnostics findings as Observed Metrics input
AI Delivery Intelligence (MF6.15) --Governs--> how Diagnostics/Decision conclusions are weighted and trusted (Delivery Confidence) [MF6.15.7]
Performance (Cost Efficiency, Budget Utilization, ROAS) --Measures--> the full Pipeline's emergent outcome [MF6.12.9/13-14]
```

### MF6.16.5 Graph Completeness Statement

Every section of this Framework (MF6.1–MF6.15, MF6.17) appears as a node or edge participant above: Delivery Architecture and Pipeline (MF6.1–2) as the root structural/sequential nodes; Auction, Ranking, Learning, Status (MF6.3–6) as the core mechanics edges; Budget Pacing, Optimization, Signals, Frequency, Inventory (MF6.7–11) as the control-lever edges; KPIs, Diagnostics, Decision, AI Intelligence, Governance (MF6.12–17) as the evidentiary and reasoning layer connecting back to every structural and mechanical node — mirroring MF2.14's, MF3.16's, MF4.16's, and MF5.17's graph structure at this Framework's own grain.

---

## MF6.17 Delivery Governance

Extends MF2.13's, MF3.15's, MF4.17's, and MF5.18's prior-layer governance patterns to the Delivery layer, using the same domain-content-governance framing (distinct from EIFS/MAIFS's document-governance meaning).

### MF6.17.1 Naming Standards (cross-reference)

No new Delivery-specific naming object exists beyond those already governed at MF1.13.1–2/MF4.17.1/MF5.18.1 (Campaign/Ad Set/Creative/Audience naming) — Delivery Governance instead requires that Delivery Status (MF6.6) and Diagnostics (MF6.13) findings be recorded using this Framework's own defined status/issue vocabulary consistently, never ad hoc terminology, so that historical Delivery records remain comparable over time.

### MF6.17.2 Documentation

Every Ad Set's Delivery history should carry, at minimum: its Learning Progress/Complete timeline (MF6.5), any Delivery Status transitions and their recorded cause (MF6.6), and a reference to any Diagnostics Framework (MF6.13) finding and its Recovery Strategy outcome — extending MF3.15.2's Ad-Set-layer Documentation requirement with Delivery-specific temporal depth.

### MF6.17.3 Version Control

Every configuration change with Delivery-relevant consequence (any significant edit per MF6.5.4, any Bid Strategy/Budget Pacing-relevant change) should be logged per the Version Control discipline already established at MF2.13.2/MF3.15.3/MF4.17.3/MF5.18.2 — this Framework does not introduce a new Version Control mechanism, only requires that Delivery-consequential changes across every prior layer be traceable specifically for Delivery Diagnostics (MF6.13.13's Poor Delivery Stability) purposes.

### MF6.17.4 Operational Standards

- Every Ad Set should have its Delivery Status (MF6.6) actively monitored, not merely checked reactively when a business stakeholder asks why performance looks off.
- Learning Progress (MF6.12.15) should be tracked against the Optimization Window (MF6.5.5) proactively, applying MF6.14.4's Decision rule before a formal Learning Limited status registers.
- Budget Pacing (MF6.7) anomalies (premature Exhaustion or persistent under-utilization) should trigger an automatic review rather than being discovered only at period-end reporting.

### MF6.17.5 QA Procedures

Before launch, verify: Required Events (MF6.5.6) feasibility given configured Budget/Audience (extending MF3.15.6's pre-launch check with this Framework's own Optimization Window awareness, MF6.5.5); Inventory Constraints (MF6.11.6) are deliberate, not accidental; Bid Strategy and Budget are jointly reviewed (MF3.7.6) to avoid an immediately-Limited launch (MF6.6.6).

### MF6.17.6 Audit Framework

- [ ] No Ad Set has been silently stuck in Learning Limited (MF6.6.3) beyond the Business's defined grace period.
- [ ] No confirmed Inventory Saturation (MF6.11.5) remains unaddressed via Horizontal Scaling or Placement/Audience Expansion.
- [ ] Delivery Status transitions (MF6.6) are documented per MF6.17.2, not left unexplained in historical records.
- [ ] Budget Pacing anomalies (MF6.13.5–6) have a documented resolution or accepted rationale on file.
- [ ] Frequency (MF6.10) is within Optimal range for every Always On (MF3.8.1) Ad Set, or a documented Rotation/Refresh schedule is in place.

### MF6.17.7 Compliance

Delivery-layer compliance is largely inherited from upstream layers (Creative compliance, MF4.17.6; Audience/Privacy compliance, MF5.18.6–7) rather than introducing new compliance categories of its own — this Framework's own compliance-relevant contribution is ensuring the Rejected/In Review Delivery Statuses (MF6.6.4–5) are treated as hard gates requiring resolution, never bypassed or worked around.

### MF6.17.8 Delivery Policies

- No Ad Set may remain in a Limited or Learning Limited status (MF6.6.3/6) past the Business's own defined maximum grace period without an active, logged Recovery Strategy (MF6.5.10) application.
- No Vertical Scaling action (MF3.13.2) may proceed without first confirming current Frequency (MF6.10.4) and Inventory Saturation (MF6.11.5) status are not already binding constraints (MF6.14.5's Decision rule).
- Every Budget Overspending or Underspending anomaly (MF6.13.5–6) exceeding the Business's defined materiality threshold must be reviewed and documented within a defined response window.

---

## MF6.18 Enterprise Best Practices

### MF6.18.1 Golden Rules

1. **Every Metric is Status-conditional** — never interpret a Delivery Metric without first checking Delivery Status (MF6.6) and Learning Stability (MF6.5.7); this is the single most important principle this entire Framework establishes.
2. **Bid is one factor among many, never the sole lever** — Auction Ranking (MF6.4) balances Bid against Ad Quality, Estimated Action Rate, and Context; a performance problem is not automatically a "raise the Bid" problem.
3. **Diagnose before deciding, decide before acting** — apply Diagnostics (MF6.13) to isolate root cause, then the Decision Framework (MF6.14) to select a Confidence-calibrated action, never skip directly from symptom to action.
4. **Isolate one variable per change** — every Recovery Strategy and Optimization action in this Framework assumes single-variable isolation (MF6.5.10, MF6.14 throughout); bundling changes destroys diagnosability.
5. **Auction Dynamics are never static** — Benchmark comparisons must always use current, not historical-only, reference points (MF6.3.9); a configuration that performed well yesterday is not guaranteed to perform identically today absent any change on the advertiser's part.
6. **Scale only from confirmed Stability, never from mere Learning Complete** — MF6.5.7's distinction between Learning Complete and genuine Learning Stability is the precondition every Scaling decision (MF6.14.5) must respect.

### MF6.18.2 Delivery Checklist

- [ ] Delivery Status (MF6.6) is actively monitored, not checked only reactively.
- [ ] Learning Progress (MF6.12.15) is tracked against the Optimization Window (MF6.5.5).
- [ ] Budget Utilization and Pacing (MF6.7/MF6.12.14) show no unexplained anomalies.
- [ ] Frequency (MF6.10) is within Optimal range for the Objective/Audience type.
- [ ] No Inventory Constraint (MF6.11.6) is unintentional or undocumented.

### MF6.18.3 Learning Checklist

- [ ] Required Events (MF6.5.6) feasibility was verified before launch against configured Budget/Audience.
- [ ] No unrecognized significant edit has caused an unexpected Learning Reset (MF6.5.4) — verified via Version Control (MF6.17.3).
- [ ] Learning Stability (MF6.5.7), not merely Learning Complete (MF6.5.3), is confirmed before treating Metrics as a trustworthy baseline.
- [ ] Learning Risks (MF6.5.9) — Premature Scaling, Repeated Reset, False Stability, Signal Starvation — have each been explicitly ruled out.

### MF6.18.4 Auction Checklist

- [ ] Bid Strategy and Budget have been reviewed jointly (MF3.7.6), not independently.
- [ ] Ad Quality Rankings (MF1.3.44–46) are healthy, or a Creative Diagnostics (MF4.13) investigation is active if not.
- [ ] Auction Win Rate (MF6.12.11) is being monitored distinctly from raw Delivery volume, to isolate competitiveness from Inventory-availability issues.
- [ ] No Common Misconception (MF6.4.10) is silently driving a flawed Bid Strategy decision (e.g., assuming highest Bid always wins).

### MF6.18.5 Scaling Checklist

- [ ] Learning Stability (MF6.5.7) is confirmed, not merely Learning Complete.
- [ ] Frequency (MF6.10.4) and Inventory Saturation (MF6.11.5) status are both checked before further Vertical Scaling (MF6.14.5).
- [ ] Scaling increments follow the incremental discipline (MF2.9.5) rather than large single-step jumps.
- [ ] A Recovery Plan is defined in advance in case Scaling triggers instability (mirroring MF2.9.8's Recovery Plans pattern).

### MF6.18.6 Optimization Checklist

- [ ] Diagnostics Framework (MF6.13) finding confirmed before selecting a Decision Framework (MF6.14) action.
- [ ] Decision Priority reflects genuine Expected Value (MF6.15.6) — probability and outcome magnitude jointly, not either alone.
- [ ] Delivery Confidence (MF6.15.7) level is stated and the action's scale/reversibility matched to it.
- [ ] Only one Optimization variable applied per iteration cycle.

### MF6.18.7 Operational Checklist

- [ ] Delivery Status monitoring, Learning Progress tracking, and Budget Pacing anomaly detection (MF6.17.4) are running continuously, not on-demand only.
- [ ] Pre-launch QA Procedures (MF6.17.5) are completed for every new Ad Set.
- [ ] Delivery Policies (MF6.17.8) grace periods and materiality thresholds are defined and enforced consistently.

### MF6.18.8 Audit Checklist (Cross-Reference)

Fully specified at MF6.17.6 — repeated here only as a pointer, per this Framework's own no-duplicate-content discipline (mirroring MF3.17.6's, MF4.18.9's, and MF5.19.8's identical cross-reference pattern at prior layers).

## Meta Framework 6 Closing Statement

**Meta Framework 6 — Delivery Framework is now complete, validated, and frozen.** It establishes the definitive technical reference explaining how Meta actually delivers advertisements — Delivery Architecture, the complete 14-stage Delivery Pipeline, the Meta Auction Framework, a full 9-factor Auction Ranking analysis, Learning, Delivery Status (10 statuses), Budget Pacing, Delivery Optimization, Delivery Signals (10 categories), Frequency, Inventory, a Delivery KPI library (15 KPIs), a complete per-Issue Diagnostics Framework (13 issues), a Delivery Optimization Decision Framework, AI Delivery Intelligence, a Knowledge Graph, Governance, and Enterprise Best Practices — governed throughout by MAIFS, built entirely on Meta Frameworks 1–5's vocabulary by citation only, and without modifying the Enterprise Architecture Baseline (§0–§60), EIFS, the Generic Enterprise Intelligence Framework Series (Frameworks 1–10), MAIFS, or Meta Frameworks 1–5. Any future change to Meta Framework 6 proceeds only through the Revision mechanism established at EIFS.7 and specialized for Meta Ads Frameworks at MAIFS.11.

# Meta Framework 7 — Optimization Framework

**Status:** Complete, Validated, and Frozen. All 17 planned sections (MF7.1–MF7.17), plus the MF7.18 Final Validation Checklist and Closing Statement, are present in full per MAIFS.8's lifecycle policy. Any future change proceeds only through the Revision mechanism (EIFS.7, MAIFS.11).
**Numbering Note:** This Framework was requested under the working name "Meta Ads Framework 8 — Optimization Framework." Per explicit user resolution, it is numbered and named **Meta Framework 7 — Optimization Framework**, filling the Series Roadmap's next sequential slot per EIFS.6/MAIFS.10's strict sequential numbering rule, rather than skipping ahead to Framework 8.
**Audience:** Performance engineers, enterprise advertisers, growth teams, marketing scientists, data scientists, AI systems, Meta specialists, business intelligence teams.
**Scope:** This Framework governs the Optimization Decision Engine only — how optimization decisions are made once measurements already exist. It does not explain Campaign/Ad Set creation (MF2/MF3), Meta Ads fundamentals, or re-derive Measurement — every Metric consumed here is cited to its existing definition (MF1.4/MF3.11/MF4.9/MF5.12/MF6.12), never redefined.
**Vocabulary Rule:** No Core Domain Object, Metric, Objective, Audience, Creative, Delivery, Auction, or Learning term already defined in Meta Frameworks 1–6 is redefined here. Every such term is cited as `MF1.x`–`MF6.x` and consumed strictly as evidentiary input to this Framework's own decision-engine content (triggers, decision trees, priority, timing, automation, validation), per this document's Meta Framework 7 Vocabulary Rule stated in the banner above and per MAIFS.19.

---

## MF7.1 Optimization Philosophy

### MF7.1.1 Optimization vs. Reaction

- **Optimization:** A deliberate, evidence-triggered change to a Campaign/Ad Set/Creative/Audience configuration, made only after (1) a specific Metric has crossed a defined Trigger threshold (MF7.10), (2) the underlying Delivery Status and Learning Stability context has been confirmed valid for interpretation (MF6.6/MF6.5.7), and (3) a specific hypothesis about expected outcome has been stated in advance (MF7.15.1).
- **Reaction:** An undisciplined, emotionally-motivated change made in response to a single data point, a short observation window, or a subjective impression of "this doesn't feel right," without confirming Statistical Significance (MF1.11.7), Learning Stability (MF6.5.7), or a stated hypothesis.
- **The Core Distinction:** Optimization is Decision-layer activity specializing the Generic Framework Series' Decision concept (`F4.5`, the Selective Decision type specifically) — a chosen resolution among alternatives, informed by Evaluation of evidence. Reaction is not a Decision in this document lineage's technical sense at all; it is an unexamined impulse that happens to change a configuration.
- **Why Optimization Must Never Happen From Emotion:** Every configuration layer in this document lineage (MF2–MF6) has established that Metrics are Status-conditional (MF6.15.1), that Auction Dynamics shift continuously and independently of advertiser action (MF6.3.9), and that a single period's fluctuation is not a Trend (MF1.11.8). An emotionally-driven Reaction systematically violates all three principles at once — it treats a possibly-Status-conditional, possibly-Dynamics-driven, single-period fluctuation as if it were a confirmed, actionable Trend. This is the single root failure mode underlying the majority of the Common Optimization Mistakes catalogued at MF7.16.

### MF7.1.2 Statistically Meaningful Evidence Requirement

Every Optimization action defined in this Framework requires, before execution: (1) Statistical Significance (MF1.11.7) or, where formal significance testing is impractical, a minimum observation period meeting MF7.12's Minimum Data Requirements; (2) confirmation that the Ad Set is not currently in Learning Phase (MF6.5.1) unless the optimization action is itself a Learning-Phase-specific Recovery Method (MF6.5.10); (3) a Trend (MF1.11.8) across multiple periods, never a single-period reading. An action failing any of these three gates is, by this Framework's own definition (MF7.1.1), a Reaction, not an Optimization, regardless of how it is otherwise labeled.

### MF7.1.3 Every Optimization Action's Required Components

Per this Framework's own governing structure, every optimization action defined anywhere in this document must specify:

| Component | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Trigger** | The specific Metric threshold or Diagnostics Framework finding (cited to MF1.4/MF3.12/MF4.13/MF5.13/MF6.13) that initiates consideration of this action |
| **Conditions** | The prerequisite state (Learning Stability, minimum observation period, Delivery Status) that must hold before the Trigger is considered valid |
| **Decision** | The specific action to be taken, cited to its governing Framework section (e.g., Creative Refresh, MF4.14.1; Audience Expansion, MF5.14.1) |
| **Expected Outcome** | The specific, falsifiable prediction of what should happen if the Decision is correct |
| **Risk** | What could go wrong, including the risk of the underlying diagnosis being incorrect |
| **Validation** | How and when the Expected Outcome will be checked against reality (MF7.15) |

This six-component structure is this Framework's own formalization and is applied consistently across every specific optimization rule in MF7.5–MF7.14.

---

## MF7.2 Optimization Hierarchy

The 23 optimization layers this Framework organizes, ordered from broadest structural scope to narrowest tactical scope. Each layer cites its full governing definition in a prior Framework; this Framework's contribution is the decision-engine logic layered on top (MF7.4–MF7.14).

### MF7.2.1 Hierarchy Table

| # | Layer | Governing Definition | This Framework's Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Campaign Optimization | MF2 (Campaign Framework) | Objective/Budget-mode decision triggers (MF7.9, MF7.7) |
| 2 | Ad Set Optimization | MF3 (Ad Set Framework) | Configuration-lever decision triggers (MF7.4, MF7.10) |
| 3 | Creative Optimization | MF4 (Creative Framework) | Creative Optimization Engine (MF7.5) |
| 4 | Audience Optimization | MF5 (Audience Framework) | Audience Optimization Engine (MF7.6) |
| 5 | Placement Optimization | MF3.4 (Placement Framework) | Placement-lever decision logic (MF7.4.4's CPM tree) |
| 6 | Budget Optimization | MF2.5/MF3.7/MF6.7 | Budget Optimization Engine (MF7.7) |
| 7 | Bid Optimization | MF2.6 (Bid Strategy Framework) | Bid-lever decision logic (MF7.4.5's CPC tree) |
| 8 | Learning Phase Optimization | MF3.10/MF6.5 | Learning Phase Optimization Engine (MF7.8) |
| 9 | Frequency Optimization | MF6.10 (Frequency Framework) | Frequency-Trigger decision logic (MF7.10.3) |
| 10 | Conversion Optimization | MF1.4.12/MF3.5.1 | CVR-decomposition decision trees (MF7.4.6) |
| 11 | ROAS Optimization | MF1.4.6/MF2.10.3 | ROAS-decomposition decision trees (MF7.4.3) |
| 12 | CPA Optimization | MF1.4.5/MF2.10.2 | CPA-decomposition decision trees (MF7.4.2) |
| 13 | CTR Optimization | MF1.4.2/MF4.13.1 | CTR-decomposition decision trees (MF7.4.1) |
| 14 | CPM Optimization | MF1.4.1/MF6.13.3 | CPM-decomposition decision trees (MF7.4.4) |
| 15 | CPC Optimization | MF1.4.3 | CPC-decomposition decision trees (MF7.4.5) |
| 16 | Video Optimization | MF4.10 (Video Framework) | Video-specific triggers (MF7.5.10) |
| 17 | Messaging Optimization | MF3.5.8/MF4.2.15 | Messaging-specific triggers (MF7.9.3) |
| 18 | Lead Optimization | MF1.5.4/MF3.5.9 | Lead-specific triggers (MF7.9.4) |
| 19 | Sales Optimization | MF1.5.6/MF3.5.11-13 | Sales-specific triggers (MF7.9.6) |
| 20 | Catalog Optimization | MF1.3.35-36/MF3.5.13 | Catalog-specific triggers (MF7.9.6) |
| 21 | App Optimization | MF1.5.5/MF3.5.10 | App-specific triggers (MF7.9.5) |
| 22 | Retargeting Optimization | MF1.8.5/MF5.8.3 | Retargeting-specific triggers (MF7.6.4) |
| 23 | Scaling Optimization | MF3.13/MF5.15/MF6.14.5 | Scaling Framework synthesis (MF7.14) |
| — | Automation Optimization | (new to this Framework) | Automation Rules Framework (MF7.13) |

### MF7.2.2 Hierarchy Interaction Principle

These 23 layers are not independent — a single Diagnostics finding (e.g., High CPA) typically implicates multiple layers simultaneously (Bid, Audience, Creative), and this Framework's Decision Tree methodology (MF7.4) exists specifically to determine which layer is the genuine root cause before an action is taken at any of them, consistent with MF7.1.2's evidence requirement and MF2.9.6/MF3.10.11's established variable-isolation discipline.

---

## MF7.3 Optimization Inputs

Every input this Optimization Engine consumes is cited to its existing Metrics/Diagnostics definition — this section is a consolidated input map, not a redefinition, per this Framework's explicit Scope (not repeating Measurement).

### MF7.3.1 Core Metrics Input Map

| Input | Governing Definition |
|---|---|
| CTR | MF1.4.2 |
| Unique CTR | MF4.9.2 |
| CPC | MF1.4.3 |
| CPM | MF1.4.1 |
| CPA | MF1.4.5 |
| CPL | MF1.4.4 |
| ROAS | MF1.4.6 |
| Purchase Value | MF1.3.31 |
| CVR | MF1.4.12 |
| Frequency | MF1.4.7/MF6.10.1 |
| Reach | MF1.3.27 |
| Impressions | MF1.3.28 |

### MF7.3.2 Creative & Video Metrics Input Map

| Input | Governing Definition |
|---|---|
| Video Metrics (ThruPlay, Watch %, Completion) | MF1.4.9/MF4.9.4-7 |
| Hook Rate | MF4.9.4 |
| Hold Rate | MF4.9.5 |
| Messaging Metrics | MF1.3.33 (Messaging Conversation volume) |
| Call Metrics | MF3.5.14 (Call initiation volume/cost) |
| Lead Metrics | MF1.4.4 (CPL), MF1.3.32 (Lead Event) |
| Catalog Metrics | MF1.3.35-36, MF3.5.13 |
| App Metrics | MF1.4.11 (CPI), MF1.5.5 |

### MF7.3.3 Diagnostic & Status Inputs

| Input | Governing Definition |
|---|---|
| Attribution Data | MF1.7 (Attribution Vocabulary) |
| Learning Phase Status | MF3.10/MF6.5/MF6.6.2-3 |
| Auction Diagnostics | MF6.4/MF6.13 |
| Audience Saturation | MF5.11 |
| Creative Fatigue | MF1.9.12/MF4.12 |

### MF7.3.4 Input Consumption Principle

This Framework consumes every input above strictly as evidence feeding the Decision Tree methodology (MF7.4) and Trigger definitions (MF7.10) — it never introduces a new formula, calculation, or measurement methodology for any of them. Where an input's interpretation is Status-conditional (per MF6.15.1's principle), this Framework's every Decision Tree and Trigger explicitly requires confirming that condition (Learning Stability, Delivery Status, minimum observation period) before treating the input as actionable evidence, per MF7.1.2.

---

## MF7.4 Optimization Decision Tree

The complete Decision Tree methodology, applied individually to every major Metric. Every tree follows MF7.1.3's six-component structure and terminates in a specific, cited Decision.

### MF7.4.1 CTR Decision Tree

```
Trigger: CTR below Benchmark (MF1.11.5) for 2+ consecutive observation periods (MF7.12.6)
  ↓
Conditions: Learning Stability confirmed (MF6.5.7); not within an active Creative Test (MF4.8)
  ↓
Check CPM (MF7.4.4's tree) — is CPM also elevated?
  ├─ Yes → Auction Competition-driven (MF6.4.6) → route to CPM tree, not a Creative issue
  └─ No (CPM normal) → Check Frequency (MF6.10.1)
       ├─ Frequency elevated at plateaued Reach → Audience Saturation (MF5.11) → Audience Expansion (MF5.14.1)
       └─ Frequency normal → Creative issue (MF4.13.1's Low CTR diagnosis)
            ↓
       Decision: Rotate Creative (MF4.8.3) — introduce a tested alternative Hook (MF4.5) or full Creative Refresh (MF4.14.1)
            ↓
       Expected Outcome: CTR returns to within Benchmark range within the Validation window
            ↓
       Risk: Creative change constitutes a significant edit (MF3.10.4), triggering a temporary Learning Reset
            ↓
       Validation: Monitor 48 hours minimum (MF7.12.6's Daily-cadence floor) before judging the rotation's effect, extending to a full Optimization Window (MF6.5.5) before concluding failure
```

### MF7.4.2 CPA Decision Tree

```
Trigger: CPA above Benchmark/acceptable threshold for 2+ consecutive periods
  ↓
Conditions: Learning Stability confirmed; Purchase/Conversion value-tracking integrity verified (MF1.3.31)
  ↓
Decompose per MF2.10.2: CPM risen? CTR fallen? CVR fallen?
  ├─ CPM-driven → route to CPM tree (MF7.4.4)
  ├─ CTR-driven → route to CTR tree (MF7.4.1)
  ├─ CVR-driven → route to CVR tree (MF7.4.6)
  └─ All three stable → Attribution/Matching Quality degradation (MF1.6.17) → verify tracking before any lever change
       ↓
  Decision: Apply the matched sub-tree's Decision, never a generic Bid/Budget increase without decomposition
       ↓
  Expected Outcome: CPA returns toward Benchmark once the decomposed root cause is resolved
       ↓
  Risk: Applying a Bid Strategy change without decomposition treats a symptom, not the cause, and may mask the true driver
       ↓
  Validation: Full Optimization Window (MF6.5.5) observation post-fix, given CPA compounds multiple upstream Metrics
```

### MF7.4.3 ROAS Decision Tree

```
Trigger: ROAS below breakeven/Benchmark for 2+ consecutive periods
  ↓
Conditions: Purchase value-passing integrity verified FIRST (MF1.3.31/MF2.10.3) — this gate is non-negotiable before any other branch
  ↓
Value tracking confirmed accurate?
  ├─ No → Fix tracking/Attribution Window/Model consistency (MF1.3.41/MF3.6) — STOP, do not proceed to other branches until resolved
  └─ Yes → Decompose per MF2.10.3: Purchase volume/value declining? Spend rising disproportionately?
       ├─ Purchase-side decline → CVR/Conversion Rate Ranking issue → route to CVR tree (MF7.4.6)
       └─ Spend-side rise → route to CPA tree (MF7.4.2)
            ↓
       Decision: Apply the matched sub-tree's Decision
            ↓
       Expected Outcome: ROAS recovers toward breakeven once the decomposed cause resolves
            ↓
       Risk: Acting on ROAS before the tracking-integrity gate risks "fixing" a measurement artifact with a real, costly configuration change
            ↓
       Validation: Full Optimization Window observation; re-confirm tracking integrity remains stable throughout
```

### MF7.4.4 CPM Decision Tree

```
Trigger: CPM above Benchmark for 2+ consecutive periods, no internal configuration change
  ↓
Conditions: Learning Stability confirmed; cross-checked against historical same-period Benchmark (MF1.11.5) for Seasonality (MF6.9.9)
  ↓
Coincides with known seasonal/competitive period?
  ├─ Yes → Expected Auction Competition (MF6.4.6) → adjust Budget/ROAS expectations, no Creative/Audience action
  └─ No → Check Ad Quality Rankings (MF1.3.44-46)
       ├─ Declining → Weak Ad Quality (MF6.13.10) → route to Creative Diagnostics (MF4.13)
       └─ Stable → Check Placement/Audience restriction (MF3.4.1/MF5.3.23)
            ↓
       Decision: Broaden Placement toward Advantage+ (MF3.4.2) or broaden Audience (MF5.14.1) if restriction-driven; remediate Creative if Ranking-driven
            ↓
       Expected Outcome: CPM normalizes toward Benchmark within one Learning cycle post-broadening
            ↓
       Risk: Broadening Placement/Audience without a genuine restriction cause wastes effort and may worsen Relevance (MF4.7.6)
            ↓
       Validation: Compare CPM trend across the next Optimization Window against the pre-change baseline
```

### MF7.4.5 CPC Decision Tree

```
Trigger: CPC above Benchmark for 2+ consecutive periods
  ↓
Conditions: Learning Stability confirmed
  ↓
Decompose per MF1.4.3: is the driver CPM or CTR?
  ├─ CPM-driven → route to CPM tree (MF7.4.4)
  └─ CTR-driven → route to CTR tree (MF7.4.1)
       ↓
  Decision: CPC itself is never the target of a direct action — always apply the decomposed sub-tree's Decision (MF1.4.3's Common Misinterpretations)
       ↓
  Expected Outcome: CPC normalizes as its decomposed driver resolves
       ↓
  Risk: Treating CPC as an independent root cause and applying an undifferentiated Bid change
       ↓
  Validation: Inherits the Validation window of the decomposed sub-tree
```

### MF7.4.6 CVR Decision Tree

```
Trigger: CVR below Benchmark for 2+ consecutive periods, despite healthy upstream CTR
  ↓
Conditions: Learning Stability confirmed; CTR confirmed healthy first (isolating the failure point downstream of the click)
  ↓
Check Conversion Rate Ranking (MF1.3.46) and Initiate-Checkout-to-Purchase ratio (MF1.6.6) if Sales-objective
  ├─ Ranking declining, Checkout ratio healthy → Audience relevance issue (MF5.13.2's Audience Too Broad) → Audience Segmentation (MF5.9)/Narrowing (MF5.3.23)
  └─ Checkout ratio itself poor → Landing-page/checkout/offer issue — outside this Framework's and this document lineage's direct remediation scope; escalate to the destination-experience owner
       ↓
  Decision: Apply Audience-side fix if Audience-driven; escalate outside the Meta Ads system if landing-page-driven (explicitly NOT a Bid/Budget/Creative action)
       ↓
  Expected Outcome: CVR recovers toward Benchmark once the isolated cause is addressed
       ↓
  Risk: Applying a Meta-Ads-side lever (Bid, Budget, Creative) to a genuinely landing-page-side problem wastes effort and delays the real fix
       ↓
  Validation: Full Optimization Window observation; landing-page fixes require coordination outside this Framework's own timeline control
```

### MF7.4.7 Decision Tree Application Principle

Every Decision Tree in this section shares the same four-stage skeleton: **Decompose** (isolate the specific driving Metric among several plausible candidates) → **Route** (send the diagnosis to the correct specialized sub-tree or Engine, MF7.5–MF7.9) → **Decide** (apply exactly one cited Decision) → **Validate** (MF7.15). This skeleton, not any individual tree's specific branches, is this Framework's actual reusable contribution — new Metrics not explicitly covered here should be decomposed using this same four-stage skeleton against their own known constituent factors (per each Metric's own decomposition guidance already established in MF1.4).

---

## MF7.5 Creative Optimization Engine

Every Creative-layer optimization action, each expressed per MF7.1.3's six-component structure, citing its full governing definition in MF4 rather than re-deriving it.

### MF7.5.1 Creative Fatigue Detection

- **Trigger:** Declining CTR/Hook Rate/Engagement Rate correlated with time-since-launch or rising Frequency (MF4.12.6).
- **Conditions:** Multi-period Trend confirmed (MF1.11.8); Learning Stability confirmed.
- **Decision:** Apply MF4.12.7's Detection methodology to decompose into the specific Fatigue sub-type (Frequency/Visual/Offer/Audience, MF4.12.2–5).
- **Expected Outcome:** A specific, actionable sub-type diagnosis rather than a generic "fatigue" label.
- **Risk:** Misdiagnosing the sub-type leads to an ineffective, mismatched Recovery action (MF4.12.8's Common Mistake).
- **Validation:** Confirm the decomposed sub-type against at least two corroborating signals (e.g., Frequency trend AND Hook Rate trend) before acting.

### MF7.5.2 Creative Rotation

- **Trigger:** Testing Calendar (MF4.8.10) scheduled cadence, or proactive Fatigue Score (MF4.9.18) threshold approach.
- **Conditions:** A validated alternative Creative variant available (MF4.8.1–2).
- **Decision:** Rotate per MF4.8.3, under Creative Isolation (MF4.8.4).
- **Expected Outcome:** Sustained KPI health without the reactive decline a purely symptomatic response would require.
- **Risk:** Rotating on too short a cycle prevents any variant from reaching Statistical Significance (MF4.8.6).
- **Validation:** Track outcome in the Creative Learning repository (MF4.8.7).

### MF7.5.3 Creative Refresh Strategy

- **Trigger:** Visual Fatigue (MF4.12.3) or Low CTR/Hook Rate (MF4.13.1/3) confirmed diagnosis.
- **Conditions:** Genuinely novel concept available, not a superficial variant (MF1.9.12's Common Mistakes).
- **Decision:** Apply MF4.14.1's Creative Refresh.
- **Expected Outcome:** Hook Rate/CTR recovery within one Learning Phase cycle.
- **Risk:** A superficial refresh fails to resolve genuine Fatigue, wasting the Learning Reset cost without the intended benefit.
- **Validation:** Compare post-Refresh Hook Rate/CTR against pre-Refresh baseline over a full Optimization Window (MF6.5.5).

### MF7.5.4 Hook Optimization

- **Trigger:** Low Hook Rate/Thumb Stop Rate (MF4.13.3) with body content confirmed sound.
- **Conditions:** Multiple Hook types available for testing (MF4.5's catalog).
- **Decision:** Apply MF4.14.5's Hook Replacement, selecting per MF4.5.17's Selection Summary.
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved Hook Rate within one Learning cycle.
- **Risk:** Replacing a working body's Hook without isolating the change (MF4.8.5) confounds attribution of the improvement.
- **Validation:** Isolated A/B comparison (MF4.8.1) against the prior Hook.

### MF7.5.5 Thumbnail Optimization

- **Trigger:** Low CTR specifically in non-autoplay/preview contexts, or an auto-selected, unclear Thumbnail frame identified at QA (MF4.17.5).
- **Conditions:** A deliberately-selected alternative frame or custom Thumbnail asset available.
- **Decision:** Apply MF4.3.6's guidance — select a frame clearly conveying subject/value proposition.
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved preview-context CTR.
- **Risk:** Low overall impact relative to other levers if the Placement mix is primarily autoplay-default.
- **Validation:** Compare preview-context CTR pre/post change.

### MF7.5.6 Headline Optimization

- **Trigger:** Diagnostics indicate a messaging-clarity issue (adequate Thumb Stop Rate, weak subsequent CTR) rather than a Hook/visual-attention issue.
- **Conditions:** Media and other components held constant (MF4.8.5).
- **Decision:** Apply MF4.14.7, testing variants emphasizing different single differentiators (MF4.3.1).
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved CTR without a corresponding Hook Rate change (confirming the isolation was correct).
- **Risk:** Changing Headline and Media simultaneously prevents attributing any resulting change correctly.
- **Validation:** Isolated A/B comparison.

### MF7.5.7 Primary Text Optimization

- **Trigger:** Diagnostics indicate a deeper message/Trust/Offer-clarity issue downstream of a working Hook and Headline.
- **Conditions:** Media, Headline, and CTA held constant.
- **Decision:** Apply MF4.14.8, testing variants emphasizing different psychological principles (MF4.4) or Trust/Social Proof components (MF4.3.10–11).
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved CVR/Engagement without a corresponding upstream Metric change.
- **Risk:** Same variable-isolation risk as Headline Optimization.
- **Validation:** Isolated A/B comparison.

### MF7.5.8 CTA Optimization

- **Trigger:** Weak CTA diagnosis (MF4.13.12) — adequate Hook/Engagement but low click-through/conversation-initiation specifically at the CTA-decision moment.
- **Conditions:** CTA-Optimization-Event alignment verified per MF4.2.15's pattern before testing alternatives.
- **Decision:** Apply MF4.14.6, selecting per MF4.6's Selection Summary Table.
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved CTR/conversation-initiation within days.
- **Risk:** Testing a CTA mismatched to the actual destination regardless of selection (MF1.9.5's Common Mistakes).
- **Validation:** Isolated A/B comparison, fast turnaround given CTA is a smaller, more contained edit.

### MF7.5.9 Offer Optimization

- **Trigger:** Offer Mismatch (MF4.13.11) or Offer Fatigue (MF4.12.4) diagnosis.
- **Conditions:** An alternative Offer structure genuinely deliverable at the actual destination (MF4.3.8's specificity standard).
- **Decision:** Apply MF4.14.9.
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved CVR/Conversion Quality (MF4.7.3).
- **Risk:** Overselling relative to actual delivery, worsening Trust (MF4.4.11) rather than improving Conversion.
- **Validation:** Full Optimization Window observation, tracking both CVR and downstream Conversion Quality where feedback is available.

### MF7.5.10 Video Length Optimization

- **Trigger:** Low Hold Rate/Completion Rate (MF4.13.4) with pacing suspected as the cause.
- **Conditions:** Alternative-length edits of the same core concept available.
- **Decision:** Apply MF4.10.1's Video Length guidance, testing length as an explicit variable.
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved Hold Rate/Completion Rate at the adjusted length.
- **Risk:** Shortening too aggressively may cut the Offer/CTA before delivery.
- **Validation:** Compare watch-percentage curve shape (MF4.9.4–6) pre/post length change, not just the endpoint Completion Rate.

### MF7.5.11 Image Optimization

- **Trigger:** Low CTR/Quality Ranking diagnosis (MF4.13.1) for static-image Creative specifically.
- **Conditions:** Image Quality/Compression (MF4.11.11–12) ruled out as the cause first.
- **Decision:** Apply MF4.14.10, testing one visual-production variable (MF4.11.1–5) at a time.
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved CTR/Quality Ranking.
- **Risk:** Testing multiple visual-production variables simultaneously confounds attribution.
- **Validation:** Isolated A/B comparison per variable.

### MF7.5.12 Carousel Optimization

- **Trigger:** Weak CTR on a Carousel-format Creative (MF4.2.3) despite strong individual-card asset quality.
- **Conditions:** Card order and content reviewed against MF4.2.3's strongest-first sequencing principle.
- **Decision:** Reorder cards leading with the strongest product/message; test card-count reduction if later cards show minimal individual engagement.
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved aggregate CTR from front-loading the strongest content.
- **Risk:** Reordering without underlying per-card engagement data is a guess, not evidence-based optimization (violating MF7.1.2).
- **Validation:** Compare aggregate and, where available, per-card engagement pre/post reorder.

### MF7.5.13 UGC (User-Generated Content) Optimization

- **Trigger:** Declining Trust/Engagement Quality (MF4.7.2) on brand-produced Creative, or a Testing Calendar (MF4.8.10) slot for Authenticity-driven variant testing.
- **Conditions:** Genuine UGC or Branded Content assets available (MF4.2.13), properly disclosed per MF4.17.6.
- **Decision:** Test UGC-style Creative against brand-produced Creative under Creative Isolation, leveraging the Social Proof/Authority principles (MF4.4.8/10) inherent to genuine creator content.
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved Trust-sensitive Metrics (Conversion Rate Ranking, MF1.3.46) relative to brand-produced Creative.
- **Risk:** Unlicensed or undisclosed UGC use is a compliance risk (MF4.17.6), independent of any performance benefit.
- **Validation:** Isolated A/B comparison with compliance sign-off confirmed before launch.

### MF7.5.14 Static vs. Video Decisions

- **Trigger:** A new Creative concept entering the Production Pipeline (MF4.1.6) with no format yet committed, or a Diagnostics finding suggesting the current format is a poor fit for the Objective/Placement.
- **Conditions:** Production capability and Budget for both formats considered (MF4.2.16's Production Complexity comparison).
- **Decision:** Select per MF4.2's per-format Ideal Objectives/Best Placements guidance — Static for low-complexity, high-iteration-speed needs (MF4.2.1); Video for storytelling/demonstration/Video Views Objectives (MF4.2.2).
- **Expected Outcome:** Format-Objective-Placement alignment improving Ad Quality (MF4.7.1) relative to a mismatched format choice.
- **Risk:** Defaulting to video for its perceived prestige without a genuine format-fit justification wastes production investment (MF4.9.19's Creative Efficiency Score consideration).
- **Validation:** Compare Creative Efficiency Score (MF4.9.19) across formats where both are tested for the same concept.

---

## MF7.6 Audience Optimization Engine

Every Audience-layer optimization action, each expressed per MF7.1.3's six-component structure, citing its full governing definition in MF5 rather than re-deriving it.

### MF7.6.1 Broad Audiences

- **Trigger:** Learning Limited (MF6.6.3) or Audience Too Small (MF5.13.1) diagnosis on a narrow Core/Lookalike Audience.
- **Conditions:** No specific, validated relevance hypothesis justifying continued narrowness.
- **Decision:** Migrate toward Advantage+ Audience (MF5.2.5/MF5.6) or a wider Core Audience configuration (MF5.3.25's default-toward-breadth principle).
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved Learning Progress (MF6.12.15) and Delivery Rate (MF6.12.10).
- **Risk:** Broadening without a Signal (MF5.6.1) may trade precision for volume more than intended.
- **Validation:** Monitor CVR/Conversion Rate Ranking alongside Delivery volume to confirm quality did not degrade disproportionately.

### MF7.6.2 Lookalikes

- **Trigger:** Reach Exhaustion (MF5.11.3) on a tight Lookalike tier, or a new high-quality Seed becoming available.
- **Conditions:** Seed Quality (MF5.5.2) validated.
- **Decision:** Apply MF5.5.14's Scaling Strategy — incremental tier expansion or a rebuilt Value-Based Lookalike (MF5.5.12).
- **Expected Outcome:** Renewed Reach growth at acceptable Cost Efficiency.
- **Risk:** Jumping tiers non-incrementally forfeits the ability to learn the true similarity-vs-quality tradeoff (MF5.5.4).
- **Validation:** Compare Cost Efficiency (MF6.12.13) across tiers before committing fully to the wider one.

### MF7.6.3 Interest Audiences

- **Trigger:** Weak Engagement (MF5.13.12) or Low CTR (MF4.13.1) traced to Audience-Creative Relevance mismatch (MF4.7.6) on an Interest-based Core Audience.
- **Conditions:** Alternative Interest/Behavior criteria available for testing (MF5.3.11–12).
- **Decision:** Re-align Interest criteria with the actual Creative concept, or reduce Layering (MF5.3.22) if over-narrowed.
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved Engagement/CTR within one Learning cycle.
- **Risk:** Interest criteria are inferred, not directly observed (MF5.3.12's Common Mistake) — treat conclusions with correspondingly moderate Confidence.
- **Validation:** Isolated comparison against the prior Interest configuration.

### MF7.6.4 Retargeting

- **Trigger:** Available Custom Audience source (MF5.4) with adequate volume but not yet deployed in a dedicated retargeting Ad Set; or existing retargeting showing Frequency Saturation (MF6.10.3).
- **Conditions:** Retention Window (MF5.4) relevance verified for the specific source.
- **Decision:** Deploy/refresh a Funnel-stage-appropriate retargeting Audience (MF5.8.2–3) matched to Buying Stage (MF5.7.11).
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved CVR/ROAS relative to broad prospecting.
- **Risk:** Over-retargeting a small pool accelerates Frequency Saturation (MF6.10.3).
- **Validation:** Monitor Frequency alongside CVR/ROAS.

### MF7.6.5 Exclusions

- **Trigger:** Confirmed Audience Overlap/Cannibalization (MF5.10.3) or existing-customer inclusion in a prospecting Ad Set.
- **Conditions:** The specific overlapping/excludable population identified (MF5.10.4's diagnostics).
- **Decision:** Apply MF5.10.8's Exclusion Strategy, unidirectionally per its Method guidance.
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved combined efficiency across the affected Ad Sets.
- **Risk:** Over-applying Exclusions shrinks eligible pools enough to risk Learning Limited (MF5.3.24's Common Mistake).
- **Validation:** Compare combined CPM/CPA across the affected Ad Sets pre/post Exclusion.

### MF7.6.6 Expansion

- **Trigger:** Reach Exhaustion (MF5.11.3), Delivery Slowdown (MF5.11.4), or planned Scaling (MF5.15).
- **Conditions:** Optimization Stability/Learning Stability confirmed (MF6.5.7) before applying.
- **Decision:** Apply the Audience-type-appropriate Expansion mechanism (MF5.11.7) — Lookalike tier widening, Advantage+ Signal loosening, or Detailed Targeting Layering reduction.
- **Expected Outcome:** Renewed Reach growth without disproportionate quality loss.
- **Risk:** Expanding before Stability is confirmed conflates Expansion effect with ordinary post-Learning variance.
- **Validation:** Full Optimization Window observation of Reach growth and Cost Efficiency together.

### MF7.6.7 Audience Consolidation

- **Trigger:** Multiple overlapping or redundant Ad Sets serving no genuinely distinct strategic purpose (MF5.10.6's Merge Strategy precondition).
- **Conditions:** Confirmed via Overlap Diagnostics (MF5.10.4) that consolidation would not sacrifice a genuinely-used reporting/Bid Strategy distinction.
- **Decision:** Apply MF5.10.6's Merge Strategy.
- **Expected Outcome:** Simplified structure with equal or improved aggregate efficiency (via reduced Internal Competition, MF5.10.2).
- **Risk:** Consolidating Ad Sets that were in fact serving a distinct, valuable purpose loses that granularity.
- **Validation:** Compare aggregate efficiency pre/post Merge over a full Optimization Window.

### MF7.6.8 Audience Fragmentation

- **Trigger:** A single broad Audience masking meaningfully different sub-segment performance, identified via Breakdown analysis (MF1.3.39).
- **Conditions:** Each resulting segment projected to retain sufficient volume for Learning Phase exit (MF3.10.5) — verified via MF5.10.7's risk guidance before splitting.
- **Decision:** Apply MF5.10.7's Split Strategy, differentiating Bid Strategy/Creative treatment per resulting segment.
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved aggregate efficiency from segment-specific treatment exceeding the cost of thinner per-segment Learning data.
- **Risk:** Over-splitting thins Learning Phase data per Ad Set below the Required Events threshold (MF3.10.5).
- **Validation:** Confirm each resulting segment reaches Learning Complete within a reasonable further Optimization Window; if not, this was over-Fragmentation and should be reconsolidated (MF7.6.7).

### MF7.6.9 Audience Saturation (Optimization-Engine View)

Cross-referenced from the full treatment at MF5.11 and MF6.10. **Decision:** Apply MF5.11.9's Saturation Response Decision Tree directly — this Framework does not introduce a separate Saturation remedy, only confirms it as a standing Trigger (MF7.10.13) within the broader Optimization Engine.

### MF7.6.10 Overlap Reduction

Cross-referenced from the full treatment at MF5.10. **Decision:** Apply MF5.10.9's Overlap Remediation Decision Tree (Merge, Split, or Exclusion per the specific diagnosed situation) — this entry confirms Overlap Reduction as a standing Optimization Engine action, not a new remedy beyond MF5.10's own catalog (MF7.6.5/7/8 above are its three constituent Decision paths).

---

## MF7.7 Budget Optimization Engine

Every Budget-layer optimization action, each expressed per MF7.1.3's six-component structure, citing its full governing definition in MF2.5/MF3.7/MF3.13/MF5.15/MF6.7 rather than re-deriving it.

### MF7.7.1 Budget Increase Rules

- **Trigger:** Confirmed Optimization Stability (MF6.5.7, not merely Learning Complete) with healthy Cost Efficiency (MF6.12.13) and headroom confirmed (no Frequency/Inventory Saturation, MF6.10.4/MF6.11.5).
- **Conditions:** No other significant edit pending simultaneously (MF2.9.6 rule 3).
- **Decision:** Apply an incremental increase (15–20%, per MF2.9.5) via Vertical Scaling (MF3.13.2).
- **Expected Outcome:** Proportional Delivery growth at maintained or near-maintained Cost Efficiency.
- **Risk:** Increasing before Stability is confirmed conflates the increase's effect with ordinary post-Learning variance.
- **Validation:** Full Optimization Window observation before the next increase (MF2.9.6 rule 4).

### MF7.7.2 Budget Reduction Rules

- **Trigger:** Sustained Decline (MF2.7.10, multi-period Trend) in Cost Efficiency without a clear, addressable root cause, or a deliberate Campaign Lifecycle de-prioritization.
- **Conditions:** Diagnostics Framework (MF6.13) has been applied first — reduction should not substitute for diagnosis.
- **Decision:** Reduce Budget incrementally, mirroring the Increase Rules' incremental discipline (MF7.7.1) in reverse.
- **Expected Outcome:** Reduced spend without disproportionate Learning disruption if the reduction stays within non-significant-edit bounds.
- **Risk:** An overly large single reduction can itself constitute a significant edit (MF3.10.4), resetting Learning unnecessarily.
- **Validation:** Confirm Delivery Status remains Active (not sliding into Limited, MF6.6.6) post-reduction.

### MF7.7.3 Scaling Thresholds

- **Definition:** The specific, Business-calibrated Cost Efficiency and Delivery Rate levels at which a Scaling decision (Increase, MF7.7.1) or a Scaling-pause decision (MF6.14.5) is triggered, rather than an arbitrary or purely calendar-driven cadence.
- **Decision:** Calibrate Thresholds from the Business's own historical Benchmark data (MF1.11.5), not a generic industry figure, per this document lineage's repeated self-referential-Benchmark preference (MF2.11.10 pattern).
- **Validation:** Revisit Thresholds periodically as Historical Signals (MF6.9.3) accumulate and the Business's own Benchmark matures.

### MF7.7.4 Vertical Scaling (Optimization-Engine View)

Cross-referenced from the full treatment at MF3.13.2/MF5.15.2/MF6.14.5. **Decision:** Apply only once Reach Exhaustion (MF5.11.3) and Frequency (MF6.10.4) are confirmed not yet binding, per MF6.14.5's Decision rule — this entry confirms Vertical Scaling as a standing Optimization Engine action gated by those prior Frameworks' own preconditions.

### MF7.7.5 Horizontal Scaling (Optimization-Engine View)

Cross-referenced from the full treatment at MF3.13.1/MF5.15.1. **Decision:** Apply when Vertical Scaling (MF7.7.4) is blocked by confirmed Inventory/Reach constraints — Horizontal Scaling accesses new Inventory rather than intensifying pressure on an already-constrained pool (MF6.11.7's Decision Tree).

### MF7.7.6 Safe Scaling

- **Definition:** The synthesis of this document lineage's incremental-change discipline (MF2.9.5–6, MF3.10.11, MF5.15.9) applied specifically to Budget decisions — small, isolated, Stability-gated increments with a full observation window between each.
- **Decision:** The default Scaling posture for this Framework; Aggressive Scaling (MF7.7.7) is the deliberate, risk-accepting exception, never the default.
- **Validation:** Every Safe Scaling step logged with its Expected Outcome (MF7.1.3) and checked against realized outcome before the next step.

### MF7.7.7 Aggressive Scaling

- **Trigger:** A time-bound, high-confidence opportunity (e.g., a confirmed favorable, temporary Auction Dynamics window, MF6.14.6) where the cost of missing the window exceeds the risk of a larger, faster Budget increase.
- **Conditions:** Explicit advertiser risk-acceptance decision, documented per MF7.17's Enterprise Standards (never a default posture).
- **Decision:** A larger, faster Budget increase than MF2.9.5's standard incremental guidance, accepting a higher probability of a temporary Learning Reset or efficiency dip.
- **Expected Outcome:** Captured incremental volume during the confirmed favorable window that Safe Scaling's slower pace would have missed.
- **Risk:** The single highest-risk action in this entire Optimization Engine — misjudging the window's genuineness produces the exact Budget Panic pattern flagged at MF7.16.5.
- **Validation:** Immediate post-action monitoring (daily, not weekly) given the elevated risk; a defined Rollback Condition (MF7.15.4) must be pre-stated before executing.

### MF7.7.8 Budget Recovery

- **Trigger:** A prior Scaling or Budget change (MF7.7.1/7) produced an unexpected Decline (MF2.7.10) or Delivery instability.
- **Conditions:** The specific prior change identified via Version Control (MF2.13.2/MF3.15.3).
- **Decision:** Revert to the last known-stable Budget configuration, mirroring MF2.9.8's Recovery Plans.
- **Expected Outcome:** Return to the pre-change stable baseline.
- **Risk:** Reverting without first diagnosing the actual cause (MF6.13) risks repeating the same mistake on a future Scaling attempt.
- **Validation:** Confirm stable Delivery Status and Cost Efficiency for a full Optimization Window post-revert before any further Scaling attempt.

### MF7.7.9 Budget Allocation

- **Trigger:** Multiple Ad Sets/Campaigns competing for a Business's total available Budget, requiring a deliberate distribution decision.
- **Conditions:** Value Segmentation (MF5.9.6) and Funnel Audience stage (MF5.8) data available for each competing allocation candidate.
- **Decision:** Prioritize allocation toward the highest Expected Value (MF6.15.6) candidates — combining confirmed Cost Efficiency with strategic Funnel-stage importance, not Cost Efficiency alone (a TOF Awareness Ad Set feeding a highly efficient BOF retargeting pool may warrant allocation despite weaker standalone Cost Efficiency).
- **Expected Outcome:** Improved aggregate portfolio outcome versus a purely per-Ad-Set Cost-Efficiency-ranked allocation.
- **Risk:** Under-crediting upper-funnel contribution (mirroring MF1.7.6's Assisted Conversion risk) leads to systematically under-allocating TOF/MOF stages.
- **Validation:** Track aggregate portfolio-level outcome (not just per-Ad-Set outcome) over successive allocation periods.

### MF7.7.10 Budget Protection

- **Trigger:** Any Budget change exceeding the Business's defined materiality threshold (MF6.17.8's Delivery Policies), or approaching a Campaign/Account-level Spend Limit (MF3.7.5).
- **Conditions:** None — this is a standing governance gate, not a diagnosis-triggered action.
- **Decision:** Route through the full Approval Workflow (MF2.13.4/MF3.15.4) before execution; maintain a Campaign/Account-level Spend Limit (MF3.7.5) as a backstop regardless of individual Ad Set Budget configuration accuracy.
- **Expected Outcome:** No unreviewed, unintended overspend event.
- **Risk:** Excessive Protection gating can slow legitimate Safe Scaling (MF7.7.6) responsiveness — calibrate the materiality threshold to balance both risks.
- **Validation:** Periodic Audit (MF6.17.6-style) confirming no Budget change bypassed the Protection gate.

---

## MF7.8 Learning Phase Optimization

Extends MF3.10/MF6.5's full Learning Framework treatment with Optimization-Engine-level decision guidance on managing Learning proactively.

### MF7.8.1 How Learning Works (cross-reference)

Fully defined at MF3.10.1/MF6.5.1. This Framework's contribution: treat every optimization decision in MF7.5–MF7.7 as carrying an implicit "does this reset Learning?" check (MF7.8.2) before execution, since nearly every lever this Engine touches is capable of triggering it.

### MF7.8.2 What Resets Learning (Consolidated)

Per MF3.10.4/MF6.5.4: any change to Audience, Placement, Creative set, Optimization Goal, or Bid Strategy. This Framework's contribution: a pre-action checklist —

| Proposed Action | Resets Learning? |
|---|---|
| Creative Rotation/Refresh (MF7.5.2–3) | Yes |
| Hook/CTA/Headline/Offer Optimization (MF7.5.4/6/8–9) | Yes (component-level edit to the live Creative) |
| Audience Expansion/Narrowing (MF7.6.6, MF5.3.23) | Yes, if the change is significant per MF6.5.4's threshold |
| Exclusions (MF7.6.5) | Depends on magnitude — a small Exclusion may not qualify as significant; verify against MF3.10.4 |
| Budget change within incremental guidance (MF7.7.1) | Generally no, if within MF2.9.5's incremental bounds |
| Bid Strategy change (MF2.6) | Yes |
| Schedule/Dayparting change (MF3.8) | Generally no, unless it substantially reduces effective eligible hours |

### MF7.8.3 How to Avoid Learning Reset

- **Batch necessary changes together** rather than serially, each triggering its own reset (MF2.8.4's Best Practice, MF3.10.11's Recovery Strategy discipline) — if two significant edits are both genuinely needed, apply them in the same action rather than two separate ones.
- **Schedule changes during lower-stakes periods**, not mid-Scaling or mid-critical-Campaign-window (MF2.8.4).
- **Prefer non-significant levers first** when a choice exists — e.g., Schedule/Dayparting adjustment (generally non-resetting) over an Audience change, if both could plausibly address the same Diagnostics finding.

### MF7.8.4 When to Duplicate

- **Trigger:** Horizontal Scaling (MF7.7.5) into a genuinely differentiated Audience/Placement, or a controlled A/B Test (MF4.8.1) requiring an isolated, undisturbed original.
- **Decision:** Duplicate (MF2.9.3/MF4.14.3) rather than editing the original in place, preserving the original's accumulated Learning/reporting history.
- **Risk:** Duplicating into an insufficiently differentiated Audience reproduces Audience Overlap (MF5.10) rather than adding genuine incremental value.

### MF7.8.5 When to Edit

- **Trigger:** A Diagnostics finding (MF6.13/MF4.13/MF5.13) clearly isolates a specific, correctable component issue on an existing, otherwise-healthy Ad Set.
- **Decision:** Edit in place (accepting the Learning Reset cost) rather than duplicating, when the goal is to fix, not to add parallel capacity.
- **Risk:** Editing when Duplication would have preserved valuable existing Learning history unnecessarily forfeits that history.

### MF7.8.6 When to Restart

- **Trigger:** Learning Limited (MF6.6.3) persisting despite the full ordered Recovery Strategy (MF6.5.10) being applied without success, indicating the current Ad Set's fundamental configuration (not a single lever) is unworkable.
- **Decision:** Retire the Ad Set (MF3.2.4) and build a fresh one incorporating the accumulated diagnostic learning (what specifically failed) rather than continuing to iterate on a structurally unworkable configuration.
- **Risk:** Restarting too early (before genuinely exhausting Recovery Strategy options) discards salvageable Learning progress unnecessarily.

---

## MF7.9 Objective-Specific Optimization

Every Meta Objective's own optimization logic, unique to its Primary KPI (MF2.11.1), extending MF2.3's per-Objective analysis with this Framework's decision-engine layer.

### MF7.9.1 Awareness Optimization Logic

- **Primary KPI:** Reach, CPM (MF2.3.1).
- **Unique Optimization Rule:** Optimize for Frequency-managed Reach growth, never for CTR/CVR (MF2.3.1's Optimization Priorities) — a Diagnostics finding of "low CTR" on an Awareness Ad Set is not a valid Trigger for any action in this Framework, since CTR is not this Objective's evidentiary basis.
- **Trigger:** Frequency (MF6.10.4) approaching Optimal ceiling at plateaued Reach.
- **Decision:** Audience Expansion (MF7.6.6) or Creative Rotation (MF7.5.2) per MF6.10.6's Frequency Diagnostics — never a Bid/CPA-driven action, which is not this Objective's concern.
- **Validation:** Reach growth rate and Frequency trend only; CTR/CVR trends are explicitly out of scope for this Objective's Validation.

### MF7.9.2 Traffic Optimization Logic

- **Primary KPI:** CTR, CPC (MF2.3.2).
- **Unique Optimization Rule:** Diagnose Click-quality specifically via the Link-Click-to-Landing-Page-View ratio (MF1.3.29's Common Mistakes) before any Creative/Bid action — a healthy CTR with a poor LPV ratio is a landing-page issue, not a Traffic-objective Creative failure.
- **Trigger:** CTR below Benchmark (MF7.4.1's tree), or a Link-Click-to-LPV ratio decline.
- **Decision:** Apply the CTR Decision Tree (MF7.4.1) for a genuine CTR issue; escalate outside this Framework's remediation scope for an LPV-ratio issue.
- **Validation:** CTR/CPC alongside the LPV ratio specifically, not CTR in isolation.

### MF7.9.3 Engagement Optimization Logic

- **Primary KPI:** Post Engagement volume or Messaging Conversation volume, sub-type-dependent (MF2.3.3).
- **Unique Optimization Rule:** Confirm sub-type/business-goal alignment (MF1.5.3's Decision Rules) before any other optimization action — the single highest-leverage decision for this Objective is verifying the correct sub-type (Messaging vs. Post Engagement vs. Video Views) is even configured, per MF2.3.3's own Decision Tree.
- **Trigger:** High engagement volume with negligible downstream business-relevant outcome (MF2.3.3's Common Failure Reasons).
- **Decision:** Correct the sub-type mismatch first (a Campaign/Ad-Set-layer Optimization Goal change, MF3.5) before applying any Creative-layer fix — a Creative Optimization action (MF7.5) cannot compensate for a fundamentally mismatched sub-type.
- **Validation:** The specific sub-type's own KPI (Messaging Conversation volume for Messaging; Cost Per Engagement for Post Engagement) tracked distinctly, never a blended Engagement figure across sub-types.

### MF7.9.4 Leads Optimization Logic

- **Primary KPI:** CPL (MF2.3.4).
- **Unique Optimization Rule:** Never optimize purely for lower CPL without a CRM-fed quality feedback check (MF1.5.4's Decision Rules) — this Objective's single most important Optimization Engine gate, since CPL alone can trivially be "improved" by degrading lead quality.
- **Trigger:** CPL below Benchmark AND downstream qualified-lead rate (external CRM data) declining — the combination, not CPL alone, is the valid Trigger for concern.
- **Decision:** Integrate CRM-fed offline Conversions (MF1.5.4's Decision Rules) to redirect Estimated Action Rate (MF6.2.6) toward quality rather than volume; only after this integration should further CPL-focused Bid/Budget optimization proceed.
- **Validation:** Qualified-lead rate trend (external CRM), never CPL trend alone.

### MF7.9.5 App Promotion Optimization Logic

- **Primary KPI:** CPI (App Installs sub-type) or cost-per-in-app-Event (App Events sub-type) (MF2.3.5).
- **Unique Optimization Rule:** Monitor for the maturity-transition Trigger from App Installs to App Events (MF1.5.5's Decision Rules, MF3.13.7) — remaining on Installs indefinitely after sufficient in-app Event volume exists is this Objective's specific, named Common Failure Reason.
- **Trigger:** Sufficient in-app Event volume accumulated to plausibly support Learning Phase exit under an App Events Optimization Goal.
- **Decision:** Migrate Optimization Event from App Installs to App Events (MF3.13.7), expecting a fresh Learning cycle.
- **Validation:** Post-install engagement/retention quality, not install volume alone — and always platform-segmented (iOS/Android) given measurement-completeness differences (MF1.5.5).

### MF7.9.6 Sales Optimization Logic

- **Primary KPI:** ROAS, CPA (MF2.3.6).
- **Unique Optimization Rule:** Purchase-value-tracking integrity verification is a non-negotiable, standing gate before any ROAS-driven decision (MF7.4.3's tree) — this Objective carries the highest Financial stakes in this Framework's entire Hierarchy (MF2.3.6's Risk Analysis), and every ROAS Trigger must pass this gate first.
- **Trigger:** ROAS below breakeven (post-tracking-verification), or Catalog data staleness (MF1.3.35) detected for Catalog Sales sub-type specifically.
- **Decision:** Apply the ROAS Decision Tree (MF7.4.3); for Catalog Sales specifically, additionally verify Catalog/Product Set data freshness before any Audience/Bid action (MF4.2.10's Common Mistakes) — Catalog Optimization's unique rule is that data-quality issues must be excluded before any Delivery-lever diagnosis proceeds.
- **Validation:** ROAS/CPA trend plus explicit re-confirmation of value-tracking integrity at each Validation checkpoint, not merely at initial diagnosis.

### MF7.9.7 Objective-Specific Optimization Cross-Reference Table

| Objective | Primary KPI | Non-Negotiable Gate | Governing Decision Tree/Engine |
|---|---|---|---|
| Awareness | Reach, CPM | None (lowest-stakes gate) | Frequency Diagnostics (MF6.10.6) |
| Traffic | CTR, CPC | LPV ratio check | CTR Tree (MF7.4.1) |
| Engagement | Post Engagement/Messaging volume | Sub-type alignment | Optimization Goal correction (MF3.5) |
| Leads | CPL | CRM-fed quality feedback | CPA-adjacent logic, quality-gated |
| App Promotion | CPI / cost-per-Event | Install-to-Event maturity check | MF3.13.7's migration logic |
| Sales | ROAS, CPA | Value-tracking integrity | ROAS Tree (MF7.4.3) |

---

## MF7.10 Optimization Triggers

The consolidated Trigger definitions this Framework's Decision Trees (MF7.4) and Engines (MF7.5–7.9) key off of. Every Trigger requires Confirmation (a multi-period Trend, MF1.11.8) before being considered valid, per MF7.1.2.

### MF7.10.1 High CPA

Cross-referenced from MF1.4.5/MF2.10.2/MF6.13. **Trigger Definition:** CPA above Benchmark/acceptable threshold, Confirmed. **Routes to:** MF7.4.2's CPA Decision Tree.

### MF7.10.2 Low ROAS

Cross-referenced from MF1.4.6/MF2.10.3/MF6.13.11. **Trigger Definition:** ROAS below breakeven, Confirmed, post-tracking-verification. **Routes to:** MF7.4.3's ROAS Decision Tree.

### MF7.10.3 High Frequency

Cross-referenced from MF1.4.7/MF6.10. **Trigger Definition:** Frequency exceeding Optimal Frequency (MF6.10.4) for the Objective/Audience type. **Routes to:** MF6.10.6's Frequency Diagnostics.

### MF7.10.4 Low CTR

Cross-referenced from MF1.4.2/MF4.13.1. **Trigger Definition:** CTR below Benchmark, Confirmed. **Routes to:** MF7.4.1's CTR Decision Tree.

### MF7.10.5 High CPC

Cross-referenced from MF1.4.3/MF3.12.9. **Trigger Definition:** CPC above Benchmark, Confirmed. **Routes to:** MF7.4.5's CPC Decision Tree.

### MF7.10.6 High CPM

Cross-referenced from MF1.4.1/MF6.13.3. **Trigger Definition:** CPM above Benchmark with no internal configuration change, Confirmed. **Routes to:** MF7.4.4's CPM Decision Tree.

### MF7.10.7 Low CVR

Cross-referenced from MF1.4.12/MF3.12.10. **Trigger Definition:** CVR below Benchmark despite healthy upstream CTR, Confirmed. **Routes to:** MF7.4.6's CVR Decision Tree.

### MF7.10.8 Poor Hook Rate

Cross-referenced from MF4.9.4/MF4.13.3. **Trigger Definition:** Hook Rate below Benchmark. **Routes to:** MF7.5.4's Hook Optimization.

### MF7.10.9 Poor Hold Rate

Cross-referenced from MF4.9.5/MF4.13.4. **Trigger Definition:** Hold Rate below Benchmark with Hook Rate confirmed healthy. **Routes to:** MF7.5.3's Creative Refresh Strategy or MF7.5.10's Video Length Optimization.

### MF7.10.10 High Bounce

- **Trigger Definition:** A high proportion of Link Clicks (MF1.3.29) failing to register a corresponding Landing Page View (MF1.6.1–2), indicating the destination page is not loading/rendering for a meaningful share of clickers.
- **Routes to:** Escalation outside this Framework's direct remediation scope (a landing-page/technical issue, not a Meta-Ads-configuration issue) — mirrors the CVR Decision Tree's (MF7.4.6) landing-page escalation branch.

### MF7.10.11 Poor Landing Page

- **Trigger Definition:** Healthy CTR and Landing Page View rate but weak downstream CVR, isolating the failure specifically to the post-arrival experience rather than any Meta-Ads-side lever.
- **Routes to:** The CVR Decision Tree's (MF7.4.6) landing-page escalation branch — explicitly not a Creative, Audience, or Bid action.

### MF7.10.12 Creative Fatigue

Cross-referenced from MF1.9.12/MF4.12. **Trigger Definition:** Declining Creative-attributed KPIs correlated with time-since-launch or Frequency. **Routes to:** MF7.5.1's Creative Fatigue Detection.

### MF7.10.13 Audience Fatigue

Cross-referenced from MF4.12.5/MF5.11. **Trigger Definition:** Persistent KPI decline surviving successive Creative-side fixes, isolating the cause to Audience exhaustion. **Routes to:** MF7.6.9's Audience Saturation handling (MF5.11.9's Decision Tree).

### MF7.10.14 Auction Competition

Cross-referenced from MF6.3.6/MF6.4.6/MF6.13.8. **Trigger Definition:** Rising CPM/CPA with no internal configuration change. **Routes to:** MF7.4.4's CPM Decision Tree's Seasonality-check branch.

### MF7.10.15 Delivery Problems

Cross-referenced from MF6.6/MF6.13 (No Delivery, Limited Delivery). **Trigger Definition:** Delivery Rate (MF6.12.10) or Budget Utilization (MF6.12.14) significantly below expectation. **Routes to:** MF6.11.7's Inventory Diagnostic Decision Tree.

### MF7.10.16 Learning Limited

Cross-referenced from MF6.5.2/MF6.6.3. **Trigger Definition:** Learning Progress (MF6.12.15) stalled short of Required Events (MF6.5.6) within the Optimization Window. **Routes to:** MF6.5.10's Recovery Strategy sequence, applied via this Framework's MF7.8.

### MF7.10.17 Limited Budget

Cross-referenced from MF6.6.6/MF6.7.5/MF6.13.5. **Trigger Definition:** Budget Utilization significantly below 100% despite adequate remaining period, with Bid Strategy confirmed as the constraint. **Routes to:** MF7.7.1's Budget Increase Rules (only after confirming the constraint is genuinely Budget, not Bid, per MF6.13.5's decomposition).

### MF7.10.18 Trigger Routing Summary

| Trigger | Primary Route |
|---|---|
| High CPA | CPA Tree (MF7.4.2) |
| Low ROAS | ROAS Tree (MF7.4.3) |
| High Frequency | Frequency Diagnostics (MF6.10.6) |
| Low CTR | CTR Tree (MF7.4.1) |
| High CPC | CPC Tree (MF7.4.5) |
| High CPM | CPM Tree (MF7.4.4) |
| Low CVR | CVR Tree (MF7.4.6) |
| Poor Hook Rate | Hook Optimization (MF7.5.4) |
| Poor Hold Rate | Creative Refresh/Video Length (MF7.5.3/10) |
| High Bounce | Escalation (outside Framework) |
| Poor Landing Page | Escalation (outside Framework) |
| Creative Fatigue | Fatigue Detection (MF7.5.1) |
| Audience Fatigue | Saturation Decision Tree (MF5.11.9) |
| Auction Competition | CPM Tree's Seasonality branch (MF7.4.4) |
| Delivery Problems | Inventory Decision Tree (MF6.11.7) |
| Learning Limited | Recovery Strategy (MF6.5.10/MF7.8) |
| Limited Budget | Budget Increase Rules (MF7.7.1) |

---

## MF7.11 Optimization Priority Matrix

### MF7.11.1 Priority Levels

| Priority | Definition | Response Time | Example Triggers |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Critical** | Directly threatens Financial viability or represents complete Delivery failure | Immediate (same day) | No Delivery (MF6.13.1), Low ROAS below breakeven (MF7.10.2), Rejected Status (MF6.6.5) |
| **High** | Materially degrading a Primary KPI (MF2.11.1/MF3.11.1) with a clear, actionable cause | Within 24–48 hours | High CPA (MF7.10.1), Learning Limited (MF7.10.16), Weak Ad Quality (MF6.13.10) |
| **Medium** | A Secondary KPI (MF2.11.2) degradation or an emerging Trend not yet Critical/High | Within the next scheduled Optimization cadence (MF7.12) | High Frequency approaching but not yet exceeding threshold (MF7.10.3), Poor Hold Rate (MF7.10.9) |
| **Low** | An efficiency opportunity rather than a failure state | Within the next Weekly/Biweekly review | Audience Consolidation opportunity (MF7.6.7), Scaling-readiness signal (MF6.14.6) |
| **Cosmetic** | No measurable KPI impact; naming, documentation, or Brand Consistency (MF4.17.7) items | Within the next scheduled Audit (MF6.17.6-style) | Naming Standard non-compliance (MF1.13.x), stale Documentation (MF4.17.2) |

### MF7.11.2 Priority Assignment Rule

Priority is assigned by combining Expected Value (MF6.15.6) — the product of the issue's probability of being genuine (Confidence Level, per every Diagnostics model in this document lineage) and its potential business-outcome magnitude — never by Trigger type alone. A "High CPA" Trigger (MF7.10.1) on a small-Budget test Ad Set may rank Medium in practice, while the same Trigger on the Business's largest Ad Set ranks Critical.

### MF7.11.3 Priority Escalation Rule

A Medium or Low Priority issue must be escalated to High if it persists unresolved across three or more consecutive scheduled review cycles (MF7.12) — sustained neglect of a minor issue is itself a Governance failure (MF7.17), independent of the issue's own original severity.

---

## MF7.12 Optimization Timing

### MF7.12.1 Daily Optimization

- **Scope:** Delivery Status (MF6.6) monitoring, Budget Pacing anomaly detection (MF6.7.8), Critical/High Priority Trigger response (MF7.11.1).
- **Not Appropriate For:** Any Creative/Audience/Bid Strategy change on an Ad Set still within Learning Phase (MF6.5.1) — daily-cadence monitoring does not mean daily-cadence editing.

### MF7.12.2 Weekly Optimization

- **Scope:** Medium Priority Trigger review, Creative KPI (MF4.9) trend review, Audience KPI (MF5.12) trend review, Frequency (MF6.10) monitoring.
- **Precondition:** Sufficient data accumulated per MF7.12.6's Minimum Data Requirements for the specific Metrics under review.

### MF7.12.3 Biweekly Optimization

- **Scope:** Scaling-readiness assessment (MF6.14.6), Audience Consolidation/Fragmentation review (MF7.6.7–8), Creative Testing Calendar (MF4.8.10) checkpoint.

### MF7.12.4 Monthly Optimization

- **Scope:** Objective-Specific Optimization Logic review (MF7.9) against updated Benchmark data (MF1.11.5), Budget Allocation review (MF7.7.9), Learning Risk audit (MF6.5.9).

### MF7.12.5 Quarterly Optimization

- **Scope:** Scaling Threshold recalibration (MF7.7.3) against matured Historical Signals (MF6.9.3), full Governance Audit (MF7.17) across every layer (MF2.13/MF3.15/MF4.17/MF5.18/MF6.17), Objective Maturity Model review (MF2.4.5).

### MF7.12.6 Minimum Data Requirements ("Do Not Optimize Too Early")

- **Rule:** No Optimization action (per MF7.1.1's definition) may be taken until: (1) the Ad Set has exited Learning Phase (Learning Complete, MF6.5.3) OR the action is itself a Learning-Phase Recovery Method (MF6.5.10); (2) a minimum of 48 hours' observation has elapsed for any single Creative-level change (MF7.4.1's tree); (3) a full Optimization Window (MF6.5.5, ~7 days) has elapsed for any Audience/Bid/Budget-level change; (4) Statistical Significance (MF1.11.7) is reached or, where impractical to formally test, the observation period is doubled from the above minimums before concluding.
- **Why:** Optimizing before these minimums are met is definitionally a Reaction (MF7.1.1), not an Optimization, regardless of the action taken — this rule is the operational enforcement mechanism for MF7.1.2's evidence requirement.
